Contraindications to vaccination that are commonly tested: live vaccines (MMR, LAIV, chickenpox) are contraindicated in immunocompromised children (including those on high-dose systemic corticosteroids — prednisolone above 2 mg/kg per day for more than one week, or above 1 mg/kg per day for more than one month). Anaphylaxis to a previous dose of the vaccine or a component (such as egg allergy for certain flu vaccines, though most flu vaccines in the UK schedule are now egg-free or low-egg-content and can be given safely to egg-allergic children in a primary care setting). There is no link between MMR vaccination and autism — the 1998 Lancet paper was retracted and the author struck off the GMC register.

Common Childhood Infections

Bronchiolitis

Bronchiolitis is the most common serious respiratory infection in infants under two years and peaks in winter. It is caused by respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) in the vast majority of cases. Clinical features: coryzal prodrome followed by tachypnoea, subcostal and intercostal recession, fine end-inspiratory crackles and wheeze, poor feeding, and oxygen desaturation. Diagnosis is clinical. Management is supportive: ensure adequate oxygenation (target SpO2 above 92%), maintain hydration (nasogastric feeding or IV fluids if oral intake is inadequate), and nurse in a slightly head-up position. Bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and antibiotics are not routinely recommended (NICE NG9). Criteria for hospital admission: SpO2 below 92%, respiratory rate above 70 in infants, severe recession, apnoeas, poor feeding, or age below six weeks. Nirsevimab (Beyfortus — a long-acting monoclonal antibody against RSV F protein) has been introduced in the UK for passive immunisation of all infants born in or entering their first RSV season.

Croup (Laryngotracheobronchitis)

Croup is viral laryngotracheobronchitis caused most commonly by parainfluenza virus, typically affecting children aged six months to three years. Presents with a barking seal-like cough, inspiratory stridor, and variable degrees of respiratory distress. The Westley Croup Score assesses severity based on stridor, retractions, air entry, cyanosis, and level of consciousness. Mild croup (Westley 0 to 2): reassurance and oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose. Moderate croup (Westley 3 to 5): oral or IM dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg, consider nebulised adrenaline (0.4 mL/kg of 1:1000 solution, maximum 5 mL). Severe croup (Westley 6 or above): nebulised adrenaline is the immediate treatment, dexamethasone, and senior airway support. Adrenaline has a short duration of action (up to two hours), so patients receiving it require observation for at least three to four hours for rebound stridor. A child with stridor at rest requires hospital admission.

Epiglottitis

Epiglottitis is a life-threatening bacterial infection of the epiglottis, classically caused by Haemophilus influenzae type b (now rare following Hib vaccination but still occurs). Presents with acute onset of high fever, drooling (inability to swallow secretions), muffled "hot-potato" voice, tripod position (sitting forward with neck extended and mouth open), and rapid deterioration. Critically, do NOT examine the throat, do NOT use a tongue depressor, and do NOT attempt IV access in a distressed child with suspected epiglottitis — any distress can precipitate complete airway obstruction. Call for senior help immediately (anaesthetics and ENT). Management is in theatre with an experienced team: gaseous induction of anaesthesia, direct laryngoscopy, intubation, and then IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone). The "cherry-red epiglottis" can be seen on laryngoscopy. On lateral neck X-ray, the "thumb sign" (swollen epiglottis resembling a thumb) is characteristic.

Scarlet Fever

Scarlet fever is caused by Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) producing erythrogenic toxins. It presents in school-aged children with fever, sore throat, and a characteristic fine sandpaper-like erythematous rash starting on the trunk and spreading to the limbs, sparing the perioral area (circumoral pallor). The tongue shows a white coating initially, then a strawberry tongue (prominent red papillae). Treatment is oral penicillin V or amoxicillin for ten days. Scarlet fever is a notifiable disease — the local health protection team must be informed.

Meningococcal Disease

Meningococcal disease (septicaemia with or without meningitis) caused by Neisseria meningitidis presents with fever, headache, vomiting, photophobia, neck stiffness, and — most importantly — a non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash. The rash indicates vasculitic skin involvement (meningococcaemia) and is associated with a high mortality. Immediate management: IV or IM benzylpenicillin (if no penicillin allergy) or ceftriaxone before hospital transfer. In hospital: blood cultures, LP (unless contraindicated), IV ceftriaxone, dexamethasone (reduces neurological sequelae of bacterial meningitis), and supportive care. Contacts of confirmed meningococcal disease receive chemoprophylaxis with ciprofloxacin or rifampicin. Note: LP is contraindicated if there are signs of raised intracranial pressure (papilloedema, focal neurological signs, reduced GCS, deteriorating consciousness), a coagulopathy, or haemodynamic instability — in these cases, give antibiotics and perform CT head first.

Asthma in Children

Asthma is the most common chronic condition of childhood in the UK. NICE guidance on paediatric asthma follows a stepwise approach similar to adults but with age-specific considerations. For children under five years: if wheeze is present, a trial of inhaled salbutamol (SABA via spacer and mask) is given. If recurrent wheeze occurs, a low-dose ICS trial (beclometasone 200 micrograms daily) is offered for eight weeks and continued only if there is a clear response. MART (Maintenance And Reliever Therapy — a single ICS-LABA inhaler used both as daily maintenance and as an as-needed reliever, such as Symbicort MART) is appropriate for children from twelve years of age and above in the UK, but is under evaluation for younger age groups. For children over five: stepwise management mirrors the adult pathway but with age-appropriate inhaler devices (spacer and mask for under three years, spacer and mouthpiece for three to five years, then dry powder or MDI without spacer for older children once technique is confirmed).

Acute severe asthma in children requires urgent assessment using PEFR (in those who can perform it — typically from age five to six years), oxygen saturation, heart rate, and respiratory rate. A PEFR below 33% predicted is life-threatening. Oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 94%, salbutamol nebulisers (driven by oxygen), ipratropium nebulisers every twenty minutes, IV/IM or oral prednisolone, and IV magnesium sulphate (single dose 40 mg/kg, max 2g over twenty minutes) for severe/life-threatening asthma not responding to initial treatment. Transfer to PICU if no improvement or deteriorating.

NICE Traffic Light System for Fever in Children

NICE NG143 provides a structured approach to the assessment of fever in children under five. The traffic light system identifies children at low (green), intermediate (amber), or high risk (red) of serious illness. Red features require immediate assessment by a senior clinician: cyanosis, SpO2 below 90%, abnormal skin colour (mottled, ashen, or blue), absent normal skin colour, non-blanching rash, bulging fontanelle, neck stiffness, focal neurological signs, RR above 60, moderate or severe subcostal recession, or an ill appearance. Amber features include pallor, tachycardia above age-specific thresholds, RR above 50 (age 6 to 12 months) or above 40 (age 12 to 24 months), not responding normally to social cues, reduced activity, fever above 39°C in two- to three-month-olds, or rigors. Age-specific tachycardia thresholds: above 160 bpm in under one year, above 150 bpm in one to two years, above 140 bpm in two to five years.

Paediatric Sepsis — NICE NG51 Thresholds

Sepsis thresholds in children differ from adults. Age-specific vital sign thresholds must be applied. In newborns (under one month): tachycardia above 180 bpm or bradycardia below 100 bpm, RR above 60, temperature above 38°C or below 36°C. In infants (one to three months): similar thresholds with additional consideration of prolonged capillary refill time (above two seconds). In older children: tachycardia and tachypnoea thresholds decrease progressively with age. The Paediatric Early Warning Score (PEWS) is used in most hospitals. If paediatric sepsis is suspected, the Sepsis 6 bundle is applied with age-appropriate fluid boluses (10 mL/kg crystalloid in most paediatric sepsis, not the 500 mL used in adults) and weight-based antibiotic dosing.

Safeguarding Children

NICE NG76 — When to Suspect Child Maltreatment

All healthcare professionals have a statutory duty to act on concerns about child maltreatment. NICE NG76 provides detailed guidance on which clinical features should raise concern. Physical abuse features: bruising in a non-mobile infant (any bruise in a baby not yet walking independently is highly concerning), bruising on the torso, back, abdomen, arms, buttocks, ears, or face in a child of any age, bruising in recognisable patterns of implements (linear, looped, circular), human bite marks, lacerations on areas not typically injured in play, burns in unusual distributions or with sharply demarcated borders suggesting deliberate immersion, unexplained fractures particularly in infants (posterior rib fractures are highly specific for non-accidental injury — caused by squeezing of the chest; metaphyseal corner fractures — "bucket handle fractures" — are caused by traction or twisting forces). Retinal haemorrhages (particularly multilayered, extending to the periphery) in infants are strongly associated with abusive head trauma (shaken baby syndrome / abusive head trauma).

Emotional abuse includes persistent emotional ill-treatment causing severe and persistent adverse effects on the child's emotional development. Features include: a child describing being made to feel worthless, unloved, or frightened, persistent low self-esteem or self-blame, and unusually compliant or fearful behaviour around parents or carers. Sexual abuse features: age-inappropriate sexual knowledge, sexualised behaviour or play, disclosure of abuse (always believe and record the child's words, do not ask leading questions), physical examination findings (rarely present, and the absence of physical findings does not exclude abuse). Neglect: persistent failure to meet a child's basic physical or psychological needs — features include faltering growth, poor hygiene, inadequate clothing for weather, dental caries, or untreated medical conditions.

Female genital mutilation (FGM) is illegal in the UK. If a girl under 18 has undergone or is at risk of FGM, there is a mandatory reporting duty for regulated health and social care professionals — report to the police. Child sexual exploitation (CSE) involves sexual abuse in the context of an exploitative relationship. It may not be immediately recognised as abuse by the young person and may involve grooming. Referral to Children's Social Care is mandatory if CSE is suspected. A child protection conference is convened when a multi-agency assessment concludes that a child has suffered or is at risk of significant harm — the outcome may be placement on the child protection register under one of the abuse categories.

Neonatal Conditions

Neonatal jaundice is extremely common — approximately 60% of term and 80% of preterm neonates develop visible jaundice in the first week of life. Physiological jaundice appears after 24 hours and resolves within two weeks. Jaundice appearing within 24 hours of birth is always pathological — most commonly due to haemolytic disease of the newborn (rhesus or ABO incompatibility). Jaundice persisting beyond two weeks in a term neonate or three weeks in a preterm neonate is prolonged and requires investigation for conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinaemia — biliary atresia is the most important cause to exclude as it requires Kasai portoenterostomy by eight weeks of life for the best outcome. Breastmilk jaundice is a common and benign cause of prolonged unconjugated jaundice in healthy, thriving breastfed infants. Management of neonatal jaundice uses phototherapy and exchange transfusion based on age-specific bilirubin thresholds.

Respiratory distress syndrome (RDS, formerly hyaline membrane disease) affects preterm neonates due to surfactant deficiency. Management includes antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone) to the mother if preterm delivery is anticipated, exogenous surfactant therapy to the neonate, and respiratory support (CPAP or mechanical ventilation). Necrotising enterocolitis (NEC) is a life-threatening condition predominantly in preterm neonates involving intestinal inflammation and necrosis. Features: abdominal distension, bloody stools, bilious vomiting, feed intolerance, and systemic instability. X-ray shows pneumatosis intestinalis (gas in the bowel wall) — pathognomonic. Treatment is bowel rest, IV antibiotics, and surgical resection if perforation occurs.

The UK newborn blood spot screening programme (heel-prick test) screens for: phenylketonuria, congenital hypothyroidism, cystic fibrosis, sickle cell disease, MCAD deficiency, maple syrup urine disease, isovaleric acidaemia, glutaric aciduria type 1, homocystinuria, and more recently, spinal muscular atrophy (SMA). Congenital hypothyroidism is screened by measuring TSH — early treatment with levothyroxine is essential to prevent intellectual disability.

High-Yield Paediatric Presentations

Intussusception

Intussusception occurs when a proximal segment of bowel telescopes into the adjacent distal segment, most commonly at the ileocaecal junction. It typically affects infants aged three months to two years and presents with intermittent, colicky abdominal pain causing the infant to draw up the legs and cry, vomiting (initially non-bilious then bilious), a sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant, and eventually "redcurrant jelly" stools (blood and mucus). Diagnosis is confirmed on ultrasound (doughnut sign or pseudokidney sign). Treatment is pneumatic or hydrostatic air/water enema reduction under radiological guidance. If unsuccessful or if there is peritonitis, surgical reduction is performed.

Pyloric Stenosis

Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis presents between two and eight weeks of age (peak at four to six weeks) with projectile non-bilious vomiting after feeds. The baby remains hungry after vomiting. Prolonged vomiting causes hypochloraemic, hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis (loss of hydrochloric acid in vomit, with paradoxical aciduria). An olive-shaped mass may be palpable in the right upper quadrant. Ultrasound confirms pyloric wall thickness above 4 mm and pyloric channel length above 16 mm. Treatment is surgical — Ramstedt's pyloromyotomy, after correcting the metabolic abnormality with IV fluid and electrolyte replacement.

Henoch-Schonlein Purpura

Henoch-Schonlein Purpura (HSP — now termed IgA vasculitis) is the most common systemic vasculitis of childhood, typically affecting children aged two to eleven years. The classic tetrad is: palpable purpuric rash on the buttocks and lower limbs (symmetrical, non-thrombocytopaenic), arthralgia (large joints — knees and ankles), colicky abdominal pain (can cause intussusception or GI bleeding), and renal involvement (haematuria, proteinuria — HSP nephritis). Investigations: urinalysis for blood and protein at presentation and at follow-up (renal disease can appear weeks later). Most cases are self-limiting. Management is supportive: analgesia for joint and abdominal pain, and monitoring for renal complications. Steroids are used for severe abdominal pain or nephritis.

Kawasaki Disease

Kawasaki disease is an acute self-limiting vasculitis predominantly affecting children under five years, particularly East Asian children. It is the most common cause of acquired heart disease in children in the developed world (coronary artery aneurysms). Diagnostic criteria require fever for five or more days plus four of five features: bilateral non-exudative conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue and cracked lips (changes in oropharynx), cervical lymphadenopathy (usually unilateral), polymorphous rash, and changes of the extremities (erythema and oedema of hands and feet acutely, with periungual desquamation in the subacute phase). Treatment: high-dose aspirin (anti-inflammatory during acute phase) plus IV immunoglobulin (IVIG — single dose reduces the risk of coronary aneurysm formation). Echocardiogram at diagnosis and follow-up. Note: aspirin is otherwise contraindicated in children under 16 due to the risk of Reye's syndrome, but is specifically indicated in Kawasaki disease.

Febrile Convulsions

Febrile convulsions (febrile seizures) occur in children between six months and five years and are the most common type of seizure in childhood, affecting 2 to 5% of children. They are triggered by a rapid rise in temperature rather than the absolute temperature. Simple febrile convulsions are generalised, last less than fifteen minutes, occur once in a twenty-four-hour period, and have complete recovery. Complex febrile convulsions are focal, last more than fifteen minutes, or recur within twenty-four hours — these require further investigation (EEG, MRI, consider LP to exclude meningitis). Management of an acute febrile convulsion: ABC, time the seizure, place in recovery position if possible, loosen clothing, do not restrain. If seizure lasts more than five minutes: administer midazolam buccal or diazepam rectal. Parents should be reassured that febrile convulsions are not associated with lasting neurological damage in simple cases, and that the risk of developing epilepsy is slightly increased but remains low (approximately 2 to 3%, compared to 0.5% in the general population).

Frequently Asked Questions About Paediatrics in the UKMLA

What developmental milestones are most tested in the AKT?

The most commonly tested milestones are the outer limits of normal — ages beyond which development should be investigated. Key red flags: no smile by ten weeks, not sitting unsupported by nine months, not walking by eighteen months, no single words by twelve months, no two-word phrases by twenty-four months. The AKT often presents a child at a given age and asks whether their development is normal or whether referral is indicated.

How do I differentiate croup from epiglottitis?

Croup presents with a gradual onset, barking cough, mild-to-moderate respiratory distress, and no drooling. The child is alert and distressed but not ill-appearing. Epiglottitis presents with sudden onset, high fever, severe toxicity, drooling, inability to swallow, no barking cough (voice is muffled), and the child sits in the characteristic tripod position. Any child with suspected epiglottitis should not have their throat examined and should go immediately to theatre for anaesthetic airway management.

Which findings on a CXR suggest pneumonia versus bronchiolitis in an infant?

Bronchiolitis shows hyperinflation (flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter) and peri-bronchial cuffing (thickening of bronchial walls) — a bilateral, diffuse pattern. Pneumonia shows lobar or segmental consolidation — an area of increased density (white) in a specific lobe. CXR is not routinely required for bronchiolitis unless there are atypical features or failure to respond to management. Conversely, CXR is useful in diagnosing pneumonia, although clinical diagnosis is often sufficient in children with characteristic features.

What is the first-line investigation for intussusception?

Ultrasound of the abdomen is the first-line investigation for suspected intussusception. It has high sensitivity and specificity and avoids radiation exposure. Plain abdominal X-ray may show signs suggestive of bowel obstruction but is less specific. Pneumatic or hydrostatic enema (performed under fluoroscopic or ultrasound guidance) is both diagnostic and therapeutic — it confirms and reduces the intussusception in approximately 80 to 90% of cases.

When is aspirin appropriate in children?

Aspirin is generally contraindicated in children and young people under sixteen due to the risk of Reye's syndrome (a rare but serious condition causing liver failure and encephalopathy following aspirin use in viral illnesses). However, aspirin is specifically indicated in two paediatric conditions: Kawasaki disease (high dose anti-inflammatory during the acute phase, then low-dose antiplatelet), and certain thromboembolic conditions under specialist guidance.

Conclusion

Paediatrics is a broad, high-yield domain that repays systematic revision across developmental milestones, immunisations, common infections, safeguarding, and emergency presentations. Mastering the NICE traffic light fever tool, knowing the UK vaccination schedule, and being confident with safeguarding recognition will consistently gain marks across the AKT and CPSA. For a full guide to UKMLA preparation, visit /preparation, and review what to expect in the clinical skills examination at /exam-pattern/cpsa.

post_title: Emergency Medicine and Acute Presentations in the UKMLA
post_excerpt: Acute and emergency presentations are heavily represented in the UKMLA. This guide covers the highest-yield scenarios and the management frameworks that score marks.
seo_title: Emergency Medicine in the UKMLA: High-Yield Revision Guide
seo_description: Acute presentations are heavily tested in the UKMLA AKT and CPSA. Our guide covers ABCDE, sepsis, anaphylaxis, chest pain, and the scenarios that score most marks.
html_content:
Emergency medicine and acute presentations for the UKMLA represent some of the most critical and frequently examined content across both the AKT and CPSA, because the GMC's primary concern at the point of licensure is ensuring that newly qualified doctors can recognise and initiate management of life-threatening conditions safely and rapidly. Every scenario involving an acutely unwell patient — from a deteriorating ward patient triggering a crash call to a patient presenting to the emergency department in anaphylaxis — requires a structured, systematic approach that the UKMLA tests directly.

The ABCDE Approach

The ABCDE approach is the cornerstone of acute patient assessment and is the framework within which all acute emergency management is organised. It is tested across both the AKT (clinical scenarios asking what to do first or next) and the CPSA (where candidates are expected to demonstrate a structured assessment in simulated acute scenarios). The approach is iterative — reassess after every intervention before moving to the next stage.

A — Airway: Is the airway patent? Listen for stridor, gurgling, or silence. If not patent, simple manoeuvres first (jaw thrust, head-tilt chin-lift, suction), then adjuncts (oropharyngeal or nasopharyngeal airway), then definitive airway (intubation or surgical airway via cricothyroidotomy if intubation fails). B — Breathing: Rate, depth, symmetry, oxygen saturation. Apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreathe mask (15 L/min) to any acutely unwell patient unless COPD with known type 2 respiratory failure (target SpO2 88 to 92% in type 2 RF, 94 to 98% in all others). C — Circulation: Heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill time (normal under two seconds), skin colour and temperature, IV access, bloods and ECG. If haemodynamically compromised, fluid challenge (250 to 500 mL crystalloid bolus in adults, 10 mL/kg in children) and identify source of compromise. D — Disability: AVPU scale (Alert, Voice, Pain, Unresponsive) or GCS (Eyes 4 + Verbal 5 + Motor 6 = 15). Check blood glucose — treat hypoglycaemia if present. Pupillary responses. E — Exposure: Full examination, temperature, check skin for rash (purpura, urticaria), look for obvious injuries. Maintain dignity and prevent hypothermia.

NEWS2 Scoring

NEWS2 (National Early Warning Score 2) is the UK standardised early warning scoring system recommended by NHS England for adult inpatients. Six physiological parameters are each scored 0 to 3: respiration rate, oxygen saturation (SpO2 — modified for patients with hypercapnic respiratory failure, using SpO2 Scale 2 target 88 to 92%), any supplemental oxygen, systolic blood pressure, pulse rate, and level of consciousness (using AVPU — a new confusion scores 3). Temperature is also scored. A cumulative NEWS2 score triggers escalation: score 0 to 4 is routine monitoring; score 5 to 6 or any single parameter scoring 3 requires urgent medical review; score 7 or above constitutes a medical emergency requiring immediate review by a senior clinician and critical care assessment.

ParameterScore 3Score 2Score 1Score 0Score 1Score 2Score 3
Respiration rate (breaths/min)≤89–1112–2021–24≥25
SpO2 Scale 1 (%)≤9192–9394–95≥96
Systolic BP (mmHg)≤9091–100101–110111–219≥220
Pulse rate (bpm)≤4041–5051–9091–110111–130≥131
ConsciousnessAlertCVPU (confusion/V/P/U)
Temperature (°C)≤35.035.1–36.036.1–38.038.1–39.0≥39.1

Sepsis

Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. NICE NG51 defines sepsis using the Sepsis 6 care bundle, which should be delivered within one hour of recognition for high-risk patients. The six elements are: take blood cultures (before antibiotics if possible but do not delay antibiotics to obtain cultures), measure serum lactate (lactate above 2 mmol/L indicates tissue hypoperfusion; above 4 mmol/L is a medical emergency), administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (choice depends on likely source — community-acquired infection: piperacillin-tazobactam or co-amoxiclav plus a macrolide for CAP; hospital-acquired: meropenem or piperacillin-tazobactam), commence IV fluid resuscitation (500 mL crystalloid bolus over 15 minutes for adults with suspected septic shock or hypoperfusion, then reassess), ensure adequate urine output (catheterise and target above 0.5 mL/kg/hour), and apply high-flow oxygen.

Septic shock is diagnosed when a patient with sepsis requires vasopressors to maintain a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 65 mmHg or above, and has a serum lactate above 2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Vasopressors of choice: noradrenaline is first-line. Vasopressin can be added as a second agent. Dobutamine is added if there is myocardial dysfunction causing low cardiac output. Source control — draining an abscess, removing an infected urinary catheter, or debriding infected tissue — is a critical component of sepsis management that is often underemphasised in exam preparation.

Anaphylaxis

Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening generalised or systemic hypersensitivity reaction. Triggers include drugs (most commonly penicillins, NSAIDs, contrast media), foods (nuts, shellfish, dairy), insect stings, and latex. Clinical features develop rapidly (minutes to hours after exposure) and involve two or more body systems: cutaneous or mucosal changes (urticaria, angioedema, flushing), respiratory compromise (wheeze, stridor, dyspnoea), cardiovascular compromise (hypotension, tachycardia, collapse), and GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain). A severe allergic reaction involving the airway or cardiovascular system confirms anaphylaxis and demands immediate treatment.

Immediate management: call for help, lie the patient flat with legs elevated (or upright if respiratory distress predominates — do not make the patient sit up if cardiovascular compromise is the main feature). Adrenaline 0.5 mg (500 micrograms) IM into the anterolateral thigh is the first-line treatment and should be given immediately. IM not IV is used in most cases because IM administration has a safer pharmacokinetic profile in non-monitored settings; IV adrenaline requires cardiac monitoring and specialist administration. The dose can be repeated at five-minute intervals if there is no improvement. High-flow oxygen. IV fluid resuscitation (500 mL to 1000 mL crystalloid bolus). Antihistamine (chlorphenamine 10 mg IM or slow IV) — adjunct only, does not treat the life-threatening features. Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV — adjunct only, does not act immediately but may prevent protracted reactions. Observe for a minimum of six to twelve hours after resolution due to the risk of a biphasic reaction (second wave of anaphylaxis occurring four to twelve hours after initial event, in approximately 5 to 20% of cases). On discharge, prescribe two adrenaline auto-injectors (EpiPens), provide written instructions on use, and arrange follow-up with an allergist.

Acute Coronary Syndromes

ACS encompasses STEMI, NSTEMI, and unstable angina. STEMI is defined by typical ischaemic symptoms plus persistent ST elevation of at least 1 mm in two or more contiguous limb leads, or at least 2 mm in two or more contiguous precordial leads, or new left bundle branch block, plus positive troponin. NSTEMI has positive troponin without ST elevation. Unstable angina has ischaemic symptoms at rest with ECG changes but no troponin rise.

STEMI management: primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PPCI) is the preferred reperfusion strategy if it can be delivered within 120 minutes of diagnosis (door-to-balloon time target: within 90 minutes of first medical contact). If PPCI is not achievable within 120 minutes, thrombolysis (tenecteplase, alteplase) is given. Antiplatelet therapy: aspirin 300 mg loading dose plus a P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor 180 mg loading dose preferred, or prasugrel, or clopidogrel if neither is available). Anticoagulation: UFH or LMWH or bivalirudin during PPCI. Oxygen: only if SpO2 below 94% (high-flow oxygen in normoxic patients is no longer recommended as it may increase myocardial injury). Morphine (or diamorphine) for pain — titrate to effect. GTN (sublingual or IV) for ongoing chest pain unless systolic BP below 90 mmHg or inferior STEMI with possible RV involvement (beware preload dependence). Beta-blocker (metoprolol or atenolol) if not contraindicated. ACE inhibitor from day one in all STEMI patients without contraindications. Statin (atorvastatin 80 mg) from day one.

NSTEMI management: risk-stratify using GRACE score (determines in-hospital and six-month mortality risk). Antiplatelet: aspirin 300 mg plus ticagrelor or clopidogrel. Anticoagulation: fondaparinux (preferred) or LMWH. Early invasive strategy (coronary angiography within 24 to 72 hours) for intermediate-to-high GRACE scores. Conservative management with optimal medical therapy for low GRACE scores.

Acute Pulmonary Oedema

Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (APO) results from elevated pulmonary venous pressure causing fluid transudation into the alveoli. Causes include STEMI, acute mitral regurgitation, hypertensive emergency, or decompensated chronic heart failure. Clinical features: severe breathlessness, orthopnoea, frothy pink sputum, widespread crepitations to the mid-zones and above, and gallop rhythm on auscultation. CXR shows bat-wing perihilar oedema, Kerley B lines (horizontal interstitial lines at the lung periphery), upper lobe blood diversion, and cardiomegaly. Immediate management: sit the patient upright (reduces preload), high-flow oxygen to maintain SpO2 above 94%, IV furosemide 40 to 80 mg (venodilatory effect within minutes, diuresis over one to two hours), sublingual or IV GTN (if systolic BP above 110 mmHg) as a vasodilator to reduce preload and afterload, CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) if available — reduces the work of breathing and improves oxygenation, and has been shown in trials to reduce the need for intubation and reduce mortality. IV diamorphine was previously used as an anxiolytic and venodilator but is now used with caution — evidence base is less robust and respiratory depression is a concern. Treat the underlying cause — ACS, hypertension, arrhythmia.

Acute Severe Asthma

Acute severe asthma in adults is defined by any of: PEFR 33 to 50% of best or predicted, RR above 25 breaths/min, HR above 110 bpm, or inability to complete sentences in one breath. Life-threatening asthma features: PEFR below 33%, SpO2 below 92%, silent chest, cyanosis, feeble respiratory effort, altered consciousness, bradycardia, or hypotension. Near-fatal asthma: raised PaCO2 (above 6.0 kPa) or requiring mechanical ventilation. Management: high-flow oxygen (via non-rebreathe mask) to maintain SpO2 above 94%, back-to-back salbutamol nebulisers (2.5 to 5 mg) driven by oxygen, ipratropium bromide 500 micrograms nebuliser (added if severe or life-threatening), oral prednisolone 40 to 50 mg (or IV hydrocortisone 100 mg if unable to take orally), IV magnesium sulphate 1.2 to 2 g over twenty minutes (for severe/life-threatening asthma not responding to initial bronchodilators — single dose only). Criteria for ITU referral: deteriorating PEFR despite treatment, hypoxia, rising PaCO2, exhaustion, confusion, or respiratory arrest.

Status Epilepticus

Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting more than five minutes, or two or more seizures within five minutes without return to baseline consciousness between them. Management follows a stepwise benzodiazepine ladder. Phase 1 (0 to 5 minutes): IV lorazepam 4 mg (if IV access available) or buccal midazolam 10 mg or rectal diazepam 10 mg (if no IV access). If no response after ten minutes, give a second dose of benzodiazepine. Phase 2 (10 to 20 minutes, if benzodiazepines have failed): IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (max 4500 mg) over five minutes, or IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg at maximum 50 mg/min with ECG monitoring (risk of cardiac arrhythmia and hypotension), or IV sodium valproate 40 mg/kg (max 3000 mg) over five minutes (avoid in women of childbearing potential). Phase 3 (20 to 40 minutes, refractory status epilepticus): call anaesthetics for rapid sequence intubation (RSI) and general anaesthesia. Treat reversible causes throughout: hypoglycaemia (check BM and give 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV or Glucogel buccally), hypoxia, hyponatraemia, alcohol withdrawal (give thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy), meningitis (give ceftriaxone empirically), or non-convulsive status epilepticus (NCSE — requires EEG for diagnosis).

Diabetic Ketoacidosis

DKA is a medical emergency characterised by the triad of ketonaemia (blood ketones above 3 mmol/L or urinary ketones 2+ or above), hyperglycaemia (blood glucose above 11 mmol/L — or known diabetes with any glucose), and metabolic acidosis (venous pH below 7.3 or bicarbonate below 15 mmol/L). Causes include new-onset T1DM, intercurrent illness, missed insulin doses, and occasionally DKA in Type 2 diabetes (particularly in certain ethnic groups on SGLT-2 inhibitors — euglycaemic DKA). The Joint British Diabetes Societies (JBDS) 2023 guidelines are the operative guidance. Key principles of DKA management:

IV fluid resuscitation: 500 mL 0.9% NaCl bolus over fifteen minutes if systolic BP below 90 mmHg. Then 1 litre 0.9% NaCl over one hour, 1 litre over two hours, 1 litre over two hours, 1 litre over four hours, 1 litre over four hours (total approximately 4 litres in first eight hours). Once blood glucose falls to 14 mmol/L, add 10% dextrose at 125 mL/hour alongside 0.9% NaCl to prevent hypoglycaemia while the fixed-rate insulin infusion continues to clear ketones. Fixed-rate insulin infusion (FRIII): 0.1 units/kg/hour of Actrapid or Novorapid in 0.9% NaCl (not in glucose). Do NOT give bolus insulin at presentation. The FRIII continues until ketones clear (below 0.3 mmol/L), pH normalises, and the patient can eat and drink — then switch to subcutaneous insulin (restart background insulin immediately when switching). Potassium replacement: DKA patients are total-body potassium depleted despite apparently normal or high serum levels (intracellular shift). Add potassium to IV fluids: if K+ 3.5 to 5.5 mmol/L, add 40 mmol KCl per litre; if K+ above 5.5 mmol/L, no added K; if K+ below 3.5 mmol/L, senior review — may need higher rate. Do not start insulin if K+ below 3.5 mmol/L until corrected. Monitoring: hourly capillary blood glucose and ketones, hourly fluid input/output, two-hourly U&E, four-hourly VBG for pH and bicarbonate. Target: ketone fall of at least 0.5 mmol/L/hour, glucose fall of at least 3 mmol/L/hour, bicarbonate rise of at least 3 mmol/L/hour.

Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State

HHS (previously HONK — hyperosmolar non-ketotic coma) occurs predominantly in Type 2 diabetes. It is characterised by severe hyperglycaemia (blood glucose typically above 30 mmol/L), marked hyperosmolality (above 320 mOsm/kg), profound dehydration, and absence of significant ketonaemia (ketones below 3 mmol/L) or acidosis. The patient is typically elderly and may present with confusion, focal neurological signs, or seizures. Management: rehydrate slowly — 0.9% NaCl as the preferred fluid; correct osmolality over 48 hours to avoid cerebral oedema. Low-dose insulin infusion or sliding scale is introduced later (once glucose fails to fall adequately with fluids alone) to avoid precipitous drops. VTE prophylaxis is essential as HHS carries a very high thrombotic risk. Patients in HHS often have significant potassium losses and require careful replacement.

Hyperkalaemia

Hyperkalaemia (K+ above 5.5 mmol/L) is a medical emergency at levels above 6.5 mmol/L, or at any level with ECG changes or symptoms. Causes include AKI/CKD, Addison's disease, drugs (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics, LMWH, NSAIDs, SGLT-2 inhibitors), acidosis, and tissue destruction (rhabdomyolysis, burns, massive haemolysis). ECG changes in progressive order: peaked (tall, narrow, tented) T waves → PR interval prolongation → P wave flattening and disappearance → QRS widening → sine wave pattern → ventricular fibrillation. Management: calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV over two to five minutes (membrane stabilisation — acts within minutes, does not lower potassium). Insulin-dextrose: ten units Actrapid in 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV over fifteen to thirty minutes (shifts K+ into cells — lowers K+ by 0.5 to 1.5 mmol/L within fifteen to thirty minutes). Salbutamol nebuliser 10 to 20 mg (also shifts K+ into cells — additive effect with insulin). Sodium bicarbonate if acidosis is contributing. Calcium resonium (sodium or calcium polystyrene sulfonate) or patiromer — binds K+ in the gut and promotes faecal excretion (takes twelve to twenty-four hours). Furosemide if renal function is adequate to promote renal K+ excretion. Haemodialysis for severe, refractory hyperkalaemia or if end-stage renal failure.

Stroke and TIA

Stroke is a clinical diagnosis: sudden onset of focal neurological deficit, lasting more than twenty-four hours or causing death. TIA (transient ischaemic attack) causes focal neurological deficit that completely resolves within twenty-four hours. The FAST acronym (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 999) has been superseded by BE-FAST (Balance, Eyes, Face, Arms, Speech, Time) to include posterior circulation symptoms. NIHSS (National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale) quantifies neurological deficit and guides treatment decisions.

Immediate management: CT head without contrast is the first investigation — this excludes haemorrhagic stroke (which appears hyperdense on CT) before thrombolysis. If ischaemic stroke is confirmed (hypodense area on CT, or CT normal within first few hours) and onset is within four and a half hours, IV alteplase (thrombolysis) is given at 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) — ten percent as IV bolus, remainder as infusion over sixty minutes. Mechanical thrombectomy (endovascular clot retrieval) is recommended for large vessel occlusion strokes (anterior circulation) if treatable within six hours (or up to twenty-four hours in selected patients with penumbra on imaging). Aspirin 300 mg is given within twenty-four hours of ischaemic stroke (but not for twenty-four hours post-thrombolysis) and continued for two weeks before switching to long-term antiplatelet therapy. Admit to a stroke unit — this is the single most effective stroke intervention and reduces mortality and dependency regardless of stroke type.

TIA risk is scored using the ABCD2 score: Age (60 or above = 1), Blood pressure (140/90 or above = 1), Clinical features (unilateral weakness = 2, speech disturbance without weakness = 1), Duration (60 minutes or more = 2, ten to fifty-nine minutes = 1), Diabetes (1). Score of 4 or above indicates high risk — specialist TIA clinic within 24 hours. Any TIA or minor stroke should receive aspirin 300 mg immediately, dual antiplatelet for the first three weeks (aspirin plus clopidogrel as per POINT and CHANCE trials), then long-term aspirin or clopidogrel monotherapy. Carotid imaging (Doppler or CTA) should be performed urgently for anterior circulation TIA — if carotid stenosis above 50%, carotid endarterectomy is recommended within two weeks.

Bacterial Meningitis

Bacterial meningitis presents with fever, severe headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, and altered consciousness. Non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash suggests meningococcal disease and indicates septicaemia. In a patient without contraindications, lumbar puncture is the definitive diagnostic investigation — opening pressure, CSF appearance, cell count, protein and glucose, Gram stain, and culture. Contraindications to LP: papilloedema, focal neurological deficit, GCS below 12, coagulopathy, or haemodynamic instability. If any contraindication is present: take blood cultures, give IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2 g IV) and dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg QDS for four days) immediately, then perform CT head before LP. Dexamethasone reduces neurological sequelae (particularly hearing loss in Streptococcus pneumoniae meningitis) and should be given with or before the first antibiotic dose.

Major Trauma

Major trauma is assessed using the primary survey: C-ABCDE — Catastrophic haemorrhage (stop exsanguinating haemorrhage first — tourniquet or direct pressure), then Airway (with C-spine protection), Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure. Haemostatic resuscitation (damage control resuscitation) involves transfusing packed red cells, fresh frozen plasma, and platelets in a 1:1:1 ratio to manage traumatic coagulopathy. Permissive hypotension (targeting systolic BP of approximately 80 to 90 mmHg) until haemorrhage is controlled avoids diluting clotting factors. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within three hours of major haemorrhage has been shown in the CRASH-2 trial to reduce mortality. Massive transfusion protocol (MTP) is activated for ongoing blood loss requiring more than ten units of red cells in twenty-four hours. Whole blood is increasingly used in some trauma centres. Damage control surgery involves temporising surgical intervention to control haemorrhage and contamination, followed by definitive repair once the patient is physiologically stabilised (the "lethal triad" of hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy is corrected).

Table: Key Emergency Drugs and Doses

EmergencyDrugDose and RouteNotes
AnaphylaxisAdrenaline0.5 mg (500 mcg) IM anterolateral thighRepeat every 5 min if no improvement; 1:1000 solution = 0.5 mL
AnaphylaxisChlorphenamine10 mg IM or slow IVAdjunct only — does not treat airway or cardiovascular compromise
AnaphylaxisHydrocortisone200 mg IVAdjunct — reduces risk of protracted reaction
HyperkalaemiaCalcium gluconate 10%10 mL IV over 2–5 minMembrane stabilisation — does not lower K+; repeat if ECG changes persist
HyperkalaemiaInsulin-dextrose10 units Actrapid in 50 mL 50% dextrose IV over 15–30 minShifts K+ into cells; monitor glucose
Status epilepticusLorazepam4 mg IV (or midazolam 10 mg buccal if no IV)First-line; repeat once after 10 min if no response
DKAFixed-rate insulin infusion0.1 units/kg/hour Actrapid in 0.9% NaClContinue until ketones clear; do not bolus
StrokeAlteplase0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg); 10% as bolus, 90% over 60 minWithin 4.5 hours of onset; exclude haemorrhage first
MeningitisCeftriaxone2 g IV (adult)With dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg QDS; do not delay for LP if suspected
SepsisPiperacillin-tazobactam4.5 g IV TDSLocal antimicrobial policy takes precedence
Acute severe asthmaMagnesium sulphate1.2–2 g IV over 20 minSingle dose for severe or life-threatening asthma not responding to bronchodilators

Frequently Asked Questions About Emergency Medicine in the UKMLA

What is the correct order of management in anaphylaxis — should I give antihistamines before adrenaline?

No. Adrenaline is always the first and most important treatment in anaphylaxis. Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts and should only be given after adrenaline. A common mistake is to reach for antihistamines first because they feel safer — this delays life-saving treatment. The mnemonic is: call for help, lie flat, adrenaline IM, then oxygen, fluids, and adjuncts.

When should I give oxygen to a COPD patient in an emergency?

In an acute emergency involving a known COPD patient, give oxygen if the patient is hypoxic, and target SpO2 88 to 92% using controlled oxygen delivery (Venturi mask at 24 to 28%). However, if the patient is in extremis (SpO2 below 85%, cyanotic, haemodynamically compromised), treat immediately with high-flow oxygen first and reassess. The risk of over-oxygenation in type 2 respiratory failure is real but should never prevent treating life-threatening hypoxia. Use ABG to guide ongoing oxygen therapy.

How do I differentiate DKA from HHS?

DKA occurs predominantly in Type 1 diabetes, with blood glucose typically 11 to 30 mmol/L, significant ketonaemia (blood ketones above 3 mmol/L), and metabolic acidosis (pH below 7.3). HHS occurs predominantly in Type 2 diabetes, with very high blood glucose (commonly above 30 mmol/L), very high osmolality (above 320 mOsm/kg), absent or mild ketonaemia, and no significant acidosis. DKA presents more acutely (hours); HHS develops over days to weeks.

What is the Sepsis 6 and when should it be used?

The Sepsis 6 is a bundle of six care elements to be delivered within one hour of recognising high-risk sepsis: take blood cultures, measure lactate, give IV antibiotics, start IV fluids (crystalloid), measure urine output, and give high-flow oxygen. It is used for patients with suspected sepsis who have red flag features (systolic BP below 90 mmHg, HR above 130 bpm, RR above 25, or NEWS2 score of 7 or above, new confusion, or lactate above 2 mmol/L). Early implementation of the Sepsis 6 has been shown to reduce mortality by 46% in UK studies.

What ECG change is most specific for pulmonary embolism?

Sinus tachycardia is the most common ECG finding in PE, but is non-specific. The S1Q3T3 pattern (deep S wave in lead I, Q wave and inverted T wave in lead III) is the classic teaching sign but is actually seen in only around 20% of PE cases. New right bundle branch block (RBBB) and right axis deviation are more specific signs of right heart strain from PE. The most specific finding, when present, is a combination of sinus tachycardia with new RBBB and right axis deviation in a patient with a clinical presentation consistent with PE.

Conclusion

Emergency medicine and acute presentations form the backbone of what the GMC expects every doctor to manage on day one of their career. The structured ABCDE approach, NEWS2 scoring, and the specific management pathways for sepsis, anaphylaxis, DKA, ACS, stroke, and status epilepticus are among the most heavily tested topics in the entire UKMLA. For a full guide to your exam preparation strategy, visit /preparation, and for details on how emergency medicine is represented in the AKT blueprint, see /exam-pattern/akt.

post_title: Radiology and Imaging Interpretation in the AKT
post_excerpt: The AKT includes data interpretation questions involving X-rays, CT findings, and other imaging. Here is what Foundation-level interpretation skills you need.
seo_title: Radiology and Imaging in the UKMLA AKT: What You Need to Know
seo_description: Imaging interpretation appears in the UKMLA AKT. Learn which radiology skills are tested at foundation level, how to approach chest X-rays, and common findings.
html_content:
Radiology and medical imaging for the UKMLA AKT is a topic area that consistently surprises candidates who underestimate its scope — it is not simply about recognising a pneumothorax on a chest X-ray, but extends to understanding which imaging modality to request, when to avoid contrast, how to interpret common CT and MRI findings, and how the IRMER regulations govern imaging requests in clinical practice. A systematic approach to image interpretation is tested in the AKT through clinical vignettes with imaging findings, and the ability to request appropriate investigations is assessed throughout both papers.

Systematic Chest X-Ray Interpretation

The chest X-ray (CXR) is the most commonly requested radiological investigation in hospital medicine and is heavily tested in the UKMLA. A systematic approach prevents missed diagnoses and is expected in both AKT extended matching questions and CPSA data interpretation stations. The most widely taught mnemonic for CXR interpretation is ABCDE: Airway, Breathing, Cardiac, Diaphragm and below, Everything else.

A — Airway

Assess the trachea: it should be central, or very slightly deviated to the right in normal adults due to the aortic arch. Significant tracheal deviation is an important sign: deviation away from the affected side suggests a tension pneumothorax, large pleural effusion, or haemothorax pushing the mediastinum away; deviation towards the affected side suggests lobar collapse or pulmonary fibrosis pulling the mediastinum across. Check the carina — the angle of the carina (normally less than 70 degrees) widens with left atrial enlargement as the left main bronchus is pushed inferiorly. Confirm an endotracheal tube tip (if present) is at or just below the clavicular heads, above the carina — ideally 3 to 5 cm above the carina.

B — Breathing (Lung Fields)

Compare both lung fields systematically, starting at the apices and working down. Normal lung appears uniformly black (air-filled) with visible lung markings extending to the periphery. Key abnormal findings:

Consolidation: increased opacity (white) within the lung fields with air bronchograms (visible air in the bronchi within consolidated lung — confirms the opacity is in the lung parenchyma, not in the pleural space). Consolidation has a non-geometric pattern that follows the shape of the lobe or segment. Common causes: bacterial pneumonia (lobar or segmental), aspiration pneumonia (right lower lobe most common due to the vertical right main bronchus), pulmonary infarction from PE (classically a "Hampton's hump" — a wedge-shaped peripheral opacity abutting the pleura).

Pneumothorax: absence of lung markings beyond a visible white pleural line. The lung edge is seen as a sharply defined white line with no lung markings beyond it. On an expiratory film, a small pneumothorax is more easily seen. Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis: the patient is haemodynamically compromised with tracheal deviation away from the pneumothorax and absent breath sounds. Do not wait for a CXR if tension pneumothorax is suspected — immediate needle decompression in the second intercostal space, mid-clavicular line.

Pleural effusion: a pleural effusion appears as increased opacity in the lower zones. A small effusion (above 200 mL) blunts the costophrenic angle on the PA film. A moderate effusion shows a meniscus sign — a curved upper border of the opacity, highest laterally and lowest medially, due to the capillary action of fluid along the pleural surface. A large effusion causes a completely white hemithorax. On a supine film (AP portable CXR), a pleural effusion causes diffuse haziness over the lung rather than a meniscus, because the fluid layers posteriorly.

Pulmonary oedema: cardiogenic pulmonary oedema has a characteristic appearance. Upper lobe blood diversion (vessels in the upper zones appear larger than those in the lower zones — the reverse of normal). Kerley B lines: short horizontal white lines perpendicular to the pleural surface at the lung periphery (costophrenic angles), representing oedema in the interlobular septa. Bat-wing or perihilar oedema: bilateral, confluent, perihilar opacities giving a characteristic bat-wing appearance. Pleural effusions (bilateral). Cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio above 0.5 on a PA film).

C — Cardiac

Assess cardiac size: cardiothoracic ratio (CTR) is the maximum transverse cardiac diameter divided by the maximum transverse thoracic diameter on a PA film. Normal CTR is below 0.5. Cardiomegaly (CTR above 0.5) suggests cardiac failure, pericardial effusion, or dilated cardiomyopathy. Pericardial effusion: the cardiac silhouette is enlarged with a globular, flask-shaped appearance and clear lung fields (important distinction from pulmonary oedema, which would show bilateral lung changes).

Mediastinal widening: a widened mediastinum (more than 8 cm at the level of the aortic knuckle on a PA film, or any mediastinal widening on a portable AP film which artificially magnifies the mediastinum) raises the concern for aortic dissection. Other causes include thoracic aortic aneurysm, mediastinal lymphadenopathy (malignancy, sarcoidosis, infection), and superior vena cava obstruction.

D — Diaphragm and Below

Both hemidiaphragms should be visible and dome-shaped. The right hemidiaphragm is usually slightly higher than the left (due to the liver below). Loss of the diaphragmatic outline (silhouette sign) indicates adjacent consolidation — loss of the right lower hemidiaphragm outline suggests right lower lobe consolidation; loss of the left heart border outline suggests lingular (left upper lobe) consolidation. Free air under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on an erect CXR or erect AXR appears as a crescent of air beneath the diaphragm — caused by bowel perforation. Check the subdiaphragmatic area for air and the costophrenic angles for pleural fluid.

E — Everything Else

Bones: look systematically at all visible ribs, clavicles, and vertebrae for fractures, lytic lesions, or sclerotic deposits. Rib fractures in unusual patterns (posterior rib fractures, multiple ribs at different stages of healing) should raise concern for non-accidental injury in children. Sclerotic (dense, white) lesions in vertebral bodies — consider bony metastases (blastic — prostate, breast). Lytic (dark) vertebral lesions — myeloma, lytic metastases. Soft tissues: check for surgical emphysema (subcutaneous air — feels crepitant clinically, appears as air tracking in the soft tissues on CXR), pacemaker leads and devices, surgical clips, and indwelling lines (central line tip should be in the SVC, not in the right ventricle — risk of arrhythmia if too far).

Abdominal X-Ray Interpretation

The abdominal X-ray (AXR) is less commonly requested now that CT scanning is so accessible, but it remains useful in specific situations and is tested in the AKT. Key findings:

Bowel Gas Pattern

Normal bowel contains small amounts of gas in the stomach and colon. The small bowel is centrally located, has a valvulae conniventes (circular folds that go completely across the bowel lumen), and is typically less than 3 cm in diameter. The large bowel is peripheral (frames the abdomen), has haustral folds (semilunar folds that do not cross the lumen completely), and is typically less than 6 cm in diameter (caecum less than 9 cm). Dilated small bowel (above 3 cm) with multiple air-fluid levels suggests small bowel obstruction. Dilated large bowel (above 6 cm) without small bowel dilatation suggests large bowel obstruction or pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's syndrome). In sigmoid volvulus, the dilated sigmoid colon has a characteristic coffee bean or inverted U shape arising from the pelvis. In caecal volvulus, a dilated caecum appears in the left upper quadrant or central abdomen.

Rigler Sign (Double Wall Sign)

In a normal AXR, only the inner (mucosal) wall of the bowel is visible because there is no air outside the bowel. In pneumoperitoneum (free intraperitoneal air), both the inner and outer bowel walls become visible because air on both sides of the wall outlines both surfaces — this is the Rigler sign. It indicates bowel perforation until proved otherwise.

Calcification

Renal calculi: approximately 90% of renal calculi are radio-opaque (calcium-containing stones) and visible on AXR — they appear as dense white opacities overlying the renal shadow, proximal ureter, or urinary bladder. Uric acid stones are radiolucent (not visible on AXR) and require CT KUB. Gallstones: only approximately 10 to 20% of gallstones are radio-opaque; most are not visible on AXR. CT or ultrasound is the appropriate investigation for suspected biliary pathology. Vascular calcification: linear calcification in the aortic wall or iliac vessels indicates atherosclerosis. An abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) may be visible as a large oval area of calcification in the midline; however, ultrasound is the definitive investigation for AAA diagnosis and screening.

CT Head Interpretation

CT head is the first-line imaging investigation for acute neurological emergencies in adults. It is performed without contrast in the acute setting (haemorrhage appears hyperdense on unenhanced CT). The key principle: bright (hyperdense) = acute blood; dark (hypodense) = infarct, oedema, or chronic change.

Types of Intracranial Haemorrhage

Extradural haematoma (EDH): biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection between the skull and the dura. It does not cross suture lines (the dura is tightly adherent to the skull at sutures). Classically caused by a blow to the temporal region rupturing the middle meningeal artery — typically follows a lucid interval (brief loss of consciousness → recovery → rapid deterioration as haematoma expands). Subdural haematoma (SDH): crescent-shaped hyperdense collection following the brain surface, crossing suture lines but not crossing the midline. Caused by bridging vein rupture — often in elderly patients (cerebral atrophy creates tension on bridging veins) after minor head trauma. Acute SDH is hyperdense; chronic SDH (weeks old) is hypodense or isodense to brain parenchyma and can be missed. Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH): blood in the subarachnoid space appears as high-density material filling the cisterns and sulci — the basal cisterns, Sylvian fissures, and interhemispheric fissure are the first areas to fill. Star-shaped pattern of blood filling the basal cisterns. CT is approximately 98% sensitive for SAH in the first six hours but sensitivity falls to approximately 85 to 90% at twelve hours and continues to decline. LP at twelve hours (looking for xanthochromia) is used if CT is negative but SAH remains clinically suspected. Most commonly caused by ruptured berry aneurysm at circle of Willis branch points. Intracerebral haematoma (ICH): a focal, homogeneous, hyperdense lesion within the brain parenchyma. Location provides important clues: hypertensive ICH typically occurs in the basal ganglia (putamen most common), thalamus, cerebellum, and pons. Lobar haematomas raise concern for underlying structural lesion (tumour, AVM, amyloid angiopathy) and may require contrast-enhanced CT or MRI for further evaluation.

Ischaemic Changes on CT Head

In the first three to six hours after ischaemic stroke, CT may appear normal or show only subtle early changes: loss of the grey-white matter differentiation (hypodense cortex and underlying white matter become indistinct), loss of the insular ribbon (normal thin hyperdense insular cortex becomes isodense to underlying white matter in MCA territory infarcts), and the hyperdense MCA sign (thrombus in the MCA appears as a hyperdense vessel compared to the contralateral side). By twelve to twenty-four hours, a clear area of low attenuation (hypodensity) becomes visible in the affected territory. Established infarct areas become progressively more hypodense, with mass effect (swelling), and cortical gyral enhancement on contrast CT due to blood-brain barrier breakdown.

Midline Shift and Herniation

The midline structures (falx cerebri, third ventricle, pineal gland) should be at the centre of the cranium. Midline shift indicates significant mass effect from a haematoma, large infarct with oedema, or tumour. More than 5 mm shift is clinically significant; above 10 mm is usually associated with altered consciousness. Transtentorial herniation (uncal herniation): the uncus of the temporal lobe herniates through the tentorium cerebelli, compressing the ipsilateral oculomotor nerve (CN III) — producing a dilated, unreactive ipsilateral pupil — and the ipsilateral cerebral peduncle, causing contralateral weakness (then may compress the contralateral peduncle against the tentorium — false localising sign — producing ipsilateral weakness). Tonsillar herniation (coning): the cerebellar tonsils herniate through the foramen magnum, compressing the medulla and causing cardiorespiratory arrest. This is the mechanism of death from elevated ICP.

MRI Versus CT — When to Use Each

Understanding the indications for MRI versus CT is directly tested in the AKT through investigation choice questions. The following principles guide the decision:

Clinical QuestionPreferred ModalityReason
Acute head injury / suspected intracranial haemorrhageCT without contrastFast, widely available, excellent for acute blood
Ischaemic stroke (acute, considering thrombolysis)CT without contrast first; MRI DWI if CT equivocalCT excludes haemorrhage; DWI-MRI detects early infarct within minutes
Brain tumour characterisation / cerebral metastasesMRI with gadolinium contrastSuperior soft tissue contrast; shows tumour extent, oedema, and enhancement
Spinal cord compressionMRI spineBest modality for cord, disc, and soft tissue assessment
Posterior fossa lesionMRICT has beam-hardening artefacts in the posterior fossa; MRI superior
Suspected MSMRI brain and spine with FLAIRT2/FLAIR sequences show periventricular white matter lesions
Bony fractures (acute)CTSuperior for cortical bone detail
Aortic dissectionCT angiogram (CTPA/CTA)Fast, shows extent of dissection and organ involvement
PE (suspected)CTPAGold standard for PE diagnosis
Soft tissue tumours / sarcomaMRIExcellent soft tissue characterisation and staging
Liver lesion characterisationMRI liver with DECT or gadoxetic acidSuperior characterisation of HCC, haemangioma, FNH
Pelvic organ pathology (ovaries, uterus, prostate)MRISuperior soft tissue contrast; no radiation
Acute abdomen / perforation / traumaCT abdomen and pelvis with contrastFast, comprehensive assessment of all abdominal organs

Ultrasound

Ultrasound uses high-frequency sound waves and has the advantage of being radiation-free, portable, and real-time. Key applications tested in the AKT: abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) screening (UK national programme — one-time scan at age 65 for men; an AAA is defined as an aortic diameter of 3 cm or above; elective repair is considered at 5.5 cm or above); FAST scan (Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) — assesses for free fluid in the pericardial sac, hepatorenal space (Morrison's pouch), splenorenal space, and pelvis (pouch of Douglas) — used in trauma to rapidly identify haemoperitoneum or haemopericardium; biliary system — first-line for suspected gallstones (sensitivity approximately 95% for gallstones; also identifies biliary dilation, cholecystitis, and common bile duct dilatation); obstetric ultrasound — dating scan at eleven to thirteen weeks (nuchal translucency for Down's syndrome risk), anomaly scan at eighteen to twenty weeks (structural defects); renal ultrasound — first-line for hydronephrosis, renal cysts, suspected renal masses, and monitoring of CKD.

Radiation Doses and Safety

Understanding relative radiation doses is essential for safe investigation requesting and is tested in the AKT. The unit of radiation dose is the millisievert (mSv). Background radiation in the UK is approximately 2.7 mSv per year. Approximate effective doses: CXR 0.02 mSv (equivalent to a few days background radiation); AXR 0.7 mSv (equivalent to approximately 3 months background); CT head 2.0 mSv (equivalent to approximately 8 months background); CT chest 7.0 mSv; CT abdomen and pelvis 10.0 mSv; PET-CT 14.1 mSv. The lifetime risk of fatal cancer from a single CT abdomen and pelvis is approximately 1 in 2000. The risk is higher in children due to greater radiosensitivity and longer life expectancy. Radiation in pregnancy: the embryo/fetus is more radiosensitive, particularly in the first trimester. Abdominal and pelvic CT during pregnancy delivers significant fetal radiation dose; alternatives (ultrasound, MRI — no ionising radiation) should be used where possible. However, if a pregnant woman has a life-threatening condition (e.g., suspected PE or trauma), CT should not be withheld.

IRMER Regulations

The Ionising Radiation (Medical Exposure) Regulations (IRMER) 2017 govern the use of ionising radiation in medical imaging in the UK. Three duty holders: the employer (the healthcare organisation — must have protocols and procedures); the referrer (the clinician requesting the investigation — must provide adequate clinical information to justify the request); and the practitioner (usually the radiologist — who justifies the referral and takes responsibility for the clinical indication). Key principle: every medical exposure must be individually justified — the benefit to the patient must outweigh the radiation risk. Referrers must provide adequate clinical information (history, relevant investigations, what question the imaging is designed to answer) to enable the practitioner to justify the exposure. Blanket or routine requests without clinical indication are not justifiable. The operator (usually the radiographer) is responsible for the practical conduct of the exposure.

Contrast Nephropathy Precautions

Iodinated contrast agents used in CT scanning can cause contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN), particularly in patients with pre-existing renal impairment, diabetes, dehydration, or heart failure, and in those taking nephrotoxic medications. Prevention: identify at-risk patients (eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m2, diabetics on metformin); stop metformin forty-eight hours before and for forty-eight hours after contrast administration (due to the risk of contrast-induced renal impairment causing metformin accumulation and subsequent lactic acidosis); IV hydration with 0.9% NaCl before and after contrast; use the lowest volume of iso-osmolar or low-osmolar contrast; withhold nephrotoxic medications where possible. N-acetylcysteine is no longer routinely recommended based on current evidence. Gadolinium-based contrast used in MRI: nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF) is a rare but serious complication of gadolinium-based agents in patients with severe renal impairment (eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73m2) — use gadolinium only if essential and inform the radiologist of renal function.

Common Emergency Imaging Decisions

The AKT frequently tests the appropriate investigation for specific clinical presentations. Key decision points: suspected PE — CTPA is first-line; V/Q scintigraphy if CTPA is contraindicated (contrast allergy, CKD, pregnancy where radiation to the chest is preferred over the abdomen). Suspected aortic dissection — CT angiogram of the thorax and abdomen (contrast-enhanced) is the investigation of choice; TOE is an alternative if CT is not immediately available or contraindicated. Acute pancreatitis — ultrasound within twenty-four hours to look for gallstones; CT with contrast at seventy-two hours if the diagnosis is uncertain or if there is clinical deterioration (to assess necrotising pancreatitis using the CT Severity Index). Head injury — NICE NG232 CT head criteria: GCS below 13 at any point, failure to reach GCS 15 within two hours, suspected open or depressed skull fracture, any sign of basal skull fracture (Battle's sign, periorbital haematoma without direct orbital trauma, CSF leakorrhea, haemotympanum), post-traumatic seizure, focal neurological deficit, or vomiting more than once. Suspected renal colic — CT KUB (unenhanced, low-dose) is the gold standard for renal calculi detection; ultrasound is the first-line modality in pregnancy and children to avoid radiation.

Frequently Asked Questions About Radiology in the UKMLA

How do I tell the difference between a pleural effusion and consolidation on a CXR?

A pleural effusion causes a homogeneous white opacity with a meniscus sign (curved upper border, highest laterally) and blunting of the costophrenic angle. The underlying lung structure is not visible. Consolidation causes a white opacity with air bronchograms visible within it (air in the bronchi outlined by the surrounding opaque consolidated lung), and the opacity tends to be more heterogeneous. The silhouette sign helps localise consolidation: if the right heart border is lost, the consolidation is in the right middle lobe (which normally touches the right heart border); if the right hemidiaphragm outline is lost, the consolidation is in the right lower lobe.

What is the difference between an extradural and subdural haematoma on CT?

An extradural haematoma (EDH) is biconvex (lens-shaped), hyperdense, and does not cross suture lines. It is typically caused by arterial bleeding (middle meningeal artery) following temporal head trauma. A subdural haematoma (SDH) is crescent-shaped, follows the brain surface, and crosses suture lines. It is caused by venous bleeding (bridging veins) and is more common in the elderly and those taking anticoagulants. An acute SDH is hyperdense on CT; a chronic SDH (more than two to three weeks old) is hypodense (appears dark) and can blend with the cortex, making it easy to miss.

When should MRI be preferred over CT for a neurological presentation?

MRI is preferred when superior soft tissue resolution is needed, when the posterior fossa is being assessed (CT has artefact here), when spinal cord pathology is suspected, when demyelinating disease (MS) is being evaluated, when a cerebral abscess needs characterisation (diffusion restriction), or when there is a need to avoid ionising radiation (pregnancy, children). CT remains preferred in acute presentations requiring rapid imaging (acute haemorrhage, trauma, PE, aortic dissection), as MRI is slower and has contraindications including pacemakers and some metallic implants.

What are the NICE criteria for CT head after head injury?

NICE NG232 recommends CT head within one hour of presentation for any of: GCS below 13 at initial assessment, GCS below 15 at two hours post-injury, suspected open or depressed skull fracture, any clinical signs of basal skull fracture, post-traumatic seizure, focal neurological deficit, or more than one episode of vomiting. CT within eight hours is recommended for those over sixty-five with any loss of consciousness or amnesia, a dangerous mechanism of injury (pedestrian or cyclist struck by vehicle, ejection from vehicle, fall above one metre), or amnesia for more than thirty minutes before the impact.

What does a Kerley B line look like and what does it mean?

Kerley B lines are short (1 to 2 cm), horizontal, white lines perpendicular to and touching the pleural surface, seen at the lateral lung bases on a CXR. They represent thickened interlobular septa due to interstitial oedema. They are a classic feature of cardiogenic pulmonary oedema and indicate an elevated pulmonary venous pressure. They can also be seen in lymphangitis carcinomatosa (tumour infiltrating the lymphatics) and rarely in other interstitial lung diseases.

Conclusion

Radiology and medical imaging is a high-yield topic for the UKMLA AKT that rewards systematic revision. Master the ABCDE approach to CXR interpretation, understand the key differences between modalities and when to use each, know the IRMER framework for justifying imaging requests, and practise interpreting common CT head findings. For a structured approach to your overall UKMLA revision, visit /preparation, and for a full description of the AKT format and question types, see /exam-pattern/akt.
