post_title: The Role of Mock Exams in UKMLA Preparation
post_excerpt: Mock exams do more than measure progress — they actively improve performance. Here is how to use mocks strategically as part of your UKMLA revision plan.
seo_title: Mock Exams in UKMLA Preparation: How to Use Them Strategically
seo_description: Mock exams are one of the most effective UKMLA preparation tools. Learn when to sit mocks, how to analyse results, and how to use them to close your weakest gaps.
html_content:
Why Mock Exams Are the Most Important Part of Your UKMLA Preparation

UKMLA mock exams are not supplementary revision tools — they are the single most effective intervention you can apply in the final weeks before your actual assessment. The UKMLA tests clinical decision-making under pressure across a vast content map, and passive reading alone will never adequately prepare you for the experience of answering 180 Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) questions in three hours or navigating a Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) circuit. This guide covers why mock exams matter, how to design and execute them properly, how to review performance analytically, and how to handle the psychology of mock testing — so that by the time you sit the real AKT or CPSA, you have already done it before.

The Science Behind Why Mock Exams Work

The Testing Effect and Retrieval Practice

Decades of cognitive psychology research have established what is known as the testing effect: the act of retrieving information from memory strengthens that memory far more than re-reading or re-watching the same content. When you read a chapter on heart failure management, your brain encodes the information once. When you then attempt a question about the four drug pillars for HFrEF, your brain is forced to reconstruct that knowledge, identify the gaps, and re-encode the corrected information in a deeper, more retrievable form. This principle underpins why question banks work better than textbooks for high-stakes examinations, and why full mock exams — which force sustained retrieval across hundreds of items — produce even stronger learning effects than individual practice questions answered casually.

The UKMLA AKT contains 180 single-best-answer questions covering twelve clinical domains. Each question is carefully mapped to the MLA Content Map and requires integration of pathophysiology, pharmacology, investigation interpretation, and management guidelines. Candidates who sit mock exams that mirror this breadth and depth consistently outperform those who complete the same number of individual practice questions spread across disconnected revision sessions, because mock exams force integration across domains in a way that isolated question practice does not.

Performance Simulation and the Exam Experience

One of the most underappreciated aspects of examination performance is the physiological and cognitive cost of the exam environment itself. The mental fatigue of sustaining concentration for three consecutive hours, the pressure of a ticking clock, the need to manage uncertainty across many questions without returning immediately to revise earlier answers — all of these factors degrade performance in candidates who have not experienced them before. Performance simulation, sometimes called stress inoculation, addresses this directly. By subjecting yourself to genuine exam conditions during practice, you train your autonomic nervous system to regulate anxiety more effectively, and you train your cognitive systems to sustain focus under time pressure.

Candidates who have sat at least two full-length UKMLA mocks under strict exam conditions report substantially lower anxiety on exam day than those who have only practised questions in a relaxed, open-book environment. This is not merely subjective: anxiety reduction has been shown in educational psychology research to improve recall, reduce working memory interference, and improve decision quality — all critical for SBA performance.

Pacing and Time Management

The AKT allows approximately 72 seconds per question across 180 questions in 180 minutes. This pacing requirement is non-trivial. Some questions — those involving straightforward factual recall or short clinical vignettes — can be answered in 30 to 40 seconds, banking time for complex multi-step pharmacology or ECG interpretation questions that may require 90 to 120 seconds. Candidates who have not practised this pacing consistently find themselves either rushing through later questions or, conversely, spending disproportionate time on early difficult questions and arriving at question 140 with only 15 minutes remaining.

Mock exams train you to develop an internal pacing rhythm. After two or three full timed mocks, you develop an intuitive sense of how long you can spend on any single question before you need to flag it and move on. This skill cannot be taught through content revision alone; it only comes through repeated performance under timed conditions.

Identifying Weak Areas Across Domains

Mock exams with domain-level performance breakdowns allow you to identify where your knowledge gaps are concentrated. A candidate might have a strong overall score of 68% but discover that their respiratory medicine performance is 48% and their pharmacology performance is 45%. Without a mock that tests you across all twelve domains simultaneously, these specific weaknesses may remain hidden until the actual examination. With six to eight weeks remaining before the exam, a focused revision sprint on weak domains can dramatically improve overall performance. This data-driven approach to revision is only possible when mock exams are analysed systematically, and it represents one of the most powerful advantages of structured mock examination practice.

Types of UKMLA Mock Exams Available

Question Bank Timed Modes

Most major question banks used for UKMLA preparation — including Passmedicine, Quesmed, Amboss, and OnExamination — offer timed examination modes that simulate the AKT format. These modes allow you to specify the number of questions, the domains to include, and the time limit, and they present questions without immediate feedback so that you experience the genuine uncertainty of not knowing whether your answers are correct until the session ends. When using question banks in timed mock mode, always configure the session to include all domains at approximately the distribution used in the real AKT, run for 180 questions over 180 minutes, and resist the temptation to pause or check the time more than once or twice during the session.

The limitation of question bank mocks is that the question style may differ subtly from official UKMLA questions. Question banks are written by educational companies rather than by the MLA itself, and while the best banks closely approximate the cognitive demand and clinical realism of real questions, candidates should be aware that performance on question bank mocks may not perfectly predict AKT performance. Question bank mocks are best used as formative learning tools with strong analytical review, rather than as precise predictors of pass or fail.

MSC Practice Exam Materials

The Medical Schools Council (MSC) has released sample questions and practice materials that are written by the same academic networks that produce real UKMLA questions. These materials represent the closest available approximation to actual AKT question style, cognitive demand, and domain weighting. Every candidate should complete the available MSC practice materials under timed conditions before sitting the real examination. Because the volume of official practice material is limited compared with commercial question banks, it is advisable to use MSC materials as a later-stage diagnostic tool — ideally in weeks 10 to 11 of a 12-week preparation plan — to gauge readiness as accurately as possible before the actual assessment.

Medical School Institutional Mocks

Most UK medical schools now run formal internal UKMLA preparation examinations, often at set points in the final year academic calendar. These institutional mocks are typically written by staff familiar with the MLA Content Map and are sat in formal examination conditions within the university. They provide two important functions beyond performance feedback: they recreate the social and environmental stressors of a formal examination setting, and they allow comparison with your peer cohort, giving contextual information about whether your performance is above or below the typical range for your year group. Institutional mocks should be taken seriously and prepared for, even though they do not contribute directly to the UKMLA result.

CPSA Mock Stations and OSCE Circuits

Preparing for the CPSA requires a different type of mock practice. The CPSA is an OSCE-style assessment involving clinical stations that test history-taking, clinical examination, procedural skills, communication, and professional judgment. Mock practice for the CPSA must therefore be active and interactive — you cannot prepare adequately by reading alone. Formal OSCE circuits run by medical schools, commercial OSCE preparation courses, and peer-to-peer role-play sessions all constitute valid CPSA mock practice. Video self-review, in which you record yourself performing a history-taking or communication station and then review the recording against a structured mark scheme, is particularly valuable for identifying habitual omissions, pace issues, and communication style problems that you are unlikely to notice in the moment of performance.

How to Set Up a Proper Mock Exam

Creating Genuine Exam Conditions

The single most common mistake candidates make with mock exams is not taking them seriously enough. If you sit a mock exam at your desk with your phone beside you, your notes open on a second screen, and permission to pause whenever you feel like it, you are not sitting a mock exam — you are sitting a comfortable practice session. A genuine mock exam requires removing all distractions from the environment, silencing all devices, having no notes or resources accessible, and committing to completing the full paper without interruption.

Set an alarm or timer for exactly 180 minutes. Sit at a desk rather than a sofa. Use only the resources that will be available in the real exam — which means no references, no calculator beyond what is provided, and no discussion with anyone during the session. Some candidates find it helpful to wear the clothes they plan to wear on exam day, or to go through a pre-exam routine such as arriving at a library at a particular time. These details matter because they help your nervous system associate the mock conditions with the real examination, improving the transfer of performance from practice to the real event.

Strict Timing and No Pausing

Strict timing is non-negotiable in a valid mock exam. The temptation to pause when a question is particularly difficult, or to allow yourself extra time at the end to review answers, invalidates the pacing and fatigue dimensions of the mock. If you find that you regularly run out of time during mocks, the solution is not to allow more time — it is to use the performance review to understand which types of questions are consuming disproportionate time and to develop faster strategies for those question types.

In the AKT, you are permitted to flag questions and return to them. Practise this strategy during mocks: if a question is consuming more than 90 seconds, flag it and move on, returning only if time permits at the end. This approach ensures that you do not sacrifice easy marks later in the paper because you spent excessive time on difficult questions earlier.

Consecutive Days for the Two-Paper Format

The UKMLA in its current format involves separate AKT and CPSA components, but some institutional preparations simulate consecutive-day testing to approximate cumulative fatigue. If your medical school schedules mock examinations across two days, sit both components even if you feel exhausted after the first day. The ability to recover overnight and perform again the next day is a real skill, and practising it reduces the risk that fatigue will impair your CPSA performance after the AKT. Ensure you maintain your normal sleep routine between mock days, eat properly, and avoid excessive evening revision that disrupts sleep quality.

Mock Exam Frequency: A 12-Week Plan Approach

First Full Mock at Week 8

In a structured 12-week UKMLA preparation plan, the first full-length mock exam should be scheduled at approximately week 8. By this point, you should have completed initial content review across all major domains and worked through a substantial volume of practice questions in learning mode. Week 8 represents the transition point between content consolidation and performance optimisation. The first mock will reveal your current performance trajectory, identify the weakest domains, and provide the data needed to prioritise revision in the final four weeks.

It is entirely normal for first mock scores to be lower than candidates hope. The first mock is diagnostic, not predictive. Do not allow a disappointing first mock score to derail your confidence — use it as a precise map of where to direct your remaining revision time.

Second Full Mock at Week 10 to 11

The second full mock should be scheduled at week 10 or 11, after you have had two to three weeks to address the weak areas identified in the first mock. The second mock serves a different purpose from the first: it is now both a second diagnostic and a confidence-building exercise. Improvement between mock 1 and mock 2 is one of the strongest predictors of final UKMLA performance, because it demonstrates that your revision strategies are working and that you are translating knowledge gains into actual performance under pressure.

If performance has not improved between mock 1 and mock 2, this is a valuable signal that your revision approach may need to change — perhaps shifting from passive content review to more active retrieval practice, or focusing more narrowly on the specific domain gaps identified in the first mock.

Additional Targeted Mocks

Beyond the two full-length mocks, many candidates benefit from additional shorter targeted mock sessions in the final two weeks. These might involve 90-question half-paper mocks focused on the weakest two or three domains, or timed question sets designed to practise pacing on question types (pharmacology, ECG, data interpretation) that have historically been slow. The goal in the final two weeks is not to introduce new content but to reinforce performance patterns and build confidence through repeated successful performance under timed conditions.

How to Review a Mock Exam

Per-Domain Performance Breakdown

Every mock exam review should begin with a per-domain performance analysis. Most question banks automatically generate this breakdown, showing your percentage correct in each domain alongside the number of questions answered. Record these scores systematically — a simple spreadsheet works well — so that you can track domain performance across multiple mocks and identify domains where performance is improving, static, or declining. Prioritise revision of domains where performance is both low and not improving, as these represent persistent knowledge gaps rather than random variation.

The twelve MLA Content Map domains include cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, neurological, musculoskeletal, renal and urology, endocrine and metabolic, reproductive and sexual health, mental health, infectious disease, paediatrics, and general principles. Most candidates have natural strengths and weaknesses across these domains based on clinical placement experience and personal interest, and the per-domain breakdown is the only reliable way to identify these patterns objectively.

Reviewing Wrong Answers

Reviewing every wrong answer in depth is standard advice that most candidates follow, but the quality of this review matters enormously. Simply reading the explanation and moving on is insufficient. For each wrong answer, you should identify which of three error types occurred: a knowledge gap (you did not know the relevant information), a reasoning error (you had the knowledge but applied it incorrectly), or a misreading error (you knew the answer but misread the question or vignette). Each error type requires a different remediation strategy. Knowledge gaps require content revision. Reasoning errors require practice with similar questions and explicit reflection on decision logic. Misreading errors require technique adjustment — slowing down on reading the clinical vignette, identifying the question stem before reading the options, and checking the exact wording of the question before confirming your answer.

Reviewing Correct Guesses

This is the aspect of mock review that most candidates overlook, and it is arguably as important as reviewing wrong answers. A correct guess is a wrong answer in disguise — it produced a mark in the mock but will not reliably do so in the real examination because the knowledge or reasoning that generated the guess is not solid. For every question you answered correctly but were not confident about, review the explanation in the same depth as you would a wrong answer. Classify the reason for your uncertainty and address it through targeted revision. Correct guesses that are not reviewed represent false confidence in your preparation.

Analysing Time Taken and Flagged Questions

Review not just which questions you got right or wrong, but which questions you flagged during the mock and how long you spent on the paper overall. A pattern of flagging more than 20 to 25 questions per 180-question paper suggests either insufficient content knowledge (leading to too many genuinely uncertain questions) or a pacing strategy that is too cautious. Review flagged questions to identify whether the uncertainty was due to knowledge gaps, ambiguous question stems, or time pressure, and address the root cause accordingly.

Interpreting Mock Scores

Trajectory Matters More Than a Single Score

The most important insight about mock exam scores is that the trajectory across multiple mocks is far more predictive of final performance than any single score. A candidate who scores 55% on their first mock and 68% on their second mock eight weeks later is in a much stronger position than a candidate who scores 65% on both mocks with no improvement. Trajectory reflects the effectiveness of your revision strategy and your capacity to translate knowledge into performance — both of which matter for the real examination.

The UKMLA AKT uses a standard-setting process that determines the pass mark based on each sitting's difficulty and the performance of a reference group. Historical data suggests that pass marks have typically fallen in the range of 60 to 70%, but candidates should not rely on this range as a firm target. Aim to perform consistently above 70% in mocks to provide a meaningful safety margin against the uncertainty of the real examination's difficulty calibration and your own performance variation on the day.

Understanding Variability Between Mocks

Performance on any single mock can vary by 5 to 8 percentage points due to factors including question difficulty, fatigue, and random domain sampling. A drop in score between two mocks does not necessarily indicate declining knowledge — it may reflect a harder question set or an off day. Rather than over-interpreting individual mock scores, focus on the average trend across multiple data points, the direction of domain-specific scores, and your subjective sense of confidence and fluency when working through questions.

The AKT Pacing Challenge: 72 Seconds Per Question

Developing Question-Type Speed Profiles

The 72-second-per-question average for the AKT is an average, not a target for every question. Effective pacing requires developing different speed profiles for different question types. Short recall questions — those that present a brief clinical scenario and ask for a diagnosis or first-line treatment — should ideally be answered in 40 to 50 seconds. Medium-complexity vignette questions with investigation interpretation or drug choice decisions typically require 60 to 80 seconds. Complex multi-step reasoning questions, ethical dilemmas with nuanced options, or questions requiring calculation should be budgeted up to 90 to 100 seconds, with the understanding that time saved on easy questions is used here.

Mock exams help you calibrate these speed profiles for your own cognitive style. Some candidates find pharmacology questions faster than average because of strong drug knowledge; others find them slower. The pacing data from your mocks tells you where to invest time and where to be more decisive.

The Flag-and-Move Strategy

Practise the flag-and-move strategy explicitly during every mock. Commit to a personal rule — for example, if I have read a question twice and I am still uncertain, I flag it and move to the next question immediately. Candidates who fail to implement this strategy often find themselves stalled on a single question for three to four minutes, generating anxiety, losing time, and triggering a cascade of rushing through subsequent questions. The flag-and-move strategy prevents this cascade and ensures that you reach every question in the paper before the time expires.

CPSA Mock Stations: A Different Kind of Practice

Role-Play Sessions with Peers

CPSA preparation requires active practice with a partner. Role-play sessions with medical school peers are the most widely accessible and effective form of CPSA mock practice. One candidate plays the examiner or patient while the other performs the station, then roles are reversed and feedback is exchanged. Effective peer role-play requires both partners to have studied the mark scheme for the station type being practised, so that feedback is specific, accurate, and referenced to CPSA assessment criteria rather than personal preference.

Organise systematic CPSA role-play sessions covering all major station types: focused history taking, clinical examination, explanation and communication, breaking bad news, ethical scenarios, procedural skills demonstration, and prescribing tasks. Each station type has specific assessment criteria that should guide both preparation and peer feedback.

Video Self-Review

Video self-review is one of the highest-yield CPSA preparation strategies available, and it is consistently underused by candidates. Recording yourself performing a mock CPSA station and then watching the recording critically — ideally against a published mark scheme or OSCE checklist — reveals behaviours and habits that are impossible to self-monitor in real time during performance. Common findings from video self-review include: speaking too quickly when anxious, failing to introduce yourself at the beginning of a station, neglecting to summarise findings back to the patient, omitting safety-netting or follow-up information at the end of a consultation, and failing to maintain eye contact when checking notes. Each of these is a directly remediable behaviour, but only if you know it is occurring.

OSCE Circuits and Commercial Courses

Commercial OSCE preparation courses — run by organisations such as Oscer, Geeky Medics, and various medical school societies — provide structured CPSA mock circuits with trained examiners or standardised patients. These circuits are particularly valuable if your medical school's peer practice opportunities are limited or if you want the experience of being assessed by someone outside your immediate social circle, which more closely approximates the real examination environment. Review what each course offers before committing financially, and prioritise courses that use mark schemes aligned with the UKMLA CPSA assessment framework rather than older OSCE formats from previous UK medical licensing examinations.

Common Mock Exam Mistakes to Avoid

Sitting Mocks Without Reviewing Them

The most damaging mistake a candidate can make with mock exams is sitting them without conducting a thorough post-mock review. The learning value of a mock is concentrated in the review phase, not the sitting phase. A three-hour mock that is followed by two hours of careful, analytical review produces far more learning than a three-hour mock that is followed by a brief glance at the score and a sense of relief that it is over. Budget at least two hours for post-mock review for every full-length mock you sit, and treat this review time as non-negotiable preparation time.

Using Open-Book or Paused Conditions

Sitting a mock with notes available, with the ability to pause and look things up, or without strict timing produces misleading performance data and no performance simulation benefit. If you regularly sit mocks in relaxed conditions, your first experience of genuine timed pressure may come in the actual examination — which is exactly the wrong place to encounter it for the first time. Commit to strict conditions from the first mock you sit.

Over-Mocking Without Revision Intervals

While mocks are valuable, sitting multiple mocks in rapid succession without intervals for targeted revision between them produces diminishing returns. The benefit of a mock comes from identifying gaps and then addressing them through focused revision before the next mock. Sitting three mocks in one week without any revision between them simply exposes you to the same gaps repeatedly without giving your knowledge base time to improve. Space mocks two to three weeks apart and use the intervening time for gap-targeted revision.

Anchoring on a Single Mock Score

Candidates sometimes make important decisions — such as delaying registration for a sitting or requesting a study break extension — based on a single poor mock score. A single mock score is a noisy signal. Before making significant decisions based on mock performance, sit at least two mocks and consider the trajectory and the pattern of domain performance, rather than reacting to a single data point.

Mock Psychology and Stress Inoculation

Understanding Pre-Mock Anxiety

Almost all candidates experience pre-mock anxiety, including elevated heart rate, intrusive thoughts, and difficulty sleeping the night before a scheduled mock. This is entirely normal and, within limits, actually improves performance by increasing arousal and alertness. The goal of stress inoculation through mock practice is not to eliminate pre-examination anxiety but to reduce it to a manageable level and to train your cognitive systems to maintain performance despite its presence.

If pre-mock anxiety is so severe that it is preventing you from sitting mocks at all, or causing you to abandon mocks partway through, speak to your medical school's pastoral support team. Specific techniques including progressive muscle relaxation, controlled breathing, and structured pre-exam routines can significantly reduce anxiety to manageable levels and should be practised alongside the mocks themselves.

Building Confidence Through Repeated Exposure

Confidence in examination performance is built through repeated successful experience — not through reassurance, affirmation, or avoidance of challenging practice. Every mock you sit under strict conditions and review thoroughly adds to a growing bank of experiential evidence that you can perform under pressure. By the time you sit the real UKMLA after two or three well-conducted mocks, you are not doing something new and frightening — you are doing something familiar that you have successfully navigated before. This shift in framing from novel threat to familiar challenge is one of the most powerful psychological effects of systematic mock practice.

Post-Mock Review Balance

Avoiding Review Paralysis

Thorough mock review is essential, but review can become counterproductive when it triggers excessive anxiety, leads to endless re-reading of explanations, or consumes so much time that it crowds out forward-looking revision. Set a clear time budget for mock review — typically two to three hours for a full 180-question mock — and complete the review systematically within that budget. Prioritise wrong answers and uncertain correct answers, note the topics requiring revision, and then move on to targeted revision rather than continuing to dwell on the mock itself.

Balancing Weakness Remediation with Strength Maintenance

Post-mock revision should be primarily directed at weak domains, but candidates should also maintain strength in domains where they are performing well. A common error is to focus so intensively on weak areas in the final weeks that strong domains begin to decay through non-use. A balanced post-mock revision plan might allocate 60% of revision time to the two or three weakest domains and 40% to maintaining breadth across stronger domains. This ensures that the performance gains in weak areas are not offset by deterioration in areas of strength.

Mock Resources: Format, Cost, and Evaluation

ResourceFormatApproximate CostProsCons
PassmedicineTimed SBA mock mode, all domains£70–£90/yearLarge question bank, domain breakdown, widely used by UK medicsQuestions written commercially, may not exactly match AKT style
QuesmedTimed mock mode, AI-driven weak-area targeting£60–£120/yearExcellent analytics, adaptive learning mode, growing question bankNewer platform, fewer total questions than established banks
AmbossSimulated exam mode, detailed explanations£90–£150/yearVery high-quality explanations, integrated learning libraryInternational focus means some content not UK-specific
OnExamination (BMJ)Timed mock mode, progress tracking£90–£130/yearStrong alignment with UK clinical practice, trusted brandInterface less modern than some competitors
MSC Sample QuestionsOfficial practice questions, SBA formatFreeClosest approximation to real AKT question styleLimited volume of questions available
Institutional Medical School MocksFormal sit, exam conditions, peer cohort comparisonFree (included in course)Real exam conditions, cohort benchmarking, staff-written questionsTiming fixed by medical school calendar, variable quality
Commercial OSCE Courses (CPSA)Circuit-based, standardised patients or trained examiners£100–£300 per courseProfessional feedback, realistic CPSA simulationCost, variable quality between providers
Peer Role-Play (CPSA)Partner practice, structured with mark schemeFreeFlexible scheduling, peer feedback, repeated practiceQuality depends on partner preparation and feedback skills

Frequently Asked Questions About UKMLA Mock Exams

How many mock exams should I sit before the UKMLA?

A minimum of two full-length AKT mocks is recommended, with the first at approximately week 8 of a 12-week preparation plan and the second at weeks 10 to 11. Additional half-paper targeted mocks in the final two weeks are optional but beneficial. For the CPSA, aim for at least two to three full OSCE circuit practices plus regular peer role-play sessions across your preparation period. Quality and systematic review of each mock matters more than the raw number of mocks completed.

What score should I be aiming for in mock exams?

Aim to achieve consistently above 70% in full-length AKT mocks by the time you sit the real examination. Because the real AKT pass mark is set through standard-setting and is not a fixed percentage, mock scores above 70% provide a meaningful safety margin. However, the trajectory of improvement between mocks is more important than absolute score. A rising score from 58% to 68% across two mocks is a strong positive signal; a static or falling score warrants a strategic revision review.

Can I use my notes or question bank answers during a mock?

No. Any mock that allows access to notes, textbooks, or question bank explanations during the sitting is not a valid mock exam. The entire value of a mock — performance simulation, pacing practice, stress inoculation, and genuine diagnostic feedback — depends on the conditions being as close as possible to the real examination. Use the post-mock review session for notes and explanations, never during the mock itself.

What should I do if my mock score drops between mock 1 and mock 2?

First, assess whether the second mock used a more difficult question set — some question bank mock modes vary in difficulty. Second, review domain performance in both mocks to identify whether the drop is generalised or concentrated in specific areas. If the drop is generalised, consider whether your revision strategy is working and whether you may be experiencing burnout or sleep deprivation. If concentrated in specific domains, increase focused practice in those areas. A single score drop is rarely cause for significant alarm; a consistent pattern of declining performance warrants a strategic reassessment of your preparation approach and, if necessary, a conversation with your academic adviser.

Should I revise right up to exam day or rest the day before?

Light review of key clinical guidelines and high-yield pharmacology on the day before the examination is generally fine and may help consolidate confidence. Avoid sitting another full mock on the day before the examination — this increases fatigue and anxiety without providing meaningful additional preparation benefit. Focus the day before on familiar material, a good evening meal, adequate sleep, and preparation of practical logistics such as travel arrangements and required identification. Arriving at the examination venue well-rested and confident is more valuable than attempting to acquire new knowledge in the final 24 hours.

Conclusion: Make Mock Exams the Core of Your UKMLA Strategy

Mock exams, conducted under strict conditions and reviewed analytically and systematically, are the single most effective intervention available in your UKMLA preparation. They train your pacing, build your psychological resilience, identify your genuine knowledge gaps, and ensure that by examination day you are not experiencing the AKT or CPSA for the first time. Use the mock resources available to you — from commercial question banks to official MSC materials to peer OSCE circuits — and invest as much time in reviewing your mocks as you do in sitting them. For a complete picture of how mock performance connects to your overall preparation strategy and what to expect from the UKMLA results and scoring process, explore the related guides on this site.

post_title: Understanding the Foundation Programme: What Awaits After the UKMLA
post_excerpt: Passing the UKMLA opens the door to the Foundation Programme. Here is what FY1 and FY2 involve and how the UKMLA competencies connect to day-one foundation practice.
seo_title: The Foundation Programme After UKMLA: What FY1 and FY2 Involve
seo_description: What happens after you pass the UKMLA? Learn about the Foundation Programme, how FY1 and FY2 build on UKMLA competencies, and what to expect as a new doctor.
html_content:
The Foundation Programme and the UKMLA: Understanding the Essential Connection

The Foundation Programme and the UKMLA are inseparably linked — passing the UKMLA is the mandatory gateway through which every UK medical graduate must pass before they can enter the Foundation Programme and begin their career as a practising doctor. Understanding how these two systems connect, how the timing works, what the Foundation Programme actually involves, and what happens if the UKMLA journey is disrupted, is essential knowledge for every final-year medical student in the United Kingdom. This guide explains the complete pathway from UKMLA success to Foundation Programme entry, the UKFPO application process, how the MLA Content Map maps to real FY1 clinical work, and the growing range of post-foundation options available to newly qualified doctors.

What Is the Foundation Programme?

Structure and Purpose

The Foundation Programme is a two-year, nationally managed, postgraduate medical training programme that bridges the gap between medical school and specialty or general practice training. It is mandatory for all doctors wishing to progress to specialty training in the UK, and it provides the structured clinical experience and professional development required to achieve full GMC registration. The programme is overseen by the UK Foundation Programme Office (UKFPO) and is delivered through twenty-five Foundation Schools across England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland, each responsible for managing a group of NHS Trusts and Health Boards that host foundation doctors.

The two years of the Foundation Programme are designated FY1 (Foundation Year 1) and FY2 (Foundation Year 2). Each year is divided into three four-month rotations, giving each foundation doctor six clinical placements across diverse specialties over the two years. These rotations are intentionally varied, typically spanning acute medical settings, surgical placements, general practice, and specialty placements such as paediatrics, emergency medicine, psychiatry, or obstetrics and gynaecology. The specific rotations allocated to each foundation doctor are determined by the Foundation School, with limited opportunity for individual preference expression.

FY1: Provisional Registration and Core Clinical Skills

FY1 is a period of supervised practice under provisional GMC registration. Provisional registration is a conditional form of medical registration that permits a newly qualified doctor to work in approved FY1 posts but does not confer the full autonomous practice rights of full GMC registration. During FY1, doctors develop core clinical competencies including clerking and managing acutely unwell patients, prescribing safely, performing common procedural skills, communicating with patients and multidisciplinary teams, and maintaining accurate medical records. FY1 doctors work under the supervision of more senior clinicians and are expected to seek support for clinical situations outside their competence.

At the end of FY1, the ARCP (Annual Review of Competence Progression) panel — described in more detail below — makes a recommendation to the GMC about whether the doctor is ready for full registration. Successful completion of FY1 and a positive ARCP outcome enables the transition from provisional to full GMC registration, which is required before progressing to FY2 and subsequently to specialty training applications.

FY2: Full Registration and Extended Competency Development

FY2 builds on the foundation of FY1 with greater clinical independence, broader competency expectations, and increased exposure to specialty medicine. FY2 doctors hold full GMC registration and carry correspondingly greater professional responsibility. During FY2, doctors are expected to demonstrate leadership within the multidisciplinary team, take on teaching responsibilities for medical students and junior colleagues, engage in quality improvement projects, and develop a clearer sense of their intended specialty career pathway. FY2 also provides opportunities to explore less common specialties through the varied rotation structure, which can be valuable for doctors who are still deciding their long-term career direction.

Foundation Programme Curriculum 2021

The current Foundation Programme is delivered against the Foundation Programme Curriculum 2021, published jointly by the Academy of Medical Royal Colleges and the UK Foundation Programme Office. This curriculum replaced earlier versions of the foundation curriculum and significantly updated the competency framework to reflect contemporary NHS practice, patient safety priorities, and the General Medical Council's Good Medical Practice guidance. The 2021 curriculum is organised around several interconnected domains: clinical and technical skills; clinical reasoning; communication and interaction; professional values and behaviours; patient safety and quality improvement; and self-development and education. Each domain contains specific outcomes that foundation doctors are expected to demonstrate progression towards through their FY1 and FY2 placements.

The curriculum emphasises that foundation doctors should be developing as reflective, self-directed learners who actively seek feedback, document evidence of their practice in their e-portfolio, and engage with educational supervision as a genuine developmental relationship rather than a bureaucratic compliance exercise. This educational philosophy aligns closely with the approach to learning that successful UKMLA candidates have typically developed during medical school.

How UKMLA Passing Enables Foundation Programme Entry

From UKMLA Pass to Provisional GMC Registration

The pathway from UKMLA success to FY1 start involves several sequential steps that must be completed in the correct order and within specific timelines. Passing both components of the UKMLA — the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) — satisfies the GMC's assessment requirements for medical licensing. Combined with your medical degree (MB ChB, MB BChir, MBBCh, BMBS, or equivalent), a satisfactory UKMLA result makes you eligible to apply for provisional GMC registration.

Provisional GMC registration requires an application to the GMC that includes your degree certificate or confirmation from your medical school that you have met all requirements for graduation, evidence that your UKMLA has been passed, a fitness to practise declaration, and identity and right to work documentation. GMC processing times vary but typically take several weeks, so candidates should initiate the provisional registration process as soon as they have their UKMLA results and their graduation documentation is available. Provisional registration cannot be granted until the GMC has verified all components of the application.

Provisional Registration and the FY1 Start

You cannot begin FY1 employment without provisional GMC registration. This creates a critical dependency: if there is any delay in obtaining provisional registration — whether due to UKMLA timing, GMC processing, fitness to practise issues, or documentation problems — your FY1 start may be delayed. Foundation Schools set fixed FY1 start dates, typically in early August, and cannot defer individual start dates except in extraordinary circumstances. Candidates who anticipate any complications with their UKMLA timing or registration documentation should contact their Foundation School well in advance to understand the options available.

Full GMC Registration at the End of FY1

Following successful completion of FY1 — defined as a satisfactory ARCP outcome and a recommendation from the Foundation School — the GMC upgrades the doctor's registration from provisional to full. Full registration removes the restrictions associated with provisional registration and confers the complete professional and legal standing required for independent medical practice. Full GMC registration is also a prerequisite for applying to specialty training programmes through the national selection processes managed by the relevant specialty training committees and Health Education England or its successor bodies.

The UKFPO Application Process

Preference-Informed Allocation Since 2024

The process by which medical graduates are allocated to Foundation Schools and foundation posts has undergone significant change in recent years. From 2024, the system is known as Preference-Informed Allocation (PIA), which replaced the previous Situational Judgement Test (SJT)-based ranking system. Under PIA, applicants complete a lottery-based allocation process where preferences for Foundation Schools are expressed, and allocation is made by a combination of preference ranking and random allocation rather than examination score. This represents a fundamental shift in the allocation philosophy, moving away from competition by standardised test performance towards a fairer, preference-based system that acknowledges the inherent arbitrariness of allocating places across a national system.

The PIA process involves applicants registering through the UKFPO application portal during their penultimate or final year of medical school, expressing preferences for Foundation Schools in rank order, completing eligibility checks, and being allocated to a Foundation School through the lottery mechanism. The specific post within the allocated Foundation School — including the Trust, the rotation sequence, and the specific specialties — is then determined through a further allocation process run by the Foundation School itself.

White Space Questions

The UKFPO application includes white space questions — short written answers that assess applicants' understanding of professionalism, clinical reasoning, teamwork, and reflective practice. These questions are not competitively scored in the current PIA system but are used to confirm eligibility and professional suitability. White space questions should be answered honestly and thoughtfully, drawing on specific examples from clinical placements, extracurricular activities, or leadership and research experience. Generic answers that could apply to any applicant are less convincing than specific, reflective responses that demonstrate genuine engagement with the question.

Foundation School Allocation and Post Matching

Once allocated to a Foundation School, applicants typically undergo a further preference process for specific posts within the school's range of Trusts and rotations. The degree of choice available in this post-matching process varies between Foundation Schools. Some schools allow applicants to rank specific rotation programmes; others use a combination of ranking and random allocation. Applicants with specific personal circumstances — such as caring responsibilities, medical conditions, or partner employment — can apply for linked applications that increase the likelihood of receiving their preferred Foundation School allocation. Evidence of the relevant circumstances must be provided, and linked applications do not guarantee a specific outcome but do weight the allocation accordingly.

How the MLA Content Map Maps to FY1 Work

Clinical Domains and FY1 Presentations

The MLA Content Map — the curriculum framework that defines the scope of the UKMLA assessment — is explicitly designed to reflect the clinical knowledge and skills required of a newly qualified foundation doctor. The conditions and presentations tested in the UKMLA are not arbitrarily selected academic content but the actual clinical problems that FY1 doctors encounter in their day-to-day work. This alignment is intentional: the UKMLA is a licensing assessment, and a licensing assessment must test fitness to practise as defined by real clinical need.

The conditions included in the MLA Content Map are categorised by their management priority: conditions that must be diagnosed and managed by the doctor personally; conditions that must be recognised and referred appropriately; and conditions that must be identified as a possible diagnosis. This three-tier classification maps almost exactly onto the clinical realities of FY1 practice: FY1 doctors are expected to independently manage common acute conditions such as urinary tract infections, hypertensive emergencies, and exacerbations of chronic disease; to recognise and refer conditions requiring specialist input such as acute coronary syndromes, new-onset malignancy, and complex surgical presentations; and to keep in mind less common conditions when they are part of a plausible differential diagnosis even if specialist management is required from the outset.

Prescribing, Safety, and Professional Practice

A substantial component of the MLA Content Map — and therefore of UKMLA preparation — is devoted to safe prescribing, medication management, and professional practice. These domains are not coincidentally prominent: prescribing errors are the leading category of avoidable patient harm associated with junior doctors, and the Foundation Programme has invested significantly in prescribing education, simulation training, and prescribing competency assessment. Candidates who develop a thorough and clinically integrated understanding of pharmacology, drug interactions, contraindications, and medication monitoring during UKMLA preparation arrive in FY1 with a meaningful safety advantage over those who have treated pharmacology as a peripheral examination topic.

Foundation Programme Curriculum 2021 Domains in Detail

Clinical and Technical Skills

This domain covers the practical clinical skills expected of foundation doctors, including history taking, clinical examination, interpretation of investigations, procedural competencies, and clinical decision-making. The procedural skills expected of FY1 doctors include venepuncture, intravenous cannulation, arterial blood gas sampling, urinary catheterisation, nasogastric tube insertion, and basic airway management. FY2 doctors are expected to have developed additional skills appropriate to their specific rotation placements. The Foundation Programme Curriculum 2021 specifies that procedural skills should be assessed through direct observation, with sign-off by a supervising clinician using the Direct Observation of Procedural Skills (DOPS) workplace-based assessment tool.

Communication and Interaction with Patients and Colleagues

Effective communication is one of the most heavily weighted domains in both the Foundation Programme Curriculum and the UKMLA CPSA. Foundation doctors are expected to communicate with patients and families about diagnosis, prognosis, and management in a way that is clear, compassionate, and appropriately calibrated to the patient's health literacy and emotional state. They are also expected to communicate effectively within the MDT — presenting patients on ward rounds, handing over clinical information safely, escalating concerns to senior colleagues, and contributing constructively to team discussions. Communication failures are a major contributor to adverse events in healthcare, and the Foundation Programme treats communication competency as a patient safety issue as well as a professional development one.

Professional Values and Behaviours

This domain encompasses the ethical and professional dimensions of medical practice: honesty and integrity, patient confidentiality, informed consent, managing conflicts of interest, maintaining appropriate professional boundaries, and understanding the regulatory framework within which doctors practice. Foundation doctors are expected to demonstrate understanding of the GMC's Good Medical Practice guidance and to apply its principles in everyday clinical decisions. The UKMLA tests professional values and behaviours through clinical scenario questions and CPSA stations that present ethical dilemmas, requests for inappropriate prescriptions, or situations requiring professional judgment about reporting or escalation.

Patient Safety and Quality Improvement

The Foundation Programme Curriculum 2021 places greater emphasis on patient safety and quality improvement than previous iterations of the curriculum. Foundation doctors are expected to engage with incident reporting systems, participate in morbidity and mortality meetings, contribute to quality improvement projects, and understand the systems-thinking approach to error analysis and harm prevention. The curriculum reflects the substantial body of evidence that patient safety culture and quality improvement capability are not innate qualities but learnable skills that can be systematically developed through structured education and supervised experience.

Education and Self-Development

Foundation doctors are expected to take active responsibility for their own learning and to contribute to the education of others — including medical students, healthcare assistants, and other trainees. This domain encompasses reflective practice (documented in the e-portfolio through reflective entries), participation in teaching activities, engagement with educational supervision, and a commitment to ongoing professional development beyond the formal curriculum. The Foundation Programme views foundation doctors as the beginning of a career of lifelong learning, and the educational habits established in FY1 and FY2 tend to persist throughout subsequent training.

The ARCP Process

What Is ARCP?

The Annual Review of Competence Progression (ARCP) is the formal mechanism through which the Foundation Programme assesses each foundation doctor's progress against the curriculum and makes recommendations about their readiness to progress to the next stage of training. The ARCP is conducted by a panel that typically includes the Training Programme Director, educational supervisors, and sometimes a lay representative. The panel reviews the foundation doctor's e-portfolio — which contains workplace-based assessments, feedback, reflective entries, audit and quality improvement work, and evidence of mandatory training completion — and makes one of several possible outcomes.

ARCP Outcomes

ARCP outcomes range from Outcome 1 (satisfactory progress — proceed to next stage) through Outcome 6 (complete — programme successfully completed). Unsatisfactory outcomes include Outcome 2 (development of specific competences required — extended or additional training recommended), Outcome 3 (inadequate progress — repeat year or remove from programme), and Outcome 4 (released from training programme with or without specified competences). Most foundation doctors receive Outcome 1 at each ARCP, reflecting satisfactory engagement with the curriculum and completion of required assessments. Outcomes 2 and 3 are uncommon but occur when e-portfolio completion is inadequate, workplace-based assessments are insufficient in number or quality, or there are concerns about professionalism or clinical performance.

What If You Fail the UKMLA?

Timing Relative to FY1 Start

The UKMLA is sat in the final year of medical school, typically in spring of the final year. The FY1 start date is in early August of the same year, creating a window of approximately three to four months between UKMLA results and FY1 commencement. Candidates who fail one or both components of the UKMLA in the first sitting have the opportunity to resit in summer, before the FY1 start date, if the resit sitting schedule permits. If a resit is not available before August, or if the candidate fails the resit, they will be unable to take up their allocated FY1 post in the August cohort.

The UKMLA results include component-specific outcomes, so a candidate who passes the AKT but fails the CPSA will need to resit only the CPSA, and vice versa. This component-specific resit policy reduces the burden on candidates who have demonstrated competence in one component of the assessment.

Resit Options and Implications

Candidates who are unable to pass the UKMLA before the August FY1 start will typically lose their allocated foundation post for that year and will need to reapply through UKFPO for a deferred start. The implications of this deferral extend beyond the immediate delay: it may affect specialty training timelines, student loan repayment schedules, and visa status for international students. Medical schools provide pastoral support for candidates in this situation, and early engagement with your medical school's support structures is strongly recommended for any candidate who has failed or is at risk of failing the UKMLA.

There is no limit to the number of UKMLA resit attempts within the rules currently published by the GMC and MSC, although policies may be reviewed over time. Candidates who require multiple resit attempts should seek targeted educational support to identify the specific competency gaps underlying their performance and address them systematically before the next attempt.

Academic Foundation Programme

What Is the AFP?

The Academic Foundation Programme (AFP) is a competitive variant of the standard Foundation Programme that incorporates a dedicated academic component — typically one four-month rotation per year — focused on research, medical education, or leadership and management. AFP posts are embedded within standard Foundation Schools but offer foundation doctors the opportunity to develop academic and scholarly skills alongside clinical competency, laying the groundwork for a career that integrates clinical practice with research or academic medicine.

AFP posts are highly competitive. Applicants are typically required to demonstrate existing research or academic experience through publications, presentations, prizes, or intercalated degrees with distinction. The application process for AFP is separate from the standard UKFPO process and involves a personal statement and, in some cases, an interview. AFP applicants typically apply in the autumn of their final year, before the standard UKFPO allocation process completes.

AFP Research, Teaching, and Leadership Tracks

AFP posts are usually designated as one of three tracks: research (involving laboratory or clinical research under a named academic supervisor, often leading to a publication or conference presentation); medical education (involving curriculum development, teaching facilitation, and educational research); or leadership and management (involving quality improvement projects, clinical leadership development, and health service management experience). The choice of track should reflect genuine interest and longer-term career plans, as the AFP year is a significant but limited opportunity and its value depends on the depth of engagement with the academic component.

F3 Year Options

What Is an F3 Year?

An F3 year is an informal term for a year taken between completing the Foundation Programme and beginning specialty training, used for travel, further clinical experience, research, personal development, or simply recovery from the demands of training. F3 years are not a formal programme — they are simply a gap between one stage of training and the next, during which the doctor may work as a locum, take an honorary research position, volunteer internationally, or pursue personal projects. F3 years are increasingly common among UK foundation graduates, reflecting both the pressures of early postgraduate training and the increasingly competitive nature of specialty training application.

F3 years can be valuable if used strategically — to gain research experience before a competitive academic training application, to develop specific clinical skills in a specialty of interest, or to recover from burnout and return to training with renewed motivation. However, F3 years that are unplanned or purely reactive can create gaps in clinical practice that require careful management, and doctors considering an F3 year should check the GMC's requirements for maintaining registration and the revalidation implications of extended absence from training.

Medical Training Prioritisation from 2026

Background and Context

Medical Training Prioritisation (MTP) is a policy mechanism developed in response to the increasing numbers of qualified doctors at the foundation stage relative to the available specialty training posts. It refers to a system that may prioritise access to specialty training applications based on the number of years since completing the Foundation Programme, favouring doctors who have waited longest for specialty training access. MTP has been discussed and piloted in various forms since approximately 2022, and the most current iteration of policy planning indicates that formal MTP mechanisms may become embedded in specialty training allocation from 2026 onwards.

The implications of MTP for foundation doctors completing their programme in 2025 onwards are significant: doctors who take extended F3 years or multiple gap years between foundation and specialty training may find themselves at a disadvantage relative to those who apply directly from foundation. Candidates planning their post-foundation trajectory should monitor UKFPO and Health Education England communications on MTP closely, and take advice from specialty training programme administrators about the potential impact on their specific intended specialty.

UKMLA Timeline Mapped to Foundation Programme Milestones

StageTimelineKey MilestoneAction Required
UKMLA AKT PreparationFinal year, autumn–winterEnrol in question banks, begin 12-week revision planRegister for UKMLA sitting, confirm eligibility with medical school
UKFPO Application OpensFinal year, autumnRegister with UKFPO, complete white space questionsGather academic and pastoral references, confirm eligibility
AFP Application (if applicable)Final year, October–NovemberSubmit AFP personal statement and applicationObtain academic referee, evidence of research/education experience
UKMLA AKT SittingFinal year, spring (approx. March–April)AKT examination dayEnsure provisional GMC registration application is prepared
UKMLA CPSA SittingFinal year, spring (approx. April–May)CPSA OSCE circuitAwait both component results before proceeding
UKMLA ResultsFinal year, late springPass/Fail notification for AKT and CPSAIf pass: apply for provisional GMC registration immediately
UKFPO Foundation School AllocationFinal year, spring (PIA lottery)Foundation School assignedAccept allocation, begin post preference process within school
GMC Provisional RegistrationSummer, June–JulyProvisional GMC number issuedProvide to employing Trust for employment checks and onboarding
FY1 StartEarly AugustFirst day as a foundation doctorComplete Trust induction, begin e-portfolio, meet educational supervisor
FY1 ARCPFollowing July/AugustCompetence progression review for FY1Ensure e-portfolio is complete; GMC upgrade to full registration if Outcome 1
FY2 StartAugust, year 2Full GMC registration, begin FY2 rotationsBegin specialty training application research
FY2 ARCP and CompletionFollowing July/AugustFoundation Programme CCT equivalent awardedApply to specialty training or consider F3/AFP options

Frequently Asked Questions About the Foundation Programme and UKMLA

Is the UKMLA the only requirement for Foundation Programme entry?

The UKMLA is the primary licensing assessment requirement, but it is not the only requirement for Foundation Programme entry. You also need to have successfully completed your medical degree and received your primary medical qualification, obtained provisional GMC registration, passed the employing Trust's pre-employment checks (including Disclosure and Barring Service check, occupational health clearance, right to work verification, and reference checks), and been allocated to a foundation post through the UKFPO process. All of these requirements must be satisfied before you can begin FY1, and delays in any one of them can affect your start date.

Can I choose which Foundation School I go to?

Under the current Preference-Informed Allocation system, you express ranked preferences for Foundation Schools and the PIA process takes these preferences into account, but allocation is ultimately determined by a combination of preference and lottery. You are not guaranteed your first-choice Foundation School, and in high-demand areas — particularly London and other major cities — competition for preferred schools can mean that many applicants receive a lower-preference allocation. Linked applications for personal circumstances can increase weighting towards preferred schools, but do not guarantee a specific outcome.

What is the e-portfolio and how important is it in FY1?

The e-portfolio is the electronic portfolio system used by foundation doctors to document evidence of their learning and professional development throughout the Foundation Programme. It is critically important: your ARCP outcome, and therefore your progression through the Foundation Programme, depends on having an adequately populated e-portfolio. The e-portfolio should contain a minimum number of workplace-based assessments (Mini-CEX, DOPS, ACAT, and CBD as appropriate), reflective entries on significant clinical experiences, evidence of teaching, quality improvement work, and completion of mandatory training. The specific requirements vary slightly between Foundation Schools, and you should familiarise yourself with your school's specific e-portfolio requirements at the start of FY1 rather than attempting to populate it retrospectively at ARCP time.

Does the UKMLA prepare me for the clinical realities of FY1?

The UKMLA is specifically designed to test the knowledge and clinical skills required for safe FY1 practice, and the MLA Content Map is explicitly aligned with the clinical presentations and management decisions that FY1 doctors encounter. Candidates who have prepared thoroughly for the UKMLA — building integrated knowledge across all clinical domains, developing safe prescribing competency, and practising the communication skills assessed in the CPSA — consistently report feeling better prepared for FY1 than those who approached the UKMLA as a purely examination-focused exercise. The UKMLA is not a perfect proxy for FY1 readiness, but it is a meaningful preparation process when engaged with seriously.

What support is available if I fail the UKMLA close to the FY1 start date?

If you fail the UKMLA close to the August FY1 start date, contact your medical school's student support services, your Dean of Medical School, and the UKFPO immediately. Your medical school has pastoral and academic support structures specifically for students in this situation. The UKFPO can advise on the implications for your foundation post allocation and the options for deferred entry. The GMC also has guidance for candidates who are unable to complete the licensing assessment before their intended FY1 start. Early, proactive communication with all relevant parties is essential — do not wait until the situation becomes critical before seeking support.

Conclusion: UKMLA Success Is Your First Step Towards a Foundation Career

The relationship between the UKMLA and the Foundation Programme is not merely administrative — it is the foundation (in every sense) of your medical career in the United Kingdom. Passing the UKMLA demonstrates that you have the knowledge and clinical skills to practice safely as a foundation doctor; the Foundation Programme then develops those skills in the richness of real clinical practice over two years. Understanding this connection — how the MLA Content Map maps to FY1 work, how the UKFPO allocation process works, what the Foundation Programme Curriculum 2021 expects, and how the ARCP keeps you on track — allows you to approach both the UKMLA and the Foundation Programme with clarity and purpose. For detailed guidance on UKMLA preparation strategies and the full AKT examination format, explore the related resources on this site.
