post_excerpt: Endocrine and diabetic presentations are among the most tested UKMLA topics. Here is a focused guide to the conditions, investigations, and management principles.
seo_title: Endocrinology and Diabetes in the UKMLA: Targeted Revision Guide
seo_description: Diabetes and endocrine conditions are high-yield UKMLA topics. Our revision guide covers T1DM, T2DM, DKA, HHS, thyroid disease, and adrenal conditions.
html_content:
Endocrinology and Diabetes for the UKMLA AKT: A Complete Revision Guide

Endocrinology and diabetes represent one of the highest-yield areas of the UKMLA Applied Knowledge Test. This specialty cuts across almost every system — cardiovascular, renal, neurological, and reproductive — making it essential to master not just individual conditions but the clinical reasoning that connects them. This guide covers the full UKMLA endocrinology syllabus with the most up-to-date guidelines, including the landmark February 2026 NICE NG28 update on type 2 diabetes management.

If you are new to the UKMLA, begin with our introduction to the UKMLA and our full syllabus breakdown before diving into individual topics.

Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus — NICE NG28 (February 2026 Update)

The February 2026 update to NICE NG28 represents the most significant change to type 2 diabetes management guidance in a decade. The key shift is the move away from metformin monotherapy as universal first-line treatment toward a stratified, dual-therapy approach from diagnosis in most patients.

Initial Pharmacological Management

For most people with type 2 diabetes at diagnosis, NICE 2026 now recommends metformin plus an SGLT-2 inhibitor as initial dual therapy. This applies particularly to patients who have established cardiovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, or are at high cardiovascular risk. The rationale is the robust evidence base showing that SGLT-2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin) reduce major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) and slow progression of diabetic nephropathy independently of their glucose-lowering effect.

SGLT-2 inhibitors work by blocking sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 in the proximal renal tubule, causing glycosuria. Key benefits include:

  • Reduction in HbA1c of approximately 7–10 mmol/mol
  • Weight loss of 2–3 kg
  • Modest blood pressure reduction
  • Proven reduction in hospitalisation for heart failure (EMPA-REG, CANVAS, DECLARE-TIMI)
  • Slowing of eGFR decline in CKD (CREDENCE, DAPA-CKD)

Important contraindications and cautions for SGLT-2 inhibitors include eGFR below 30 ml/min/1.73m² (not licensed for glucose lowering at this level, though dapagliflozin retains heart failure and CKD indication), recurrent urinary tract infections, genital mycotic infections, and patients with type 1 diabetes (risk of euglycaemic DKA). Patients should be counselled to withhold SGLT-2 inhibitors during acute illness or surgical starvation — the so-called sick-day rules.

Individualising HbA1c Targets

NICE 2026 maintains the principle of individualised HbA1c targets. The general target is 48 mmol/mol (6.5%) for most people newly diagnosed on lifestyle or single non-hypoglycaemia-risk drugs. For those on drugs that carry hypoglycaemia risk (sulfonylureas, insulin), the target is relaxed to 53 mmol/mol (7.0%). Targets should be further relaxed to 58–64 mmol/mol for frail elderly patients, those with limited life expectancy, or where tight control poses more risk than benefit.

GLP-1 Receptor Agonists

GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide) have a specific role in NICE guidance for patients with:

  • BMI ≥35 kg/m² with significant psychological or other comorbidity
  • Established cardiovascular disease where weight management is a priority
  • Inadequate control on dual or triple oral therapy

Semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy) has attracted enormous public attention as a weight-loss agent. In the UKMLA context, the key facts are: GLP-1 RAs stimulate glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite. They carry low intrinsic hypoglycaemia risk. Common side effects are nausea and vomiting (usually transient). They are not used in patients with personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MEN2).

Insulin Initiation in Type 2 Diabetes

When oral/injectable therapies have failed to achieve targets, insulin is initiated. NICE recommends starting with once-daily basal insulin (isophane/NPH or insulin glargine/detemir). Key principles for AKT questions: start at 10 units at bedtime, titrate according to fasting glucose, combine with metformin to reduce weight gain and total dose requirements, and educate on hypoglycaemia recognition and DVLA reporting requirements.

Drug ClassExample DrugsMechanismKey UKMLA Points
BiguanidesMetforminReduces hepatic glucose output, improves insulin sensitivityHold if eGFR <30, contrast media, surgery; lactic acidosis risk; GI side effects
SGLT-2 inhibitorsEmpagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozinInhibit renal glucose reabsorptionCV and renal protection; genital infections; DKA risk; sick-day rules
GLP-1 receptor agonistsSemaglutide, liraglutideIncretin mimetics — glucose-dependent insulin releaseWeight loss; nausea; avoid in MEN2/medullary thyroid Ca
DPP-4 inhibitorsSitagliptin, saxagliptinInhibit DPP-4 enzyme, increasing endogenous GLP-1Weight neutral; saxagliptin linked to HF risk; dose reduce in CKD
SulfonylureasGliclazide, glibenclamideClose K-ATP channels on beta cells, stimulate insulin releaseHypoglycaemia risk; weight gain; glibenclamide avoid in elderly
InsulinMultiple formulationsDirect insulin replacementHypoglycaemia; weight gain; storage; injection technique; DVLA rules

Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus — NICE NG17

Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition caused by T-cell mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells. Insulin deficiency is absolute, making insulin therapy non-negotiable from diagnosis.

Structured Education

NICE NG17 places enormous emphasis on structured self-management education. The DAFNE (Dose Adjustment For Normal Eating) programme is specifically recommended — a 5-day group course teaching carbohydrate counting and dose adjustment. Evidence shows DAFNE improves HbA1c, reduces hypoglycaemia, and improves quality of life. All patients with type 1 diabetes should be offered structured education within 6–12 months of diagnosis.

Technology in Type 1 Diabetes

The UKMLA AKT increasingly tests knowledge of diabetes technology:

  • Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM): Flash glucose monitoring (FreeStyle Libre) measures interstitial glucose every minute. NICE now recommends offering CGM to all people with type 1 diabetes as standard. Key metrics: time-in-range (TIR) target >70% between 3.9–10.0 mmol/L; time below range <4%.
  • Sensor-augmented pump (SAP) therapy: Insulin pump (CSII) combined with CGM. NICE recommends for those with frequent severe hypoglycaemia, hypoglycaemia unawareness, or HbA1c ≥69 mmol/mol despite optimal MDI.
  • Closed-loop systems: Automated insulin delivery systems (hybrid closed-loop or artificial pancreas) — insulin delivery adjusted automatically based on CGM readings.

Sick-Day Rules in Type 1 Diabetes — SADMANS

Patients should know to withhold certain drugs during illness. For type 1 diabetes, the SADMANS mnemonic identifies drugs that worsen DKA or AKI during illness:

  • Sulfonylureas
  • ACE inhibitors / ARBs
  • Diuretics
  • Metformin
  • Agents for NSAIDs (NSAIDs themselves)
  • NSGLT-2 inhibitors
  • SGLT-2 inhibitors (repeated for emphasis)

Insulin must never be stopped in type 1 diabetes even if not eating — dose may need adjustment but omitting insulin risks DKA.

Hypoglycaemia

Hypoglycaemia is defined biochemically as plasma glucose <4 mmol/L, though symptoms vary. Whipple's triad remains the diagnostic cornerstone: (1) symptoms consistent with hypoglycaemia, (2) low plasma glucose at the time of symptoms, (3) relief of symptoms with glucose correction.

Symptoms

Autonomic (adrenergic) symptoms occur first: sweating, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, hunger. Neuroglycopaenic symptoms occur at lower glucose levels: confusion, aggression, seizures, loss of consciousness. Hypoglycaemia unawareness — absent autonomic warning — is a particular danger and develops after recurrent hypoglycaemia or long-standing diabetes.

Management

  • Conscious patient: 15–20g fast-acting carbohydrate (150–200ml fruit juice, Lucozade, or glucose tablets), recheck after 15 minutes, repeat if still <4 mmol/L. Follow with long-acting carbohydrate to prevent recurrence.
  • Impaired consciousness: IM glucagon 1mg (caution: ineffective in alcohol-related hypoglycaemia and after prolonged fasting as glycogen stores are depleted) OR IV 75–100ml 20% dextrose or 150–200ml 10% dextrose.
  • Identify and address precipitant: missed meal, excess insulin/sulfonylurea dose, increased exercise, alcohol.

Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) — JBDS 2023 Protocol

DKA is a life-threatening emergency occurring almost exclusively in type 1 diabetes (rarely type 2, and increasingly described with SGLT-2 inhibitors even at normal glucose — euglycaemic DKA).

Diagnostic Criteria

The JBDS 2023 protocol defines DKA by ALL THREE of:

  • Blood glucose >11 mmol/L (or known diabetes)
  • Blood ketones >3 mmol/L (or urine ketones ≥2+)
  • Bicarbonate <15 mmol/L or venous pH <7.3

Severity classification: mild (pH 7.25–7.30), moderate (pH 7.10–7.24), severe (pH <7.10).

Management Protocol

Fluid resuscitation: 1L 0.9% sodium chloride over first hour, then subsequent litres over 2 hours, 4 hours, 4 hours, 6 hours based on assessment. Avoid hypotonic fluids. Switch to 10% glucose when blood glucose <14 mmol/L to allow continued fixed-rate insulin infusion.

Fixed-rate insulin infusion: 0.1 units/kg/hour using a separate syringe pump. Do NOT bolus. Continue until ketones <0.6 mmol/L, pH >7.35, and patient eating/drinking — then convert to subcutaneous insulin and discontinue infusion 30–60 minutes after first subcutaneous dose.

Potassium management: Insulin drives potassium intracellularly — hypokalaemia is a major risk. Check potassium before starting insulin. If K <3.5 mmol/L, correct before or simultaneously with starting insulin. Replace potassium in IV fluids (add 40 mmol/L if K 3.5–5.5 mmol/L). Stop potassium replacement if K >5.5 mmol/L.

Cerebral oedema: A rare but devastating complication, particularly in children and young adults. Risk factors include rapid rehydration, excessive insulin, high initial urea. Management: IV mannitol 0.5–1g/kg or hypertonic saline 2.7% 5ml/kg. Have neurosurgical input.

Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS)

HHS is the equivalent emergency in type 2 diabetes. Key differences from DKA: glucose is typically very high (>30 mmol/L), osmolality >320 mOsm/kg, minimal ketosis (residual insulin prevents lipolysis), and develops gradually over days to weeks. Patients are profoundly dehydrated.

Management: Gradual rehydration over 48 hours with 0.9% sodium chloride. Target reduction of osmolality by 3–8 mOsm/kg/hour. Low-dose insulin (0.05 units/kg/hour) is introduced only after fluid resuscitation has begun and glucose is not falling with fluids alone. Thromboprophylaxis with LMWH is essential — HHS carries very high VTE risk. Monitor for cerebral oedema with rapid correction.

Thyroid Disorders

Hypothyroidism

Primary hypothyroidism (elevated TSH, low free T4) most commonly results from Hashimoto thyroiditis (autoimmune) in iodine-replete countries, or post-thyroidectomy/radioiodine. Symptoms: weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hair loss, bradycardia, depression, menorrhagia, myalgia, slowed reflexes, carpal tunnel syndrome, macrocytic anaemia.

Treatment: Levothyroxine, starting dose usually 50 micrograms daily. In elderly patients and those with known or suspected ischaemic heart disease, start at 25 micrograms to avoid precipitating angina or arrhythmia from sudden increase in metabolic demand. Titrate TSH to 0.4–4.0 mU/L (target lower end in symptomatic patients, allow slightly higher in elderly). Taken on empty stomach, 30 minutes before food. Interactions: calcium, iron, PPIs reduce absorption.

Myxoedema coma: Extreme untreated hypothyroidism — hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, altered consciousness. Medical emergency: IV T3 (liothyronine) or IV T4, IV hydrocortisone (adrenal insufficiency commonly coexists), gentle rewarming, supportive care in ICU.

Hyperthyroidism and Graves Disease

Graves disease accounts for ~75% of hyperthyroidism. Autoimmune — TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb) stimulate the thyroid continuously. Symptoms: weight loss despite increased appetite, heat intolerance, sweating, palpitations, tremor, diarrhoea, anxiety, oligomenorrhoea, lid lag, proptosis (exophthalmos — specific to Graves).

Investigation: Suppressed TSH with elevated free T4 and/or T3. TRAb elevated in Graves. Thyroid uptake scan differentiates causes (diffuse uptake in Graves, single hot nodule in toxic adenoma, low/absent uptake in thyroiditis).

Management options:

  • Carbimazole: First-line antithyroid drug. Blocks thyroid peroxidase. Risk of agranulocytosis (<1%) — warn patients to report sore throat/fever urgently and check FBC. Block-and-replace or titration regimen.
  • Propylthiouracil (PTU): Preferred in first trimester of pregnancy (lower teratogenic risk than carbimazole) and thyroid crisis. Risk of serious hepatotoxicity — switch to carbimazole after first trimester.
  • Radioiodine (I-131): Definitive treatment; not in pregnancy, active Graves ophthalmopathy (may worsen), or breastfeeding. Hypothyroidism almost inevitable — patients need lifelong levothyroxine.
  • Surgery (thyroidectomy): For large goitres, suspected malignancy, patient preference, or where drugs/radioiodine contraindicated.

Thyroid crisis (thyroid storm): Life-threatening exacerbation — tachycardia, hyperpyrexia, confusion, vomiting, multi-organ failure. Precipitated by surgery, infection, or radioiodine. Management: PTU (blocks hormone synthesis and peripheral conversion), potassium iodide (blocks release), dexamethasone, propranolol, supportive care in ICU.

Thyroid Nodules and Cancer

Thyroid nodules are common (prevalence ~50% in adults on ultrasound). Majority are benign. Malignant features on ultrasound (microcalcification, irregular margins, taller-than-wide shape, solid hypoechoic nodule) prompt fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC).

Thyroid cancer prognosis (best to worst): Papillary (most common, excellent prognosis, lymph node spread but indolent) > Follicular (blood-borne spread, good prognosis) > Medullary (calcitonin-secreting, C-cell origin, associated with MEN2, intermediate prognosis) > Anaplastic (undifferentiated, rapidly fatal, median survival weeks).

Adrenal Disorders

Addison's Disease (Primary Adrenal Insufficiency)

Autoimmune destruction of adrenal cortex is the most common cause in the UK. Results in deficiency of cortisol (glucocorticoid) and aldosterone (mineralocorticoid).

Symptoms and signs: Fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, nausea, abdominal pain, postural hypotension (aldosterone deficiency), hyperpigmentation (elevated ACTH drives MSH production — bronze skin especially in skin creases, scars, buccal mucosa), hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, eosinophilia.

Investigation: Short synacthen test — measure cortisol at 0 and 30 minutes after IM/IV tetracosactide 250 mcg. Normal: cortisol rises to >450–500 nmol/L. Addison's: subnormal response. Elevated ACTH confirms primary (vs secondary where ACTH is low/normal).

Treatment: Hydrocortisone (replacement dose 15–20mg daily in divided doses, higher morning dose to mimic diurnal rhythm) + fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid replacement, typically 100 micrograms daily). Medical alert bracelet essential.

Sick-day rules (steroid dose doubling): During illness, fever, or surgery, patients must double or triple hydrocortisone dose. For vomiting, IM hydrocortisone 100mg is given.

Addisonian crisis: Acute adrenal insufficiency — circulatory shock, severe hypoglycaemia. Management: IV hydrocortisone 100mg STAT, IV 0.9% saline 1L rapidly, 10% glucose if hypoglycaemic, treat precipitant.

Cushing's Syndrome

Excess cortisol. Causes: exogenous steroids (most common overall), ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma (Cushing's disease, ~70% of endogenous), ectopic ACTH (small cell lung cancer, carcinoid), adrenal adenoma/carcinoma.

Clinical features: Central obesity, buffalo hump, moon face, purple striae (wide), proximal myopathy, bruising, poor wound healing, hypertension, hyperglycaemia, osteoporosis, hirsutism, mood disturbance.

Investigation: Screening tests — late-night salivary cortisol, 24-hour urinary free cortisol, overnight low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (1mg at midnight, measure cortisol at 9am — failure to suppress below 50 nmol/L = positive). Then localise: ACTH level (high in pituitary/ectopic, low in adrenal), MRI pituitary, CRH stimulation test, high-dose dexamethasone suppression, inferior petrosal sinus sampling if doubt.

Hyperaldosteronism (Conn's Syndrome)

Primary hyperaldosteronism from an aldosterone-secreting adrenal adenoma (Conn syndrome) or bilateral adrenal hyperplasia. Key biochemical triad: hypertension + hypokalaemia + suppressed renin. Aldosterone:renin ratio elevated. Adrenal CT to locate adenoma. Unilateral adenoma: adrenalectomy. Bilateral hyperplasia: spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist).

Phaeochromocytoma

Catecholamine-secreting tumour of adrenal medulla (80%) or extra-adrenal paraganglioma. Associated with MEN2, NF1, VHL disease. Presenting triad: episodic hypertension, severe headache, sweating, and palpitations (the "hypertensive paroxysms"). Hypertensive crisis can be precipitated by abdominal palpation, contrast media, certain drugs (TCAs, metoclopramide).

Investigation: 24-hour urinary metanephrines (normetanephrine + metanephrine) or plasma metanephrines. CT/MRI adrenals. MIBG scan for metastatic disease.

Pre-operative management: Alpha-blockade first (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) for 10–14 days before surgery, then add beta-blocker. Never give beta-blocker first — unopposed alpha stimulation causes severe hypertensive crisis.

Pituitary Disorders: Acromegaly

GH-secreting pituitary adenoma causes acromegaly in adults (gigantism if before epiphyseal fusion). Features: coarsening of facial features, macroglossia, prognathism, enlarging hands/feet (ring/shoe size change), hyperhidrosis, headache, bitemporal hemianopia (chiasm compression), carpal tunnel, arthropathy, hypertension, diabetes mellitus.

Investigation: IGF-1 elevated (best screening test). Glucose suppression test: oral glucose 75g → normally GH suppresses to <0.4 µg/L; in acromegaly, GH fails to suppress or paradoxically rises. MRI pituitary to visualise adenoma.

Treatment: Transsphenoidal surgery first-line. If residual/recurrent: somatostatin analogues (octreotide, lanreotide) reduce GH secretion; pegvisomant (GH receptor antagonist) for resistant cases; radiotherapy.

Hypercalcaemia

The CHIMPANZEES mnemonic: Calcium supplements, Hyperparathyroidism (primary), Iatrogenic/Immobility, Metastases/Myeloma, Paget disease, Addison's disease, Neoplasia (malignancy — PTHrP), Zollinger-Ellison, Excess vitamin D, Excess thyroxine, Sarcoidosis (and other granulomatous disease).

Symptoms: "Bones, groans, moans, and stones" — bone pain, abdominal pain/constipation (groans), psychosis/depression (moans), renal calculi (stones). ECG: shortened QT interval.

Management of severe hypercalcaemia (>3.5 mmol/L or symptomatic): IV 0.9% saline rehydration (dilution + promotes urinary calcium excretion) — this is the most important initial step. IV bisphosphonate (pamidronate or zoledronic acid) — onset 24–48 hours, peak effect 4–7 days. Treat underlying cause. Denosumab for malignancy-related hypercalcaemia. Calcitonin for rapid short-term reduction.

SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone)

Diagnostic criteria: Hyponatraemia (Na <135 mmol/L), plasma osmolality <275 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated), urine sodium >30 mmol/L, no oedema, no hypovolaemia, no adrenal/thyroid/renal insufficiency.

Causes: CNS disease (meningitis, SAH, head injury), pulmonary disease (pneumonia, TB, SCLC), drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, PPIs, opiates), postoperative, pain.

Management: Fluid restriction (500–1000ml/day) is first-line for chronic. Demeclocycline (tetracycline antibiotic — causes nephrogenic DI) for persistent cases. Hypertonic saline (1.8% or 3%) for severe/symptomatic (Na <120 with seizures/coma) — correct no faster than 6–8 mmol/L in first 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome. Tolvaptan (V2 receptor antagonist) for resistant cases — licensed for SIADH.

FAQ: Endocrinology for the UKMLA

What is the most important change in NICE NG28 (2026) for type 2 diabetes?

The most significant change is the shift to metformin plus an SGLT-2 inhibitor as initial dual therapy for most patients with type 2 diabetes at diagnosis, replacing the previous stepwise approach starting with metformin monotherapy. This change is driven by robust evidence for cardiovascular and renal protection from SGLT-2 inhibitors beyond their glucose-lowering effect.

What is the JBDS DKA diagnostic criteria?

DKA is diagnosed when all three criteria are met: blood glucose >11 mmol/L (or known diabetes), blood ketones >3 mmol/L or urine ketones ≥2+, and bicarbonate <15 mmol/L or venous pH <7.3. The fixed-rate insulin infusion is 0.1 units/kg/hour and must not be started before checking and correcting potassium.

How do you differentiate Cushing's syndrome causes?

After confirming hypercortisolaemia, measure ACTH: elevated ACTH indicates pituitary or ectopic source; suppressed ACTH indicates adrenal adenoma/carcinoma. High-dose dexamethasone (8mg) suppression differentiates pituitary (>50% suppression) from ectopic (no suppression). MRI pituitary and inferior petrosal sinus sampling further localise pituitary sources.

Why must you alpha-block before beta-block in phaeochromocytoma?

Beta-blockade without prior alpha-blockade removes beta-2-mediated vasodilation while leaving alpha-1 vasoconstriction unopposed, causing severe hypertensive crisis. Alpha-blockade first prevents this. The standard regimen is phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin for at least 10–14 days, then add a beta-blocker.

What are the sick-day rules for patients on steroids (Addison's disease)?

Patients with adrenal insufficiency must double or triple their oral hydrocortisone during fever, illness, or physical stress. If vomiting or unable to take oral medication, they must use emergency IM hydrocortisone (100mg) and attend hospital. All patients should carry a steroid emergency card and medical alert bracelet. During surgery, parenteral hydrocortisone cover is mandatory.

What is the test of choice for primary adrenal insufficiency?

The short synacthen test is the investigation of choice. Cortisol is measured at baseline and 30 minutes after 250 micrograms tetracosactide IM/IV. A peak cortisol below 450–500 nmol/L is abnormal. ACTH measurement differentiates primary (elevated ACTH) from secondary/tertiary (low or normal ACTH).

Conclusion: Mastering Endocrinology for the UKMLA

Endocrinology appears throughout the UKMLA AKT in clinical scenarios that require you to interpret blood results, select investigations, manage emergencies, and apply up-to-date guidelines. The February 2026 update to type 2 diabetes management is particularly high-yield given its recency and clinical importance. Focus your revision on understanding the rationale behind guidelines — why SGLT-2 inhibitors are now first-line, why alpha-blockade precedes beta-blockade in phaeochromocytoma, why potassium must be checked before insulin in DKA — rather than memorising isolated facts.

Review our AKT exam format guide and UKMLA preparation strategies to build your revision plan around high-yield topics like this one. Endocrinology combined with cardiology and respiratory medicine forms the backbone of internal medicine questions in the AKT — master these and your performance across the exam will improve substantially.

seo_title: Rheumatology in the UKMLA: Key Conditions and Revision Tips
seo_description: Rheumatology features regularly in the UKMLA AKT and CPSA. Revise rheumatoid arthritis, gout, SLE, osteoporosis, and musculoskeletal examinations with this guide.
html_content:
Rheumatology for the UKMLA: A Complete Revision Guide

Rheumatology is a specialty rich with clinical reasoning, pattern recognition, and guideline application — exactly what the UKMLA AKT tests. From autoimmune conditions with multi-system manifestations to degenerative joint disease management, this guide covers every major rheumatological condition on the UKMLA syllabus. This is one of the areas where understanding diagnostic criteria and distinguishing between similar conditions pays the highest dividends in the exam.

Before diving in, review our UKMLA overview and the full syllabus to understand how rheumatology fits within the broader exam blueprint.

Rheumatoid Arthritis

Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic systemic inflammatory disease characterised by symmetrical polyarthritis affecting small joints, with extra-articular manifestations. It affects approximately 1% of the UK population, with a female:male ratio of 3:1 and peak onset in the 4th–5th decade.

2010 ACR/EULAR Classification Criteria

The 2010 criteria replace the older 1987 criteria and are better at identifying early RA. A score ≥6/10 points classifies definite RA:

  • Joint involvement (0–5): 1 medium-large joint = 0; 2–10 medium-large = 1; 1–3 small joints = 2; 4–10 small joints = 3; >10 joints including at least 1 small joint = 5
  • Serology (0–3): Negative RF and anti-CCP = 0; low positive = 2; high positive = 3
  • Acute phase reactants (0–1): Normal CRP/ESR = 0; abnormal = 1
  • Duration of symptoms (0–1): <6 weeks = 0; ≥6 weeks = 1

Anti-CCP (anti-citrullinated protein antibodies) are more specific for RA than rheumatoid factor (RF) and are associated with more erosive, severe disease. RF is positive in ~70–80% of RA but also found in Sjögren syndrome, SLE, chronic infections, and normal elderly. Anti-CCP is positive in ~75–80% of RA with specificity ~95%.

DMARD Therapy

Methotrexate is first-line DMARD for RA. Weekly oral or subcutaneous administration (7.5–25mg weekly). Folic acid 5mg weekly (not same day as methotrexate) reduces side effects. Monitoring: FBC and LFTs every 2–3 months. Contraindicated in pregnancy, significant hepatic or renal impairment, immunodeficiency. Side effects: hepatotoxicity, myelosuppression, mucositis, interstitial lung disease (ILD), teratogenicity.

Other conventional DMARDs: hydroxychloroquine (antimalarial, safest — requires annual eye monitoring for maculopathy), sulfasalazine, leflunomide.

Combination DMARD therapy (typically methotrexate + sulfasalazine + hydroxychloroquine — "triple therapy") is recommended if single DMARD fails.

Biologic and Targeted Therapies

Indicated for moderate-to-severe RA failing conventional DMARDs (DAS28 >5.1 with inadequate response to 2 conventional DMARDs including methotrexate).

  • TNF inhibitors: Adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, certolizumab, golimumab. Screen for latent TB before starting (tuberculin skin test or IGRA). Contraindicated in active infection, heart failure (NYHA III/IV), demyelinating disease, malignancy in last 5 years.
  • IL-6 inhibitors: Tocilizumab, sarilumab. Can mask infection (CRP suppressed).
  • JAK inhibitors: Tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib. Oral targeted synthetic DMARDs. Black box warnings: VTE risk, malignancy, serious infections, MACE. Check lipids at initiation.
  • Rituximab: B-cell depletion, given in 2-cycle infusion, reserved for seropositive RA or where biologics failed/contraindicated.
  • Abatacept: T-cell costimulation blocker.

DAS28 Score

Disease Activity Score 28 assesses 28 joints for tenderness and swelling, plus ESR or CRP, and patient global assessment. Thresholds: remission <2.6; low disease activity 2.6–3.2; moderate 3.2–5.1; high >5.1. NICE uses DAS28 >5.1 as threshold for biologic eligibility.

Extra-Articular Manifestations

SystemManifestationKey Points
SkinRheumatoid nodulesSubcutaneous, over extensor surfaces and pressure points; associated with RF seropositivity
LungsILD, pleural effusion, Caplan syndromeCaplan: large pulmonary nodules in RA + pneumoconiosis
EyesScleritis, episcleritis, keratoconjunctivitis siccaScleritis — severe, painful, requires urgent treatment
BloodAnaemia of chronic disease, neutropenia, thrombocytosisFelty syndrome: RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia
CardiovascularAccelerated atherosclerosis, pericarditisCVD risk doubled in RA — treat aggressively
NervousPeripheral neuropathy, carpal tunnel, atlanto-axial subluxationAtlanto-axial subluxation: check pre-anaesthesia with flexion/extension cervical X-rays
KidneysAA amyloidosis (secondary)Proteinuria in longstanding RA — investigate with SAP scan

Osteoarthritis

Osteoarthritis (OA) is a degenerative joint disease characterised by cartilage loss, subchondral bone changes, osteophyte formation, and synovial inflammation. It is the most common musculoskeletal condition globally. Risk factors: age, female sex, obesity, previous joint injury, repetitive loading, genetic predisposition.

NICE NG226 (2022) Management

NICE 2022 emphasises a biopsychosocial approach:

  1. Education and self-management: Explain OA is not just "wear and tear" and that exercise helps.
  2. Exercise: Aerobic and strengthening exercise is the cornerstone of OA management — reduces pain and improves function regardless of severity. Refer to physiotherapy or group exercise programme.
  3. Weight management: Particularly important for hip and knee OA — every 1kg weight loss reduces knee load by 4kg.
  4. Analgesia: Topical NSAIDs first (better safety profile than oral, especially for knee/hand OA). Paracetamol has limited evidence for sustained benefit — do not use as regular long-term treatment in isolation. Oral NSAIDs for short-term pain relief with lowest effective dose and PPI gastroprotection. Weak opioids only if other options inadequate. Capsaicin cream for hand/knee OA.
  5. Intra-articular injections: Corticosteroid injections for flares — short-term benefit. Hyaluronic acid injections: NICE does not routinely recommend but some evidence for knee OA.
  6. Surgery: Joint replacement for severe OA with significant pain and functional limitation not responding to conservative measures. Oxford score used to assess eligibility. Arthroscopic lavage and debridement not recommended.

Gout — NICE NG219 (2022)

Gout is the most common inflammatory arthritis in adults. Caused by monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints and soft tissues due to hyperuricaemia. Risk factors: male sex, obesity, CKD, hypertension, diuretics (particularly thiazides and loop diuretics), alcohol (especially beer and spirits), red meat and organ meat intake, fructose-rich drinks, ciclosporin, low-dose aspirin.

Acute Gout Management

Acute attack: intensely painful, hot, swollen joint — first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra) most classic, also ankle, knee, wrist.

  • NSAIDs (naproxen or indomethacin): First-line if no contraindication (not in CKD, peptic ulcer disease, anticoagulation). Give at maximum dose for 2–5 days.
  • Colchicine: Alternative to NSAIDs, particularly useful if renal impairment mild/moderate. 500 micrograms 2–4 times daily. Avoid in severe renal impairment (eGFR <10). GI side effects (diarrhoea) common.
  • Systemic corticosteroids: If NSAIDs and colchicine contraindicated. Prednisolone 30mg daily for 3–5 days or intra-articular steroid injection.
  • Do NOT start urate-lowering therapy during an acute attack (may prolong it). Do NOT stop ongoing urate-lowering therapy during an acute attack.

Urate-Lowering Therapy (ULT) — Prophylaxis

Indications: ≥2 attacks per year, tophaceous gout, gout with CKD, gout with renal calculi. NICE NG219 recommends offering ULT after first attack if CKD, hypertension, or other risk factors are present.

  • Allopurinol: Xanthine oxidase inhibitor — first-line ULT. Start at low dose (100mg daily, 50mg in CKD) at least 2–4 weeks after acute attack settles. Titrate monthly to achieve target serum urate <360 µmol/L (<300 µmol/L for tophaceous gout). Co-prescribe low-dose colchicine or NSAID for 6 months to prevent flares during initiation. HLA-B*5801 screening before allopurinol in those of Han Chinese, Korean, or Thai ethnicity (risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
  • Febuxostat: Alternative if allopurinol intolerant or ineffective. NICE: use with caution in cardiovascular disease (FAST trial showed non-inferiority to allopurinol on CV outcomes but slight excess all-cause mortality).
  • Lifestyle: Adequate hydration, reduce alcohol, reduce purine-rich foods (offal, red meat, shellfish, anchovies), avoid fructose-rich drinks.

Psoriatic Arthritis

Inflammatory arthritis associated with psoriasis (~30% of psoriasis patients). Can precede skin disease. Seronegative (RF negative). Classified by CASPAR criteria (must have inflammatory articular disease plus ≥3 points from: current psoriasis = 2 points; personal/family history psoriasis = 1; nail dystrophy = 1; negative RF = 1; current dactylitis or history = 1; juxta-articular new bone on X-ray = 1).

Five clinical subtypes: DIP joint involvement (characteristic — nail pitting, onycholysis), asymmetric oligoarthritis, symmetric polyarthritis (RA-like), spondylitis, and arthritis mutilans (most destructive — "pencil-in-cup" deformity on X-ray).

Features distinguishing from RA: nail involvement, DIP involvement, asymmetry, enthesitis (inflammation at tendon/ligament insertions — Achilles, plantar fascia), dactylitis ("sausage digit" — diffuse swelling of whole digit due to tenosynovitis), psoriatic skin/nail changes, RF negative.

Treatment: conventional DMARDs (methotrexate, leflunomide), TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab — very effective for psoriasis and arthritis), IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab), JAK inhibitors.

Ankylosing Spondylitis (Axial Spondyloarthropathy)

Chronic inflammatory disease primarily affecting the axial skeleton (sacroiliac joints and spine). Male predominance (3:1). Age of onset typically 20–40 years. Strong association with HLA-B27 (present in ~90% of AS patients vs ~8% of general population).

Clinical features: Insidious onset of inflammatory back pain (worse at night and in morning, improves with activity, not rest), progressive stiffness, restricted spinal mobility (modified Schober test: mark L5 and 10cm above — normal increase ≥5cm on full forward flexion; <4cm suggests restriction), reduced chest expansion (<5cm), uveitis (25%), enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia), aortic regurgitation (late complication).

Investigations: X-ray shows sacroiliitis (erosions, sclerosis, eventual fusion), syndesmophytes (bony bridges between vertebrae — bamboo spine on X-ray is late sign). MRI is more sensitive for early sacroiliitis before X-ray changes (shows bone marrow oedema).

Management: Physiotherapy is cornerstone — exercises maintain spinal mobility. NSAIDs (regular, not just PRN — disease-modifying effect in early AS). TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab) for active disease failing NSAIDs (NICE criteria: BASDAI ≥4 on 2 occasions 4 weeks apart, failed 2 NSAIDs). IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab) for axial disease. Conventional DMARDs (methotrexate) not effective for axial manifestations.

Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)

SLE is a multisystem autoimmune condition characterised by autoantibody production and immune complex deposition. Female:male 9:1. Peak onset reproductive years. More common and severe in Black Caribbean and South Asian populations.

2019 EULAR/ACR Classification Criteria

ANA (antinuclear antibody) titre ≥1:80 is the entry criterion (sensitivity ~98%). Weighted scoring across seven domains: constitutional, haematological, neuropsychiatric, mucocutaneous, serosal, musculoskeletal, and renal. Score ≥10 classifies SLE.

Clinical Features

  • Mucocutaneous: Malar rash (butterfly rash — spares nasolabial folds), discoid rash (scarring, atrophic), photosensitivity, oral ulcers, alopecia
  • Musculoskeletal: Arthralgia/arthritis (non-erosive in SLE, unlike RA)
  • Serositis: Pleuritis, pericarditis
  • Renal: Lupus nephritis — commonest cause of serious morbidity/mortality. Class III/IV most severe (proliferative nephritis)
  • Neuropsychiatric: Seizures, psychosis, stroke (especially with antiphospholipid syndrome)
  • Haematological: Haemolytic anaemia, leucopaenia, thrombocytopaenia

Autoantibodies in SLE

  • Anti-dsDNA: Highly specific for SLE (~70% sensitivity, ~95% specificity). Titres correlate with disease activity, especially lupus nephritis.
  • Anti-Smith (Sm): Highly specific (~99%) but low sensitivity (~30%). Pathognomonic of SLE.
  • Anti-Ro (SSA) and anti-La (SSB): Associated with neonatal lupus (congenital heart block — screen all anti-Ro positive pregnant women) and Sjögren overlap.
  • Antiphospholipid antibodies (anticardiolipin, anti-β2-glycoprotein I, lupus anticoagulant): Present in ~30% of SLE; associated with thrombosis and miscarriage.
  • Complement: C3 and C4 consumed during active SLE (low levels indicate active disease).

Treatment

  • Hydroxychloroquine: All patients with SLE unless contraindicated. Reduces flares, damage accrual, and mortality. Annual ophthalmology review for maculopathy (dose-dependent).
  • NSAIDs: For musculoskeletal symptoms and serositis — use with caution given renal involvement.
  • Corticosteroids: For flares and organ-threatening disease.
  • Immunosuppressants: Azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) for maintenance. Cyclophosphamide (IV pulses) for severe lupus nephritis class III/IV — given with mesna uroprotection.
  • Belimumab: Anti-BLYS biologic for persistently active seropositive SLE despite standard therapy.
  • Anifrolumab: Type I interferon receptor antagonist — newer biologic for moderate-to-severe SLE.

Polymyalgia Rheumatica and Giant Cell Arteritis

Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR)

Inflammatory condition affecting adults >50 years (rarely <50). Female predominance. Presents with: bilateral shoulder and/or pelvic girdle pain and stiffness, worse in morning (>45 minutes), systemic symptoms (fatigue, weight loss, low-grade fever). ESR typically >50 mm/hr, CRP elevated, CK normal (distinguishes from myositis). Bursitis on USS (typically bicipital and subdeltoid bursae in shoulders, trochanteric bursae in hips).

Treatment: Prednisolone 15mg daily — dramatic response within days (failure to respond should prompt diagnostic reconsideration). Gradually taper over 18–24 months. Monitor for relapse (rising ESR/CRP, return of symptoms).

Giant Cell Arteritis (Temporal/Cranial Arteritis)

Large vessel vasculitis affecting branches of external carotid artery and aorta. Occurs almost exclusively over 50 years. Features: new severe headache (temporal, occipital), scalp tenderness (difficulty combing hair), jaw claudication (highly specific — pain on chewing), visual loss (ischaemic optic neuropathy — amaurosis fugax may precede permanent blindness), diplopia, constitutional symptoms. 50% coexist with PMR.

Investigation: ESR typically >80–100 mm/hr, CRP markedly elevated. Temporal artery biopsy (TAB): skip lesions mean negative biopsy does not exclude diagnosis — treat empirically if clinical suspicion high. PET-CT or MRA for large vessel involvement.

Treatment: Prednisolone 60mg daily IMMEDIATELY if visual symptoms — do not delay for biopsy. Biopsy remains positive for 2 weeks after starting steroids. For visual loss: IV methylprednisolone 500–1g for 3 days. Low-dose aspirin. Tocilizumab (IL-6 inhibitor) is licensed for GCA as steroid-sparing agent.

Systemic Vasculitis

Vasculitides are classified by vessel size:

ClassificationConditionsKey FeaturesAntibodies/Tests
Large vesselGCA, Takayasu arteritisAorta and major branches; Takayasu: young women, Asian, arm claudication, absent pulsesNone specific
Medium vesselPAN, Kawasaki diseasePAN: no glomerulonephritis, hepatitis B association; Kawasaki: children, fever >5 days, IVIG treatmentANCA negative in PAN
Small vessel (ANCA)GPA, MPA, EGPAGPA: nose/sinus, saddle-nose, lung cavities, renal; MPA: pulmonary haemorrhage; EGPA: asthma + eosinophiliac-ANCA/PR3 in GPA; p-ANCA/MPO in MPA; EGPA both
Small vessel (immune complex)IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein), cryoglobulinaemia, lupus vasculitisIgA: palpable purpura, arthritis, abdominal pain, renal; typically post-upper respiratory infection in childrenElevated IgA in IgA vasculitis

Reactive Arthritis

Previously called Reiter syndrome. Post-infectious arthritis developing 1–4 weeks after urogenital (Chlamydia trachomatis) or gastrointestinal (Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia) infection. Classic triad: urethritis, arthritis, conjunctivitis — "can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree." Asymmetric oligoarthritis, predominantly lower limbs. HLA-B27 associated. Usually self-limiting over weeks to months. NSAIDs for symptom control. DMARDs if chronic (>6 months).

Fibromyalgia

Chronic widespread pain syndrome without structural joint disease or inflammation. 2010/2016 ACR diagnostic criteria: Widespread Pain Index (WPI) ≥7 + Symptom Severity Score (SSS) ≥5, or WPI 4–6 + SSS ≥9, symptoms present >3 months, no other disorder explaining pain. Associated features: fatigue, non-restorative sleep, cognitive symptoms ("fibro fog"), headache, IBS, anxiety/depression.

Management: Structured exercise (most evidence — aerobic and strengthening). CBT to address pain catastrophising and improve coping. Sleep hygiene. Low-dose amitriptyline or duloxetine for sleep/pain. Pregabalin — licensed in US but not recommended by NICE for fibromyalgia in UK. Avoid opioids — no evidence of benefit, risk of dependency and worsening central sensitisation. Avoid polypharmacy. Patient education and self-management groups.

Crystal Arthropathies Comparison

FeatureGoutPseudogout (CPPD)
CrystalMonosodium urateCalcium pyrophosphate dihydrate
Shape/appearanceNegatively birefringent, needle-shapedPositively birefringent, rhomboid-shaped
Common joints1st MTP, ankle, kneeKnee (most common), wrist, hip, shoulder
X-ray findingPunched-out erosions with overhanging edge, tophiChondrocalcinosis (calcification of cartilage)
AssociationsHyperuricaemia, renal disease, diuretics, alcoholHyperparathyroidism, haemochromatosis, hypomagnesaemia, hypothyroidism, advancing age
ManagementNSAIDs/colchicine for acute; allopurinol for prophylaxisNSAIDs/colchicine/steroids for acute; no proven chronic prevention

FAQ: Rheumatology for the UKMLA

What is the most specific antibody for SLE?

Anti-Smith (Sm) antibodies are the most specific (~99%) for SLE, though present in only ~30% of cases (low sensitivity). Anti-dsDNA antibodies are also highly specific (~95%) and are clinically useful because titres correlate with disease activity, particularly lupus nephritis. ANA is highly sensitive (~98%) but not specific — it is the entry criterion for the 2019 classification criteria.

What is the first-line treatment for an acute gout attack according to NICE NG219 2022?

NSAIDs (naproxen or indomethacin at maximum tolerated dose) are first-line for acute gout. Colchicine is an alternative, particularly when NSAIDs are contraindicated (renal impairment, GI history, anticoagulation). Corticosteroids are used if both NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated. Urate-lowering therapy should NOT be started during an acute attack but should not be stopped if already prescribed.

What distinguishes Graves ophthalmopathy and when does it affect treatment choice?

Graves ophthalmopathy (thyroid eye disease) is specific to Graves disease and is caused by TSH receptor antibody stimulation of orbital fibroblasts. Radioiodine can worsen ophthalmopathy — if active or severe Graves eye disease is present, radioiodine should be avoided or used with concomitant corticosteroids. Carbimazole is preferred in this setting. Active ophthalmopathy is treated with IV methylprednisolone and selenium supplementation; severe cases may require orbital decompression.

How is ankylosing spondylitis distinguished from mechanical back pain?

Inflammatory back pain (suggesting AS) has these features: onset <45 years, insidious onset, improves with exercise not rest, worse at night (wakes patient), morning stiffness >30–60 minutes, and good response to NSAIDs. Mechanical back pain is worse with activity, better with rest, no morning stiffness pattern. Positive family history of spondyloarthropathy, extra-articular features (uveitis, psoriasis, IBD), and HLA-B27 positivity further support the diagnosis.

When should DMARDs be started in rheumatoid arthritis?

DMARDs should be started as soon as the diagnosis of RA is confirmed — ideally within 3 months of persistent synovitis to prevent irreversible joint damage. Methotrexate is first-line. Rapid referral to rheumatology is essential. Treat-to-target strategy: aim for remission (DAS28 <2.6) or low disease activity (DAS28 <3.2) within 6 months. NICE recommends monthly monitoring of DAS28 until target is achieved, then 3–6 monthly in remission.

What investigations should be performed before starting TNF inhibitors?

Before initiating TNF inhibitors: IGRA (interferon-gamma release assay) or tuberculin skin test (TST) to screen for latent TB — treat latent TB before starting. Hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C antibody (risk of reactivation). HIV testing if risk factors. Chest X-ray. Full blood count, renal and liver function. Assess for demyelinating disease history. Assess cardiovascular risk. Review vaccination status (live vaccines contraindicated once on biologics).

Conclusion

Rheumatology rewards candidates who can distinguish between similar presentations — RA versus psoriatic arthritis, SLE versus other connective tissue diseases, septic arthritis versus crystal arthropathy. The UKMLA AKT tests these discriminating features alongside management knowledge and guideline application.

Focus on the diagnostic criteria for RA (2010 ACR/EULAR), SLE (2019 EULAR/ACR), and gout management (NICE NG219 2022) — these are particularly high yield. Understanding the biologic therapy landscape and its contraindications is increasingly tested as these treatments enter routine clinical practice.

Build your revision plan using our UKMLA preparation guide and review our results and scoring page to understand how marks are distributed across specialties.

post_excerpt: Renal presentations feature heavily in the UKMLA AKT. Here is a focused guide to AKI, CKD staging, electrolyte disorders, and the investigations that matter.
seo_title: Nephrology in the UKMLA: AKI, CKD, and Electrolytes Revision
seo_description: Renal conditions are heavily tested in the UKMLA AKT. Our revision guide covers AKI, CKD, electrolyte disorders, nephrotic syndrome, and urinalysis interpretation.
html_content:
Nephrology and Renal Medicine for the UKMLA: A Complete Revision Guide

Nephrology is an intellectually demanding specialty that underpins clinical practice across multiple domains. Fluid management, electrolyte disorders, kidney injury, and proteinuria assessment appear in almost every clinical setting. The UKMLA AKT tests nephrology extensively, requiring both an understanding of pathophysiology and the ability to apply the latest NICE and KDIGO guidelines in clinical scenarios.

This guide provides a comprehensive review of the nephrology UKMLA syllabus. Before starting, familiarise yourself with the UKMLA overview, the AKT format, and our preparation guide.

Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) — KDIGO 2024

AKI is a rapid deterioration in kidney function over hours to days. The KDIGO (Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes) 2024 criteria maintain the staging system established in 2012 but include updated guidance on management, biomarkers, and long-term follow-up.

KDIGO AKI Staging Criteria

StageSerum Creatinine CriteriaUrine Output Criteria
Stage 1×1.5–1.9 baseline within 7 days, OR rise ≥26.5 µmol/L within 48 hours<0.5 ml/kg/hour for 6–12 hours
Stage 2×2.0–2.9 baseline<0.5 ml/kg/hour for ≥12 hours
Stage 3×3.0 baseline, OR creatinine ≥354 µmol/L with acute rise ≥44 µmol/L, OR renal replacement therapy initiated, OR eGFR <35 ml/min in <18 years<0.3 ml/kg/hour for ≥24 hours, OR anuria ≥12 hours

Classification by Cause

Pre-renal AKI: Reduced kidney perfusion. Causes: hypovolaemia (haemorrhage, vomiting, diarrhoea, burns), reduced cardiac output (heart failure, cardiogenic shock), sepsis (vasodilation), renal artery stenosis. Urine sodium <20 mmol/L, FENa <1%, urine osmolality >500 mOsm/kg (kidneys conserving sodium and concentrating urine). Management: fluid resuscitation, treat cause.

Intrinsic (intrarenal) AKI:

  • Acute tubular necrosis (ATN): Most common intrinsic cause. Ischaemic (prolonged pre-renal) or nephrotoxic (aminoglycosides, contrast, myoglobinuria, haemolysis). Urine sodium >40 mmol/L, FENa >2%, muddy brown granular casts on microscopy.
  • Glomerulonephritis: Acute — dysmorphic red cells and red cell casts on urine microscopy.
  • Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN): Drug-induced (NSAIDs, penicillins, PPIs, allopurinol), infection, sarcoidosis. Fever, rash, eosinophilia, eosinophiluria (late sign). Renal biopsy may show eosinophilic infiltrate. Steroids if drug-induced and not resolving after stopping causative drug.
  • Vascular: Thrombotic microangiopathy (TTP/HUS), renal artery occlusion, atherosclerotic emboli (livedo reticularis, eosinophilia, renal failure after angiography or anticoagulation).

Post-renal AKI: Obstruction. Causes: BPH, bladder tumour, cervical cancer, bilateral ureteric obstruction (retroperitoneal fibrosis, malignancy), pelvic haematoma. Ultrasound shows hydronephrosis. Urgent urological input — nephrostomy or catheter decompression. AKI may worsen temporarily with decompression (post-obstructive diuresis).

Contrast-Induced AKI

Risk factors: pre-existing CKD (eGFR <60), diabetes, dehydration, high-volume contrast, repeated exposure. Prevention: use lowest effective contrast dose, IV 0.9% saline hydration pre- and post-procedure (KDIGO recommends if eGFR <30), stop nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB — withhold 48 hours before and after). N-acetylcysteine: not recommended by current KDIGO (no consistent benefit in trials).

Hepatorenal Syndrome (HRS)

Functional renal failure in severe hepatic disease (cirrhosis with ascites, acute liver failure). HRS-AKI (previously HRS-1): rapidly progressive, defined by AKI diagnostic criteria in a patient with cirrhosis, absence of shock, no sustained improvement after 2 days of diuretic withdrawal and IV albumin, no nephrotoxin exposure, no structural kidney disease. Pathophysiology: severe splanchnic vasodilation → reduced effective circulating volume → intense renal vasoconstriction. Treatment: vasoconstrictors (terlipressin IV with albumin — evidence from CONFIRM trial), liver transplantation for HRS-2 (chronic). Very poor prognosis without liver transplant.

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) — NICE NG203 (2021)

Staging

CKD is defined as eGFR <60 ml/min/1.73m² or evidence of kidney damage (proteinuria, haematuria, structural abnormality) persisting >3 months.

GFR categories (G1–G5) are combined with albuminuria categories (A1–A3) to provide a risk matrix:

GFR StageeGFR RangeDescription
G1≥90Normal or high — only CKD if other markers present
G260–89Mildly decreased
G3a45–59Mildly to moderately decreased
G3b30–44Moderately to severely decreased
G415–29Severely decreased
G5≤15Kidney failure (with or without dialysis)

Albuminuria: A1 <3 mg/mmol (normal); A2 3–30 mg/mmol (moderately increased); A3 >30 mg/mmol (severely increased).

Management of CKD

Blood pressure: Target <140/90 mmHg (<130/80 if diabetes or proteinuria). ACE inhibitor or ARB first-line, particularly with proteinuria — reduces progression (RENAAL, IDNT, REIN trials). Monitor potassium and creatinine after initiation (up to 30% rise in creatinine acceptable).

SGLT-2 inhibitors in CKD: Dapagliflozin (DAPA-CKD trial) and empagliflozin (EMPA-KIDNEY trial) demonstrate significant reductions in eGFR decline, end-stage renal disease, and cardiovascular events in CKD G2–G4 with albuminuria A2–A3, regardless of diabetes status. NICE now recommends dapagliflozin for CKD with eGFR 25–75 and ACR ≥22.6 mg/mmol.

Anaemia of CKD: Caused by reduced erythropoietin production, iron deficiency, and reduced red cell survival. Diagnose when Hb <110 g/L (or <120 g/L in men) with eGFR <60 and no other obvious cause. Investigate and correct iron deficiency first (ferritin <100 µg/L or transferrin saturation <20% — give IV iron if oral poorly tolerated or absorbed). Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESA): EPO/darbepoetin — target Hb 100–120 g/L. Avoid exceeding 120 g/L (increased VTE and stroke risk). Roxadustat (HIF-PHI inhibitor) — oral ESA now available.

CKD-Mineral Bone Disease (CKD-MBD): Rising phosphate (reduced renal excretion), reduced 1-alpha-hydroxylase → low calcitriol → secondary hyperparathyroidism → PTH rises to maintain calcium. Treatment: dietary phosphate restriction, phosphate binders (calcium-based — calcium acetate, non-calcium-based — sevelamer, lanthanum carbonate). Active vitamin D (alfacalcidol or calcitriol) to suppress PTH. Calcimimetics (cinacalcet) for severe secondary hyperparathyroidism.

Referral to nephrology: NICE recommends referral at eGFR <30 (all patients), eGFR <45 with rapid progression (>5 ml/min/1.73m² per year), persistent proteinuria ACR >70 mg/mmol, proteinuria ACR >30 with haematuria, AKI without clear cause, refractory hypertension (4+ agents), rare or inherited CKD.

Nephrotic Syndrome

Defined by the triad: proteinuria >3.5g/24 hours (or ACR >300–350 mg/mmol), hypoalbuminaemia (<25 g/L), and oedema. Hyperlipidaemia (reactive synthesis by liver) and lipiduria are also characteristic.

Causes and Key Features

CauseAge GroupKey FeaturesTreatment
Minimal Change DiseaseChildren (commonest), adultsNormal light microscopy, effacement of foot processes on EM; idiopathic or NSAIDs, lymphomaPrednisolone — excellent response (80–90%); cyclophosphamide or rituximab for frequent relapsers
Membranous NephropathyAdults (commonest adult nephrotic in UK)Anti-PLA2R antibodies in 70%; spike and dome on EM; associated with malignancy, HBV, SLE, NSAIDsMonitor anti-PLA2R; rituximab now first-line immunosuppression; cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids (Ponticelli regimen)
Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS)Adults, particularly Black patientsSegmental sclerosis on biopsy; secondary causes: HIV, heroin, obesity, reflux nephropathyHigh-dose prednisolone; tacrolimus/cyclosporin; poor prognosis, frequent progression to ESRD
Diabetic NephropathyAdults with diabetesKimmelstiel-Wilson nodules on biopsy; proteinuria precedes renal failure by yearsACEi/ARB + SGLT-2i; glycaemic control; BP control

Complications of Nephrotic Syndrome

  • VTE: Loss of anticoagulant proteins (protein C, protein S, antithrombin III) in urine → hypercoagulable state. Renal vein thrombosis particularly associated with membranous nephropathy. Consider prophylactic anticoagulation if albumin <20 g/L.
  • Infection: Loss of immunoglobulins in urine, oedema as culture medium, immunosuppressive treatment. Pneumococcal vaccine mandatory. Particular risk of encapsulated bacteria (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae).
  • Hyperlipidaemia: Increased hepatic lipoprotein synthesis. Long-term atherosclerosis risk.

Nephritic Syndrome

Nephritic syndrome presents with: haematuria (dysmorphic red cells, red cell casts), hypertension, oliguria, mild-moderate proteinuria (<3.5g/24hr), oedema. The pathophysiology is glomerular inflammation causing haematuria and GFR reduction.

Key Causes

IgA Nephropathy (Berger Disease): Commonest glomerulonephritis worldwide. Mesangial IgA deposits. Presents with macroscopic haematuria coinciding with or 1–3 days after upper respiratory or GI infection ("synpharyngitic haematuria" — unlike post-streptococcal GN which has a 2–3-week latency). Many patients have mild microscopic haematuria with mild proteinuria. ACEi/ARB for proteinuria control. Sparsentan (dual endothelin/angiotensin receptor antagonist) now approved for IgA nephropathy. Targeted-release budesonide (Nefecon) — oral intestinal-targeted immunosuppression. Prognosis variable — 20–30% reach ESRD over 20 years.

Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis: 2–3 weeks after Group A streptococcal pharyngitis or 4–6 weeks after skin infection. Children predominantly. Diffuse proliferative GN on biopsy. Low complement C3. Elevated ASOT titre. Usually self-limiting. Penicillin for residual streptococcal infection.

Rapidly Progressive Glomerulonephritis (RPGN): Aggressive form causing rapid loss of renal function over days to weeks. Crescent formation on biopsy (proliferating parietal epithelial cells ± fibrin). Three types by immunofluorescence: Type I — linear IgG (anti-GBM disease/Goodpasture syndrome); Type II — granular immune complex (post-infectious, SLE, IgA); Type III — pauci-immune (ANCA-associated — GPA, MPA).

Anti-GBM Disease (Goodpasture Syndrome): Linear IgG deposition against type IV collagen in GBM. Pulmonary-renal syndrome — haemoptysis + rapidly progressive GN. Anti-GBM antibodies specific. Treatment: plasma exchange (removes antibodies) + cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids. Poor prognosis if dialysis-dependent at presentation or >50% crescents on biopsy.

Renal Calculi — NICE NG118 (2019)

Renal calculi affect ~10% of the population at some point. More common in men, white ethnicity, hot climates (dehydration), certain metabolic conditions.

Presentation

Sudden onset severe loin-to-groin pain (ureteric colic) — cannot find a comfortable position (unlike peritonitis where still position helps). Associated with haematuria (frank or microscopic), nausea/vomiting, urinary frequency if stone at VUJ.

Investigation

CT KUB (computed tomography of kidney, ureter, bladder) is the gold standard — identifies all stone types (except pure indinavir stones in HIV patients on antiretroviral therapy), stone size, and degree of obstruction. No contrast required. USS is used in pregnancy and follow-up (avoids radiation). Plain X-ray (KUB) detects calcium-containing stones (radiopaque) but misses radiolucent uric acid stones and most cystine stones — not first-line for diagnosis.

Stone Composition and Metabolic Assessment

  • Calcium oxalate (~75%): Most common. Risk factors: low urine volume, hypercalciuria (primary hyperparathyroidism, vitamin D excess, immobility, loop diuretics), hyperoxaluria (inflammatory bowel disease, high oxalate diet), hypocitraturia (RTA, diarrhoea).
  • Calcium phosphate (~10%): Often associated with RTA or primary hyperparathyroidism.
  • Uric acid (~10%): Radiolucent on X-ray. Low urine pH (<5.5). Associations: gout, myeloproliferative disorders, high purine diet. Treatment: alkalinise urine (potassium citrate or sodium bicarbonate), allopurinol, increased fluids.
  • Struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate): Infection stones, urease-producing organisms (Proteus, Klebsiella). Staghorn calculi. Treat infection and stone.
  • Cystine: Rare, autosomal recessive cystinuria. Yellow-brown, soft hexagonal crystals in urine. Radiolucent. Alkalinise urine, penicillamine.

Management by Stone Size

  • <5mm: Likely to pass spontaneously (~90%). Medical expulsive therapy with tamsulosin (alpha-blocker relaxes ureter). Analgesia: NSAIDs first-line (diclofenac, ketorolac) superior to opioids for pain intensity. Adequate hydration.
  • 5–10mm: 50–75% pass spontaneously. Tamsulosin. Active intervention if pain uncontrolled, infection present, or persistent obstruction.
  • >10mm: Unlikely to pass. Active removal: ureteroscopy (URS) with laser lithotripsy for ureteric stones; extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy (ESWL) for renal stones; percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL) for large renal stones (>2cm). Absolute indication for emergency decompression: infected obstructed system (pyonephrosis) — nephrostomy or retrograde ureteric stent urgently.

Hyperkalaemia

Potentially life-threatening due to risk of cardiac arrhythmia. UKKA (UK Kidney Association) 2023 guidance provides thresholds for treatment and approach to management.

ECG Changes in Hyperkalaemia

Progressive with rising potassium: peaked T waves (K 5.5–6.5) → prolonged PR interval → loss of P waves → widened QRS complex → sine wave pattern → ventricular fibrillation/asystole. Any new ECG change in hyperkalaemia requires immediate cardiac monitoring.

Emergency Management

  1. Cardiac membrane stabilisation: IV calcium gluconate 10ml 10% over 5 minutes (repeat if no ECG improvement). Protects myocardium — does NOT lower potassium.
  2. Shift potassium intracellularly: IV insulin 10 units + IV 50ml 50% dextrose (or 125ml 20%). Onset 15–30 minutes, lasts 4–6 hours. Nebulised salbutamol 10–20mg. IV sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50ml if metabolic acidosis.
  3. Remove potassium from body: Furosemide IV (if renal function allows, promotes urinary K excretion). Patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (SZC, Lokelma) — cation exchange polymers that bind potassium in gut (onset hours to days — maintenance therapy not acute emergency use). Dialysis for refractory severe hyperkalaemia or AKI/CKD precluding other measures.

SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH Secretion)

Diagnostic Criteria

SIADH is euvolaemic hyponatraemia caused by inappropriately elevated ADH. Diagnostic criteria: serum sodium <135 mmol/L, serum osmolality <275 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg (key: inappropriately concentrated), urine sodium >30 mmol/L (kidneys excreting sodium despite hyponatraemia), euvolaemia (no clinical signs of dehydration or fluid overload), normal thyroid/adrenal/renal function, no diuretic use.

Causes

CNS: meningitis, encephalitis, SAH, stroke, head injury, brain tumour. Pulmonary: pneumonia, TB, SCLC, positive pressure ventilation. Drugs: SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide, tricyclics, chlorpropamide, desmopressin, opiates, PPIs. Postoperative. Pain and nausea (both stimulate ADH release).

Distinguish SIADH from CSWS (cerebral salt wasting — occurs in CNS disease, particularly SAH): CSWS involves hypovolaemia and natriuresis, while SIADH is euvolaemic. Important distinction because treatment differs: SIADH requires fluid restriction; CSWS requires fluid and sodium replacement.

Management

  • Fluid restriction (500–800ml/day): First-line for chronic SIADH — correction is gradual (goal 1–2 mmol/L/hour, maximum 8–10 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome).
  • Demeclocycline: Tetracycline antibiotic that causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus — used for resistant chronic SIADH.
  • Hypertonic saline (1.8% or 3% NaCl): For severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (Na <120 with seizures, severe confusion, or coma). Give 100–150ml 3% NaCl over 20 minutes, repeat ×2 if needed. Aim to raise sodium 4–6 mmol/L rapidly to control symptoms, then slow correction. Use in ICU setting.
  • Tolvaptan: V2 receptor antagonist — blocks ADH action in collecting duct. First licensed vaptane in UK for SIADH and autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD). Risks: rapid over-correction of sodium, hepatotoxicity.
  • Treat underlying cause.

Diabetes Insipidus and Polyuria

Diabetes insipidus (DI) causes polyuria (usually >3L/day) with dilute urine and polydipsia. Two types: central (ADH deficiency — pituitary/hypothalamic disease, head injury, neurosurgery, craniopharyngioma) and nephrogenic (ADH resistance — renal tubular unresponsiveness: lithium toxicity, hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia, genetic).

Water deprivation test: Fluid deprivation for up to 8 hours (stop if >3% body weight lost). Measure urine osmolality. Normal response: urine osmolality rises >800 mOsm/kg. Primary polydipsia (psychogenic): urine concentrates to >600 mOsm/kg. DI: urine remains dilute (<300 mOsm/kg). Then give desmopressin (DDAVP): central DI → urine osmolality increases >50%. Nephrogenic DI → minimal or no response.

Treatment: Central DI — desmopressin (intranasal or oral). Nephrogenic DI — stop causative drug; thiazide diuretics (paradoxical reduction in urine output by volume depletion); low-sodium diet; NSAIDs (reduce prostaglandin-mediated inhibition of ADH).

FAQ: Nephrology for the UKMLA

What are the KDIGO 2024 AKI staging criteria?

Stage 1: creatinine ×1.5–1.9 baseline within 7 days, or rise ≥26.5 µmol/L within 48 hours, or urine output <0.5 ml/kg/hr for 6–12 hours. Stage 2: creatinine ×2.0–2.9 or UO <0.5 ml/kg/hr for ≥12 hours. Stage 3: creatinine ×3.0 or ≥354 µmol/L with acute rise, or initiation of renal replacement therapy, or UO <0.3 ml/kg/hr for ≥24 hours or anuria ≥12 hours.

How do nephrotic and nephritic syndromes differ?

Nephrotic syndrome is characterised by massive proteinuria (>3.5g/24hr), hypoalbuminaemia, oedema, and hyperlipidaemia — without haematuria as a primary feature. Nephritic syndrome presents with haematuria (red cell casts), hypertension, oliguria, and mild proteinuria (<3.5g/24hr) — reflecting glomerular inflammation. Both can coexist in mixed syndromes such as membranoproliferative GN or lupus nephritis.

What is the gold-standard investigation for suspected renal calculi?

CT KUB (non-contrast CT of kidneys, ureters and bladder) is the gold standard. It identifies all stone types, determines stone size and location, and assesses degree of obstruction. Ultrasound is used when CT is contraindicated (pregnancy) or for follow-up. Plain KUB X-ray has poor sensitivity and misses radiolucent stones (uric acid, indinavir) — it should not be used as the primary diagnostic tool.

What is the emergency management of hyperkalaemia?

Step 1 — Cardiac protection: IV calcium gluconate 10ml 10% IV over 5 minutes (stabilises cardiac membrane, does not lower potassium). Step 2 — Shift potassium into cells: insulin-dextrose (10 units insulin + 50ml 50% dextrose), nebulised salbutamol 10–20mg, IV sodium bicarbonate if acidotic. Step 3 — Remove potassium: furosemide if kidneys working, patiromer or SZC orally for ongoing management, dialysis if renal replacement needed or refractory.

What distinguishes SIADH from CSWS?

Both cause hyponatraemia with high urine sodium and occur in CNS disease. The key distinguishing feature is volume status: SIADH patients are euvolaemic (no signs of dehydration — JVP normal, no postural hypotension, skin turgor normal). CSWS patients are hypovolaemic (low JVP, postural hypotension, negative fluid balance) because the kidneys inappropriately waste sodium. This distinction is clinically vital — treating SIADH with fluid restriction would dangerously worsen CSWS.

When should CKD patients be referred to nephrology?

NICE NG203 recommends nephrology referral for: eGFR <30 ml/min/1.73m² (all patients for transplant planning and CKD-related complications); progressive CKD with loss >5 ml/min/1.73m² per year; ACR >70 mg/mmol; ACR >30 with haematuria not explained by urological cause; suspected rare or inherited CKD (young age, family history, extrarenal features); refractory hypertension on ≥4 agents; AKI without clear cause or not recovering as expected.

Conclusion

Nephrology in the UKMLA AKT tests clinical reasoning at its most precise — applying staging criteria to diagnose AKI, interpreting urine biochemistry to classify electrolyte disorders, and selecting guideline-concordant management for complex conditions. A solid grasp of fluid and electrolyte physiology is the foundation upon which all nephrology knowledge is built.

The most important recent developments are the incorporation of SGLT-2 inhibitors for CKD beyond diabetes, the KDIGO 2024 AKI criteria updates, and the UKKA 2023 hyperkalaemia management pathway. These are all high-yield for the AKT given their recency and clinical relevance.

Integrate this nephrology knowledge with your broader revision using our UKMLA preparation strategies and check our results and scoring guide to understand how to maximise your AKT performance.
