post_title: Haematology in the UKMLA: Anaemia, Clotting, and Blood Cancers
post_excerpt: Blood disorders are tested across both UKMLA components. Here is a targeted guide to anaemia workup, clotting disorders, and the haematological malignancies you need to know.
seo_title: Haematology in the UKMLA: Anaemia, Clotting, and Blood Cancer
seo_description: Haematological presentations are high-yield in the UKMLA AKT. Our guide covers anaemia workup, DVT/PE, coagulation disorders, leukaemia, and lymphoma.
html_content:
Haematology for the UKMLA AKT: A Complete Revision Guide

Haematology spans from common anaemias encountered in primary care to rare and life-threatening blood dyscrasias requiring specialist management. The UKMLA AKT tests haematology extensively — expect questions on diagnosis, investigation, and management of anaemias, leukaemias, lymphomas, coagulation disorders, and transfusion medicine. This guide provides complete coverage of the haematology syllabus with an emphasis on clinical reasoning and guideline-based management.

Begin your UKMLA revision with our UKMLA overview, syllabus breakdown, and AKT exam format.

Anaemia: Classification and Approach

Anaemia is defined as haemoglobin below the lower limit of normal: <130 g/L in adult males, <120 g/L in adult non-pregnant females, <110 g/L in pregnancy. The most useful initial classification is by MCV (mean corpuscular volume).

MCVCategoryCommon CausesKey Tests
Low (<80 fl)MicrocyticIron deficiency (most common), thalassaemia, anaemia of chronic disease (usually normocytic but can be microcytic), sideroblastic anaemia, lead poisoningFerritin, serum iron/TIBC, Hb electrophoresis, blood film
Normal (80–100 fl)NormocyticAnaemia of chronic disease, acute blood loss, haemolytic anaemia, aplastic anaemia, bone marrow infiltration, hypothyroidism, renal failureReticulocyte count, DAT, LDH, haptoglobin, bone marrow biopsy
High (>100 fl)MacrocyticB12/folate deficiency, alcohol, liver disease, hypothyroidism, drugs (methotrexate, hydroxyurea, azathioprine, zidovudine), haemolysis, myelodysplasiaB12, folate, TFTs, LFTs, reticulocytes, blood film (oval macrocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils in B12/folate)

Iron Deficiency Anaemia

Iron deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide and the most common cause of anaemia in primary care. Symptoms: fatigue, dyspnoea, palpitations, pale skin, angular cheilitis, koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails), atrophic glossitis, Plummer-Vinson syndrome (iron deficiency + oesophageal web + dysphagia).

Investigation

Serum ferritin is the single most useful test for diagnosing iron deficiency — levels <30 µg/L are diagnostic (in the absence of inflammation, which raises ferritin as an acute phase reactant). In inflammatory states, transferrin saturation <20% and serum iron <10 µmol/L with raised TIBC support iron deficiency. Blood film: microcytic hypochromic red cells, pencil cells, anisocytosis, poikilocytosis.

Investigation for Underlying Cause

The key clinical principle for the UKMLA: iron deficiency in any adult without an obvious cause (e.g., heavy menstruation, inadequate dietary intake) requires investigation for gastrointestinal blood loss. Upper and lower GI endoscopy should be arranged. NICE NG12 (suspected cancer recognition): refer urgently (2-week wait) for upper GI endoscopy if iron deficiency anaemia with no other obvious cause in any man, or postmenopausal woman.

Treatment: oral ferrous sulfate 200mg three times daily (contains 65mg elemental iron per tablet). Take on empty stomach for best absorption. Vitamin C enhances absorption. GI side effects (constipation, nausea) common — take with food to reduce, or switch to ferrous fumarate/gluconate. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose, iron sucrose) for intolerance, malabsorption, or need for rapid repletion. Haemoglobin should rise ~10 g/L per 1–2 weeks. Continue iron for 3 months after Hb normalises to replenish stores.

Vitamin B12 and Folate Deficiency

Both B12 and folate are required for DNA synthesis — deficiency causes megaloblastic anaemia (large, hypersegmented neutrophils; megaloblasts in bone marrow). Clinically indistinguishable haematologically, but B12 deficiency causes neurological complications that folate does not.

B12 Deficiency

Causes: Pernicious anaemia (autoimmune gastric atrophy — antibodies to intrinsic factor and parietal cells; associated with other autoimmune conditions, slightly increased risk of gastric carcinoma), gastrectomy, terminal ileum resection (B12 absorbed in terminal ileum), strict vegan diet (B12 only in animal products), malabsorption (coeliac, Crohn's), metformin (reduces B12 absorption — check B12 annually), proton pump inhibitors (reduce acid required for B12 release from food).

Neurological features of B12 deficiency: Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord — demyelination of dorsal columns (loss of proprioception, vibration sense) and corticospinal tracts (UMN signs: spasticity, hyperreflexia, upgoing plantars). Also: peripheral neuropathy (glove-and-stocking), optic atrophy, dementia. Critical: these neurological features can occur without anaemia and can be irreversible if treatment delayed. Do NOT give folate alone in B12 deficiency (can precipitate or worsen neurological damage).

Treatment: IM hydroxocobalamin 1mg on alternate days for 2 weeks (total 6 doses), then maintenance every 3 months for life (if cause is pernicious anaemia or other irreversible cause). Oral cyanocobalamin or hydroxocobalamin 1000 µg daily if cause is dietary or malabsorption managed.

Folate Deficiency

Causes: Poor diet (elderly, alcoholics, eating disorder), malabsorption (coeliac), increased requirements (haemolysis, pregnancy, psoriasis, dialysis), drugs (methotrexate, phenytoin, trimethoprim — all inhibit dihydrofolate reductase). Folic acid 5mg daily for 4 months. In pregnancy: 400 µg folic acid pre-conception until 12 weeks (5mg if high risk: previous neural tube defect, diabetes, BMI >30, anti-epileptic drugs).

Haemolytic Anaemia

Premature red cell destruction. Evidence of haemolysis: elevated LDH, elevated unconjugated bilirubin, reduced haptoglobin (binds free haemoglobin, consumed in intravascular haemolysis), elevated reticulocyte count (regenerative response), urobilinogen in urine. Intravascular haemolysis (within blood vessels): haemoglobinuria (dark urine), haemoglobinaemia, haemosiderinuria. Extravascular (spleen/liver phagocytosis): jaundice.

Inherited Causes

Sickle Cell Disease — NICE CG143: Autosomal recessive — HbSS. Substitution of glutamic acid for valine at position 6 of beta-globin chain. Under deoxygenation, sickling → vascular occlusion → ischaemia/infarction. Presentations: painful crises (acute vaso-occlusive crisis — precipitated by cold, dehydration, infection, hypoxia), acute chest syndrome (fever + new pulmonary infiltrate on CXR + chest pain/respiratory symptoms — life-threatening), stroke (cerebral infarction), splenic sequestration, aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19 infection → transient red cell aplasia → precipitous drop in Hb), avascular necrosis, chronic organ damage (renal, retinal).

Management: All patients — penicillin prophylaxis (encapsulated organism risk due to functional hyposplenism), pneumococcal/meningococcal/Hib vaccines, folic acid 5mg daily. Hydroxycarbamide (hydroxyurea): increases HbF production, reduces sickling frequency and severity — NICE recommends for frequent crises. Transcranial Doppler USS annually in children to detect cerebral vasculopathy (elevated velocity >200 cm/sec → prophylactic exchange transfusion). Exchange transfusion for acute chest syndrome, stroke, pre-operatively.

Thalassaemia: Reduced or absent globin chain synthesis. Alpha thalassaemia (HBA1/HBA2 gene deletion) — from silent carrier (one gene) to haemoglobin Bart's hydrops fetalis (all four genes — fatal in utero). Beta thalassaemia (HBB gene mutation) — trait (minor): one mutation, mild microcytic anaemia, normal life expectancy; intermedia: moderate; major (two mutations): severe transfusion-dependent anaemia from 6 months (when HbF replaced by HbA). Management of thalassaemia major: regular blood transfusions every 3–4 weeks, iron chelation (desferrioxamine SC infusion or oral deferasirox) to prevent iron overload (causes cardiomyopathy, liver cirrhosis, endocrine failure). Bone marrow transplant curative in selected cases. Gene therapy approaching availability.

G6PD Deficiency: X-linked recessive (predominantly male, common in Mediterranean, Middle East, Africa — malaria-endemic regions — heterozygous female protective against malaria). Deficiency of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase → reduced NADPH → oxidative haemolysis on exposure to triggers. Heinz bodies (denatured Hb) on blood film. Triggers: infections, fava beans, oxidising drugs (primaquine, dapsone, rasburicase, high-dose aspirin, nitrofurantoin). Avoid triggers; supportive care during haemolytic episodes.

Hereditary Spherocytosis: Autosomal dominant (mostly) spectrin/ankyrin gene mutations → spherical, fragile red cells → splenic destruction. Spherocytes on blood film. Negative DAT (distinguishes from AIHA). Osmotic fragility increased. Splenectomy effective but delayed until after 5–6 years (infection risk — require vaccinations beforehand and lifelong penicillin).

Acquired Haemolytic Anaemia

Autoimmune Haemolytic Anaemia (AIHA): Direct antiglobulin test (DAT/Coombs test) positive — antibody on red cell surface. Warm AIHA (70%): IgG antibodies, complement activation, extravascular haemolysis. Causes: idiopathic, SLE, CLL, methyldopa. Treatment: prednisolone, rituximab, splenectomy. Cold AIHA: IgM antibodies, cold agglutinins, complement-mediated. Causes: Mycoplasma pneumoniae, EBV, lymphoma. Treatment: avoid cold, rituximab, chlorambucil.

Leukaemias

TypeAgeKey FeaturesKey FindingTreatment
AMLAdults (median 68)Fatigue, infection, bleeding; >20% blasts in marrow; Auer rods in blastsVarious cytogenetic abnormalities; APML (M3): t(15;17) → PML-RARA, DIC, ATRA responsiveIntensive chemotherapy (3+7: daunorubicin + cytarabine); ATRA + arsenic trioxide for APML
ALLChildren (peak 2–5 years), adultsCommonest childhood cancer; lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, CNS involvement; >20% blastsPhiladelphia chromosome (BCR-ABL) in 25% adults (poor prognosis); hyperdiploidy in children (good prognosis)Multi-agent chemotherapy + CNS prophylaxis; TKI (imatinib) if Ph+; stem cell transplant in high-risk
CMLMiddle-aged adultsMassive splenomegaly, weight loss, sweats; Hb low, WBC very high (often >100), low LAP scoret(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome — BCR-ABL fusion gene (constitutively active tyrosine kinase)Imatinib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor) — first-line; dasatinib/nilotinib if intolerant/resistant; stem cell transplant
CLLElderly (median 70)Often incidental finding; lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly; Richter transformation (to DLBCL); autoimmune cytopenias (AIHA, ITP)Lymphocyte count >5×10⁹/L; smudge cells on blood film; CD5+, CD23+, CD19+Watch and wait for early disease; FCR (fludarabine, cyclophosphamide, rituximab); ibrutinib (BTK inhibitor); venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor)

Lymphoma

Hodgkin Lymphoma

Bimodal age distribution (15–35 and over 55). Associated with EBV infection. Classical Hodgkin lymphoma: Reed-Sternberg cells (large binucleate cells with owl-eye nucleoli) in a background of inflammatory cells. B symptoms: unexplained fever (>38°C), night sweats, unintentional weight loss >10% in 6 months. Staging: Ann Arbor system (I–IV based on lymph node regions involved, with A/B suffix for absence/presence of B symptoms; E for extranodal extension). Treatment: ABVD chemotherapy (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) ± radiotherapy. Excellent prognosis — 80–90% curable.

Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma

Diverse group. Follicular lymphoma: indolent B-cell NHL; nodal, bone marrow involvement common; translocation t(14;18) — BCL-2 overexpression; responds to treatment but incurable with standard therapy; watch-and-wait for low tumour burden; rituximab ± bendamustine. Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL): most common aggressive NHL; treatment: R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, hydroxydaunorubicin/doxorubicin, oncovin/vincristine, prednisolone) — 60–70% curable. Burkitt lymphoma: highly aggressive, associated with EBV and malaria, t(8;14) — c-MYC overactivation, jaw tumour in endemic form; emergency treatment with intensive chemotherapy.

Multiple Myeloma

Malignant proliferation of plasma cells in bone marrow, producing monoclonal immunoglobulin (M-protein, paraprotein). Median age 70. CRAB criteria for end-organ damage requiring treatment:

  • Calcium elevated (>2.75 mmol/L)
  • Renal impairment (creatinine >177 µmol/L or eGFR <40)
  • Anaemia (Hb <100 g/L)
  • Bone lesions (osteolytic lesions, osteoporosis with fractures, or pathological fracture)

Additional features: fatigue, recurrent infections (hypogammaglobulinaemia — normal immunoglobulins suppressed), bone pain (diffuse, particularly spine/pelvis), hyperviscosity (visual disturbance, confusion, bleeding), Bence-Jones proteinuria (free light chains in urine — detected by Bence-Jones test or urine protein electrophoresis), rouleaux formation on blood film (red cells stacking like coins due to high paraprotein). Bone marrow biopsy: >10% clonal plasma cells. Radiological: whole-body MRI or PET-CT preferred over skeletal survey (more sensitive). Bisphosphonates for bone protection.

Treatment: proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib), immunomodulatory agents (lenalidomide, thalidomide), anti-CD38 monoclonal antibody (daratumumab), dexamethasone, autologous stem cell transplant in fit patients.

Thrombocytopaenia

ITP vs TTP vs HUS

FeatureITPTTPHUS
Full nameImmune ThrombocytopaeniaThrombotic Thrombocytopaenic PurpuraHaemolytic Uraemic Syndrome
PathophysiologyAnti-platelet antibodies (IgG) → platelet destruction by spleenADAMTS13 deficiency → vWF multimers → platelet microthrombiShiga toxin (E. coli O157:H7) → endothelial injury → platelet microthrombi
Clinical featuresPetechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding; no haemolysis; no renal failurePentad: MAHA, thrombocytopaenia, fever, renal impairment, neurological symptomsDiarrhoea prodrome (bloody), MAHA, thrombocytopaenia, acute renal failure (predominant)
AnaemiaNormal (no haemolysis)Microangiopathic haemolytic (schistocytes, elevated LDH, reduced haptoglobin)Microangiopathic haemolytic
Key investigationAnti-platelet antibodies (diagnosis of exclusion); bone marrow shows normal/increased megakaryocytesADAMTS13 activity <10% (send before plasma exchange)Stool culture for E. coli O157, verotoxin; ADAMTS13 normal
Platelet transfusionReserved for severe bleeding or pre-procedureDO NOT TRANSFUSE PLATELETS — worsens thrombosisDO NOT TRANSFUSE PLATELETS — worsens thrombosis
TreatmentPrednisolone, IV immunoglobulin (for acute/severe), rituximab, splenectomy, thrombopoietin receptor agonists (eltrombopag)Emergency plasma exchange (PEX) + rituximab + corticosteroidsSupportive (hydration, dialysis if needed); NO antibiotics (may release more toxin); complement inhibition (eculizumab) for atypical HUS

Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)

DIC is simultaneous activation of coagulation and fibrinolysis, leading to microvascular thrombosis and consumption of clotting factors/platelets with bleeding. Causes: sepsis (most common), obstetric emergencies (placental abruption, amniotic fluid embolism, PPH), malignancy (especially APML — most DIC-prone cancer), major trauma, massive transfusion.

Investigations: elevated PT and APTT, low fibrinogen (<1.5 g/L — most specific), elevated D-dimer, low platelets, schistocytes on blood film. Treatment: treat underlying cause (most important). Replace clotting factors (FFP), fibrinogen (cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5 g/L), platelets if <50 with active bleeding.

Venous Thromboembolism (VTE) — NICE NG158

DVT Diagnosis

Wells score for DVT: 1 point each for: active cancer, paralysis/immobilisation of lower limb, bedridden >3 days or major surgery in 12 weeks, localised tenderness along deep venous system, entire leg swollen, calf swollen ≥3cm more than other side, pitting oedema greater in symptomatic leg, collateral superficial veins, previously documented DVT. Subtract 2 points if alternative diagnosis at least as likely. Score ≥2 = DVT likely → proceed to USS; score <2 = unlikely → D-dimer; if D-dimer positive → USS.

PE Diagnosis

Wells score for PE: clinical signs of DVT (3 points), PE most likely diagnosis (3 points), heart rate >100 (1.5), immobilisation >3 days or surgery in last 4 weeks (1.5), previous DVT/PE (1.5), haemoptysis (1), malignancy (1). Score >4 = PE likely → CTPA. ≤4 = PE unlikely → D-dimer; if positive → CTPA. PERC rule (8 criteria) can be used to exclude PE without D-dimer in very low-risk patients.

V/Q scan: preferred in pregnancy (lower fetal radiation dose) and renal impairment, or when CTPA technically difficult. Echo in massive PE (right heart strain — RV dilation, interventricular septal flattening, TR).

Anticoagulation

DOACs (direct oral anticoagulants) are first-line for most DVT and PE: apixaban or rivaroxaban (dose-intensive regimen in first 7–21 days then maintenance). Dabigatran and edoxaban used after initial parenteral treatment. Duration: 3 months for provoked VTE; extended/indefinite for unprovoked (consider patient preference, bleeding risk — HAS-BLED score); indefinite if malignancy (LMWH or DOAC).

HIT (Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopaenia)

Type 1 HIT: non-immune, self-limiting, mild thrombocytopaenia within 48 hours, not clinically significant. Type 2 HIT: immune-mediated — IgG antibodies against heparin-PF4 complex → platelet activation → paradoxical thrombosis. Suspect if platelets fall >50% from baseline 5–14 days after heparin (or within 24 hours if recent heparin exposure). 4T score assesses probability. Diagnosis: ELISA for anti-PF4 antibodies, functional assay (serotonin release assay — gold standard). Management: STOP heparin immediately (all formulations including flushes). Start alternative anticoagulation (argatroban IV or fondaparinux SC or bivalirudin). Do NOT transfuse platelets. Do NOT switch to warfarin until platelet count >150 (warfarin alone causes skin necrosis in HIT).

Blood Transfusion

ABO compatibility is essential — ABO-incompatible transfusion causes fatal acute haemolytic reaction (anti-A or anti-B IgM → complement activation → intravascular haemolysis → renal failure, DIC, death). Group O negative is universal donor.

Transfusion Reactions

ReactionOnsetFeaturesManagement
Acute haemolytic (ABO incompatibility)MinutesFever, rigors, loin pain, haemoglobinuria, hypotension, renal failure, DICStop transfusion immediately; IV fluids; check identity and blood product; SHOT report
Febrile non-haemolyticDuring/shortly afterFever (>1°C rise), chills; due to cytokines in stored blood or anti-HLA antibodiesParacetamol; slow or pause transfusion; exclude haemolytic reaction
Allergic/anaphylaxisMinutesUrticaria, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis; IgE-mediated, often to plasma proteinsStop transfusion; chlorphenamine; adrenaline if anaphylaxis; SHOT report
TACO (Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload)During/up to 6 hrs afterPulmonary oedema, hypertension, raised BNP; occurs in elderly, cardiac patientsStop/slow transfusion; diuretics (furosemide); oxygen; position upright
TRALI (Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury)Within 6 hoursARDS picture — non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema; hypotension (vs hypertension in TACO); fever; anti-HLA/anti-neutrophil antibodies in donor bloodStop transfusion; supportive — oxygen, ITU if needed; SHOT report; inform blood bank

Massive transfusion protocol: 1:1:1 ratio of packed red cells : fresh frozen plasma : platelets. Targets: platelets >50, fibrinogen >1.5, PT/APTT <1.5× normal. Tranexamic acid within 3 hours of major haemorrhage (CRASH-2 trial).

SHOT (Serious Hazards of Transfusion) reporting: UK haemovigilance scheme for adverse events. All serious adverse events must be reported.

FAQ: Haematology for the UKMLA

What is the key distinction between TTP and HUS?

TTP is caused by ADAMTS13 deficiency (acquired or congenital), while HUS is caused by Shiga toxin from E. coli O157:H7 (typical HUS). TTP typically presents with prominent neurological features and less renal impairment; HUS has more severe renal failure and follows a diarrhoeal prodrome. In both conditions, platelet transfusion is contraindicated as it can worsen microvascular thrombosis. TTP is treated with emergency plasma exchange; typical HUS is managed supportively.

What is the Philadelphia chromosome and why is it important?

The Philadelphia chromosome is a translocation t(9;22) creating the BCR-ABL fusion gene, which encodes a constitutively active tyrosine kinase driving uncontrolled cell proliferation. It is found in virtually all CML patients (defining the disease) and in 25% of adult ALL (poor prognostic factor). Imatinib (Gleevec), the first targeted TKI, revolutionised CML treatment, converting a disease with 5-year survival of ~30% to >90%.

Why must B12 deficiency not be treated with folate alone?

Giving folic acid alone to a patient with B12 deficiency corrects the haematological abnormality but does not correct — and may actually unmask or worsen — the neurological damage (subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord). The pathophysiology involves methionine synthase requiring both B12 and methyltetrahydrofolate; folate supplementation bypasses the B12 block metabolically but cannot enter the neurological pathway that B12 regulates. Always treat both if in doubt.

When should you suspect HIT and what is the initial management?

Suspect HIT Type 2 if platelet count falls >50% from baseline 5–14 days after starting any heparin product (LMWH included), or if new thrombosis develops despite anticoagulation. Use the 4T score (thrombocytopaenia magnitude, timing, thrombosis or other sequelae, alternative causes) to assess probability. Management: stop heparin immediately (all sources), start a non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban, bivalirudin, fondaparinux), avoid platelet transfusion, avoid warfarin until platelet count recovers, send HIT antibody tests.

What are the B symptoms in lymphoma and why are they important?

B symptoms in the Ann Arbor staging of lymphoma are: unexplained fever above 38°C, drenching night sweats, and unexplained weight loss of more than 10% of body weight in the preceding 6 months. The presence of B symptoms (designated by the suffix "B" in staging) indicates systemic disease activity and generally confers a worse prognosis, potentially influencing treatment intensity. Their absence is designated "A."

What is TACO and how does it differ from TRALI?

TACO (Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload) is pulmonary oedema from fluid overload during transfusion — features include hypertension, raised JVP, raised BNP, bilateral crackles, hypoxia. Most at risk: elderly patients and those with pre-existing cardiac/renal disease. TRALI (Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury) is a non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema caused by donor anti-HLA or anti-neutrophil antibodies activating recipient neutrophils in the pulmonary vasculature. TRALI is associated with hypotension (not hypertension), normal filling pressures, and usually occurs within 6 hours. TRALI is a medical emergency requiring ITU-level respiratory support; treatment is supportive.

Conclusion

Haematology in the UKMLA rewards systematic thinking — starting with MCV to classify anaemia, applying the 4T score to assess HIT probability, using CRAB criteria to recognise myeloma, or knowing when NOT to transfuse platelets in TTP and HUS. These clinical rules are tested repeatedly in SBA format.

Master the high-yield topics: iron deficiency anaemia investigation and management, sickle cell disease crises, leukaemia classification, TTP versus HUS distinction, and transfusion reactions. These appear across clinical scenarios from emergency medicine to oncology to primary care in the UKMLA AKT.

For further preparation strategies see our UKMLA preparation guide and our results and scoring page.

post_title: Infection and Microbiology in the UKMLA: Antibiotic Stewardship and More
post_excerpt: Infectious diseases are a major AKT topic. This guide covers common infections, empiric antibiotic choices, and the broader principles of antimicrobial stewardship.
seo_title: Infection and Microbiology in the UKMLA: Antibiotics and Stewardship
seo_description: Infection is a high-yield UKMLA domain. Our revision guide covers empiric antibiotics, sepsis, common organisms, resistance, and antimicrobial stewardship principles.
html_content:
Infection, Microbiology and Antimicrobials for the UKMLA: A Complete Revision Guide

Infection and microbiology permeate every specialty. The UKMLA AKT expects candidates to recognise serious infections early, apply antimicrobial stewardship principles, use appropriate investigations, and select guideline-concordant treatments. This guide covers the full infection syllabus including sepsis management, common bacterial infections, antimicrobial choices, sexually transmitted infections, tuberculosis, HIV, and the major global infections a UK doctor must recognise.

Familiarise yourself with the broader exam context at our UKMLA overview, syllabus, and AKT format pages before beginning topic-specific revision.

Sepsis — NICE NG51 (2016) and NICE NG253 (2024 Update)

Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. It is responsible for over 44,000 deaths per year in the UK. The Sepsis-3 definition (2016) defines sepsis as suspected or confirmed infection with acute organ dysfunction, measured by a SOFA (Sequential Organ Failure Assessment) score increase of ≥2 points.

Recognition Tools

qSOFA (quick SOFA): Bedside screening tool — score 1 point each for: respiratory rate ≥22 breaths/min, altered mentation (GCS <15 or any acute confusion), systolic BP ≤100 mmHg. Score ≥2 suggests high risk of sepsis — escalate immediately. Suitable for non-ICU settings. Note: qSOFA is a screening tool, not diagnostic — it should prompt full assessment, not be used in isolation.

Septic shock (Sepsis-3 definition): Sepsis with vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg AND lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. Hospital mortality >40%.

Sepsis 6 — The "Hour-1 Bundle"

The NICE NG253 (2024) update and the UK Sepsis Trust maintain the Sepsis 6 bundle — six interventions within 1 hour of recognition, divided as "take 3, give 3":

Take 3 (investigate):

  1. Blood cultures (before antibiotics where possible — minimum 2 sets peripheral, plus additional cultures if indwelling lines)
  2. Serum lactate (venous or arterial)
  3. Urinalysis (urgent — start MSU culture if indicated)

Give 3 (treat):

  1. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (within 1 hour of recognition)
  2. IV fluid bolus 500ml 0.9% sodium chloride or Hartmann's over 15 minutes if systolic BP <90 or evidence of hypoperfusion — reassess response, further fluid guided by clinical response. NICE NG253 emphasises avoiding fluid overload — reassess after each bolus.
  3. Supplemental oxygen — target SpO₂ ≥94% (or 88–92% if at risk of hypercapnia)

Antimicrobial stewardship in sepsis: NICE 2024 emphasises that antibiotics should be reviewed at 24–48 hours with microbiology guidance and de-escalated or stopped as soon as appropriate. The mantra is "start smart, then focus." Duration should be the minimum effective course. Blood cultures must be taken before antibiotics but should not delay antibiotic administration by more than 45 minutes.

Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)

CAP is one of the most common causes of emergency hospital admission in the UK. Caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae (most common, ~40%), Haemophilus influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis, atypical organisms (Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Legionella pneumophila, Chlamydophila pneumoniae — no cell wall, not covered by beta-lactams), viruses.

Severity Assessment — CURB-65

Score 1 point each for: Confusion (new, AMTS ≤8), Urea >7 mmol/L, Respiratory rate ≥30 breaths/min, Blood pressure (systolic <90 or diastolic ≤60 mmHg), age ≥65.

  • Score 0–1: Low severity, community treatment appropriate
  • Score 2: Moderate severity, consider hospital admission
  • Score 3–5: High severity, hospital admission required; score ≥4 consider ICU

Antibiotic Choice — NICE NG138

  • Low severity (CURB-65 0–1): Amoxicillin 500mg–1g three times daily for 5 days. If allergy: doxycycline 200mg loading then 100mg daily, or clarithromycin 500mg twice daily.
  • Moderate severity (CURB-65 2): Amoxicillin 500mg–1g TDS + clarithromycin 500mg BD (to cover atypicals), or doxycycline 200mg/100mg BD as monotherapy. Oral if able to swallow.
  • High severity (CURB-65 3–5): IV co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS + IV clarithromycin 500mg BD. If penicillin allergy: IV levofloxacin 500mg BD or IV co-trimoxazole. Legionella and pneumococcal urinary antigens.

Chest X-ray — shadowing consistent with pneumonia (consolidation, air bronchograms). Repeat CXR at 4–6 weeks for those aged >50 or smokers (exclude underlying lung malignancy).

Urinary Tract Infections

UTIs are the most common bacterial infection in adults. Escherichia coli causes 70–80% of uncomplicated UTIs.

Treatment Choices

  • Uncomplicated lower UTI (women): Nitrofurantoin 100mg modified release BD for 3 days (avoid if eGFR <30 — risk of drug accumulation and pulmonary toxicity), or trimethoprim 200mg BD for 3 days (avoid if high resistance rates or pregnant in first trimester — antifolate). Pivmecillinam as alternative.
  • Uncomplicated lower UTI (men): Treat for 7 days. Trimethoprim or nitrofurantoin. If recurrent, consider prostate pathology.
  • Uncomplicated upper UTI (pyelonephritis): Cefalexin 500mg QDS or co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS for 7–14 days. Hospitalise if severe: IV cefuroxime or co-amoxiclav. If ESBL-producing organism: ertapenem or meropenem.
  • Catheter-associated UTI (CAUTI): Do NOT treat asymptomatic bacteriuria (common in catheterised patients — not clinically significant unless immunocompromised, pregnant, or pre-urological procedure). Treat only if symptomatic (fever, rigors, loin pain, new confusion, worsening renal function). Change catheter if been in place >7 days. Antibiotic choice guided by local sensitivities.

Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

Cellulitis

Infection of dermis and subcutaneous tissue, usually caused by Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) or Staphylococcus aureus. Classifies as non-purulent (streptococcal, most common) or purulent (more likely S. aureus). Treatment: flucloxacillin 500mg–1g QDS for 5–7 days (covers both organisms). Oral for mild/moderate; IV flucloxacillin for severe (Eron class III–IV), systemic features, or failure of oral treatment. Add MRSA cover (vancomycin, daptomycin) if: purulent, failed prior antibiotic treatment, recent hospitalisation, or known MRSA colonisation. Limb elevation and mark extent of erythema to monitor progress.

Necrotising Fasciitis

Rapidly spreading life-threatening infection of deep fascia and subcutaneous tissue. Two types: Type 1 (polymicrobial — mixed aerobic/anaerobic, in diabetics, elderly, immunocompromised, post-abdominal surgery) and Type 2 (mononicrobial — Group A Streptococcus — commonest in previously healthy individuals). Features suggesting NF: pain disproportionate to appearance, skin blistering, crepitus (gas in tissue), dark/purple discolouration, numbness over erythema (anaesthesia — nerve necrosis), rapid spread. LRINEC score (Laboratory Risk Indicator for Necrotising Fasciitis) uses CRP, WBC, Hb, sodium, glucose, creatinine. Diagnosis confirmed intraoperatively — friable grey necrotic fascia, failure of normal tissue planes to dissect, dishwater fluid.

Management: Surgical emergency — immediate and aggressive surgical debridement is the only life-saving treatment. Delay increases mortality significantly. Simultaneously: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin + IV vancomycin for MRSA risk), ITU admission, repeat surgery every 24–48 hours until clean margins achieved. Mortality 25–35% even with optimal treatment.

Clostridioides difficile (C. diff) — NICE NG199 (2021)

C. difficile is the most common cause of healthcare-associated diarrhoea in the UK. Spore-forming gram-positive anaerobic rod. Produces toxins A and B which damage the colonic mucosa causing pseudomembranous colitis. Classic presentation: profuse watery diarrhoea ≥3 loose stools/day after antibiotic use.

Risk Factors

  • Recent broad-spectrum antibiotic use (highest risk: clindamycin, cephalosporins, quinolones, co-amoxiclav)
  • PPIs (alter gut pH and microbiome)
  • Age >65
  • Hospitalisation
  • Immunocompromise
  • Recent gastrointestinal surgery

Diagnosis

Stool toxin PCR (glutamate dehydrogenase antigen + toxin immunoassay ± NAAT for clarification). Do NOT test formed stool. Do NOT test during or immediately after treatment (persistence of positive test does not indicate ongoing infection).

Treatment — NICE NG199

  • First episode: Oral vancomycin 125mg QDS for 10 days. (Metronidazole is no longer first-line.) Stop precipitating antibiotics where possible.
  • First recurrence: Fidaxomicin 200mg BD for 10 days (preferred over repeat vancomycin — reduces recurrence rate). Bezlotoxumab (monoclonal antibody against toxin B) can be given alongside antibiotics to reduce recurrence risk in high-risk patients.
  • Multiple recurrences or severe refractory disease: Faecal microbiota transplant (FMT) — recommended by NICE, highly effective (cure rate ~80–90%).
  • Severe C. diff (WBC >15, creatinine rising, colitis features): Oral vancomycin 500mg QDS ± IV metronidazole 500mg TDS. Surgical input if megacolon, perforation, or failure to improve.

Infection Control

Spores are NOT killed by alcohol gel — soap and water handwashing is essential. Gloves and aprons for contact. Dedicated toilet. Thorough environmental cleaning with chlorine-based disinfectants. Avoid antidiarrhoeal drugs (mask severity, may worsen toxic megacolon).

MRSA (Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus)

MRSA carries the mecA gene encoding a modified penicillin-binding protein (PBP2a) that is not inhibited by beta-lactam antibiotics. Spread by contact — hand hygiene is the primary prevention measure.

Screening: NHS policy — all elective admissions screened (nose, throat, groin swabs). Emergency admissions screened at admission. Positive carriers decolonised before elective surgery.

Decolonisation: Mupirocin 2% nasal ointment TDS for 5 days, chlorhexidine 4% body wash daily for 5 days, chlorhexidine mouthwash.

Treatment of MRSA infection: IV vancomycin (monitoring trough levels/AUC-guided dosing, nephrotoxic) or IV teicoplanin (glycopeptides). Daptomycin (lipopeptide) — good for skin/soft tissue and bacteraemia (not effective for pneumonia — inactivated by pulmonary surfactant). Linezolid (oxazolidinone) — oral bioavailability excellent, good for MRSA pneumonia and bone/joint infections; risk of thrombocytopaenia, serotonin syndrome with SSRIs, optic neuropathy with prolonged use. Ceftaroline — fifth-generation cephalosporin active against MRSA.

Sexually Transmitted Infections — BASHH 2024/2025

STICausative OrganismClinical FeaturesDiagnosisTreatment (BASHH 2024/2025)
ChlamydiaChlamydia trachomatis (intracellular)Often asymptomatic; dysuria, discharge, pelvic pain, epididymo-orchitis; PID in womenNAAT (urine, vulvovaginal swab, urethral swab)Doxycycline 100mg BD 7 days (preferred) or azithromycin 1g stat (lower efficacy, use in pregnancy)
GonorrhoeaNeisseria gonorrhoeaeUrethral discharge (yellow/green), dysuria; often asymptomatic in women; pharyngeal, rectal infection; disseminated GC: skin lesions, arthritisNAAT + culture (for sensitivity testing — increasing resistance)Ceftriaxone 1g IM stat (BASHH 2019 updated 2023 — dose increased from 500mg due to resistance)
SyphilisTreponema pallidum (spirochaete)Primary: painless chancre; secondary: rash (palms/soles), condylomata lata, lymphadenopathy; tertiary: gumma, CVS (aortic aneurysm), neurosyphilis; latent: asymptomaticTPHA/TPPA + VDRL (non-treponemal, titres correlate with activity)Benzathine penicillin G 2.4 MU IM (primary/secondary); IV procaine penicillin for neurosyphilis. Doxycycline if penicillin allergy.
Herpes SimplexHSV-1 (often oral), HSV-2 (often genital)Painful vesicles → ulcers on genitalia, perianal area; systemic features in primary infection; recurrent (HSV-2 more recurrent); aseptic meningitisPCR swab of lesionAciclovir 200mg 5× daily for 5 days (or 400mg TDS) for primary. Suppressive therapy (aciclovir 400mg BD) for frequent recurrences (>6/year)
HIVHIV-1 / HIV-2 (retrovirus)Primary HIV: glandular fever-like illness 2–6 weeks post-exposure; latent; AIDS (CD4 <200)4th-generation ELISA (antigen/antibody), confirmed by Western blotART (antiretroviral therapy) — all diagnosed patients regardless of CD4 count. PrEP for high-risk. PEP within 72 hours of exposure.

PrEP and PEP

PrEP (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis): Tenofovir/emtricitabine (Truvada or Descovy). Daily or event-based regimen. Highly effective (>99%) at preventing HIV acquisition when taken correctly. Available on NHS for eligible individuals at substantial HIV risk.

PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis): Must be started within 72 hours of exposure (earlier = more effective). Standard regimen: tenofovir/emtricitabine + raltegravir or dolutegravir, for 28 days. Available from sexual health clinics, A&E, GUM clinics. Efficacy declines rapidly — ineffective if >72 hours post-exposure.

Tuberculosis — NICE NG33 (2024)

TB remains the world's leading cause of death from a single infectious agent. In the UK, incidence is higher in urban areas and in migrants from high-burden countries.

Active TB — RIPE Regimen

Standard four-drug initial phase for 2 months followed by two-drug continuation phase for 4 months (total 6 months for pulmonary TB):

  • Rifampicin (RIF): Most potent anti-TB drug. Induces hepatic enzymes — many drug interactions (reduces efficacy of oral contraceptive pill, anticoagulants, antiretrovirals). Turns body fluids (urine, tears, sweat) orange-red. Hepatotoxic.
  • Isoniazid (INH): Bactericidal. Peripheral neuropathy (prevents by prescribing pyridoxine/vitamin B6 with it). Hepatotoxic. SIADH. Check for slow vs fast acetylator status.
  • Pyrazinamide (PZA): Active in acidic environment of macrophages. Hepatotoxic. Hyperuricaemia → gout flares. Arthralgia.
  • Ethambutol (EMB): Optic neuritis — colour vision loss, reduced visual acuity. Check baseline visual acuity and colour vision. Dose reduce in renal impairment.

Continuation phase (months 3–6): rifampicin + isoniazid. Total 6 months. Extend to 9 months for CNS TB. Monitor LFTs monthly for first 2 months.

Latent TB Infection (LTBI)

NICE NG33 recommends screening all new entrants from high-incidence countries. Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) is preferred in the UK (QuantiFERON-TB Gold or T-SPOT.TB). Mantoux test used in children under 5. Treatment: 3 months isoniazid + rifampicin, or 6 months isoniazid alone, or 3 months rifampicin alone. Screen before biologic therapy.

Contact Tracing

Identify close contacts of smear-positive (infectious) pulmonary TB. Public Health England (now UKHSA) responsibilities. Household contacts screened with IGRA and CXR.

BCG Vaccination

BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin) is offered in the UK to: all neonates in areas of high TB incidence (London boroughs), neonates whose parents/grandparents were born in high-incidence countries (>40/100,000), previously unvaccinated individuals under 35 who are new entrants from high-incidence countries, and healthcare workers. Not offered routinely to all UK children (unlike some countries).

Neutropenic Sepsis — NICE CG151

Defined as neutrophil count <0.5 ×10⁹/L (or <1.0 and expected to fall) with temperature >38°C or clinical signs of sepsis in a patient receiving anticancer treatment. Medical emergency — high mortality without rapid treatment.

  • Start IV piperacillin-tazobactam (Tazocin) within 1 hour of recognition. If penicillin allergic: meropenem or ceftazidime + gentamicin.
  • Blood cultures (2 sets peripheral + central line), FBC, U&E, LFTs, LDH, CRP, CXR.
  • Add MRSA cover (teicoplanin) if indwelling central catheter infection suspected.
  • Antifungal (posaconazole/fluconazole/micafungin) if fever persistent >4–5 days on antibiotics or known fungal colonisation.
  • G-CSF (granulocyte colony-stimulating factor — filgrastim) — consider if severe or prolonged neutropaenia. Reduces duration but not mortality in most standard-risk patients.

HIV — Antiretroviral Therapy and Opportunistic Infections

WHO Staging and ART

All patients diagnosed with HIV should be offered ART regardless of CD4 count (U=U — Undetectable = Untransmittable — preventing onward transmission). Standard first-line ART: 2 NRTIs + 1 INSTI (integrase strand transfer inhibitor). Preferred: tenofovir alafenamide (TAF)/emtricitabine + bictegravir (or dolutegravir). Efavirenz is no longer first-line (neuropsychiatric side effects).

CD4 Count Thresholds for Opportunistic Infection (OI) Prophylaxis

  • CD4 <200 cells/µL: Start PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia) prophylaxis — co-trimoxazole (trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole) one double-strength tablet daily. Alternative: dapsone + pyrimethamine, or nebulised pentamidine monthly.
  • CD4 <100 cells/µL: Toxoplasma prophylaxis — co-trimoxazole (same as PCP). MAC (Mycobacterium avium complex) prophylaxis — azithromycin weekly.
  • CD4 <50 cells/µL: CMV retinitis prophylaxis (valganciclovir) in high-risk. Cryptococcal meningitis prophylaxis in high-prevalence settings.

PCP Pneumonia

Subacute onset in HIV with CD4 <200: progressive dyspnoea on exertion, dry cough, low-grade fever, desaturation with exercise. CXR — bilateral perihilar infiltrates (or normal in early disease). LDH elevated. BAL with Gomori methenamine silver stain or immunofluorescence. High-resolution CT: ground-glass opacification. Treatment: high-dose co-trimoxazole 120mg/kg/day in divided doses for 21 days. Add prednisolone if PaO₂ <9.3 kPa (reduces inflammatory response as P. jirovecii dies).

Malaria

Must be considered in any febrile patient returning from a malaria-endemic area. ABCDE for returning traveller: A — Aware of risk, B — Bitten less (insect repellent, treated nets, clothing), C — Chemoprophylaxis, D — Diagnose promptly, E — Emergency treatment.

Plasmodium falciparum: most dangerous species, causes cerebral malaria, severe anaemia, acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), AKI, hypoglycaemia, coagulopathy. Definitive diagnosis: thick and thin blood films ×3 (need three negative films 12–24 hours apart to exclude). Rapid antigen test (RDT). PCR for species identification and resistance. Treatment of uncomplicated falciparum malaria: artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) — artemether/lumefantrine (Riamet) or artesunate/amodiaquine. Severe/complicated falciparum: IV artesunate (preferred over IV quinine).

Non-falciparum (vivax, ovale): chloroquine sensitive (mostly). P. vivax and P. ovale have liver hypnozoites causing relapse — primaquine after chloroquine (test G6PD before prescribing). P. malariae: chloroquine. P. knowlesi: chloroquine or ACT.

FAQ: Infection and Microbiology for the UKMLA

What are the Sepsis 6 interventions and within what timeframe?

The Sepsis 6 bundle should be completed within 1 hour of recognition. Take 3: blood cultures (2 sets), serum lactate, urinalysis. Give 3: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, IV fluid bolus 500ml crystalloid (if hypotensive or evidence of hypoperfusion), supplemental oxygen targeting SpO₂ ≥94%. NICE NG253 (2024) emphasises reassessment after each fluid bolus to avoid fluid overload, and de-escalation of antibiotics at 24–48 hours based on culture results.

What is the first-line treatment for C. difficile according to NICE NG199 2021?

Oral vancomycin 125mg four times daily for 10 days is first-line for a first episode of C. difficile infection — metronidazole is no longer recommended as first-line. For a first recurrence, fidaxomicin 200mg twice daily for 10 days is preferred as it significantly reduces the risk of further recurrence compared to repeat vancomycin. Faecal microbiota transplant (FMT) is recommended for multiple recurrences.

Why is alcohol gel ineffective against C. difficile?

Clostridioides difficile forms spores that are highly resistant to alcohol-based hand sanitisers. Alcohol gels denature proteins but cannot penetrate the spore coat. Physical removal with soap and water — mechanical action of scrubbing — is required to reduce spore transmission from hands. This is why soap and water handwashing (not gel) is mandatory for HCWs before and after contact with C. difficile patients or their environment.

What is the management of necrotising fasciitis?

Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency. Management requires immediate surgical debridement of all necrotic tissue — this is the primary life-saving intervention and must not be delayed for investigation or CT scanning if clinical diagnosis is clear. Simultaneously: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin ± vancomycin), ITU admission, and repeated surgical review every 24–48 hours until clean wound margins are achieved. Hyperbaric oxygen is used as adjunct in some centres but evidence is limited.

What is the RIPE regimen for TB and what are the main side effects of each drug?

RIPE: Rifampicin (hepatotoxicity, enzyme induction — reduces OCP and anticoagulant efficacy, orange body fluids), Isoniazid (peripheral neuropathy — prevent with pyridoxine, hepatotoxicity, SIADH), Pyrazinamide (hepatotoxicity, hyperuricaemia, arthralgia), Ethambutol (optic neuritis — monitor visual acuity and colour vision, dose reduce in renal impairment). Initial phase: all 4 drugs for 2 months. Continuation phase: rifampicin + isoniazid for 4 months (6 months total for pulmonary TB; 9 months for CNS TB).

When should PEP be started and what does it consist of?

PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis) for HIV must be started within 72 hours of exposure — the earlier the better, ideally within 24 hours. It consists of tenofovir/emtricitabine (2-in-1 tablet) plus raltegravir or dolutegravir, taken for 28 days. PEP is available from emergency departments, sexual health clinics, and GUM clinics. It is highly effective if started promptly but reduces in efficacy rapidly after 24 hours and is not effective after 72 hours.

Conclusion

Infection and microbiology in the UKMLA requires both breadth and clinical precision. The key skills are: recognising sepsis and initiating the Sepsis 6 bundle within 1 hour; selecting the correct antibiotic for the commonest infections (using current NICE guidance); knowing when NOT to treat (asymptomatic bacteriuria, C. diff during treatment); and understanding antimicrobial stewardship principles that underpin all prescribing decisions.

Pay particular attention to the recently updated NICE NG253 (2024) sepsis guidance, NICE NG199 (2021) C. difficile management, and BASHH 2024/2025 STI guidelines — these are high-yield given their recency and clinical relevance across all settings.

Build your broader revision strategy using our UKMLA preparation guide. Review how the AKT is scored to understand which topics carry the most marks.

post_title: Palliative Care and End-of-Life Decisions in the UKMLA
post_excerpt: Palliative care is explicitly assessed in the UKMLA. Here is what to know about symptom management, advance care planning, and the ethical and legal framework.
seo_title: Palliative Care in the UKMLA: Symptom Management and Ethics
seo_description: Palliative care is assessed in both the UKMLA AKT and CPSA. Learn symptom management, advance care planning, DNACPR decisions, and the legal framework.
html_content:
Palliative Care and End-of-Life Medicine for the UKMLA: A Complete Revision Guide

Palliative care is a specialty that every doctor must practice, regardless of their field. The UKMLA AKT tests this area not only through symptom management questions but through scenarios involving advance care planning, consent, capacity, and ethical decision-making at the end of life. Candidates who master this topic demonstrate not just medical knowledge but the clinical and ethical reasoning that defines good doctoring.

This guide covers the complete UKMLA palliative care syllabus — symptom control, syringe drivers, recognition of dying, advance care planning, organ donation, and communication skills. Begin by understanding the exam format at our AKT guide and our UKMLA overview.

Principles of Palliative Care

The WHO (2020) definition of palliative care describes it as "an approach that improves the quality of life of patients and their families facing the problems associated with life-threatening illness, through the prevention and relief of suffering by means of early identification and impeccable assessment and treatment of pain and other problems, physical, psychosocial and spiritual."

Key principles that distinguish palliative care from purely curative medicine:

  • Palliative care is applicable earlier in illness — it should be introduced alongside curative or life-prolonging treatment, not only when curative options are exhausted
  • It affirms life and regards dying as a normal process
  • It neither hastens nor postpones death
  • It addresses pain and other distressing symptoms
  • It integrates physical, psychological, social, and spiritual care
  • It supports both patient and family, including bereavement
  • The goal is the best possible quality of life for both patient and family

The concept of goals of care conversations is central to modern palliative medicine — establishing with the patient and family what matters most, what they understand about their condition, and aligning medical treatment to their values. These conversations should happen early, be revisited regularly, and be documented.

Pain Management in Palliative Care — NICE NG31

The WHO Analgesic Ladder

The WHO analgesic ladder (1986, revised principles still applied) provides a step-wise framework for cancer pain management:

  • Step 1 — Non-opioid: Paracetamol 1g four times daily ± NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac — use with PPI, caution in renal impairment, cardiac disease)
  • Step 2 — Weak opioid: Add codeine 30–60mg four times daily (prodrug converted to morphine by CYP2D6 — ultra-rapid metabolisers at risk of toxicity, poor metabolisers get no analgesia) or tramadol. Dihydrocodeine alternative.
  • Step 3 — Strong opioid: Replace weak opioid with strong opioid (morphine, oxycodone, hydromorphone, fentanyl, buprenorphine). Continue Step 1 analgesics if appropriate. Add adjuvants throughout.

The principle is to prescribe analgesia by mouth, by the clock, by the ladder — regular dosing prevents pain returning (do not wait until pain is severe to medicate).

Opioid Prescribing in Practice

Immediate-release (IR) morphine (Oramorph 10mg/5ml solution or Sevredol tablets): Onset 20–30 minutes, duration 4 hours. Used for initiation and dose titration. Starting dose for opioid-naive patient: 2.5–5mg every 4 hours (lower in elderly, renal impairment). Titrate dose by 25–33% every 24–48 hours based on pain control and breakthrough use.

Modified-release (MR) morphine (MST Continus, Zomorph): Twice daily dosing, onset 1–2 hours. Converts to MR once total daily IR dose established. Calculate total daily dose of IR morphine → prescribe half as MR BD.

Breakthrough analgesia: Always prescribe alongside regular opioids. Dose = 1/6 of the total daily oral morphine equivalent. For example: total daily MR morphine 60mg → breakthrough IR morphine 10mg PRN every 1–4 hours. If patient using ≥3 breakthrough doses per 24 hours, increase regular dose by approximately total breakthrough used in that 24 hours.

Opioid dose conversion (equianalgesic dosing): Key conversions for UKMLA:

DrugRouteConversion to Oral MorphineNotes
Oral codeine 240mg/dayOral≈ Morphine 24mg/day (codeine:morphine = 10:1)Divide by 10
Oral tramadol 300mg/dayOral≈ Morphine 30mg/day (5:1 ratio)Divide by 5 (approximate)
Oral oxycodoneOral×1.5 to get oral morphine equivalent (oxycodone more potent)Oxycodone 10mg ≈ morphine 15mg oral
SC morphineSC/IVOral morphine ÷ 2SC/IV dose is half oral dose (reduced first-pass)
Oral hydromorphoneOral×7.5 to get oral morphine equivalentHydromorphone 2mg ≈ morphine 15mg oral
Fentanyl patch (72-hour)TransdermalPatch 25 mcg/hr ≈ oral morphine 60–90mg/day12–17 hours to reach steady state; not for acute pain or opioid-naive

Opioid switching: Indicated for inadequate analgesia, intolerable side effects, or change in route. When switching, reduce calculated dose by 25–30% to account for incomplete cross-tolerance. Titrate and monitor closely.

Adjuvant Analgesics

  • Neuropathic pain (burning, shooting, lancinating, allodynia): Amitriptyline (10mg at night, titrate) or gabapentin (start 100–300mg three times daily, titrate) or pregabalin. Duloxetine (SNRI) — licensed for diabetic neuropathy and cancer pain. If one fails, try another class before combining.
  • Bone pain: NSAIDs (naproxen, diclofenac) ± dexamethasone (reduces oedema and inflammation). Bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) reduce pain from bone metastases and pathological fracture risk. Denosumab. Radiotherapy is highly effective for localised bone metastases (single fraction commonly used — equivalent efficacy to multiple fractions for pain).
  • Raised intracranial pressure (headache, nausea, vomiting from cerebral oedema/metastases): Dexamethasone 8–16mg daily (reduces vasogenic oedema). Omeprazole gastroprotection. Whole-brain radiotherapy or stereotactic radiosurgery for multiple/single metastases.
  • Visceral pain: Antispasmodics — hyoscine butylbromide (Buscopan) for bowel/bladder spasm. Octreotide for secretory diarrhoea and bowel obstruction symptoms.

Nausea and Vomiting in Palliative Care

Nausea is experienced by up to 70% of patients with advanced cancer. Effective antiemetic prescribing requires identifying the likely mechanism:

Mechanism/CauseExample CausesFirst-Line AntiemeticMechanism of Action
Chemical/metabolic (chemoreceptor trigger zone)Opioids, uraemia, hypercalcaemia, drugsHaloperidol 0.5–1.5mg BD SC/oralD2 antagonist at CTZ
Gastric stasis/dysmotilityOpioids, ascites, hepatomegaly, autonomous gastroparesisMetoclopramide 10mg TDS oral/SCD2 antagonist + 5-HT4 agonist → prokinetic
Raised intracranial pressure/vestibularBrain metastases, motion sicknessCyclizine 25–50mg TDS oral/SCH1/muscarinic antagonist
Bowel obstructionPeritoneal metastases, adhesionsCyclizine + haloperidol ± hyoscine butylbromide (to reduce secretions)Combined antiemetic and antisecretory
Chemotherapy-inducedCisplatin, carboplatin, cyclophosphamideOndansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) ± dexamethasone ± NK1 antagonist (aprepitant for highly emetogenic regimens)5-HT3 antagonism at gut and CTZ

Note: Metoclopramide is NOT suitable for complete bowel obstruction (contraindicated — prokinetic increases colic). Use cyclizine + haloperidol + hyoscine butylbromide in obstruction. Levomepromazine (methotrimeprazine) is a broad-spectrum antiemetic for refractory nausea — useful in syringe drivers.

Breathlessness

Dyspnoea is one of the most distressing symptoms in advanced illness. Causes include pleural effusion, lung metastases, pulmonary embolism, lymphangitis carcinomatosa, anaemia, COPD, cardiac failure, anxiety. The UKMLA tests symptom management, not the underlying aetiology alone.

Non-pharmacological measures: Fan directed at face (activates cold receptors on trigeminal nerve — reduces breathlessness perception significantly), cool room, open window, positioning (upright or supported), breathing control techniques (respiratory physiotherapy), occupational therapy (energy conservation, pacing).

Pharmacological management:

  • Low-dose opioids: Most evidence-based pharmacological treatment for dyspnoea. Oral or SC morphine (starting 2.5–5mg every 4 hours in opioid-naive) reduces the sensation of breathlessness without causing respiratory depression at appropriate palliative doses. Mechanism: reduces central response to hypercapnia, reduces anxiety.
  • Benzodiazepines: Lorazepam 0.5–1mg sublingual PRN for acute episodic breathlessness with anxiety. Midazolam SC for continuous subcutaneous infusion if distressing breathlessness at end of life. Not first-line — risk of respiratory depression if used without concurrent opioid; most effective as adjunct.
  • Oxygen: Indicated for hypoxic patients (SpO₂ <90%). Not routinely beneficial for normoxic patients with breathlessness — does not reduce dyspnoea sensation in non-hypoxic patients (Cochrane review). Can be burdensome and restricts mobility. Discuss realistic expectations with patient.

Secretions at End of Life (Death Rattle)

Noisy secretions from the upper airway and oropharynx due to inability to clear secretions in the unconscious/semiconscious patient. Present in up to 50% of dying patients. Distressing for families though usually not distressing for the patient (who is unconscious).

Management:

  • Repositioning (lateral or semi-prone) to allow drainage
  • Gentle mouth care (avoid deep suctioning — distressing and counterproductive)
  • Family education — explain that secretions cause noise but are unlikely to be distressing to patient
  • Antisecretory drugs: Hyoscine butylbromide (Buscopan) 20mg SC PRN or by syringe driver — does not cross blood-brain barrier (no sedating effect), dries secretions. Glycopyrronium 200 micrograms SC PRN — alternative, longer duration. Hyoscine hydrobromide (scopolamine) — crosses BBB (mild sedation — may be benefit at end of life but choose consciously).

Agitation at End of Life (Terminal Restlessness)

Restlessness, agitation, confusion, and distress are common in the final hours to days of life. Causes include retained urine/faeces, pain, breathlessness, cerebral irritation (uraemia, hypercalcaemia, encephalopathy), drugs, fear, and existential distress. Reversible causes should be sought and treated where appropriate.

Pharmacological management:

  • Midazolam: Benzodiazepine, first-line for terminal agitation. SC via syringe driver, typically 5–30mg/24 hours (start low, titrate). PRN dose 2.5–5mg SC.
  • Levomepromazine (methotrimeprazine): Broad-spectrum antipsychotic with antiemetic and sedating properties. 6.25–25mg SC PRN, or by syringe driver for refractory agitation. Hypotensive — caution.
  • Haloperidol: For agitation with confusion/hallucinations, 0.5–1.5mg SC PRN. Less sedating than levomepromazine.
  • Consider ketamine (low-dose) for complex refractory pain with agitation in specialist palliative care setting.

Continuous Subcutaneous Infusion (CSCI) — Syringe Driver

When oral medications can no longer be taken (dysphagia, coma, intractable vomiting), a syringe driver (CSCI) delivers drugs continuously subcutaneously over 24 hours.

Setting Up a Syringe Driver

The McKinley T34 is the standard syringe driver in UK hospitals. Drugs are usually diluted in 0.9% sodium chloride or water for injection (check individual drug compatibilities — use water for injection for drugs that precipitate in saline, e.g., cyclizine).

Common drug combinations in syringe drivers:

DrugStandard CSCI RangeIndicationCompatibility Notes
Morphine sulphate10–200+ mg/24hrPain, dyspnoeaGenerally compatible with most palliative drugs
Midazolam5–60mg/24hrAgitation, anxiety, seizures, dyspnoeaCompatible with morphine and most others
Haloperidol1.5–5mg/24hrNausea, agitation, confusionCompatible with morphine in NaCl
Levomepromazine6.25–50mg/24hrRefractory nausea, agitationCompatible in small volumes; limit to 3 drugs max per syringe ideally
Hyoscine butylbromide20–120mg/24hrSecretions, bowel obstruction colicCompatible with morphine, midazolam; incompatible with ketorolac
Cyclizine75–150mg/24hrNausea, vomitingIncompatible with saline — use water for injection. Precipitates at higher morphine concentrations
Ketamine50–500mg/24hr (specialist use)Neuropathic/complex refractory painRequires specialist palliative care advice

CSKV (continuous subcutaneous cannula via venflon) is sometimes used as a port for intermittent SC administration rather than a continuous infusion.

Recognition of Dying — NICE NG142

NICE NG142 (Care of Dying Adults in the Last Days of Life, 2015) provides guidance on recognising dying and individualising care.

Clinical Signs of Dying

Recognition that a person may be in the last days or hours of life: clinical judgement based on — deteriorating despite disease-appropriate treatment, profound weakness, drowsiness, reduced oral intake, reduced urine output, mottling and cooling of peripheries, Cheyne-Stokes breathing, increased secretions. The multidisciplinary team should reach a consensus decision.

The Amber Care Bundle identifies patients in the last weeks to months of life where there is uncertainty about recovery, prompting proactive planning conversations.

Individualised End-of-Life Care Plan

NICE NG142 specifies that when a clinical decision is made that someone is dying:

  1. Communicate clearly with patient (if able to understand) and family/those important to them
  2. Develop an individualised care plan with the patient and family — document goals, preferences for place of death, wishes regarding resuscitation, spiritual needs, preferred name
  3. Review all medications — stop non-essential drugs (lipid-lowering agents, antihypertensives, preventive medications), continue essential symptom control drugs via syringe driver if needed
  4. Prescribe anticipatory medications PRN (see below)
  5. Assess needs four-hourly for: pain, breathlessness, nausea, secretions, agitation, hydration/nutrition (IV fluids if felt appropriate — decision with family, not routine)
  6. Do not impose eating/drinking but provide good mouth care
  7. Spiritual and psychological care for patient and family

Anticipatory Prescriptions

When a patient is recognised as dying, subcutaneous PRN prescriptions for common end-of-life symptoms should be written in advance so they can be administered promptly without a new prescription. Typical anticipatory prescriptions:

  • Pain/dyspnoea: SC morphine 2.5–5mg PRN hourly (or 1/6 total daily dose if already on opioids)
  • Agitation: SC midazolam 2.5–5mg PRN hourly
  • Nausea: SC haloperidol 1.5mg PRN 6-hourly OR SC levomepromazine 6.25mg PRN 6-hourly
  • Secretions: SC hyoscine butylbromide 20mg PRN 4-hourly

Advance Care Planning

Advance Decisions to Refuse Treatment (ADRT)

An ADRT (also called "advance directive" or "living will") is a legally binding document allowing a capacitated adult to refuse specified medical treatment in advance, to take effect if they subsequently lack capacity. Governed by the Mental Capacity Act 2005 (England and Wales).

Requirements for a valid ADRT refusing any treatment:

  • Adult aged ≥18 with capacity when made
  • Must specify the treatment being refused
  • Must specify the circumstances in which the refusal applies
  • Not made under undue influence

Additional requirements for an ADRT to refuse LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT (e.g., CPR, artificial ventilation, artificial hydration/nutrition):

  • Must be in writing
  • Must be signed by the person (or by another in their presence if physically unable)
  • Must be witnessed (one witness, who must also sign)
  • Must include the statement: "even if my life is at risk" (or equivalent wording clearly indicating life-sustaining treatment is meant)
  • An ADRT refusing life-sustaining treatment can be overridden by a subsequent LPA for Health and Welfare (if the LPA has authority to give or withhold consent to life-sustaining treatment)

ReSPECT Form (Recommended Summary Plan for Emergency Care and Treatment)

ReSPECT replaced the DNACPR form in most UK trusts. It is a patient-held document summarising personalised recommendations for emergency care. Unlike a pure DNACPR, ReSPECT captures broader goals: clinical interventions that would and would not be appropriate, CPR recommendation, place of care preferences, and what matters most to the patient. It is a recommendation, not a legally binding document (unlike an ADRT).

DNACPR (Do Not Attempt Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)

Key legal and ethical principles:

  • A DNACPR decision is a clinical recommendation — it does not require patient consent, but must involve discussion with the patient or proxy if the patient lacks capacity, unless this discussion would cause harm or is not practicable in an emergency
  • DNACPR cannot be placed without the knowledge of the patient unless there is a documented clinical reason why the conversation would cause harm
  • A DNACPR is not a blanket "do not treat" order — it applies only to CPR. All other treatments (antibiotics, fluids, analgesia, surgery) are assessed separately on clinical grounds
  • Family cannot override a clinical DNACPR decision, but should be involved in discussions
  • A patient with capacity can refuse CPR (this would be documented as an ADRT or DNACPR at the patient's request)

Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) for Health and Welfare

A Lasting Power of Attorney for Health and Welfare allows a nominated attorney (donee) to make decisions about health and welfare on behalf of a person who lacks capacity. The LPA must be registered with the Office of the Public Guardian. The attorney can consent to or refuse treatments on the person's behalf, including life-sustaining treatment (only if this power was explicitly granted in the LPA). An LPA for Property and Financial Affairs is separate and does not cover health decisions. Clinicians should request to see the registered LPA document, not just be told about it.

Best Interests Decision Making

When a patient lacks capacity and there is no valid ADRT or LPA, the clinical team makes a best interests decision under the MCA 2005. Best interests must consider: the patient's previously expressed wishes (verbal or written), their values and beliefs, the views of family and those important to them, and what a reasonable, objective assessment concludes is in the patient's best interest. For serious or contested decisions, an Independent Mental Capacity Advocate (IMCA) should be appointed or a Court of Protection application made.

Organ Donation in England

England introduced an opt-out system (Max and Keira's Law) in May 2020. Under deemed consent:

  • Adults who have not registered an objection to donation are considered to have consented — their organs can be used after death unless they are in an excluded group
  • Excluded from deemed consent: Under 18 years of age, those who lack capacity and have never had it in adulthood, those who lived in England for less than 12 months, those in regulated care (care homes, prisons) for most of the preceding 12 months, and those with registered objections on religious, cultural or personal grounds
  • Wales introduced opt-out in 2015; Scotland in 2021; Northern Ireland in 2023
  • Family are always consulted and their knowledge of the deceased's wishes respected — the specialist nurse for organ donation (SNOD) facilitates discussions

Donation categories: donation after brainstem death (DBD — ventilated patients with confirmed brainstem death) and donation after circulatory death (DCD — follows planned withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment). Living donation (primarily kidney, partial liver) also important.

Breaking Bad News — SPIKES Protocol

SPIKES is a structured 6-step protocol for delivering difficult news:

  • S — Setting up: Private room, patient can bring a support person, sit at same level, introduce everyone, avoid interruptions, switch off pagers where possible
  • P — Perception: Assess what the patient already knows. "What have you been told about your condition?" This prevents false assumptions and identifies misconceptions
  • I — Invitation: Ask how much information the patient wants to receive. "How much detail would you like about your results?" Some patients want everything; others prefer family to receive information first. Respect autonomy
  • K — Knowledge: Give information in clear, non-technical language. Use a "warning shot" — "I'm afraid the results are not what we hoped" before delivering the news. Give information in small steps and check understanding after each
  • E — Empathy and Emotions: Name the emotion — "It sounds like this is devastating news." Allow silence. Normalise distress. Don't rush to fill silence with information
  • S — Strategy and Summary: Explain the plan clearly. Provide written information. Arrange follow-up. Provide palliative care referral if appropriate. Ensure patient has support

Spiritual and Cultural Care

A person's spiritual and religious beliefs may profoundly influence their wishes at end of life — attitudes to analgesia, sedation, blood products, withdrawal of treatment, organ donation, autopsy, and funeral rites. Clinicians should ask sensitively about spiritual needs and involve hospital chaplaincy (who can support people of all faiths and none). Do not assume beliefs based on ethnicity or cultural background.

Bereavement support: Offer to families before and after death. Community bereavement services. Cruse Bereavement Support. Structured support groups. Complicated grief (prolonged, dysfunctional grief exceeding 6 months with significant impairment) may require specialist psychological input.

FAQ: Palliative Care for the UKMLA

What is the correct breakthrough dose formula for opioids?

Breakthrough (PRN) analgesia dose is calculated as 1/6 of the total daily oral morphine equivalent dose. For example: a patient on MST 30mg twice daily (total = 60mg oral morphine per 24 hours) should have a PRN dose of 60 ÷ 6 = 10mg oral morphine (or 5mg SC morphine, as SC = half the oral dose). If the patient is using more than 3 breakthrough doses in 24 hours, the total breakthrough used should be added to the background dose and the regular dose increased accordingly.

When is an ADRT legally binding for life-sustaining treatment?

An ADRT refusing life-sustaining treatment is legally binding under the Mental Capacity Act 2005 when it: is made by an adult aged ≥18 with capacity at the time; is in writing; is signed by the person (or by another at their direction, in their presence); is witnessed (witnessed signature required); and contains a specific statement that the decision stands even if life is at risk. Without all of these elements, an ADRT refusing life-sustaining treatment is not legally binding (though it may still be taken into account in best interests decisions). An ADRT refusing any other treatment (non-life-sustaining) does not require written form, though writing is strongly advisable.

What is the opt-out organ donation system introduced in England and who is excluded?

Max and Keira's Law introduced an opt-out (deemed consent) system in England in May 2020. Adults are deemed to have consented to organ donation after death unless they have actively registered an objection or fall into an excluded category. Exclusions: those under 18 years, people who lack capacity and have never had it as an adult, those residing in England for less than 12 months, those in regulated care (care home, prison) for most of the preceding 12 months, and those with registered objections (religious, cultural, or personal). Family members are always consulted and their knowledge of the patient's wishes is considered.

What drugs are commonly prescribed in a syringe driver for end-of-life care?

Common syringe driver drugs include: morphine (or alternative strong opioid) for pain and dyspnoea; midazolam for agitation and anxiety; haloperidol for nausea and mild agitation; levomepromazine for refractory nausea or agitation; hyoscine butylbromide for secretions and bowel colic. Drug compatibility must be checked before mixing — cyclizine is incompatible with 0.9% saline (use water for injection) and may precipitate at high morphine concentrations. A specialist palliative care pharmacist should be involved for complex combinations.

What is the difference between a DNACPR and an ADRT?

A DNACPR is a clinical recommendation made by the medical team (with patient/family involvement) that CPR should not be attempted in the event of cardiac or respiratory arrest — it is not legally binding in itself but represents good clinical decision-making. An ADRT is a legally binding document made by the patient (with capacity) in advance, which can refuse any treatment including CPR and other life-sustaining treatments. A clinically made DNACPR can be overridden by the patient with capacity or revoked; an ADRT that validly and specifically refuses CPR is legally binding and cannot be overridden by the clinical team or family.

What is the SPIKES protocol and when should it be used?

SPIKES is a structured framework for delivering bad news: Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Empathy, Strategy and Summary. It is used whenever significant or distressing information must be communicated — a cancer diagnosis, prognosis conversations, withdrawal of treatment discussions, or notifying death to family. The key clinical skills tested are: tailoring information to what the patient already knows (Perception), respecting preferences for information (Invitation), using clear language without jargon (Knowledge), and acknowledging emotions before moving to a plan (Empathy). These scenarios appear in UKMLA OSCEs as well as AKT clinical reasoning questions.

What is the role of dexamethasone in palliative care?

Dexamethasone has multiple palliative indications: raised intracranial pressure (8–16mg daily to reduce vasogenic oedema around brain metastases), spinal cord compression (16mg daily as high-dose dexamethasone — urgent alongside radiotherapy), bone pain (anti-inflammatory adjuvant), appetite stimulation and general wellbeing (low dose 2–4mg daily — effect tends to wane after 4–6 weeks), superior vena cava obstruction (reduces oedema), airway obstruction, nausea (as antiemetic adjuvant in chemotherapy). Side effects: hyperglycaemia, insomnia (give in morning), mood changes, proximal myopathy, immunosuppression, Cushing features with prolonged use. Taper when stopping.

Conclusion: Palliative Care in the UKMLA

Palliative care in the UKMLA AKT is not merely about memorising drug doses — it tests the integration of ethical principles, legal frameworks, clinical symptom management, and communication skills. Candidates who approach end-of-life questions with a holistic framework — considering what matters most to the patient, applying the Mental Capacity Act correctly, and choosing evidence-based symptom management — will perform well across this domain.

The most tested areas are: opioid dose calculation and conversion, syringe driver drug compatibility, ADRT legal validity for life-sustaining treatment refusal, the opt-out organ donation system (recent and high-yield), and DNACPR principles. The SPIKES protocol is often tested in clinical communication scenarios within the UKMLA assessment framework.

For a comprehensive revision plan that integrates palliative care with your other UKMLA preparation, visit our UKMLA preparation strategies guide. Review our results and scoring page to understand how the AKT is marked and where these clinical reasoning questions appear within the exam.
