post_title: UKMLA Results: How to Interpret Your Feedback Report
post_excerpt: Getting your UKMLA result is just the first step. Here is how to read your feedback report, understand your performance profile, and use it to guide what comes next.
seo_title: UKMLA Results: How to Interpret Your Feedback Report
seo_description: Understanding your UKMLA feedback report helps you respond to your result effectively. Learn how to read your performance profile and plan your next steps.
html_content:
Receiving your UKMLA results is a significant moment — but the result itself is only the beginning of a process. Whether you receive a pass or a fail, the feedback report that accompanies your UKMLA sitting is a structured document designed to inform your understanding of your own performance, guide professional development, and — if needed — form the basis of a resit strategy. This guide explains exactly what to expect from your UKMLA results and feedback, how to interpret every element of the information you receive, and what to do next regardless of the outcome.

How UKMLA Results Are Released

The AKT and CPSA are marked and reported separately. Results are typically released to candidates through their medical school, not directly from the GMC or the examination providers. The timeline from sitting to receiving your result varies by institution and examination window — your medical school will communicate expected result dates in advance of the sitting.

For the MS-AKT (Medical School Applied Knowledge Test — the version sat by UK medical students), results are processed centrally by the assessment provider working with the Medical Schools Council (MSC) and communicated to medical schools, which then release them to individual students according to their own protocols: some via student portals, some via email, some through formal results ceremonies.

For PLAB 1 (the equivalent AKT pathway for international medical graduates), results are released by the GMC directly, typically within a few weeks of sitting.

You should not attempt to infer your result from the examination room experience. The UKMLA uses standard setting (Angoff method for the AKT, borderline regression for the CPSA) to establish the pass mark after each sitting — meaning the pass mark you needed to meet was not fixed in advance, and what felt like a difficult or easy paper does not reliably predict whether you passed.

Understanding the AKT Feedback Report: Structure and Content

The AKT feedback report is structured around the content domains defined in the MLA Content Map. Rather than giving you a raw percentage score or a number of correct answers, the report typically describes your performance relative to the standard — specifically, in which content areas you performed at, above, or below the expected level for a minimally competent foundation doctor.

Overall pass/fail outcome: The first and most important piece of information. A pass means you met or exceeded the pass standard for this sitting. A fail means your overall performance fell below the pass standard.

Domain-level performance breakdown: The AKT is structured around several broad competency domains drawn from the MLA Content Map. Your feedback indicates your relative performance across these domains — for example, whether you performed particularly well or poorly in pharmacology, applied knowledge of clinical presentations, data interpretation, or professional and legal knowledge.

Comparison to the pass standard: Feedback is usually expressed relative to the pass standard, not as a raw percentage. A statement that you "scored at the 45th percentile" or "performed below the borderline standard in clinical knowledge" is more informative for resit planning than knowing you answered 67% of questions correctly. This is because the pass standard varies between sittings.

Sub-domain detail: Some institutions provide more granular feedback, breaking down performance within domains — for example, distinguishing between cardiovascular pharmacology and respiratory pharmacology, or between communication skills and clinical examination within the CPSA. The level of detail varies by sitting and by your medical school's reporting arrangements with the assessment provider.

Percentile rank: Some feedback reports include your performance percentile within the cohort. This is a norm-referenced descriptor — it tells you where you sat relative to other candidates in the same sitting, which is informative for self-calibration but should not be confused with the criterion-referenced pass standard.

Understanding the CPSA Feedback Report

The CPSA is an OSCE-format examination, and its feedback differs structurally from the AKT because each station assesses a specific competency in a specific clinical context.

Station-level pass/fail or score: Each OSCE station is marked individually. You will typically receive information about which stations you passed and which you did not reach the required standard in. In some formats, you receive a score out of the maximum for each station; in others, a simplified pass/borderline/fail classification.

Domain-based OSCE marking: Within each station, marks are typically allocated across several domains: clinical examination technique, clinical reasoning and interpretation, communication skills, and professionalism. Your feedback may indicate where within a station your performance was stronger or weaker.

Global rating: Most CPSA marking schemes include a global rating by the examiner — a holistic assessment of whether your performance was appropriate for a foundation doctor at that station. This rating is weighted heavily in the overall mark. Candidates who score well on individual checklist items but receive a low global rating have typically performed the right actions but without the appropriate clinical demeanour, patient-centredness, or safety awareness that the examiner expected to see.

Comparison to pass standard: Like the AKT, the CPSA pass standard is set after each sitting using borderline regression. In most CPSA models, compensatory passing is possible — strong performance in some stations can compensate for weaker performance in others, provided no single station falls below a specified minimum threshold (the so-called "bare fail" or "fail" category that cannot be compensated).

What Statistical Concepts Appear in the Feedback Report

AKT feedback may include statistical language that candidates find confusing. Understanding these concepts helps you read your report accurately:

TermWhat It MeansHow to Use It
Scaled score A transformation of your raw score that accounts for sitting difficulty and allows comparison across different sittings Use to compare performance across resit sittings; a scaled score of the same value means the same performance level regardless of sitting difficulty
Cut score / pass mark The minimum scaled score required to pass for this specific sitting, derived by the Angoff standard-setting process Compare your scaled score to the cut score to understand how much above or below the standard you fell
Standard Error of Measurement (SEM) A measure of the precision of the test — the range within which your true score is likely to fall with 68% confidence A score within 1 SEM of the cut score is a borderline result; the examination cannot discriminate reliably at this level
Percentile rank The percentage of candidates in your cohort who scored below you Gives context for your performance relative to peers, but is norm-referenced — a high percentile does not guarantee a pass; a low percentile does not guarantee a fail
Domain score Your performance in a specific content domain (e.g., pharmacology, clinical presentations) expressed relative to the overall cohort or the domain-specific expected standard Use domain scores to identify specific content gaps for resit revision planning

What a Pass Result Means — and What to Do With It

A pass in the AKT and CPSA means you have met the standard of a minimally competent foundation doctor. It is a significant achievement — the standard is set to reflect what is genuinely required at the point of independent practice, not a lowered bar.

However, a pass is not the end of the feedback report's usefulness. Even candidates who pass often have identifiable areas of weaker performance. The feedback report serves several ongoing purposes:

Identifying foundation year learning priorities: If AKT feedback shows relatively weaker performance in, for example, clinical pharmacology or data interpretation, this is a signal that these areas warrant continued attention in your portfolio of learning as a foundation doctor. The AiF (Assessment in Foundation) programme expects you to address learning needs that emerge from performance feedback.

Informing specialty training applications: Specialty training applications (core medical training, core surgical training, general practice, anaesthetics, psychiatry, etc.) require structured reflection on strengths and development areas. A specific analysis of your UKMLA feedback — including examples of how you have addressed identified gaps — provides concrete evidence for portfolio entries and application statements.

Calibrating self-assessment: Many doctors overestimate competence in areas where they feel confident and underestimate it in areas they find uncomfortable. External feedback from a standardised assessment provides an objective counterweight to self-perception. This calibration effect persists through training and influences how doctors seek learning opportunities.

CPD portfolio entry: Your UKMLA results can function as a formal reflective entry in your foundation portfolio. Documenting your performance, your interpretation, and your planned response to the feedback demonstrates the reflective practice that professional development frameworks expect.

What a Fail Result Means — and How to Use the Feedback

A fail in either the AKT or the CPSA is deeply disappointing, and it is important to acknowledge that emotional reality before attempting to use the feedback analytically. However, the feedback report after a failed sitting is the most important document you will receive in planning a resit.

Step 1 — Understand what you failed: Is your fail a global fail (overall performance below the pass standard, with weaknesses across multiple domains) or a targeted fail (strong overall performance but significant weakness in one or two specific areas)? The answer determines whether you need a broad revision strategy or a targeted one.

Step 2 — Quantify the gap: If you can determine how far below the pass standard you were, this helps calibrate the scale of preparation needed. A candidate who narrowly failed needs a different resit strategy from a candidate who failed significantly across most domains.

Step 3 — Map feedback to content: Take each area of relative weakness identified in the feedback and map it to specific content in the MLA Content Map. Use this mapping to construct a revision plan that allocates time proportionally — more time to areas of significant weakness, maintenance time to areas of strength.

Step 4 — Change your approach, not just your effort: Many candidates who fail a UKMLA sitting respond by revising more of the same material using the same method. But if the original approach did not produce a pass, the resit strategy should examine whether the revision method itself needs to change. If your preparation was reading-heavy and your performance was weak on applied reasoning questions, switching to active question practice addresses the root cause.

Step 5 — Seek support: Contact your educational supervisor and student support team promptly. A fail in the UKMLA requires a structured institutional response as well as a personal one. Your educational supervisor can provide pastoral support, arrange additional clinical supervision if CPSA skills are the concern, and help access specialist resources.

Domain-Specific Feedback Interpretation

Domain / Feedback SignalWhat It SuggestsResit Strategy
Weak in clinical knowledge of presentations Gaps in the systematic approach to common AKT presentations Revise the 130 MLA presentations systematically using the Content Map; question bank testing of each presentation
Weak in pharmacology / prescribing Difficulty applying drug knowledge to clinical scenarios; may include dose calculation, interactions, or contraindications Revise high-risk drug classes; practise prescribing scenario questions with BNF available; use medication reconciliation scenarios
Weak in data interpretation Difficulty reading blood results, imaging descriptions, ECGs, or statistical data in question stems Practise data interpretation daily; ECG workbooks; ABG interpretation practice; haematology and biochemistry result exercises
Weak in professional and legal knowledge Difficulty with GMC Good Medical Practice, consent, confidentiality, capacity, and patient safety questions Read Good Medical Practice (2024); practise SJT-style scenarios; review Mental Capacity Act, Gillick competence, confidentiality exceptions
Weak in CPSA clinical examination stations Technique errors, missed clinical signs, or poor presentation of findings Arrange regular physical examination practice with clinical supervisor using structured mark scheme; video self-assessment
Weak in CPSA communication stations Difficulty structuring conversations, handling difficult scenarios, applying professional frameworks Role-play communication scenarios with peers; analyse mark schemes for disclosure, consent, breaking bad news, and safeguarding stations
Low global rating in CPSA despite reasonable checklist scores Performing tasks correctly but without the clinical manner, empathy, or safety awareness the examiner expected Seek senior feedback on clinical manner in real clinical settings; integrate communication into examination practice (not as a separate skill)

The Role of Standard Setting in Interpreting Your Result

The UKMLA does not use a fixed percentage pass mark. The pass standard is set after each sitting using the Angoff method (for the AKT) or borderline regression (for the CPSA). This means the pass mark varies slightly between sittings depending on the difficulty profile of that sitting's questions or stations.

A practical implication: if your feedback tells you that you fell below the standard, it does not tell you by exactly how many percentage points of absolute correctness. What it tells you is that your performance fell below what a panel of medical education experts determined was the minimum acceptable for safe independent practice on that sitting. The comparison between your performance and the pass standard is what matters — not a comparison between your score and a friend's score.

Two candidates with identical raw scores could have different outcomes if they sat in different windows where the standard-setting process produced different pass marks. This is an inherent and well-justified feature of criterion-referenced assessment, reflecting genuine differences in paper difficulty rather than inconsistency in the standard applied.

Using UKMLA Feedback for CPD and Portfolio

Good Medical Practice (2024) requires doctors to engage in continuing professional development and to reflect on their performance to identify learning needs. Your UKMLA feedback report is a formal, externally validated assessment output — exactly the kind of evidence that a professional portfolio entry should be built on.

A high-quality portfolio entry based on your UKMLA feedback would include:

  • A factual summary of your performance: which domains were strong, which were weaker
  • A reflective analysis: what you believe caused the performance profile (gaps in clinical exposure, revision method, time management, examination technique)
  • A learning plan: specific actions you have taken or plan to take in response to identified gaps (targeted reading, clinical supervision, skill practice)
  • Evidence of implementation: a record of what you did (log of question bank sessions, clinical supervisor feedback, OSCE practice sessions)
  • Revised self-assessment: what you believe your current level is after the learning intervention, and how you tested this

This reflective cycle — observation, analysis, planning, action, reassessment — is the structure of the formal CPD requirement and is demonstrated explicitly in the feedback report response.

Supporting a Peer Through a Fail Result

If a colleague receives a fail result, your response matters. Medical culture has historically been poor at supporting colleagues through examination failure — a combination of competitive culture, stigma around academic difficulty, and a general reluctance to discuss vulnerability in professional settings.

What actually helps:

  • Acknowledge the result directly — don't avoid the topic or pretend everything is fine
  • Listen without problem-solving in the first instance — the emotional impact is primary
  • Offer practical support: study together for the resit, share resources, provide accountability
  • Encourage them to access support services (medical school wellbeing, BMA Doctors for Doctors) rather than treating this as a solo problem
  • Maintain normal social engagement — isolation during a difficult period compounds the difficulty

What does not help: minimising ("everyone finds it hard"), comparing ("I found it easy"), advising before listening, or withdrawing.

Appeals Against UKMLA Results

If you believe there were procedural irregularities in your examination — not simply that you disagree with the pass standard — an appeals pathway exists. For the MS-AKT, appeals are handled through your medical school in the first instance. For PLAB 1, the GMC manages appeals directly.

Valid grounds for appeal typically include:

  • Evidence that examination conditions were materially disrupted and not appropriately addressed at the time
  • Evidence that agreed reasonable adjustments were not implemented
  • Evidence of an administrative error in marking, recording, or reporting your result
  • Evidence of a procedural irregularity affecting the fairness of your assessment

Grounds that are generally not accepted: disagreement with the pass standard; believing you "should have passed" based on your revision; or believing specific questions were ambiguous (unless the question was subsequently identified as faulty and removed from marking). Contact your medical school or the GMC (for PLAB) promptly — appeal timeframes are typically within 10–20 working days of the result date.

Supporting Your Wellbeing After a Difficult Result

Receiving a fail result affects identity, confidence, and relationships — not just academic progress. Medical students who fail licensing examinations are at elevated risk of anxiety, depression, and burnout, particularly if they have not experienced major examination failure before.

Helpful responses: give yourself time before entering analytical mode; access pastoral support available to you; reframe the narrative (a setback, not a permanent verdict); and reconnect with your reasons for entering medicine. Many excellent doctors have needed more than one attempt at licensing examinations — this does not define professional trajectory.

Unhelpful responses: isolating yourself, immediate unstructured cramming without a changed strategy, or catastrophising the outcome.

Frequently Asked Questions: UKMLA Results and Feedback

When will I get my UKMLA result?
Typically four to eight weeks after your sitting date for the AKT. Your medical school will communicate exact timelines. PLAB 1 results are typically available within a few weeks of sitting.

Can I see my marked paper?
No. You will not receive a copy of your individual question paper or see which questions you answered correctly. This is standard practice in high-stakes medical licensing examinations where item security is essential.

How long do I have to wait to resit if I fail?
Resit timelines depend on the examination calendar, your medical school's regulations, and your institutional policies on resit eligibility. Contact your medical school's student affairs office promptly after receiving a fail — there are often strict enrolment windows for supplementary sittings.

Does failing the UKMLA affect my medical registration?
You must pass both the AKT and CPSA before the GMC will grant full registration and a licence to practice. You can graduate from medical school before passing the UKMLA, but you cannot begin foundation training or work as a licensed doctor until both components are passed.

If I pass the AKT but fail the CPSA, do I have to resit both?
In most formats, each component is assessed independently. If you pass the AKT and fail the CPSA, you resit only the CPSA — your AKT pass is retained subject to the validity period specified by your medical school or the GMC. Confirm the specific rules with your medical school, as these can differ by institution.

Can my resit result be worse than my first attempt?
In theory, yes — performance in any sitting is subject to variation. However, candidates who make specific, targeted changes to their revision strategy between sittings based on their feedback report typically improve their scaled score. The key is addressing the root cause of the failure, not simply repeating the same preparation approach.

Conclusion: Use Your Results as a Clinical Document

Your UKMLA results and feedback report are not administrative bureaucracy — they are clinical information about your readiness to practice safely. Treat them with the same analytical rigour you would apply to any clinical investigation result: understand what the result means, identify what caused this outcome, and develop a management plan.

Whether you are celebrating a pass or beginning the work of planning a resit, the feedback report provides the most specific and objective information available about your performance at this stage of training. Use it fully — not just as a one-time document, but as the starting point for ongoing professional development.

For more on the UKMLA process, see our Results and Scoring guide and our guide on Appeals and Resits.

How Formative Assessments Prepare You for Summative UKMLA Performance

The UKMLA is the summative gate — but the formative assessments embedded in your medical programme are where your preparedness is built and tested incrementally. Treating formative assessments as meaningful is one of the highest-leverage habits you can develop in medical school.

Progress tests: Many UK medical schools use regular longitudinal progress tests — standardised multiple-choice papers sat by all years simultaneously to test accumulating clinical knowledge. Progress test performance in years three and four is one of the strongest predictors of UKMLA AKT performance. If your progress test domain breakdown shows a persistent weakness in, for example, pharmacology or professionalism questions, this signal arrives before the licensing examination and gives you time to address it. Do not disregard progress test feedback.

Clinical placement assessments: DOPS (Direct Observation of Procedural Skills), mini-CEX (mini Clinical Evaluation Exercise), CBD (Case-Based Discussion), and ACAT (Acute Care Assessment Tool) all generate supervisor feedback on specific clinical skills. This feedback is your closest available proxy to a CPSA mark before you sit the CPSA itself. Actively seek this feedback throughout your placements, and ask supervisors specifically which aspects of your clinical examination or communication you should develop — this question is almost universally welcomed by supervisors and produces the most specific developmental guidance.

OSPEs and formative OSCEs: Many institutions run formative OSCE circuits in the penultimate year. Performance in these is highly predictive of summative CPSA performance — both the level and the domain profile. Treat formative OSCE feedback with the same analytical rigour you would apply to summative feedback: identify which stations you fell below the borderline standard in, analyse the specific mark scheme elements you missed, and address these systematically.

Using the UKMLA Feedback Report for Specialty Training Applications

The Foundation Programme and subsequent specialty training applications use the Educational Performance Measure (EPM) and Situational Judgement Test (SJT) for initial allocation, but specialty training applications from foundation onwards increasingly require structured reflection on performance and learning. The UKMLA feedback report is a legitimate piece of evidence in these applications when used correctly.

For core medical training (CMT) and acute care specialties: These applications reward demonstrated clinical reasoning ability and patient safety focus. A UKMLA feedback report showing above-average performance in clinical knowledge of presentations and pharmacology, with specific reflection on how you have built on this in foundation year, provides concrete evidence for portfolio statements about clinical competence.

For general practice specialty training (GPST): GP registrar applications reward breadth across the clinical domains and demonstrated professionalism and communication skills. Your CPSA feedback — particularly on communication stations — is directly relevant portfolio evidence. A statement such as "in my CPSA, I identified a relative weakness in breaking bad news stations; I have addressed this by attending the palliative care communication skills training in FY1 and seeking feedback from my GP supervisor" demonstrates exactly the reflective, responsive learning that GP applications look for.

For psychiatry, paediatrics, obstetrics and gynaecology: These applications typically weight demonstrated commitment to the specialty and specific competency evidence. UKMLA feedback showing strength in the relevant clinical domain, supplemented by portfolio evidence of specific learning activities, strengthens these applications.

For surgical specialties: Core surgical training applications weight procedural and technical skill evidence. CPSA clinical examination station performance — particularly on examination technique and clinical reasoning stations — provides relevant evidence of early clinical technical competence, supplemented by clinical logbooks and DOPS from foundation year.

The Psychological Impact of UKMLA Results at Different Life Stages

The UKMLA is sat at a specific life stage — the transition from medical student to doctor — that carries its own psychological weight regardless of examination outcome. Understanding the emotional landscape around results helps you respond adaptively whether you pass or fail.

For younger students (traditional five-year pathway, early twenties): The UKMLA may be the first major high-stakes assessment failure experience for students who have previously succeeded through school and early university. Failure challenges a core identity narrative ("I am someone who succeeds academically") in ways that are uniquely disorienting. The adaptive response: separate performance from identity; recognise that a single examination outcome does not define professional capability or trajectory; access support promptly rather than isolating.

For GEM students and older candidates: Older students who have previous professional careers and significant life investments (financial, relational, time) carry additional weight around UKMLA outcomes. Failure may activate concerns about whether the career change was the right decision, about financial pressure, or about the timeline to practicing income. These concerns are real and legitimate — and they need to be addressed alongside the practical resit strategy, not instead of it. A good educational supervisor will be alert to these broader life context pressures and factor them into support planning.

For international medical graduates taking PLAB: PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 are high-stakes not only for licensing but often for visa status, employment timelines, and family situations. The pressure is qualitatively different from that on a UK final-year student, and the support resources available are also different. The GMC's website provides guidance specific to international medical graduates, and the BMA's international graduate support resources are also available.

Long-Term Use of Feedback: The CPD Cycle in Foundation Training

The skills of using performance feedback effectively — objective interpretation, honest self-assessment, targeted learning planning, verification of learning — are not just UKMLA skills. They are the skills of a reflective clinician, required throughout a medical career by professional regulatory frameworks.

The GMC's revalidation framework requires all licensed doctors to demonstrate engagement with CPD and to collect multi-source feedback at regular intervals. The portfolio tools used in Foundation Programme (AiF), core training, and specialty training all instantiate the same cycle: gather evidence of performance, reflect on what it shows, identify learning needs, plan and implement learning, reassess. The UKMLA feedback report is your first formal opportunity to practise this cycle at a professional level.

Candidates who leave medical school having practised this reflective cycle genuinely — not as a portfolio box-ticking exercise, but as a real cognitive habit — are better prepared for the continuous self-directed learning that professional medicine requires. The UKMLA is not the end of the assessment journey; it is the entry point to a career of ongoing performance review.

Resources for Post-Result Support

SituationResourceHow to Access
Emotional support after a fail result BMA Doctors for Doctors; medical school wellbeing service; your GP BMA website; medical school student services; self-refer to GP
Resit information and timeline Medical school academic support office Email or in-person appointment; contact promptly after result
Reasonable adjustments for resit Medical school disability/accessibility team; GMC (for PLAB) Request via student services; provide evidence of diagnosis
Academic appeals Medical school academic appeals process Check student handbook for appeal submission process and deadline
Independent advice about appeals Student Union advice service; Citizen's Advice Student Union welfare office; local Citizen's Advice Bureau
Professional development support Educational supervisor; Foundation Programme pastoral support Contact educational supervisor directly

Understanding Your AKT Scaled Score: What the Numbers Mean

The AKT result is reported as a scaled score rather than a raw mark or percentage. Understanding what a scaled score is — and what it is not — helps you read your feedback accurately and make sensible comparisons between sittings if you resit.

A raw score is the number of questions you answered correctly. A scaled score is a transformation of the raw score that accounts for the statistical difficulty of that particular paper and enables comparison across different sittings. The specific scale used for the UKMLA AKT is set by the examination authority; common medical licensing examination scales range from 1–100 or 100–300 (as in the USMLE), but the specific scale used in your sitting will be communicated in the assessment framework guidance from your medical school.

Why scaling matters: If two sittings of the AKT contain questions of different overall difficulty, a raw score of 130/180 in a hard sitting is not equivalent to 130/180 in an easy sitting. The easier sitting's 130 required less clinical knowledge to achieve. Scaling corrects for this by anchoring both scores to the same competency scale, so that a scaled score of X in any sitting reflects the same underlying level of clinical knowledge.

Why your percentage is not the same as your scaled score: A percentage correct (e.g. 72%) is a raw metric — it does not account for sitting difficulty. Your scaled score is the appropriate basis for comparing your performance to the pass standard and to other sittings. When interpreting your feedback, focus on the scaled score and its relationship to the scaled pass mark (cut score), not on the raw percentage.

What to Expect in the Weeks Between Sitting and Results

The period between completing the AKT or CPSA and receiving your result can be one of the more anxious phases of medical school. Understanding what happens during this period can reduce uncertainty.

Marking and quality assurance: AKT items are machine-marked. The time between sitting and results is used for item analysis — checking for malfunctioning items, reviewing items flagged for DIF, running equating procedures if multiple examination forms were used, and finalising the standard-setting process. For the CPSA, examiner mark sheets are collated and standardised, and station performance data is processed through the borderline regression model.

Examination board review: An examination board composed of the assessment director, lead psychometrician, and senior clinical examiners formally reviews the performance data, confirms item removals and scoring adjustments, approves the final pass mark, and signs off the results. This process typically takes three to six weeks for the AKT. CPSA results may follow a slightly different timeline.

Result release: Your medical school will communicate results via their standard student communication channels — student portal, email, or a formal ceremony depending on institution. Check your student portal's guidance on expected result dates rather than trying to infer from the sitting date.

How Resit Performance Compares to First-Attempt Performance

Candidates who resit the AKT after a first-attempt failure show a range of outcomes. Research on resit performance in medical licensing examinations consistently shows that a significant proportion — typically 50–70% of resit candidates — pass on the second attempt. The factors most strongly associated with resit success are:

  • A systematic change in revision approach (not just more of the same)
  • Targeted revision addressing the specific domains identified as weak in the first-attempt feedback
  • Use of active recall methods (question banks, practice tests) rather than passive review
  • Adequate time between the first attempt and the resit to allow meaningful preparation (minimum six to eight weeks; ideally three to four months)
  • Access to pastoral and academic support during the resit preparation period

Candidates who resit with the same approach that produced the initial failure typically show less improvement than those who change their strategy. If you are preparing for a resit, the feedback report from your first attempt is your most important planning document — use it specifically to guide where your preparation time goes.

post_title: Clinical Examination Stations in the CPSA: A Practical Guide
post_excerpt: Physical examination stations are a core CPSA component. Here is how to approach cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, and neurological examinations under timed conditions.
seo_title: Clinical Examination Stations in the CPSA: A Practical Guide
seo_description: Clinical examination stations are a core CPSA component. Learn how to approach cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, and neurological exams in OSCE conditions.
html_content:
The Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) is the objective structured clinical examination (OSCE) component of the UKMLA. It is sat in the final year of medical school and tests the physical examination, clinical reasoning, communication, and professional skills that every foundation doctor must demonstrate before entering independent practice. This guide covers everything you need to know about CPSA clinical examinations: the format, the mark scheme, how to approach every major system examination, common examiner expectations, and a structured preparation strategy.

CPSA Format and Structure

The CPSA is an OSCE — a series of timed clinical stations, each testing a defined set of skills. Candidates rotate through stations in sequence, with rest stations interspersed. Examiners mark each station using a standardised mark scheme while you complete the examination or communication task.

Key structural features:

  • Multiple stations (typically 16–20 in full OSCE format), each lasting five to ten minutes
  • Mix of clinical examination stations, communication/history stations, procedural skills stations, and clinical reasoning/data interpretation stations
  • Standardised patients or clinical mannequins, depending on station type
  • Each station marked by one or two examiners using a structured mark scheme
  • A global rating component in addition to task-specific checklist marks
  • Compensatory passing: strong performance in some stations can compensate for weaker performance in others, provided no single station falls below the minimum threshold

The CPSA mark scheme typically allocates marks across three to four domains within each station: clinical technique (performing the examination correctly); clinical interpretation (correctly identifying findings and reasoning about them); communication (explaining findings to the patient, discussing a plan); and professionalism (introduction, consent, patient comfort, hand hygiene).

Core Principles for Every CPSA Station

Before examining individual system examinations, the following principles apply to every station and are marked in every station:

WIPING — the acronym used by many medical schools for the opening sequence:

  • Wash hands (gel or soap — gel is acceptable and typically available at the station)
  • Introduce yourself (full name, role: "I'm [name], a final-year medical student")
  • Patient's name and date of birth (confirm you have the correct patient)
  • Informed consent ("Would it be alright if I examined your chest today?")
  • Not in pain (check the patient is comfortable and not in acute pain before examining)
  • General exposure (position and expose appropriately for the examination you are performing)

Positioning and exposure: Each examination has a specific starting position. Getting this right before you begin earns marks and demonstrates clinical knowledge. Cardiovascular: 45 degrees, chest and abdomen exposed. Respiratory: 45 degrees, chest exposed. Abdominal: lying flat at 45 degrees. Neurological (upper limb): sitting. Neurological (lower limb): lying flat or sitting for different components.

Verbalize your findings as you go: Many CPSA mark schemes award marks for verbalising findings, not just performing the action. If you find a normal apex beat, say so: "The apex beat is in the fifth intercostal space, mid-clavicular line — this is normal." If you find dullness to percussion, say "I can hear dullness to percussion here — this is abnormal." Examiners can only mark what they hear you say.

End with a presentation: Most stations require a verbal presentation of your findings at the end. Structure this as: "On examination, this patient's cardiovascular system appears to be within normal limits. I found no peripheral stigmata of heart disease, the JVP was not elevated, the pulse was regular at 70 beats per minute, heart sounds one and two were present with no additional sounds, and there was no peripheral oedema."

Cardiovascular Examination

This is the most commonly tested system in CPSA OSCE stations and is reliably tested in almost every sitting.

Peripheral examination (hands and arms): Look for clubbing (loss of angle between nail and nail bed; subungual fluctuation — the "floating nail" sign); cyanosis (peripheral — blue digits from poor perfusion; central cyanosis is checked at the lips and tongue); splinter haemorrhages (linear brown lines beneath the nail — associated with infective endocarditis but also found with nail trauma); koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails — associated with iron deficiency); leukonychia (white nails — hypoalbuminaemia); capillary refill (press for 5 seconds, refill should be less than 2 seconds).

Pulse assessment: Radial pulse — rate (count for 15 seconds × 4 if regular; count for 60 seconds if irregular), rhythm (regular, irregularly irregular = AF, regularly irregular = ectopics or second-degree heart block), character (describe as normal in most cases; collapsing/bounding pulse of aortic regurgitation confirmed by lifting the arm). Simultaneously assess for radio-radial delay (coarctation of aorta, cervical rib) and radio-femoral delay (coarctation — feel femoral pulse with other hand).

Blood pressure measurement: The examiner may ask you to describe how you would measure blood pressure rather than perform it in the station. Know the correct technique: seat the patient, cuff at heart level, appropriate cuff size (bladder 80% of arm circumference), inflate to 30 mmHg above obliteration of radial pulse, deflate at 2 mmHg/second, record Korotkoff 1 (systolic) and Korotkoff 5 (diastolic). Explain that you would measure postural BP (lying and standing) if clinically indicated.

Face and eyes: Corneal arcus (white/grey ring around the iris — associated with hyperlipidaemia in patients under 50; non-specific in older patients); xanthelasma (yellow cholesterol deposits around the eyes — hyperlipidaemia); pallor (conjunctival pallor for anaemia — pull down lower lid); malar flush (mitral stenosis facies — now rare).

JVP assessment: Position patient at 45 degrees. Look for the JVP at the right internal jugular vein, lateral to the sternocleidomastoid, medial to the external jugular. Normal: <3 cm above the sternal angle. Raised JVP: right heart failure, cardiac tamponade, SVC obstruction, fluid overload. Characteristics: pulsatile (two waves per cardiac cycle), descends on inspiration, obliterated by pressure (unlike a carotid pulse which is palpated, not obliterated). Hepatojugular reflux: apply firm pressure to the right upper quadrant — a sustained rise in JVP suggests right heart failure.

Precordium inspection and palpation: Look for scars (midline sternotomy — previous CABG or valve surgery; lateral thoracotomy — previous lung surgery or mitral valvotomy; pacemaker or ICD scar below left clavicle). Feel the apex beat: locate position (normally 5th ICS, MCL), assess character (tapping — mitral stenosis; heaving/pressure-loaded — aortic stenosis; thrusting/volume-loaded — aortic or mitral regurgitation; diffuse/displaced — dilated cardiomyopathy). Parasternal heave: right ventricular hypertrophy. Thrills (palpable murmurs).

Auscultation: Mitral area (apex, 5th ICS MCL with diaphragm); tricuspid area (lower left sternal edge, 4th ICS); pulmonary area (upper left sternal edge, 2nd ICS); aortic area (upper right sternal edge, 2nd ICS). Additionally auscultate in the axilla (radiation of mitral regurgitation) and carotids (radiation of aortic stenosis or carotid bruit). Comment on S1 (mitral and tricuspid valve closure), S2 (aortic and pulmonary valve closure), additional sounds (S3 = ventricular gallop — volume overload/heart failure; S4 = atrial gallop — stiff ventricle in hypertension or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy), and murmurs (timing, location, character, radiation, response to manoeuvres if asked).

Completing the cardiovascular examination: Check for peripheral oedema (sacral and pedal — press for 30 seconds). Auscultate the lung bases bilaterally (bibasal crackles in pulmonary oedema from left heart failure). Palpate the abdomen for hepatomegaly (hepatic congestion in right heart failure). State you would review observations, perform an ECG and request a chest X-ray and echocardiogram.

Respiratory Examination

General inspection: Cyanosis; respiratory rate and work of breathing; accessory muscle use; position (patients with severe COPD may sit forward, using the tripod position); oxygen delivery devices; sputum pot; inhaler devices.

Hands and arms: Clubbing (bronchogenic carcinoma, bronchiectasis, pulmonary fibrosis, cystic fibrosis — note that COPD does NOT cause clubbing); peripheral cyanosis; carbon dioxide retention flap (asterixis — coarse tremor when wrists extended — in severe hypercapnia); tar staining (cigarette smoking — clinical sign, not a finding to emphasise positively).

Face and neck: Pursed-lip breathing (COPD); central cyanosis (tongue and lips); trachea position (midline, deviated towards collapse, deviated away from tension pneumothorax/massive effusion); Horner's syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis — Pancoast tumour).

Expansion: Place hands on the chest wall symmetrically, thumbs meeting in the midline. Ask patient to take a deep breath. Asymmetric expansion: reduced on the affected side in effusion, collapse, consolidation, pneumothorax. Reduced bilaterally: COPD, pulmonary fibrosis.

Percussion: Compare left and right at each level — anterior, lateral, posterior. Dull: consolidation, effusion, collapse. Stony dull: effusion. Resonant: normal. Hyper-resonant: pneumothorax, emphysema.

Auscultation: Vesicular (normal); bronchial breathing (consolidation); absent breath sounds (effusion, pneumothorax); added sounds — crackles (fine crackles = pulmonary fibrosis; coarse crackles = pulmonary oedema, bronchiectasis; crackles that clear on coughing = secretions); wheeze (polyphonic — asthma/COPD; monophonic — local obstruction); pleural rub (pleurisy — creaking/rubbing quality).

Vocal resonance: Ask patient to say "99". Normal: muffled. Increased: consolidation (bronchophony). Absent: effusion. Whispering pectoriloquy (consolidation — whispered words transmitted clearly) and egophony ("e" sounds like "a" — consolidation above effusion).

Completing the respiratory examination: Review oxygen saturations, check peak flow if appropriate, review observations chart and any recent sputum cultures or spirometry. State: "I would like to complete my examination by reviewing the chest X-ray, spirometry, and arterial blood gas results."

Abdominal Examination

General inspection: Jaundice; pallor; cachexia; abdominal distension (fluid, fat, flatus, faeces, fetus, fibroids — the "6 Fs"); visible peristalsis; herniae; prominent venous pattern (caput medusae in portal hypertension — veins radiate from umbilicus).

Hands and periphery: Clubbing (Crohn's disease, liver disease); leukonychia (hypoalbuminaemia); koilonychia (iron deficiency from GI blood loss); palmar erythema (chronic liver disease, pregnancy); spider naevi (chronic liver disease — more than five over the SVC territory); Dupuytren's contracture (alcohol-related liver disease); asterixis (hepatic encephalopathy); gynaecomastia (chronic liver disease); testicular atrophy (chronic liver disease).

Face: Jaundice (scleral icterus — check conjunctiva; yellow skin); anaemia (conjunctival pallor); parotid enlargement (alcohol-related liver disease); Kayser-Fleischer rings (green-brown rings at corneal periphery — Wilson's disease — rare but tested).

Palpation: Light palpation first (9 regions, start away from pain); then deep palpation. Liver (start from right iliac fossa, move upwards — percuss first to identify lower border); spleen (start from right iliac fossa, move towards left hypochondrium — turn patient right side up to bring spleen forward; confirm with splenic notch); kidneys (bimanual palpation — ballotable, moves on inspiration, not crossing midline — distinguishes from spleen); aorta (pulsatile midline mass above umbilicus).

Percussion: Liver span (upper border = hepatic dullness on percussion from resonance — typically 5th ICS MCL; lower border = first resonance below lower edge). Splenic dullness. Shifting dullness and fluid thrill for ascites (test if you suspect ascites from inspection).

Auscultation: Bowel sounds (active, absent, tinkling — small bowel obstruction); renal bruits (renal artery stenosis); hepatic bruit (hepatoma, acute alcoholic hepatitis); splenic rub (rare — splenic infarct, polycythaemia).

Completing the abdominal examination: State you would examine the hernia orifices (inguinal canals, umbilicus, scars); perform a rectal examination if indicated; examine external genitalia in male patients if indicated; request LFTs, FBC, U&Es, abdominal ultrasound.

Neurological Examination: Upper Limb

The standard approach: inspection, tone, power (MRC grade), reflexes, coordination, sensation, functional assessment.

Inspection: Wasting (loss of muscle bulk — suggests lower motor neuron lesion or primary muscle disease); fasciculations (involuntary muscle twitching — motor neuron disease, radiculopathy); deformity; abnormal posture (flexion at elbow and wrist — upper motor neuron lesion affecting upper limb).

Tone: Passively flex and extend each joint, asking the patient to relax. Spasticity (velocity-dependent increased tone — upper motor neuron lesion; "clasp-knife" quality); rigidity (lead-pipe — basal ganglia disease; cogwheel — Parkinson's plus tremor).

Power: MRC grading: 0 = no movement, 1 = flicker only, 2 = movement with gravity eliminated, 3 = movement against gravity but not resistance, 4 = movement against some resistance, 5 = normal. Test shoulder abduction (C5), elbow flexion (C5,6), elbow extension (C7), wrist extension (C7), finger extension (C7), finger abduction (T1), grip strength.

Reflexes: Biceps (C5,6), brachioradialis (C5,6), triceps (C7). Grade: 0 = absent, + = present only with reinforcement, ++ = normal, +++ = brisk (UMN sign), ++++ = clonus. Hyperreflexia (UMN); hyporeflexia/areflexia (LMN, peripheral neuropathy, cerebellar).

Coordination: Finger-nose test (past-pointing = cerebellar); dysdiadochokinesis (rapid alternating movements — cerebellar). Intention tremor (worsens on approaching target — cerebellar). Resting tremor (present at rest, reduced by movement — Parkinson's).

Sensation: Light touch (cotton wool — spinothalamic and dorsal column); pinprick (neurological pin — spinothalamic); vibration sense (128 Hz tuning fork at bony prominences — dorsal column); proprioception (joint position sense — dorsal column). Test from periphery inward to identify the level of any deficit.

Neurological Examination: Lower Limb

Follows the same structure as upper limb but adapted for lower limb anatomy. Additional assessments: gait, Romberg's test, plantar reflexes.

Power: Hip flexion (L1,2), hip extension (L5,S1), knee flexion (L5,S1), knee extension (L3,4), ankle dorsiflexion (L4,5), plantar flexion (S1,2), great toe extension (L5).

Reflexes: Knee jerk (L3,4), ankle jerk (S1,2), plantar response (Babinski — normal = downgoing; UMN = upgoing/extensor, with fanning of toes).

Gait: Observe normal walking: step length, arm swing, heel strike. Then ask the patient to walk heel-to-toe (tandem walking — tests cerebellar). Note: broad-based gait (cerebellar); high-stepping gait (foot drop — common peroneal nerve palsy, L4/5 radiculopathy); scissor gait (UMN — spastic); antalgic gait (pain); Parkinsonian gait (shuffling, reduced arm swing, festination, difficulty initiating).

Romberg's test: Stand with feet together, eyes open (uses vision, vestibular, and proprioception); then close eyes (removes visual input — now relies on proprioception and vestibular). Positive Romberg = sways or falls with eyes closed but not open = proprioceptive deficit (dorsal column lesion, peripheral neuropathy). Note: cerebellar ataxia causes swaying with eyes open — Romberg's test is positive only for proprioceptive deficits.

Cranial Nerve Examination

Cranial nerve examination is occasionally tested as a complete station or as part of a broader neurological assessment. Know all 12 cranial nerves and their examination:

NerveFunctionExamination
I – OlfactorySmellTest each nostril with standardised smell (coffee, vanilla); rarely tested in CPSA
II – OpticVisionVisual acuity (Snellen chart); visual fields (confrontation); pupils (direct and consensual light reflex); fundoscopy
III, IV, VI – Oculomotor, Trochlear, AbducensEye movementsEye movements in H pattern; ptosis; pupil size; nystagmus
V – TrigeminalFacial sensation; jaw movementsLight touch and pinprick in 3 divisions (ophthalmic, maxillary, mandibular); corneal reflex (afferent V, efferent VII); jaw opening and clenching against resistance
VII – FacialFacial movementsRaise eyebrows; close eyes tight; puff cheeks; smile; UMN (forehead spared) vs LMN (whole face affected)
VIII – VestibulocochlearHearing and balanceWhisper test or tuning fork (Rinne's and Weber's); balance testing
IX, X – Glossopharyngeal, VagusSwallowing; gag reflex; voicePalate elevation (IX,X); gag reflex (IX afferent, X efferent); voice (hoarse = vocal cord palsy, X); dysarthria
XI – AccessoryNeck and shoulder musclesSternocleidomastoid (turn head against resistance); trapezius (shrug against resistance)
XII – HypoglossalTongue movementsProtrude tongue (deviates towards lesion in LMN); observe for wasting and fasciculations

Thyroid and Endocrine Examination

Thyroid examination is a reliable CPSA station in many institutions. It combines peripheral assessment, neck examination, and specific thyroid signs.

Peripheral signs of thyroid disease: Hands — fine tremor (thyrotoxicosis); onycholysis (Plummer's nails — separation of distal nail from bed, thyrotoxicosis); dry, coarse skin and hair (hypothyroidism); pretibial myxoedema (bilateral non-pitting oedema below the knees — Graves' disease specifically, not all hyperthyroid states); acropachy (periosteal new bone formation — Graves').

Face: Exophthalmos/proptosis (Graves' disease); lid lag and lid retraction (thyrotoxicosis); periorbital oedema (hypothyroidism); loss of outer third of eyebrows (hypothyroidism — classical sign).

Neck inspection: A visible goitre (diffuse, nodular, or focal); ask patient to swallow a sip of water — thyroid tissue rises on swallowing (distinguishing it from lymph nodes and lipomas).

Palpation: Stand behind the patient; use both hands to palpate the gland; feel both lobes and the isthmus; identify whether diffuse (Graves', Hashimoto's, simple goitre) or nodular (multinodular goitre, adenoma, carcinoma); note tenderness (thyroiditis); ask patient to swallow again — confirm movement on swallowing.

Percussion: Percuss over sternum for retrosternal goitre (dullness rather than expected resonance).

Auscultation: Listen over the goitre with the bell — a thyroid bruit indicates increased vascularity (Graves' disease; large goitre).

Peripheral Vascular Examination

Inspection: Skin changes (shiny, hairless, pale skin — arterial insufficiency); colour changes (pallor on elevation, rubor on dependency — Buerger's test); ulceration (arterial ulcers: punched-out, painful, necrotic, on pressure points and distally; venous ulcers: shallow, wet, sloughy, gaiter distribution above medial malleolus); gangrene; varicose veins; lipodermatosclerosis (brown pigmentation from haemosiderin deposition in chronic venous disease).

Palpation: Temperature (cooler peripherally — arterial insufficiency); capillary refill. Pulses: femoral (midpoint of inguinal ligament), popliteal (knee slightly flexed, two-handed technique, feel behind knee), posterior tibial (behind medial malleolus), dorsalis pedis (dorsum of foot, lateral to extensor hallucis longus). An absent pulse is a significant finding — document and report.

ABPI (Ankle Brachial Pressure Index): State you would measure the ABPI with a handheld Doppler — systolic ankle pressure divided by systolic brachial pressure. Normal: 0.9–1.3. >1.3 suggests calcified vessels (diabetes, renal failure). 0.5–0.9: mild-moderate peripheral arterial disease. <0.5: severe ischaemia. <0.3: critical ischaemia (rest pain, ulceration, gangrene).

Buerger's test: Elevate legs to 45 degrees — pallor of the foot suggests arterial insufficiency. Then lower to dependent position — reactive hyperaemia (bright red/purple foot) = Buerger's sign = critical ischaemia.

How to Present Your CPSA Findings

A structured verbal presentation at the end of each CPSA station is a key mark-scoring opportunity. Use this structure:

  1. State what you have done: "I have performed a cardiovascular examination on [patient's name]."
  2. Present positive findings in a logical order: "On examination, I found that the pulse was irregularly irregular at a rate of 80 beats per minute. The blood pressure was not measured but I noted no peripheral stigmata of heart disease. The JVP was elevated at 4 cm above the sternal angle. On precordial auscultation I heard an early diastolic murmur loudest at the left sternal edge, consistent with aortic regurgitation."
  3. Summarise your impression: "In summary, the findings are consistent with aortic regurgitation with evidence of volume overload."
  4. State investigations: "I would like to confirm this with an ECG, chest X-ray, and echocardiogram, and I would discuss these findings with my senior colleague."

CPSA Preparation Strategy

Six to eight months before the CPSA, establish a weekly practice routine. The minimum effective practice frequency is two to three full system examinations per week on real patients. OSCE preparation without real patient contact is substantially less effective — mark schemes test clinical technique that can only be refined with practice on actual human anatomy.

TimeframePriorityMethod
6+ months before Establish mark schemes for each examination; build initial competency Clinical placements — ask to examine patients daily; compare findings with seniors
3–6 months before Structured timed practice; peer feedback Weekly OSCE practice sessions with peers; time each station to 8 minutes; use mark schemes for feedback
1–3 months before Simulate exam conditions; identify and address specific weaknesses Mock OSCE circuits; video self-assessment; senior clinician feedback; targeted revision of weak stations
Final weeks Consolidate and maintain; manage examination anxiety Light daily practice; focus on opening sequences and presentations; rest adequately

Frequently Asked Questions: CPSA Clinical Examinations

How long do I have for each CPSA station?
Station length varies by institution, but typically five to eight minutes for a single-system examination, with a brief reading time (one to two minutes) before entry to read the station stem.

Are all findings in the CPSA real clinical findings or simulated?
Standardised patients in CPSA stations may have real clinical signs, simulated clinical signs (using make-up, props, or trained patients who can mimic examination findings), or no findings at all (a "normal" examination is equally valid and must be correctly identified as normal). Mannequins are used for procedural stations.

What if I cannot find a clinical sign that I believe should be present?
If you have performed a thorough, systematic examination and have not elicited a finding, present your negative finding confidently: "On examination of the respiratory system, I found no clinical signs — specifically, the chest expansion was equal bilaterally, percussion was resonant throughout, and breath sounds were vesicular with no added sounds." A false finding is worse than a correct negative finding.

Should I ask the patient about symptoms during a CPSA examination station?
Usually not — examination stations test examination technique. The station stem will specify whether you should take a history as well. Asking the patient "are you having any pain?" before you start examining is appropriate (part of the WIPING sequence); but launching into a full history in an examination station will cost you time and marks.

What is the global rating and how much does it affect the mark?
The global rating is a holistic assessment by the examiner of whether your overall performance was appropriate for a borderline foundation doctor at this station. In most CPSA marking schemes, the global rating contributes 20–40% of the total station mark. Candidates who perform well on the checklist but receive a low global rating have typically been technically correct but clinically robotic, unsafe, or poorly patient-centred.

Can I go back to a previous step if I forget it?
Yes — within reason. If you have omitted the JVP assessment and remember before you have completed the precordial examination, go back and check it. Most examiners will award the mark even if the sequence was not perfectly ordered. What you cannot do is go back to a step after you have already completed the station and moved to your presentation.

Conclusion: Clinical Examination in the CPSA Rewards Systematic Practice

The CPSA rewards candidates who have practised clinical examination systematically, deliberately, and repeatedly in real clinical environments. There is no substitute for examining actual patients. The mark schemes are learnable, the expected sequence is teachable, and the clinical signs are finite — but only real clinical experience builds the pattern recognition, clinical confidence, and patient-centred fluency that produces a high global rating from the examiner.

Start early, practise often, seek senior feedback, and approach every ward patient as a potential CPSA station. For more information on the UKMLA overall, see our What Is the UKMLA page, and for the AKT clinical knowledge component, see our UKMLA Preparation Guide.

Handling Unexpected Findings in CPSA Examination Stations

One of the most anxiety-provoking moments in a CPSA station is finding something unexpected — a murmur you did not expect, a cranial nerve deficit you are unsure how to characterise, or apparent clinical signs you have never seen before. The correct approach is systematic and calm.

Complete the examination before interpreting: Do not stop mid-examination to announce your interpretation. Complete the full sequence, then present. This is how real clinical examinations work, and CPSA mark schemes reward completion of the full examination even when you have identified a positive finding early.

Describe what you find, even if you cannot name it: If you find an irregular cardiac rhythm and cannot confidently name the arrhythmia, say "I noted an irregularly irregular pulse that I would investigate with an ECG." If you find an abdominal mass you cannot characterise fully, describe its location, size, surface (smooth or irregular), consistency, borders, and whether it moves on respiration. Accurate description is rewarded; an incorrect diagnosis is not penalised the same way as missing the finding entirely.

Never fabricate findings: If you genuinely cannot find a clinical sign that you believe is present, present a thorough negative finding. Inventing auscultatory findings or claiming to feel an organomegaly you cannot find will undermine your credibility and lose you marks. Examiners are looking for clinical honesty.

Physical Examination in the Context of UKMLA AKT Questions

Physical examination knowledge is not confined to the CPSA — it also appears in AKT questions. These questions typically take one of three forms:

Sign interpretation: "A 67-year-old man is found to have a pansystolic murmur radiating to the axilla. What is the most likely diagnosis?" The answer is mitral regurgitation. The AKT expects you to know the characteristic features of common heart murmurs, chest signs, abdominal findings, and neurological signs — not just how to examine for them, but what the findings mean clinically.

Examination technique decision: "Which physical examination finding would best differentiate cardiac failure from a pleural effusion in a patient with acute dyspnoea?" The answer requires knowledge of the distinctive clinical signs of each condition — the AKT is not testing your examination sequence but your understanding of which findings discriminate between diagnoses.

Investigation choice based on examination finding: "A 55-year-old woman is found to have a raised JVP, bibasal crackles, and pedal oedema. What is the most appropriate initial investigation?" The physical examination findings lead directly to the clinical reasoning and investigation choice. This integrates examination knowledge with clinical management decision-making — a common AKT structure.

The Role of Clinical Placements in CPSA Preparation

There is no substitute for clinical placement experience as preparation for the CPSA. Every patient you examine on a ward, in an outpatient clinic, or in a primary care setting is CPSA practice — provided you approach the examination systematically, verbalise your findings, and seek supervisor feedback.

Make specific requests of your clinical supervisors and registrars during placements:

  • "Please can you watch me examine this patient and give me feedback on my technique?"
  • "I found X on examination — is that accurate and what does it indicate?"
  • "I struggled with the cardiovascular auscultation on this patient — could we go through the murmur together?"
  • "Would you be willing to assess me on a structured history-taking station with feedback?"

Consultants and registrars who have been through UKMLA-era medical training will often be willing to provide structured CPSA-oriented feedback — particularly if you frame the request as: "I'm working towards my CPSA and would find it really helpful to get some clinical examination feedback." This framing is more likely to produce useful, specific feedback than a generic "can I get some feedback?"

Simulation as a CPSA Preparation Tool

Clinical simulation centres — available at most medical schools — offer opportunities to practise procedural skills (venepuncture, cannulation, catheterisation, basic life support) and clinical examination on standardised mannequins before the CPSA. For CPSA procedural stations, simulation is the primary preparation pathway: you cannot practise IV cannulation on a real patient for the first time in a licensing examination.

Use simulation sessions to practise not just the technical procedure but also the communication that accompanies it: introducing yourself, obtaining consent, explaining the procedure to the simulated patient (or talking aloud through the steps as if addressing a patient), and checking the patient's comfort throughout. CPSA procedural stations often include a communication component alongside the technical skill, and candidates who only practise the technical procedure in silence lose easy communication marks.

Common Technical Errors That Cost CPSA Marks

ErrorStationImpactHow to Avoid
Forgetting to wash hands at the start Any clinical station Loses professionalism marks; examiner notes immediately Make it the first thing you do, without exception, every practice session
Palpating for the liver starting at the wrong quadrant Abdominal examination Misses an enlarged liver; loses marks for technique Always start in right iliac fossa and move upward; practise this consistently
Not looking at the patient's face when performing an abdominal examination Abdominal examination Misses pain response; appears automated rather than patient-centred Make eye contact with the patient on deep palpation; ask "is this painful?"
Forgetting ankle clonus and plantar responses in a lower limb neurological examination Neurological (lower limb) Incomplete examination; loses specific marks Build a set sequence ending with reflexes and plantars; rehearse this sequence every time
Finishing without a verbal summary of findings Any clinical examination station Significant mark loss — presentation is a separately scored component Practise ending every examination with a structured verbal summary; use a template
Over-interpreting normal findings as abnormal Any clinical examination station Inaccurate clinical findings; reduces global rating Examine real patients regularly to calibrate your sense of normal; when uncertain, describe what you find rather than over-diagnose

Lymph Node Examination

Lymphadenopathy is a common presentation in the AKT (differential diagnosis of cervical lymphadenopathy, axillary lymphadenopathy in suspected breast disease, inguinal lymphadenopathy in lower limb and genital conditions) and is tested as a clinical examination skill in the CPSA.

The systematic approach to lymph node examination follows anatomical groups. For cervical lymphadenopathy — the most commonly tested region:

Inspect first: visible swellings, overlying skin changes (redness suggests infection; adherence to skin suggests malignancy). Palpate from behind the seated patient, using the flat of the fingers. The systematic cervical sequence: submental (under the chin), submandibular (under the mandible), pre-auricular (in front of the ear), post-auricular (behind the ear), occipital (posterior skull), anterior cervical chain (along the anterior border of sternocleidomastoid), posterior cervical chain (along the posterior border of sternocleidomastoid), supraclavicular (in the supraclavicular fossa — supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is particularly significant as it suggests intrathoracic or intra-abdominal malignancy; a hard, fixed left supraclavicular node is Virchow's node — associated with gastric carcinoma, also called Troisier's sign).

When palpating lymph nodes, characterise each by: size (in centimetres); consistency (soft = reactive lymphadenitis or viral infection; rubbery = lymphoma; hard/stony = malignancy); surface (smooth or irregular); tenderness (tender = infective; non-tender = malignancy or lymphoma); fixity (mobile = reactive; fixed/matted = malignancy or TB); overlying skin (warm and erythematous = infection; fluctuant = abscess; peau d'orange = malignancy with dermal lymphatic involvement).

Complete a lymph node examination by examining all lymph node groups relevant to the clinical presentation — not just the group the patient presents with. A patient with cervical lymphadenopathy requires examination of axillary nodes, epitrochlear nodes, and inguinal nodes to determine whether the lymphadenopathy is localised (suggests a local cause) or generalised (suggests a systemic cause such as lymphoma, leukaemia, viral illness such as EBV, or HIV).

Breast Examination

Breast examination is a CPSA station at many institutions and tests both examination technique and communication skills (obtaining consent for an intimate examination; maintaining patient dignity throughout).

Inspection: Patient seated, arms by sides — inspect for asymmetry, skin changes (dimpling, peau d'orange, erythema), nipple changes (inversion, discharge, eczematous change, retraction). Then ask patient to raise arms above head — accentuates tethering from underlying tumour. Then hands on hips pressing inward — contracts pectoralis major, accentuating medial tethering.

Palpation: Patient supine, arm raised on side being examined. Use the flat of the hand in a systematic pattern (radial or quadrant). Feel each quadrant and the axillary tail. If a lump is found, characterise it: site, size, shape, consistency, surface, edge, tenderness, tethering to skin or deep fascia, nipple involvement. Palpate the axillary, supraclavicular, and infraclavicular nodes for lymphadenopathy. Examine the contralateral breast equally.
