post_title: UKMLA Exam Fees for International Medical Graduates: 2026 Cost Guide
post_excerpt: A complete 2026 breakdown of UKMLA exam fees for IMGs — every charge from EPIC verification to GMC registration, plus the total cost of the pathway by country.
seo_title: UKMLA Exam Fees for IMG: 2026 Full Cost Guide
seo_description: UKMLA exam fees for IMG in 2026: AKT, CPSA, GMC registration, EPIC verification and total pathway cost for international medical graduates.
html_content:
UKMLA exam fees for IMG candidates rarely stop at the three headline charges most guides quote. The Applied Knowledge Test, the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment, and initial GMC registration together come to roughly £1,800, but that figure is only the starting point of what an international medical graduate actually spends on the road to a UK licence. By the time you add primary source verification, an English language test, flights and accommodation for a CPSA sitting in Manchester, a preparation course or question bank subscription, and the first annual retention fee, the realistic total for most IMGs lands well above £3,000 — and for candidates travelling long-haul with no local support network, it can exceed £4,500.

This guide is built specifically to answer the question most fee pages skip: what does the whole pathway cost, end to end, once every unavoidable expense is included? We walk through the core GMC charges briefly (with a link to the complete AKT, CPSA and registration fee breakdown for readers who only need the headline numbers), then go category by category through everything that happens before you even book the AKT, the real logistics cost of sitting the CPSA in Manchester, and what it costs to stay registered once you're through. A regional budget table gives illustrative totals for candidates travelling from South Asia, West Africa, the Middle East and Gulf, and Southeast Asia.

None of the figures below replace official guidance. Core GMC amounts are cited as such; everything else — flights, hotels, courses, question banks — is presented as an indicative planning range so you can build a realistic personal budget rather than being caught out by costs nobody mentioned until it was too late. You can always check the GMC's official fees for doctors page for the current core charges before relying on any figure quoted here or elsewhere.

Why does this distinction matter so much? Because most IMGs plan their finances around the number they first encounter — usually the £283 AKT fee or the combined £1,800 core total — and only discover the rest of the spending piecemeal, at the exact moments they can least afford a surprise: an EPIC invoice a few weeks before a planned AKT booking, a language test result that has quietly expired, or a Manchester hotel search two weeks before a CPSA date when prices have already climbed. Building the full picture upfront, even as a rough range rather than an exact figure, converts several unpredictable shocks into one manageable, plannable total.

UKMLA Exam Fees for IMG: The Core Charges

Before adding up the wider pathway, it helps to restate the three charges every IMG pays directly to the GMC. The AKT costs £283 per attempt, the CPSA costs £1,036 per attempt, and initial GMC registration costs £481, bringing the core total to approximately £1,800 for a candidate who passes both assessments first time. These are the same figures IMGs will have seen quoted elsewhere, and they are set out in full — including the tiered cancellation and refund policy, currency conversions, and historical fee changes — in the complete AKT, CPSA and registration fee breakdown, so we won't repeat that detail here.

What matters for this guide is that these three charges are the floor, not the ceiling, of what the pathway costs. They assume a candidate who is already eligible to book (meaning primary qualification verification and English language requirements are already sorted), who passes both exams on the first attempt, and who lives close enough to a Pearson VUE centre and to Manchester that travel is negligible. For the large majority of IMGs, none of those three assumptions hold, which is why the categories below matter just as much as the headline fees. If you do need to resit either exam, the resit rules and repeat costs if you don't pass first time cover the financial and procedural implications in detail. Before booking anything, it's also worth checking UKMLA eligibility requirements and UKMLA key dates and booking windows so your spending lines up with a workable timeline.

Costs Before You Even Book the AKT

A significant share of total IMG spending happens before a single GMC exam fee is paid. Primary source verification, English language testing, and — for some candidates — document translation are all prerequisites to booking the AKT, and skipping or underestimating them is one of the most common budgeting mistakes IMGs make.

Primary Source Verification and EPIC Fees

The GMC requires independent verification that your primary medical qualification is genuine before it will accept you onto the UKMLA pathway. For most IMGs this is done through the Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials (EPIC), the verification service operated by the Educational Commission for Foreign Medical Graduates (ECFMG) on behalf of medical regulators worldwide, including the GMC. Setting up an EPIC account and submitting your primary medical qualification for verification typically costs in the region of £100–£140, though the exact figure depends on your country of qualification and whether your medical school has previously used the system (schools already integrated into EPIC's verification network tend to process faster and sometimes more cheaply than first-time submissions).

Verification is not instantaneous. Depending on how quickly your medical school responds to EPIC's request to confirm your qualification, the process can take anywhere from a few weeks to several months. Candidates from medical schools with a track record of slow administrative response should budget both extra time and, in some cases, a follow-up fee if documents need to be resubmitted or a verification lapses and must be renewed. Because EPIC verification sits on the critical path to booking the AKT, delays here have a knock-on financial effect: a slipped exam booking window can mean losing a preferred test centre slot or having to wait for the next available cycle, both of which can add indirect cost through extended preparation time and, in some cases, additional question bank subscription months.

It is worth starting the EPIC process as early as possible relative to your intended AKT booking date, ideally as soon as you have decided to pursue the UK pathway rather than waiting until you are ready to sit the exam. Because EPIC verification does not expire quickly once completed, there is little downside to beginning it well in advance, and doing so removes one of the most common causes of avoidable delay — and avoidable cost — from the rest of your timeline. Candidates weighing up the UK route against other destinations may also want to check UKMLA eligibility requirements at this stage, since primary qualification recognition rules differ by country of training and can affect whether EPIC verification proceeds smoothly or requires additional supporting evidence.

English Language Test Fees

Every IMG must demonstrate English language proficiency to the GMC's required standard, and this is one of the largest "hidden" costs in the whole pathway simply because it's easy to treat as a formality rather than a genuine budget line. The two accepted tests are IELTS Academic and OET, and their costs differ meaningfully:

  • IELTS Academic costs approximately £180–£200 per sitting in most countries, with the GMC requiring an overall score of 7.5 and no individual component below 7.0.
  • OET (Occupational English Test) costs approximately £500 per sitting, with a minimum Grade B required across all four sub-tests (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking).

OET is considerably more expensive per attempt, but many clinicians find its healthcare-specific format easier to pass on the first try, which can make it cheaper overall despite the higher headline price — a single failed IELTS attempt followed by a pass adds £180–£200, narrowing or eliminating the gap. Both test results are valid for two years from the test date, so timing matters: sitting your language test too early relative to the rest of your pathway risks the result expiring before you reach registration, forcing a costly repeat sitting purely due to a timeline mismatch rather than any change in your ability.

Beyond the test fee itself, most candidates also spend on preparation — practice books, mock speaking sessions, or short preparation courses specific to IELTS or OET, typically in the range of £20–£150 depending on how much structured support is used. Test centre availability also varies by country and city; candidates in smaller cities may need to travel to a regional hub to sit either test, adding a modest but real travel cost on top of the test fee itself. Because both tests require an in-person sitting (OET Speaking can sometimes be taken at a different time from the other components, but Listening, Reading and Writing are typically sat together), it is not possible to reduce this cost through remote or online-only test formats in the way some other English proficiency tests allow.

Document Translation and Notarisation Costs

IMGs whose primary medical qualification, transcripts, or supporting documents were issued in a language other than English typically need certified translations before these documents will be accepted by EPIC or the GMC. Certified translation costs vary widely by country and document length but commonly fall in the range of £30–£80 per document, and a full application file often includes three to six documents requiring translation (degree certificate, transcript, internship completion certificate, good standing certificate, and any name-change or identity documents). Some jurisdictions also require notarisation or apostille certification of original documents before submission, adding a further £20–£60 per document depending on local notary fees. Candidates from countries where medical qualifications are issued bilingually, or where English-language transcripts are standard, can often avoid this cost entirely — it's worth checking with your medical school's registrar early, since a same-language original document is one of the easiest categories of cost to eliminate through advance planning.

The Real Cost of Sitting the CPSA in Manchester

Unlike the AKT, which can be sat at Pearson VUE centres in most IMG source countries, the CPSA is administered exclusively at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester. This single fact turns the CPSA from a £1,036 exam fee into what is, for most IMGs, a multi-thousand-pound international trip once flights, accommodation, local transport, meals, and preparation are factored in. This section breaks down each component with indicative ranges by region.

Flights to Manchester

Flight costs vary substantially depending on your departure region, how far in advance you book, and whether you can travel on flexible dates outside peak periods. As indicative return fares:

  • South Asia (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka): approximately £450–£850 return, depending on city of departure and season.
  • West Africa (Nigeria, Ghana): approximately £550–£950 return, with Lagos and Accra typically at the lower end when booked well ahead.
  • Middle East and Gulf (UAE, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Jordan): approximately £350–£650 return, generally the cheapest of the major IMG source regions due to strong connectivity and competition among Gulf carriers.
  • Southeast Asia (Philippines, Malaysia, Myanmar): approximately £600–£1,100 return, reflecting the greater distance and typically at least one connection.

These are illustrative ranges only, not live fares — actual prices swing with fuel surcharges, season, and how far ahead you book. Booking six to twelve weeks in advance and remaining flexible on exact travel dates typically secures meaningfully lower fares than booking within two to three weeks of a CPSA date, particularly for departures from South Asia and West Africa where demand from other CPSA candidates can push up prices around popular exam sitting dates.

Connecting flights add a further layer of cost variability worth planning around. Direct or one-stop routes from major Gulf and South Asian hub cities to Manchester are increasingly common, but candidates departing from smaller cities often need to first fly domestically or regionally to a major international hub before the long-haul leg, effectively splitting the journey into two separate fares. Where possible, checking connecting itineraries out of a nearby major hub — even if it means a short additional domestic flight or a longer ground journey to the departure airport — can sometimes beat the price of a single itinerary booked from a smaller regional airport, particularly for candidates in Southeast Asia and parts of West Africa where domestic and regional carrier competition is more limited.

Accommodation for a One-to-Two-Week Stay

Most IMGs do not fly in the day before their CPSA and fly out immediately after — the exam is demanding enough that most candidates build in several days to a couple of weeks locally beforehand to adjust to the time zone, complete a final round of practice, and reduce the risk of travel disruption affecting exam-day attendance. Accommodation options near the GMC Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester range widely:

  • Budget hotel or hostel: approximately £40–£70 per night.
  • Mid-range hotel or serviced apartment: approximately £70–£120 per night.
  • Shared candidate accommodation (rooms or flats split with other CPSA candidates, commonly arranged through preparation course cohorts or online candidate groups): approximately £25–£45 per person per night.

Over a one-week stay, this translates to roughly £280–£840 depending on accommodation type; over a two-week stay, roughly £560–£1,680. Sharing accommodation with other candidates preparing for the same sitting is consistently the single biggest lever IMGs have over this cost category, often cutting it by 40–50% compared with a solo mid-range hotel booking.

Local Transport and Meals

Local transport in Manchester — buses, trams, and occasional taxis to and from the Clinical Assessment Centre — typically adds £30–£60 for a one- to two-week stay if using public transport for most journeys. Meals, allowing for a mix of self-catering and eating out, typically run £15–£30 per day, or roughly £105–£420 across a one- to two-week visit. Candidates staying in serviced apartments or shared accommodation with kitchen access can bring this down considerably by cooking rather than relying on restaurants throughout the stay.

CPSA Preparation Courses

Many IMGs supplement self-study with a structured CPSA/OSCE preparation course, either in person in the UK or online. In-person, multi-day intensive courses typically cost £500–£1,500, often scheduled in the days immediately before the exam so candidates can combine travel for the course and the exam into a single trip — a sensible way to consolidate travel costs rather than making two separate journeys. Online preparation packages, including video content, mock stations, and feedback, typically cost £100–£400 and can be started well in advance of travel, reducing time pressure during the in-person stay. Preparation course spend is optional rather than mandatory, but for candidates without access to a UK-based clinical mentor or peer practice group, a structured course is often the most efficient way to close specific skill gaps before a £1,036 exam attempt.

Total Estimated Budget by Region

Bringing every category together, the table below sets out an illustrative total pathway cost by IMG source region. These figures combine the core GMC fees (assuming first-attempt passes), EPIC verification plus one English language test sitting, and CPSA-related travel and accommodation for a one- to two-week Manchester stay. They exclude optional preparation courses, question bank subscriptions, and any visa costs, which are covered separately below. All regional figures are indicative estimates for planning purposes only — not official GMC or third-party pricing — and individual circumstances will vary considerably.

RegionCore GMC FeesEPIC + English TestTravel/Accommodation for CPSAEstimated Total
South Asia (India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka)£1,800£280–£340£730–£1,690£2,810–£3,830
West Africa (Nigeria, Ghana)£1,800£280–£340£830–£1,790£2,910–£3,930
Middle East and Gulf (UAE, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Jordan)£1,800£280–£340£630–£1,490£2,710–£3,630
Southeast Asia (Philippines, Malaysia, Myanmar)£1,800£280–£340£880–£1,940£2,960–£4,080

Every region clusters in a broadly similar £2,700–£4,100 range for a first-attempt pathway, with the spread driven mainly by flight distance and accommodation choices rather than by fundamental differences in the process itself. Adding a preparation course (£100–£1,500), a comprehensive question bank subscription (£40–£200), or a single resit of either exam (£283 for AKT, £1,036 for CPSA) pushes the realistic upper end of most candidates' total spend towards £4,500–£5,500. Candidates from Southeast Asia and West Africa, who tend to face the highest flight costs, benefit most from early booking and shared accommodation strategies, since travel and lodging make up a proportionally larger share of their total budget than for candidates travelling from the Gulf.

It is also worth noting what this table deliberately excludes, since candidates sometimes mistake a regional estimate for a complete personal budget. None of the figures above include a CPSA preparation course, a question bank subscription used during AKT preparation, visa costs where applicable, document translation or notarisation, or any resit fees. They also assume a single one- to two-week Manchester visit covering only the CPSA itself, rather than a longer stay for in-person preparation or informal clinical exposure beforehand. Candidates who plan for any of these additional elements should treat the table as a floor for their region rather than a ceiling, and add the relevant category from the sections above onto their personal total.

Currency movement is a further factor the table cannot fully capture. Because all four regions convert from a home currency into pounds sterling to make GMC and UK-based payments, a shift in the exchange rate between the time a candidate starts saving and the time they actually pay each fee can meaningfully change the local-currency cost of the pathway, even though the pound figure stays fixed. Candidates with a long runway between deciding to pursue the UK route and actually booking exams may want to monitor exchange rate trends and, where practical, convert and hold funds in pounds sterling ahead of major payment dates to avoid a late, unfavourable conversion at the point of booking.

Ongoing Costs After Registration: The Annual Retention Fee

Passing the CPSA and being granted registration is not the end of GMC-related costs. Every doctor on the UK medical register, whether trained in the UK or abroad, pays an annual retention fee of £431 to remain registered and licensed to practise. This fee is billed each January and funds the GMC's ongoing regulatory functions: maintaining the medical register, running revalidation, investigating fitness-to-practise concerns, and operating the standard-setting and quality assurance processes that underpin the MLA framework itself.

The retention fee is typically collected by direct debit, set up when you first register, and is a legal requirement to remain on the register — non-payment can result in removal from the register and loss of your licence to practise, so it is not a fee that can be deferred or skipped. Newly registered doctors in their first partial year sometimes pay a pro-rated amount depending on when in the registration cycle they joined, with the full £431 applying from the following January onward. Over the course of a career, this is a substantial cumulative cost: at the current rate, thirty years of registration totals roughly £12,930, and forty years roughly £17,240, though the GMC reviews and periodically adjusts this fee, so actual lifetime totals will differ. This is worth factoring into long-term financial planning even though it falls well outside the upfront pathway budget most candidates focus on.

For newly arrived IMGs beginning work in the NHS, the retention fee is worth budgeting for separately from the upfront pathway costs covered elsewhere in this guide, since it is easy to treat registration as the finish line and forget that a recurring annual charge follows immediately behind it. Doctors who move between different types of registration status, take a career break, or temporarily leave the register and later reapply should also be aware that reinstatement can carry its own fee, separate from the standard annual retention charge, so maintaining continuous registration where possible is usually the more cost-effective path if a temporary pause is not strictly necessary. Completing the GMC registration process correctly the first time, with the retention fee direct debit set up promptly, avoids the administrative complications — and potential late fees — that can arise from a missed or failed payment.

How to Budget and Reduce Your Total IMG Pathway Cost

Given that total pathway costs commonly reach £3,000–£4,500 or more, deliberate cost management makes a real difference, particularly for candidates converting from currencies where the pound has significant purchasing power. The following strategies address the largest cost levers identified throughout this guide.

Pass on the first attempt wherever possible. This is the single highest-leverage cost-saving strategy available. A failed AKT adds £283 to re-sit; a failed CPSA adds £1,036, plus a second round of flights and accommodation to Manchester that can easily exceed £1,000 on its own. A candidate who needs a second CPSA attempt is not just paying £1,036 twice — they are effectively duplicating their entire travel budget. Thorough preparation, realistic mock assessments, and honest self-assessment of readiness before booking are worth far more than their time cost in avoided resit expenses. For full detail on how resits work procedurally and what they cost, see the resit rules and repeat costs if you don't pass first time.

Book CPSA travel early. Flights and accommodation both get more expensive, and availability tighter, the closer you get to a popular exam sitting date. Booking flights six to twelve weeks ahead and accommodation as soon as your CPSA date is confirmed typically saves 20–40% compared with booking in the final two to three weeks before travel.

Share accommodation where possible. Splitting a room or flat with one or two other CPSA candidates — often arranged through preparation course cohorts, university alumni networks, or online IMG candidate communities — is consistently the most effective way to cut the accommodation line of the budget, frequently by close to half compared with a solo hotel stay.

Time your English test validity against the rest of your timeline. Both IELTS and OET results are valid for two years. Sitting your language test too far ahead of your planned AKT and CPSA dates risks the result lapsing before you reach registration, which means paying for and sitting the test again purely due to poor sequencing rather than any change in ability. Map out your expected timeline across UKMLA key dates and booking windows before committing to a language test date, so the two-year window comfortably covers your realistic path through to registration.

Budget deliberately for question banks and mock tests rather than over-subscribing. Many candidates subscribe to multiple overlapping AKT question banks out of anxiety rather than genuine need, which can add hundreds of pounds without proportionate benefit. A focused approach — one comprehensive question bank used systematically through the full MLA content map, supplemented by timed mock papers closer to the exam date — is usually both cheaper and more effective than subscribing broadly. For a fuller look at what's worth paying for, see the guidance on budgeting for question banks and mock tests.

Factor in visa costs if relevant to your travel. Depending on your nationality, a short-term visit visa may be required to travel to the UK for the CPSA. Visa application fees, biometric appointment costs, and any associated travel to a visa application centre in your home country should be added to your personal budget where applicable, as these are separate from and additional to any GMC or exam-related charge.

Financial Support and Concessions for IMGs

The GMC operates one formal fee concession scheme relevant to IMGs: the refugee doctor assistance programme. Under this scheme, doctors with recognised refugee status, or a pending asylum application accepted by the relevant national authority, can sit their first two AKT (PLAB 1) attempts free of charge, and their first two CPSA (PLAB 2) attempts at 50% of the standard fee, meaning roughly £518 per sitting rather than £1,036. To access the concession, evidence of refugee or asylum-seeker status must be submitted to and accepted by the GMC before the discounted booking is applied — it is not retrospective, so candidates who may qualify should establish their eligibility before booking rather than after paying full price.

Outside this specific scheme, there is currently no general income-based hardship fund available to IMGs. Candidates from lower-income countries, where the pound cost of the pathway represents a proportionally much larger sum relative to local earnings, do not have access to a sliding-scale fee structure or GMC-administered bursary. In practice, most IMGs fund the pathway through personal savings, family contribution, or, in some cases, informal interest-free loans arranged through diaspora professional networks and alumni associations. Some professional bodies and diaspora medical associations in source countries offer small grants or loan schemes specifically for licensing exam costs, though availability and terms vary significantly and should be verified directly with the relevant organisation rather than assumed. As with the core exam fees, it's worth reiterating that no agent or intermediary can legitimately facilitate or discount GMC payments — any service claiming to arrange reduced-fee GMC bookings, expedited registration for a fee, or "guaranteed" concessionary rates outside the official refugee doctor scheme should be treated as a red flag, and all fee payments should be made directly through your own GMC Online account.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does the full UKMLA pathway cost for an IMG?

Including the core GMC fees, primary source verification, an English language test, and CPSA-related travel and accommodation, most IMGs should budget between £2,700 and £4,100 for a first-attempt pathway, depending on their departure region. Adding a preparation course, question bank subscriptions, or any resit pushes many candidates' realistic total towards £4,500–£5,500. These are planning estimates, not fixed costs, and individual circumstances vary considerably.

Are there fee discounts for IMGs from low-income countries?

No general discount exists based on country of origin or income level. The only formal GMC concession is the refugee doctor assistance scheme, which offers free AKT attempts and half-price CPSA attempts to doctors with recognised refugee or pending asylum status. IMGs from lower-income countries who do not qualify for this scheme pay the same standard fees as all other IMGs.

What is the cost of UKMLA exam for IMG 2026 compared with previous years?

Core fees have risen over the past decade, with PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 fees (now the AKT and CPSA) increasing from roughly £225 and £665 respectively in 2015 to £283 and £1,036 in 2026. Full detail on year-by-year changes is available via how GMC fees have changed over time. The GMC reviews its fee schedule periodically; always verify current charges on the GMC website before booking.

Do EPIC verification and English test fees count towards the "official" UKMLA fee?

No. EPIC/primary source verification and English language test fees (IELTS or OET) are paid to separate organisations, not to the GMC, and are not part of the official AKT/CPSA/registration fee schedule. They are, however, mandatory prerequisites for most IMGs and should be included in any realistic total budget, which is why this guide treats them as core pathway costs even though they don't appear on the GMC's own fee page.

Is it cheaper to prepare for the CPSA in my home country or in the UK?

Preparing primarily in your home country using online resources and question banks, then travelling to the UK only for a short, focused period immediately before the CPSA, is generally the cheaper approach, since it minimises UK accommodation and living costs. Some candidates choose a longer UK stay to attend an in-person preparation course or gain informal clinical exposure, which raises costs but may improve first-attempt pass chances — a trade-off worth weighing against the roughly £1,036 cost of a resit.

How does the UKMLA pathway cost compare to alternatives like the USMLE or staying in India for NEET PG?

The UKMLA pathway is generally less expensive upfront than the USMLE route, where total US exam fees alone exceed $3,500 before travel — see how these costs compare to the US's USMLE route for a full breakdown. For Indian doctors specifically weighing UK registration against pursuing postgraduate training domestically, how this compares to staying in India for NEET PG covers the cost and career trade-offs in detail.

Do visa costs need to be added to the IMG pathway budget?

If your nationality requires a visa to enter the UK for the CPSA, yes — visa application fees, biometric appointment costs, and any related travel to a visa centre are additional to every figure in this guide and should be researched based on your specific nationality and visa category well before your planned travel date.

Where can I find country-specific guidance on the full route to UK registration?

Detailed, country-specific route guides are available for several major IMG source countries, including the full route guide for doctors from India, the equivalent route guide for Pakistani doctors, and the equivalent route guide for Nigerian doctors. Each covers eligibility nuances, typical timelines, and country-specific practical considerations alongside the cost information in this guide.

Can an agent or consultancy reduce my GMC fees or speed up payment processing?

No. GMC fees are fixed, non-negotiable, and payable only through your own GMC Online account using a valid debit or credit card. No agent, consultancy, or third-party service can legitimately reduce these charges, secure a discount unavailable to other candidates, or speed up GMC processing for a fee. Any service claiming otherwise should be treated with caution, and all payments should be made directly by you, not routed through an intermediary.

Should I budget separately for a first attempt versus a "worst case" pathway with resits?

It is sensible to plan around two figures rather than one: a first-attempt budget using the regional totals in this guide, and a contingency figure that adds one AKT resit (£283) and one CPSA resit (£1,036 plus a second Manchester trip) in case either exam is not passed first time. Building this contingency into your savings target from the outset, rather than treating a resit as an unplanned emergency, makes the financial impact of a resit far easier to absorb if it happens.

Conclusion

The true cost of the IMG pathway to UK registration is rarely the £1,800 headline figure most candidates start with — by the time verification, English testing, and a CPSA trip to Manchester are added, a realistic budget sits closer to £2,700–£4,500, before ongoing annual retention costs even begin. Planning against the full picture, rather than the core GMC fees alone, is what separates candidates who reach registration on schedule from those caught short partway through. For the complete official charges and payment process, see the full UKMLA fee schedule →

post_title: UKMLA for Bangladeshi Doctors: Eligibility, Fees and the Route to GMC Registration
post_excerpt: A complete route guide for Bangladeshi medical graduates covering UKMLA eligibility, BM&DC-to-GMC recognition, exam costs in taka, and GMC registration.
seo_title: UKMLA for Bangladeshi Doctors: Route & Fees
seo_description: UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors: BM&DC-to-GMC recognition, AKT and CPSA costs in taka, English testing, and the full 2026 GMC registration route.
html_content:
UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors describes a well-defined, increasingly popular pathway to UK medical registration for graduates of Bangladeshi medical schools, and it follows the same core structure the GMC applies to every international medical graduate: degree recognition, credential verification, English language testing, two structured examinations, and a formal registration application. None of these steps is conceptually difficult on its own, but together they involve enough administrative detail, cost, and waiting time that many first-time applicants from Bangladesh underestimate how long the full journey actually takes.

This guide walks through the entire UKMLA pathway specifically as it applies to doctors trained in Bangladesh — from confirming that an MBBS degree from a medical college recognised by the Bangladesh Medical and Dental Council (BM&DC) satisfies GMC eligibility rules, through primary source verification, booking exams, and budgeting in taka for a pathway priced in pounds sterling. It also covers where Bangladeshi candidates can sit the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), why the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) must be taken in Manchester, and the realistic timeline and common mistakes that shape how quickly a Bangladeshi candidate actually reaches registration.

Bangladesh is one of the fastest-growing source countries for internationally trained doctors joining the UK medical register, and interest in the route has grown considerably in recent years as more Bangladeshi graduates look beyond traditional destinations. Consequently, the volume of practical, first-hand experience with the UKMLA process is still smaller than for more established diaspora routes, which makes a clear, accurate guide to eligibility, cost, and sequencing especially valuable for Bangladeshi applicants planning their journey.

The UKMLA Route From Bangladesh: A Quick Overview

Before examining each stage in detail, it helps to see the whole pathway from BM&DC registration to NHS practice at a glance. UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors follows a defined seven-stage route, and the sequence for a Bangladeshi-trained doctor generally runs as follows:

  1. Confirm eligibility: verify that your MBBS degree from a BM&DC-recognised Bangladeshi medical college is accepted by the GMC, and that you hold full, unrestricted BM&DC registration following your compulsory internship year.
  2. Create a GMC Online account and begin primary source verification of your medical qualification through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials).
  3. Sit and pass an approved English language test — IELTS Academic or OET Medicine — unless you hold an accepted exemption.
  4. Book and pass the UKMLA AKT (the PLAB 1 equivalent for IMGs), sat at a Pearson VUE test centre.
  5. Book and pass the UKMLA CPSA (the PLAB 2 equivalent), which can only be sat in person at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester.
  6. Apply for GMC registration with a licence to practise once both assessments are passed and all supporting documents are verified.
  7. Apply for NHS posts — typically clinical fellow roles, trust-grade positions, or Foundation Year 2-equivalent posts — and begin practising in the UK.

Each stage carries its own processing time, cost, and typical delay, all covered in the sections below. Understanding the full sequence early prevents the single most common planning mistake made by Bangladeshi candidates: assuming that passing the two exams is the hardest part, when in practice credential verification and document logistics frequently take longer than exam preparation itself. Many candidates who are used to the relatively centralised, self-contained structure of Bangladeshi postgraduate processes are surprised by how much of the UKMLA pathway depends on external parties responding promptly — medical college registrars, the BM&DC head office in Dhaka, and GMC caseworkers — rather than on their own study effort alone. Treating the administrative track and the exam preparation track as two separate projects, run in parallel rather than one after the other, is therefore the single biggest determinant of how quickly a Bangladeshi candidate reaches registration.

BM&DC Recognition and UKMLA Eligibility for Bangladeshi Doctors

The starting point for any Bangladeshi doctor exploring the UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors pathway is confirming basic eligibility. The GMC requires that your primary medical qualification comes from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools and that your degree is recognised by the relevant national regulator — in Bangladesh's case, the Bangladesh Medical and Dental Council (BM&DC). Graduates of BM&DC-accredited institutions such as Dhaka Medical College, Chittagong Medical College, and Sir Salimullah Medical College generally satisfy this first eligibility hurdle without difficulty, provided the college was properly accredited at the time of graduation.

Beyond the medical college itself, the GMC also expects evidence that you completed the mandatory one-year internship required by the BM&DC and that you hold full, unrestricted BM&DC registration rather than provisional or temporary registration. A Certificate of Good Standing confirming your registration status and disciplinary history is typically required as part of your application, and this must be requested directly from the BM&DC in Dhaka — a step many candidates leave too late, since the council can take several weeks to process such requests, particularly outside standard office hours in the working week.

Full detail of who is permitted to sit the exam, including specific rules for degrees awarded outside standard timeframes or through non-standard routes, is set out in the site's guide to UKMLA eligibility requirements. It is worth reading this in full before committing time and money to the process, since eligibility is assessed on a case-by-case basis for some qualification types. Comparing the Bangladeshi route against a broadly similar South Asian pathway is also useful context here — the requirements facing Sri Lankan doctors pursuing the same route follow an almost identical structure, even though the specific verification body and typical timelines differ.

Primary Source Verification via EPIC

Once basic eligibility is confirmed, the GMC requires primary source verification of your Bangladeshi medical degree, meaning your qualification is confirmed directly with your medical college rather than accepted on the strength of a certificate alone. This is handled through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials), the platform used globally for this purpose. Bangladeshi medical colleges vary considerably in how quickly they respond to EPIC verification requests; well-established government colleges with dedicated academic affairs offices tend to respond within four to eight weeks, whereas others can take three months or longer, particularly during examination periods or national holidays.

Full detail on how primary source verification works, what documents are required, and how the GMC uses the resulting evidence is available directly from the regulator: see the GMC's primary source verification requirements for international medical graduates. Although the GMC's own worked examples on that page tend to reference Indian institutions, the underlying EPIC process is identical for every IMG source country, including Bangladesh; the detailed walk-through in the site's guide to GMC registration and EPIC verification is written around an Indian example but applies in exactly the same way to a Bangladeshi applicant's own EPIC submission. Candidates are strongly advised to read both resources before initiating their EPIC application, since incomplete submissions are one of the most common causes of delay.

What Counts as BM&DC Recognition in Practice

It is worth being precise about what "BM&DC-recognised" actually means for GMC purposes, because the two regulators assess slightly different things. BM&DC registration confirms that you are entitled to practise medicine within the Bangladeshi health system; on its own, it does not transfer any rights or recognition in the UK. The GMC instead looks at three separate elements: whether your medical college appears on the World Directory of Medical Schools for the relevant graduation year, whether the degree programme met minimum duration and curriculum standards, and whether you can produce primary evidence — degree certificate, full transcripts, and internship completion documentation — that stands up to independent verification. A doctor can hold entirely valid BM&DC registration and still face delays at the GMC stage if, for example, a college's WDOMS listing lapsed for a period, or academic transcripts are incomplete. Checking your medical college's current WDOMS status before you begin is a five-minute task that can save weeks of confusion later. It is also worth noting that Bangladeshi qualifications are sometimes confused with degrees from elsewhere in South Asia during preliminary desk checks, so applicants should be precise and consistent about naming their exact college and graduation year on every form submitted.

Documents Bangladeshi Candidates Should Gather Early

Beyond the primary degree certificate itself, most Bangladeshi applicants need a consistent set of supporting documents, and gathering them in parallel rather than sequentially saves considerable time:

  • MBBS degree certificate and full academic transcripts, year by year, from your medical college.
  • Internship completion certificate confirming you completed the mandatory one-year BM&DC-supervised internship.
  • BM&DC Certificate of Good Standing, confirming your registration status and disciplinary record, requested directly from the BM&DC head office in Dhaka.
  • International passport, valid well beyond your expected registration date.
  • Evidence of any name changes — marriage certificates or statutory declarations — where your degree name and passport name differ, which is a common source of query for Bangladeshi applicants whose documents record names in slightly varying transliterations.
  • Certified translations of any document not already issued in English, from a recognised professional translation service.

Requesting the BM&DC Certificate of Good Standing early is particularly important, since this document is issued by a separate national body from your medical college and follows its own, often slower, administrative timeline. Building in a buffer of several extra weeks for this single document alone is a sensible default for most Bangladeshi applicants.

How to Apply for the UKMLA From Bangladesh: Step by Step

With eligibility confirmed, the practical application process behind UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors follows six sequential steps. Each is described below in the order it should typically be actioned.

Step 1: Create Your GMC Online Account

Registration begins with creating a GMC Online account at the GMC's official website. This account becomes your central hub for the entire process — you will use it to track your EPIC verification status, book both the AKT and CPSA, submit your English language evidence, and eventually complete your registration application. Use a personal email address you will retain long-term, not a college or hospital email that may be deactivated after you leave a post, since GMC Online correspondence can span well over a year for most Bangladeshi candidates.

Step 2: Begin EPIC Verification Early

As covered above, EPIC verification of your MBBS degree is usually the single longest step in the entire pathway. Start this as early as possible — ideally before you have finalised your English test date or exam bookings — because it runs in parallel with other preparation and does not require your active involvement once submitted, beyond following up with your medical college if there are delays. Keep a written record of every communication with your college's registrar, since this becomes useful evidence if you need to escalate a delayed response.

Step 3: Gather and Submit English Language Evidence

In parallel with EPIC verification, sit your English language test (IELTS Academic or OET Medicine — full detail in the dedicated section below) and upload your certificate to GMC Online once results are issued. Many Bangladeshi candidates assume that strong academic English from school and college reduces the testing requirement; in practice, formal evidence is still required in almost all cases, so it is worth booking a test slot early rather than waiting to find out whether an exemption applies.

Step 4: Book and Prepare for the AKT

Once your GMC Online account is active, you can book the AKT through the Pearson VUE booking system linked from your account. Booking windows typically open several months ahead of each testing period, and popular dates fill quickly, so book as soon as a suitable slot appears rather than waiting until the last moment. Preparation should draw on the official MLA content map, which defines exactly what conditions, presentations, and professional knowledge areas are examinable, alongside a structured question bank — see the guidance on choosing a good AKT question bank for a comparison of the main preparation options available to Bangladeshi candidates.

Step 5: Book and Prepare for the CPSA

The CPSA can only be booked once you have passed the AKT. Because the CPSA is only available in Manchester, Bangladeshi candidates need to plan a UK trip around this stage, factoring in visa arrangements, flights, and several days of accommodation for preparation immediately before the assessment. Many candidates combine the trip with a short period of UK clinical observation or shadowing beforehand, which helps with the specific communication and consultation style the CPSA examines.

Step 6: Submit Your GMC Registration Application

Once both the AKT and CPSA are passed, and your EPIC verification, English language evidence, and any outstanding documents (Certificate of Good Standing, internship completion certificate, identity documents) are all confirmed, you can submit your full application for GMC registration with a licence to practise. Full step-by-step detail on this final stage is available in the site's GMC registration process guide, which covers document checklists, processing times, and what to do if your application is queried.

UKMLA AKT Test Centres for Bangladeshi Candidates

One of the most practical advantages of UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors, compared with some other overseas licensing routes, is that the AKT can often be sat regionally rather than requiring a long-haul trip purely for the exam. The AKT is delivered through the Pearson VUE global network of test centres, and candidates should check current centre locations and availability directly through their GMC Online account or the Pearson VUE booking portal, since options in and around South Asia change between sitting windows. For a full walk-through of how to find, compare, and book a suitable centre, see the site's dedicated AKT test centres guide, which explains the booking workflow and what to check before confirming a date.

This is a significant contrast with the CPSA. Unlike the AKT, the CPSA cannot be sat anywhere in Bangladesh, or indeed anywhere outside the UK — it is held exclusively at the GMC's purpose-built Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional additional London dates during periods of high demand. This means every Bangladeshi candidate must, at minimum, make one trip to the UK to complete the UKMLA pathway, even if the AKT is passed entirely without leaving South Asia. Budgeting for this single mandatory UK trip — flights, a UK visitor visa, accommodation, and living costs for the preparation and exam period — should be built into financial planning from the very start of the process, not left until after the AKT is passed.

UKMLA Exam Cost in Bangladesh: Fees in Taka

Cost is consistently one of the biggest practical concerns for Bangladeshi doctors evaluating UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors as a realistic career pathway, both because the fees themselves are substantial in absolute terms and because the taka has shown notable volatility against the pound in recent years. The table below sets out the core GMC and Pearson VUE fees in pounds sterling alongside an approximate taka equivalent, so Bangladeshi candidates can budget realistically. For the full IMG fee breakdown across every national route, including re-sit charges, see the dedicated guide to UKMLA fees for IMGs.

Fee ItemAmount (GBP)Approximate Amount (BDT)When Payable
AKT / PLAB 1 exam fee£283~৳44,000At booking
CPSA / PLAB 2 exam fee£1,036~৳161,000At booking
GMC initial registration fee£481~৳75,000On application, after both exams passed
Annual retention fee£431~৳67,000Annually in January, once registered
Total core fees (first attempt)£1,800~৳280,000Across the full pathway to registration

These taka figures are indicative, calculated at a broadly representative and approximate exchange rate of roughly £1 = ৳155; they will shift with market movements between the time this guide is written and the time you actually make a payment. Exchange rates for the taka have fluctuated over recent years, and a gap of even a few months between budgeting and paying can materially change the taka cost of a given fee. The GMC reviews its fee schedule periodically; always verify current rules and current fee amounts on the official GMC website before applying, and check live exchange rates at the time you actually transfer funds rather than relying on figures calculated months in advance.

Beyond these core fees, Bangladeshi candidates should budget separately for EPIC verification charges, English language test fees, flights and accommodation for the mandatory Manchester CPSA trip, UK visitor visa costs, and study materials. Together these additional costs commonly add £1,500–£3,000 on top of the core GMC fees, so a realistic total budget for a first-attempt pass across the whole pathway is closer to £3,300–£4,800 once every cost is included.

Because so much of this budget is denominated in pounds while income and savings are typically held in taka, many Bangladeshi candidates choose to build their fund gradually over several months rather than attempting a single large currency purchase close to a payment deadline. Spreading currency purchases over time, using a specialist international transfer provider rather than a standard bank counter rate, and tracking the mid-market rate rather than relying on a single money-changer quotation are all practical ways to reduce the effective cost of converting taka to pounds across the pathway. Some candidates also time larger purchases around periods of relative taka stability rather than during periods of sharp depreciation, though this requires ongoing attention to the foreign exchange market rather than a one-off decision.

English Language Requirements for Bangladeshi Doctors

English language evidence is one of the most consistently underestimated requirements within UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors applications. Medical education in Bangladesh is delivered substantially in English from the pre-clinical years onward, which leads many Bangladeshi doctors to assume that formal English testing will not apply to them. In practice, the GMC requires documentary evidence of English language proficiency from the great majority of international medical graduates regardless of the language of their medical training, and Bangladeshi degrees are not automatically exempt. Candidates should plan for formal testing as a standard part of the pathway rather than hoping for a waiver.

Two tests are accepted: IELTS Academic, where the GMC requires an overall score of 7.0, with no individual component (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking) below 7.0, and OET (Occupational English Test), Medicine version, where the GMC requires a minimum of Grade B in all four sub-tests. OET is often more intuitive for practising doctors because its scenarios are built entirely around healthcare communication — referral letters, doctor-patient consultations, and clinical case notes — rather than general academic topics. IELTS Academic, by contrast, draws on a broader range of subject matter and has more widely available preparation material and test centres across Bangladesh.

Both test certificates have a defined validity period from the date of the test, so timing matters: sitting the test too early relative to your overall pathway risks the certificate expiring before you submit your registration application, which would require a costly and time-consuming re-sit. Full detail on accepted scores, accepted test providers, and how evidence is submitted is available directly from the regulator. Although the site's own detailed walk-through of the English language requirement, the English language requirement for the UKMLA, is written with an Indian example, the underlying scoring bands, test formats, and submission process are identical for a Bangladeshi applicant. It is worth reading both this guide and the official GMC page in full, since the specific evidentiary requirements are updated from time to time and always take precedence over general summaries.

Life and Career for Bangladeshi Doctors in the NHS After Registration

Completing UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors is only the midpoint of the journey, not the destination. Once GMC registration is secured, Bangladeshi doctors join a growing and increasingly visible community within the NHS. Although the diaspora is not yet as large as those from India or Nigeria, Bangladesh has climbed steadily up the list of source countries for internationally trained doctors on the UK medical register, and Bangladeshi doctor associations and informal peer networks are now active in most major NHS regions, particularly in areas with established Bangladeshi communities such as East London, Birmingham, and parts of the North West.

Career progression after registration generally follows one of several routes: a trust-grade or clinical fellow post (the most common entry point for newly registered IMGs, offering FY2-equivalent clinical responsibility without requiring a Foundation Programme place), a Specialty Training application once sufficient UK experience and portfolio evidence are in place, or locum work through NHS staff banks and agencies for those who want flexibility while building their UK clinical track record. Many Bangladeshi doctors who enter through clinical fellow posts subsequently progress into core or specialty training within two to three years, particularly in specialties experiencing significant workforce shortages such as general internal medicine, psychiatry, and emergency medicine, where competition ratios for IMG applicants tend to be more favourable.

It is worth noting that the broad shape of this journey — degree recognition, credential verification, English testing, a knowledge exam, a clinical skills exam, then registration — mirrors the equivalent routes for doctors from other major South Asian source countries. The pathway followed by Pakistani doctors pursuing UK registration uses an almost identical sequence, and the much larger and longer-established route followed by doctors from India shares the same structural skeleton, even though the specific verification bodies, typical processing times, and diaspora scale differ meaningfully between all three countries.

Day-to-day life for newly registered Bangladeshi doctors in the NHS typically involves an adjustment period covering both clinical and cultural dimensions. Clinically, the biggest early adjustments tend to be around NHS documentation standards (structured handover formats such as SBAR), a strong institutional emphasis on multidisciplinary team working, and a more codified approach to consent and capacity than many candidates are accustomed to from Bangladeshi practice. Socially, Bangladeshi doctors moving to the UK benefit from established Bangladeshi community networks, particularly in London and the South East, alongside a growing number of dedicated professional associations offering mentorship to newly arrived doctors. Salary progression follows the standard NHS pay scale for the relevant grade, and while it can take time to reach specialty training, most Bangladeshi doctors who remain committed to the pathway report that the structured nature of UK training — clear competency frameworks, protected training time, and defined progression points — compares favourably with less standardised systems elsewhere.

Timeline: From Graduation in Bangladesh to NHS Registration

Bangladeshi candidates consistently underestimate how long the full UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors journey takes, largely because exam preparation time is easy to plan for while administrative processing time is not. The table below sets out a realistic stage-by-stage timeline based on typical current processing periods.

StageTypical DurationKey Variable
BM&DC Certificate of Good Standing request2 to 8 weeksBM&DC processing backlog
EPIC primary source verification4 weeks to 4 monthsSpeed of your medical college's response
English language test preparation and sitting1 to 4 monthsBaseline English proficiency
AKT preparation3 to 6 monthsStudy intensity, prior NHS knowledge
AKT booking to sitting4 to 12 weeksTest centre date availability
CPSA preparation, including UK travel planning2 to 4 monthsVisa processing, budget readiness
CPSA booking to sitting (Manchester)2 to 6 monthsLimited sitting capacity
GMC registration application processing4 to 8 weeksDocument completeness
NHS job search and start date1 to 3 monthsNHS recruitment cycles, specialty demand

Adding these stages together, most Bangladeshi candidates should plan for a realistic total timeline of 18 to 30 months from beginning EPIC verification to starting their first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. Candidates who fail either assessment on their first attempt, or who face delays at the BM&DC or EPIC stage, should expect this to extend further. Planning around the longer end of this range from the outset avoids the financial and psychological strain of a timeline that assumes everything goes smoothly.

Common Mistakes Bangladeshi Doctors Make on This Route

Several avoidable errors recur consistently among Bangladeshi candidates pursuing UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors as their route to GMC registration. Most of these mistakes are not clinical — they are planning and administrative errors that have nothing to do with medical knowledge, yet they are responsible for a disproportionate share of the delays and extra costs Bangladeshi candidates experience. Being aware of them in advance, before committing money to a specific exam date or travel booking, can save months of delay and hundreds of pounds in wasted fees.

  • Starting EPIC verification too late. Because EPIC processing time depends heavily on your medical college's administrative speed rather than your own effort, this step should be initiated before — not after — you begin AKT preparation. Candidates who wait until they are ready to book the AKT before starting EPIC often lose two to three months they could have used productively.
  • Underestimating the true cost of the Manchester CPSA trip. The £1,036 exam fee itself is only part of the cost. Flights from Dhaka or Chittagong, several nights of Manchester accommodation, local transport, food, and — for many candidates — a short UK preparation course before the exam, can together add £1,000–£2,000 on top of the fee. Candidates who budget only for the headline fee frequently find themselves scrambling for additional funds close to their exam date.
  • Not accounting for taka exchange rate volatility. Because all UKMLA and GMC fees are charged in pounds sterling, a Bangladeshi candidate who calculates their taka budget many months before actually paying can find the real cost has risen substantially by the time payment is due. Build a buffer of at least 15–20% above your initial taka budget calculation, and monitor exchange rates in the weeks leading up to each payment rather than assuming an old estimate still holds.
  • Letting English test certificates expire. Both IELTS and OET certificates are valid for a defined period from the test date. Candidates who sit their English test early in the process — before EPIC verification or AKT preparation is complete — sometimes find their certificate has expired by the time they are ready to submit their full registration application, forcing a costly re-sit. Sequence your English test so its validity period comfortably covers your expected registration application date.
  • Treating document translation and certification casually. Any document not already issued in English requires a certified professional translation, not an informal translation from a bilingual colleague. Underestimating the time this takes, or submitting translations that do not meet the GMC's certification standard, is a common cause of application delay.
  • Assuming AKT and CPSA preparation can be compressed into the final weeks. Both assessments draw on a specific, defined content base — the official MLA content map for the AKT and a structured OSCE circuit for the CPSA — and candidates who begin serious preparation only a few weeks before their booked date typically underperform relative to their actual clinical knowledge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is a Bangladeshi MBBS degree accepted by the GMC?

Degrees from BM&DC-accredited Bangladeshi medical colleges, such as Dhaka Medical College, Chittagong Medical College, and Sir Salimullah Medical College, generally satisfy the GMC's basic eligibility requirement, provided the college was properly accredited at the time you graduated and your qualification appears on the World Directory of Medical Schools. However, acceptance also depends on completing primary source verification through EPIC and holding full, unrestricted BM&DC registration following your internship year. Always confirm your specific situation against the current guidance on UKMLA eligibility requirements rather than assuming eligibility automatically.

Where can Bangladeshi doctors sit the UKMLA AKT?

The AKT is delivered through the Pearson VUE global network of test centres, and candidates should check current locations and availability directly through their GMC Online account or the Pearson VUE booking portal, since options can change between testing windows. The site's AKT test centres guide explains how to search for and compare suitable centres before booking a date.

Do I have to travel to the UK for the CPSA?

Yes, this step cannot be avoided. The CPSA is held exclusively at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional additional London dates during periods of high demand. There is no option to sit the CPSA remotely or anywhere within Bangladesh, so every Bangladeshi candidate must budget for at least one trip to the UK to complete the pathway.

How much does the whole UKMLA process cost in taka?

The core GMC and exam fees — AKT, CPSA, and initial registration — total approximately £1,800, which converts to roughly ৳280,000 at broadly representative and approximate exchange rates, though this figure moves with the market. Once English testing, EPIC verification, Manchester travel and accommodation, and study materials are included, a realistic total budget is closer to £3,300–£4,800. Because the taka has fluctuated against the pound in recent years, candidates should recheck current rates and current GMC fee levels close to the time of each payment rather than relying on an estimate made months in advance.

Do Bangladeshi doctors need to take an English language test if their degree was taught in English?

In almost all cases, yes. Bangladeshi medical education being conducted substantially in English does not automatically exempt a candidate from the GMC's formal English language evidence requirement. Most Bangladeshi candidates need to submit a valid IELTS Academic score (7.0 overall, no component below 7.0) or OET Medicine result (Grade B minimum in all four components) as part of their application.

What is the realistic timeline from graduating in Bangladesh to working in the NHS?

Most candidates should plan for 18 to 30 months from starting EPIC verification to beginning their first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. This accounts for BM&DC document processing, EPIC verification delays, English test preparation, exam preparation for both assessments, and the GMC registration application itself. Candidates who need to re-sit either exam, or who face slower-than-average verification, should expect this timeline to extend.

How does the Bangladeshi route compare to other South Asian IMG routes?

The overall structure — degree recognition, primary source verification, English testing, a knowledge exam, a clinical skills exam in the UK, then registration — is broadly the same across South Asian source countries, including the routes taken by Sri Lankan doctors and Pakistani doctors. What differs between countries is the specific verification body, typical EPIC processing times, and the size and structure of the existing diaspora network available to support new arrivals. Candidates weighing up degree recognition more broadly may also find it useful to compare how the GMC treats Bangladeshi qualifications against the question of whether an Indian MBBS is considered valid in the UK, since the underlying recognition principles are shared across the region.

Can a Bangladeshi doctor with several years of post-internship experience skip the AKT?

No. There is no experience-based exemption from either the AKT or the CPSA. Regardless of how many years a Bangladeshi doctor has practised following internship — whether in general practice, a teaching hospital, or a specialist unit — both components of the UKMLA must be passed to gain full GMC registration with a licence to practise. Substantial post-qualification clinical experience is genuinely valuable preparation for the CPSA in particular, since it builds the clinical reasoning and consultation fluency the assessment examines, but it does not substitute for sitting the exams themselves.

Conclusion

The UKMLA for Bangladeshi doctors is a clear, well-structured pathway that rewards early planning around credential verification, realistic budgeting in taka, and careful sequencing of English testing against exam dates. Candidates who start EPIC verification early, prepare systematically against the official content map and a reliable AKT question bank, and plan properly for the mandatory Manchester trip consistently move through the pathway faster and with fewer costly setbacks. Comparing notes with candidates following the equally structured Nigerian doctors' route can also be useful, since both countries' diaspora communities have learned similar lessons about sequencing and budgeting even though the specific regulators differ.
