post_title: UKMLA for Sri Lankan Doctors: Eligibility, Fees and the Route to GMC Registration
post_excerpt: A complete route guide for Sri Lankan medical graduates covering UKMLA eligibility, SLMC-to-GMC recognition, exam costs in rupees, and GMC registration.
seo_title: UKMLA for Sri Lankan Doctors: Route & Fees
seo_description: UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors: SLMC-to-GMC recognition, AKT and CPSA costs in Sri Lankan rupees, English testing, and the 2026 registration route.
html_content:
UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors is becoming one of the most closely watched international pathways to UK medical registration, as growing numbers of graduates from Sri Lanka's medical faculties look beyond the region for postgraduate training and long-term career opportunities. For doctors holding a medical degree recognised by the Sri Lanka Medical Council (SLMC), the route to sitting the UKMLA and eventually gaining full GMC registration follows a defined sequence: degree recognition, primary source verification, English language testing, two structured examinations, and a formal registration application. None of these stages is individually difficult, but together they demand a level of planning, patience, and budgeting that catches many first-time applicants off guard.

This guide walks through the entire journey specifically as it applies to doctors trained in Sri Lanka — from confirming that an MBBS degree from an SLMC-recognised medical faculty satisfies GMC eligibility rules, through primary source verification, booking exams, and budgeting in Sri Lankan rupees for a pathway priced in pounds sterling. It also covers where Sri Lankan candidates can sit the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), why the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) must be taken in the UK, and the realistic timeline and common pitfalls that shape how long the whole process actually takes.

Sri Lankan doctors are a relatively small but steadily growing group within the UK's international medical graduate population, and the practical route for Sri Lankan candidates shares much of its structure with the routes followed by doctors from other South Asian countries, including the equivalent pathway for doctors trained in Bangladesh. This guide sets out the formal requirements for the UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors and pairs them with the practical detail that matters most for planning a successful application.

The UKMLA Route From Sri Lanka: A Quick Overview

Before examining each stage in detail, it helps to see the whole pathway from SLMC registration to NHS practice at a glance. The route for a Sri Lankan-trained doctor generally runs as follows:

  1. Confirm eligibility: Verify that your MBBS degree from an SLMC-recognised Sri Lankan medical faculty is accepted by the GMC, and that you hold full, unrestricted SLMC registration following completion of your internship year.
  2. Create a GMC Online account and begin primary source verification of your medical qualification through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials).
  3. Sit and pass an approved English language test — IELTS Academic or OET Medicine — unless you hold an accepted exemption.
  4. Book and pass the UKMLA AKT (the knowledge-test equivalent for IMGs), sat at a Pearson VUE test centre.
  5. Book and pass the UKMLA CPSA (the clinical skills equivalent), which can only be sat in person at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in the UK.
  6. Apply for GMC registration with a licence to practise once both assessments are passed and all supporting documents are verified.
  7. Apply for NHS posts — typically clinical fellow roles, trust-grade positions, or Foundation Year 2-equivalent posts — and begin practising in the UK.

Each stage carries its own processing time, cost, and set of common delays, all covered in the sections below. Understanding the full sequence early prevents the single most common planning mistake made by candidates pursuing the UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors: assuming that passing the exams is the hard part, when in practice credential verification and document logistics often take longer than exam preparation itself. Many Sri Lankan candidates who have already sat competitive postgraduate entrance exams at home, or who are used to the relatively compact administrative structure of the SLMC, are surprised by how much of the UKMLA pathway depends on external parties responding promptly — medical faculty registrars, the SLMC head office, and GMC caseworkers — rather than on their own study effort alone. Treating the administrative track and the exam preparation track as two separate projects, run in parallel rather than one after the other, is consequently the single biggest determinant of how quickly a Sri Lankan candidate reaches registration.

SLMC Recognition and UKMLA Eligibility for Sri Lankan Doctors

The starting point for any Sri Lankan doctor considering the UKMLA route is confirming basic eligibility. The GMC requires that your primary medical qualification comes from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools and that your degree is recognised by the relevant national regulator — in Sri Lanka's case, the Sri Lanka Medical Council (SLMC). Graduates of SLMC-accredited institutions such as the Faculty of Medicine, University of Colombo, the Faculty of Medicine, University of Peradeniya, and the Faculty of Medicine, University of Sri Jayewardenepura generally satisfy this first eligibility hurdle without difficulty, provided the faculty was accredited at the time of graduation. Other SLMC-recognised medical faculties across the country are assessed on the same basis.

Beyond the medical faculty itself, the GMC also expects evidence that you have completed the mandatory SLMC internship year — the Sri Lankan equivalent of the UK's pre-registration house officer year — and that you hold full, unrestricted registration with the SLMC rather than provisional or temporary registration. A Certificate of Good Standing confirming your registration status and disciplinary history is typically required as part of your application, and this must be requested directly from the SLMC — a step many candidates leave too late, given that processing such requests can take several weeks. This same underlying principle, that national regulatory recognition and GMC recognition are related but distinct assessments, also governs questions such as whether an Indian MBBS degree is valid in the UK, since the GMC applies broadly consistent criteria to primary medical qualifications regardless of the country of origin.

Full details of who is permitted to sit the exam, including specific rules for degrees awarded outside standard timeframes or through non-standard routes, are set out in the site's guide to UKMLA eligibility requirements. It is worth reading this in full before committing time and money to the process, since eligibility is assessed on a case-by-case basis for some qualification types, and the requirements that determine UKMLA eligibility for Sri Lankan doctors are identical in structure to those applied to every other international medical graduate.

Primary Source Verification via EPIC

Once basic eligibility is confirmed, the GMC requires primary source verification of your Sri Lankan medical degree — meaning your qualification is confirmed directly with your medical faculty rather than accepted on the strength of a certificate alone. This is handled through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials), the platform used globally for this purpose. Sri Lankan medical faculties vary in how quickly they respond to EPIC verification requests; well-established faculties with dedicated academic affairs offices tend to respond within four to eight weeks, whereas others can take three months or longer, particularly during examination periods or academic holidays.

A fuller walkthrough of how this process works in practice — including the documents required and the sequence of steps inside the GMC Online portal — is available in the site's guide to GMC registration and EPIC verification. That guide is written using an Indian example, but the EPIC process itself is identical for every international medical graduate, including candidates pursuing the UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors, so the same steps and timelines apply directly. For the regulator's own account of how primary source verification works, see the GMC's primary source verification requirements for international medical graduates. Candidates are strongly advised to read this guidance before initiating their EPIC application, as incomplete submissions are one of the most common causes of delay.

What Counts as SLMC Recognition in Practice

Understanding what SLMC recognition really means is central to UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors, because it is worth being precise about what "SLMC-recognised" actually means for GMC purposes, because the two regulators assess slightly different things. SLMC registration confirms that a doctor is entitled to practise within the Sri Lankan health system; it does not, on its own, transfer any rights or recognition in the UK. The GMC instead looks at three separate elements: whether the medical faculty appears on the World Directory of Medical Schools for the relevant graduation year, whether the degree programme met minimum duration and curriculum standards, and whether primary evidence — degree certificate, full transcripts, and internship completion documentation — stands up to independent verification. A doctor can hold perfectly valid SLMC registration and still face delays at the GMC stage if, for example, academic transcripts are incomplete or a faculty's WDOMS listing needs updating. Checking a medical faculty's current WDOMS status before beginning the process is a five-minute task that can save weeks of confusion later.

Documents Sri Lankan Candidates Should Gather Early

Document preparation is one of the most time-consuming parts of UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors. Beyond the primary degree certificate itself, most Sri Lankan applicants need a consistent set of supporting documents, and gathering them in parallel rather than sequentially saves considerable time:

  • MBBS degree certificate and full academic transcripts, year by year, from your medical faculty.
  • SLMC internship completion certificate, confirming you completed the mandatory internship year supervised by the SLMC.
  • SLMC Certificate of Good Standing, confirming your registration status and disciplinary record, requested directly from the SLMC.
  • International passport, valid well beyond your expected registration date.
  • Evidence of any name discrepancies across documents, such as marriage certificates or statutory declarations, where certificate and passport names differ.
  • Certified translations of any document not already issued in English, from a recognised professional translation service — though most Sri Lankan medical degree documentation is issued in English as standard.

Requesting the SLMC Certificate of Good Standing early is particularly important, since this document follows its own administrative timeline that is entirely separate from your medical faculty's EPIC response time.

How to Apply for the UKMLA From Sri Lanka: Step by Step

With eligibility confirmed, the practical application process for UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors follows six sequential steps. Each is described below in the order it should typically be actioned.

Step 1: Create Your GMC Online Account

The practical, hands-on part of UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors begins with creating a GMC Online account through the GMC's official website. This account becomes your central hub for the entire process — you will use it to track your EPIC verification status, book both the AKT and CPSA, submit your English language evidence, and eventually complete your registration application. Use a personal email address you will retain long-term, not a hospital or university email that may be deactivated after you move on, since GMC Online correspondence can span well over a year for most candidates pursuing the UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors.

Step 2: Begin EPIC Verification Early

As covered above, EPIC verification of your MBBS degree is usually the single longest step in the entire pathway. Start this as early as possible — ideally before finalising your English test date or exam bookings — because it runs in parallel with other preparation and requires little active involvement once submitted, beyond following up with your medical faculty if delays occur. Keep a written record of every communication with your faculty's registrar, since this becomes useful evidence if you need to escalate a delayed response.

Step 3: Gather and Submit English Language Evidence

In parallel with EPIC verification, sit your English language test (IELTS Academic or OET Medicine — full detail in the dedicated section below) and upload your certificate to GMC Online once results are issued. Many Sri Lankan candidates assume that because much of their medical education was conducted in English, this requirement can be waived; in practice, formal evidence is still required in almost all cases, so it is worth booking a test slot early rather than waiting to find out whether an exemption applies. Further detail on how the English requirement works in practice is set out in the site's guide to the English language requirement for the UKMLA, which — although illustrated with an India-based example — describes exactly the same testing process and score thresholds that apply to every international medical graduate.

Step 4: Book and Prepare for the AKT

Once your GMC Online account is active, you can book the AKT through the Pearson VUE booking system linked from your account. Booking windows typically open several months ahead of each testing period, so it is worth booking a suitable slot as soon as it appears rather than waiting until the last moment. Preparation should draw on the official MLA content map, which defines exactly what conditions, presentations, and professional knowledge areas are examinable, alongside a structured question bank. For a full picture of where Sri Lankan candidates can actually sit this exam, see the site's guide to UKMLA AKT test centres.

Step 5: Book and Prepare for the CPSA

The CPSA can only be booked once the AKT has been passed. Because the CPSA is only available in the UK, Sri Lankan candidates need to plan a UK trip around this stage, factoring in visa arrangements, flights, and several days of accommodation for final preparation immediately before the assessment. Many candidates combine the trip with a short period of UK clinical observation or shadowing beforehand, which helps with the specific communication and consultation style the CPSA examines.

Step 6: Submit Your GMC Registration Application

This final step marks the formal completion of UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors. Once both the AKT and CPSA are passed, and your EPIC verification, English language evidence, and any outstanding documents are all confirmed, you can submit your full application for GMC registration with a licence to practise. Full step-by-step detail on this final stage is available in the site's GMC registration process guide, which covers document checklists, processing times, and what to do if your application is queried.

UKMLA AKT Test Centres for Sri Lankan Candidates

One of the most practical aspects of UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors is understanding exactly where the AKT can be sat. The AKT is delivered through the Pearson VUE global network of test centres. At the time of writing, Sri Lanka does not have its own dedicated Pearson VUE test centre offering the UKMLA AKT, so most candidates pursuing the UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors currently need to travel to a regional test centre, commonly in India or elsewhere in South Asia, or sit the exam in the UK itself. Centre availability changes between testing windows, however, so candidates should always check the live, current list of approved centres through their GMC Online account or the Pearson VUE booking portal before finalising travel plans. The site's dedicated guide to UKMLA AKT test centres sets out the full current network and how to check which locations are open for a given sitting period.

This is an important contrast with the CPSA. Unlike the AKT, the CPSA cannot be sat anywhere outside the UK — it is held exclusively at the GMC's purpose-built Clinical Assessment Centre, with occasional additional dates in periods of high demand. This means every Sri Lankan candidate must, at minimum, make one trip to the UK to complete the UKMLA pathway. Budgeting for this mandatory UK trip — flights, a UK visitor visa if required, accommodation, and living costs for the preparation and exam period — should be built into financial planning from the very start of the process, not left until after the AKT is passed.

UKMLA Exam Cost for Sri Lankan Doctors: Fees in Rupees

Cost is consistently one of the biggest practical concerns for doctors evaluating UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors as a career route, both because the fees themselves are substantial in absolute terms and because the Sri Lankan rupee has experienced notable volatility against the pound in recent years. The table below sets out the core GMC and Pearson VUE fees in pounds sterling alongside an approximate rupee equivalent, calculated at an indicative rate of roughly £1 ≈ LKR 390, so Sri Lankan candidates can budget realistically. For the complete picture across every route, see the site's guide to the full IMG fee breakdown across every route.

Fee ItemAmount (GBP)Approximate Amount (LKR)When Payable
AKT exam fee£283~Rs. 110,000At booking
CPSA exam fee£1,036~Rs. 404,000At booking
GMC initial registration fee£481~Rs. 188,000On application, after both exams passed
Annual retention fee£431~Rs. 168,000Annually in January, once registered
Total core fees (first attempt)£1,800~Rs. 702,000Across the full pathway to registration

These rupee figures are indicative only, calculated at a broadly representative and clearly approximate exchange rate; they will shift with market movements between the time this guide is written and the time a candidate actually makes a payment. The Sri Lankan rupee has fluctuated significantly against the pound in recent years, and a gap of even a few months between budgeting and paying can materially change the rupee cost of a given fee. The GMC reviews its fee schedule periodically; therefore, candidates should always verify current rules and current fee amounts directly on the GMC website before applying, and check live exchange rates at the time of transfer rather than relying on figures calculated months in advance.

Beyond these core fees, Sri Lankan candidates should budget separately for EPIC verification charges, English language test fees, flights and accommodation for the mandatory UK trip to sit the CPSA, any travel required to reach an AKT test centre, visa costs where applicable, and study materials. Together these additional costs commonly add £1,500–£3,000 on top of the core GMC fees, so a realistic total budget for a first-attempt pass across the whole pathway is closer to £3,300–£4,800 once every cost is included. This overall cost structure is broadly comparable to what doctors following the UKMLA pathway for Pakistani doctors typically budget for, since the underlying GMC and Pearson VUE fees are identical for every IMG regardless of country of origin.

Because so much of this budget is denominated in pounds while income and savings are typically held in rupees, many Sri Lankan candidates choose to build their fund gradually over several months rather than attempting a single large currency purchase close to a payment deadline. Spreading currency purchases over time, using a specialist international transfer provider rather than a standard bank counter rate, and tracking the mid-market rate rather than relying on a single bank's quotation are all practical ways to reduce the effective cost of converting rupees to pounds across the pathway. Some candidates also time larger purchases around periods of relative rupee stability rather than during periods of sharp depreciation, although this requires ongoing attention to the foreign exchange market rather than a one-off decision.

English Language Requirements for Sri Lankan Doctors

English language evidence is one of the most misunderstood requirements within UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors, since English is used extensively throughout Sri Lankan medical education, particularly for clinical teaching, textbooks, and hospital documentation, which leads many Sri Lankan doctors to assume that formal English testing will not apply to them. In practice, the GMC requires documentary evidence of English language proficiency from the great majority of international medical graduates regardless of the language of medical training, and Sri Lankan degrees are not automatically exempt. Candidates should plan for formal testing as a standard part of the pathway rather than hoping for a waiver.

Two tests are accepted: IELTS Academic, where the GMC requires an overall score of 7.0, with no individual component (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking) below 7.0, and OET (Occupational English Test), Medicine version, where the GMC requires a minimum of Grade B in all four sub-tests. OET is often more intuitive for practising doctors because its scenarios are built entirely around healthcare communication — referral letters, doctor-patient consultations, and clinical case notes — rather than general academic topics. IELTS Academic, by contrast, draws on a broader range of subject matter and is widely available through test centres in Colombo and other major Sri Lankan cities.

Both test certificates have a defined validity period from the date of the test, so timing matters: sitting the test too early relative to the overall pathway risks the certificate expiring before the registration application is submitted, which would require a costly and time-consuming re-sit. A fuller breakdown of accepted scores and how evidence is submitted is available in the site's guide to the English language requirement for the UKMLA, which describes the same universal process that applies regardless of which country a candidate is applying from. For the regulator's own current guidance, see the GMC's English language evidence requirements, since specific evidentiary requirements are updated from time to time and always take precedence over general summaries.

Life and Career for Sri Lankan Doctors in the NHS After Registration

Completing UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors is only the beginning of the story. Once GMC registration is secured, Sri Lankan doctors join a smaller but steadily growing community of South Asian international medical graduates within the NHS. Career progression after registration generally follows one of several routes: a trust-grade or clinical fellow post, offering FY2-equivalent clinical responsibility without requiring a Foundation Programme place; a Specialty Training application once sufficient UK experience and portfolio evidence are in place; or locum work through NHS staff banks and agencies for those who want flexibility while building a UK clinical track record. Many Sri Lankan doctors who enter through clinical fellow posts subsequently progress into core or specialty training within two to three years, particularly in specialties experiencing workforce shortages such as general internal medicine, psychiatry, and emergency medicine, where competition ratios for IMG applicants tend to be more favourable.

It is worth noting that the broad shape of this journey — degree recognition, credential verification, English testing, a knowledge exam, a clinical skills exam, then registration — mirrors the equivalent route for doctors coming from India, the largest single source country for international medical graduates in the NHS, even though the specific verification bodies and typical processing times differ between the two countries. Sri Lankan candidates can therefore benefit from reading about the Indian pathway for a sense of scale and process, while remembering that SLMC-specific timelines and document requirements will differ in the details.

Day-to-day life for newly registered Sri Lankan doctors in the NHS typically involves an adjustment period covering both clinical and cultural dimensions. Clinically, the biggest early adjustments tend to be around NHS documentation standards, such as structured handover formats, a strong institutional emphasis on multidisciplinary team working, and a more codified approach to consent and capacity than many candidates are accustomed to from practice in Sri Lanka. Socially, Sri Lankan doctors moving to the UK are joining a smaller but active diaspora community, with informal mentorship networks increasingly present in cities with established Sri Lankan populations, including London, Manchester, and Birmingham. Salary progression follows the standard NHS pay scale for the relevant grade, and while it can take time to reach specialty training, most Sri Lankan doctors who remain committed to the pathway report that the structured nature of UK training — clear competency frameworks, protected training time, and defined progression points — compares favourably with less standardised systems elsewhere.

UKMLA for Sri Lankan Doctors Compared With Other Global Routes

Doctors weighing the UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors against other options sometimes also consider whether the United States route might suit them better, particularly if they have family ties there or are drawn to the US training system. In practice, the two pathways differ substantially in cost, structure, and timeline, and the site's detailed UKMLA versus USMLE comparison sets out exactly how the exams, fees, and registration processes diverge. For most Sri Lankan candidates, the UK route offers a shorter overall pathway to full registration and a lower total cost once travel and preparation are accounted for, although the USMLE route can offer earlier access to a wider range of US residency specialties for candidates prepared to commit to a longer and more expensive process.

Within South Asia specifically, the underlying GMC framework behind UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors — degree recognition, EPIC verification, English testing, AKT, CPSA, registration — is identical whether a candidate is coming from Sri Lanka, Pakistan, Bangladesh, or India. What varies between countries is the strength of the existing diaspora network, typical EPIC response times from individual medical faculties, and local awareness of the process. Consequently, much of the practical guidance developed for Bangladeshi doctors pursuing the UKMLA and for candidates following the Pakistani route to UKMLA registration applies with only minor adjustment to Sri Lankan candidates as well.

Timeline: From Graduation in Sri Lanka to NHS Registration

Sri Lankan candidates pursuing UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors consistently underestimate how long the full pathway takes, largely because exam preparation time is easy to plan for while administrative processing time is not. The table below sets out a realistic stage-by-stage timeline based on typical current processing periods.

StageTypical DurationKey Variable
SLMC Certificate of Good Standing request2 to 8 weeksSLMC processing backlog
EPIC primary source verification4 weeks to 4 monthsSpeed of your medical faculty's response
English language test preparation and sitting1 to 4 monthsBaseline English proficiency
AKT preparation3 to 6 monthsStudy intensity, prior clinical exposure
AKT booking to sitting4 to 12 weeksTest centre date and location availability
CPSA preparation, including UK travel planning2 to 4 monthsVisa processing, budget readiness
CPSA booking to sitting (UK)2 to 6 monthsLimited sitting capacity
GMC registration application processing4 to 8 weeksDocument completeness
NHS job search and start date1 to 3 monthsNHS recruitment cycles, specialty demand

Adding these stages together, most candidates following the UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors should plan for a realistic total timeline of 18 to 30 months from beginning EPIC verification to starting their first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. Candidates who fail either assessment on their first attempt, or who face delays at the SLMC or EPIC stage, should expect this timeline to extend further. Planning around the longer end of this range from the outset avoids the financial and psychological strain of a schedule that assumes everything goes smoothly.

Common Mistakes Sri Lankan Doctors Make on This Route

Several avoidable errors recur consistently among candidates pursuing UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors, and recognising them early is one of the most valuable things a new candidate can do. Most of these mistakes are not clinical — they are planning and administrative errors that have nothing to do with medical knowledge, yet they are responsible for a disproportionate share of the delays and extra costs candidates experience. Being aware of them in advance, before committing money to a specific exam date or travel booking, can save months of delay and hundreds of pounds in wasted fees.

  • Starting EPIC verification too late. Because EPIC processing time depends heavily on a medical faculty's administrative speed rather than the candidate's own effort, this step should be initiated before — not after — beginning AKT preparation. Candidates who wait until they are ready to book the AKT before starting EPIC often lose two to three months they could have used productively.
  • Underestimating the true cost of the mandatory UK trip. The CPSA exam fee itself is only part of the cost. Flights from Colombo, several nights of UK accommodation, local transport, food, and — for many candidates — a short UK preparation course before the exam, can together add £1,000–£2,000 on top of the fee. Candidates who budget only for the headline fee frequently find themselves scrambling for additional funds close to their exam date.
  • Not accounting for rupee exchange rate volatility. Because all UKMLA and GMC fees are charged in pounds sterling, a candidate who calculates a rupee budget many months before actually paying can find the real cost has risen substantially by the time payment is due. Building a buffer of at least 15–20% above an initial rupee budget calculation, and monitoring exchange rates in the weeks leading up to each payment, is therefore a sensible precaution rather than an optional extra.
  • Letting English test certificates expire. Both IELTS and OET certificates are valid for a defined period from the test date. Candidates who sit their English test early in the process — before EPIC verification or AKT preparation is complete — sometimes find their certificate has expired by the time they are ready to submit their full registration application, forcing a costly re-sit. Sequencing the English test so its validity period comfortably covers the expected registration application date avoids this entirely.
  • Underestimating AKT test centre logistics. Because Sri Lanka does not currently host its own dedicated AKT test centre, candidates sometimes assume a convenient nearby slot will always be available. In practice, travelling to a regional centre requires its own visa, cost, and scheduling considerations, so checking the current test centre network well ahead of a target sitting date is essential.
  • Assuming AKT and CPSA preparation can be compressed into the final weeks. Both assessments draw on a specific, defined content base — the official MLA content map for the AKT and a structured OSCE circuit for the CPSA — and candidates who begin serious preparation only a few weeks before their booked date typically underperform relative to their actual clinical knowledge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can Sri Lankan doctors sit the UKMLA AKT without travelling to the UK?

In many cases, yes, since the AKT is delivered through the global Pearson VUE test centre network rather than exclusively in the UK. However, Sri Lanka does not currently have its own dedicated test centre offering the exam, so most candidates need to travel to a regional centre, commonly in South Asia, or sit the AKT in the UK itself. The site's guide to UKMLA AKT test centres sets out the current network and how to check live availability before booking.

Do Sri Lankan doctors have to travel to the UK for the CPSA?

Yes, this step cannot be avoided. The CPSA is held exclusively at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in the UK, with no option to sit it remotely or anywhere in Sri Lanka. Every candidate pursuing the UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors must therefore budget for at least one trip to the UK to complete the pathway.

Is a Sri Lankan MBBS degree automatically accepted by the GMC?

Degrees from SLMC-accredited Sri Lankan medical faculties, such as the Faculty of Medicine at the University of Colombo or the University of Peradeniya, generally satisfy the GMC's basic eligibility requirement, provided the faculty was properly accredited at the time of graduation and the qualification is listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools. However, acceptance also depends on completing primary source verification through EPIC and holding full, unrestricted SLMC registration following the internship year. Always confirm your specific situation against the current guidance on UKMLA eligibility requirements rather than assuming eligibility automatically — this eligibility check is the true starting point of UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors.

How much does the whole UKMLA process cost in Sri Lankan rupees?

The core GMC and exam fees — AKT, CPSA, and initial registration — total approximately £1,800, which converts to roughly Rs. 700,000 at a broadly representative exchange rate of about £1 to LKR 390, though this figure moves with the market and should always be checked against live rates before paying. Once English testing, EPIC verification, UK travel and accommodation, and study materials are included, a realistic total budget is closer to £3,300–£4,800. Because the rupee has fluctuated significantly against the pound in recent years, candidates should recheck current rates and current GMC fee levels close to the time of each payment rather than relying on an estimate made months in advance.

Do Sri Lankan doctors need to take an English language test if their degree was taught partly in English?

In almost all cases, yes. Partial or extensive use of English during Sri Lankan medical education does not automatically exempt a candidate from the GMC's formal English language evidence requirement. Most candidates need to submit a valid IELTS Academic score (7.0 overall, no component below 7.0) or OET Medicine result (Grade B minimum in all four components) as part of their application.

What is the realistic timeline from graduating in Sri Lanka to working in the NHS?

Most candidates completing UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors should plan for 18 to 30 months from starting EPIC verification to beginning their first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. This accounts for SLMC document processing, EPIC verification delays, English test preparation, exam preparation for both assessments, and the GMC registration application itself. Candidates who need to re-sit either exam, or who face slower-than-average verification, should expect this timeline to extend.

Can a Sri Lankan doctor with several years of post-internship experience skip the AKT?

No. There is no experience-based exemption from either the AKT or the CPSA. Regardless of how many years a Sri Lankan doctor has practised following the SLMC internship year, both components of the UKMLA must be passed to gain full GMC registration with a licence to practise. Substantial post-qualification clinical experience is genuinely valuable preparation for the CPSA in particular, since it builds the clinical reasoning and consultation fluency the assessment examines, but it does not substitute for sitting the exams themselves.

Conclusion

The UKMLA for Sri Lankan doctors is a clearly defined, increasingly well-trodden pathway, but it rewards early planning around credential verification, realistic budgeting in Sri Lankan rupees, and careful sequencing of English testing against exam dates. Candidates who start EPIC verification early, prepare systematically against the official content map, and plan properly for the mandatory UK trip consistently move through the pathway faster and with fewer costly setbacks. Start your UKMLA preparation plan →

post_title: UKMLA for South African Doctors: Eligibility, Fees and the Route to GMC Registration
post_excerpt: A complete route guide for South African medical graduates covering UKMLA eligibility, HPCSA-to-GMC recognition, exam costs in rand, and GMC registration.
seo_title: UKMLA for South African Doctors: Route & Fees
seo_description: UKMLA for South African doctors: HPCSA-to-GMC recognition, AKT and CPSA costs in rand, English testing, and the full 2026 GMC registration route.
html_content:
UKMLA for South African doctors is becoming an increasingly common search among graduates trained at South Africa's well-regarded medical schools who want a clear, defined route into NHS practice. South Africa has long produced doctors who go on to work internationally, and the UK is a natural destination given the shared medical training heritage, a common working language, and a long history of South African-trained doctors already established across the NHS. For graduates holding a medical degree recognised by the Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA), the path to sitting the UKMLA and ultimately gaining full GMC registration follows a defined sequence of credential verification, English language evidence, two structured examinations, and a formal registration application.

This guide walks through that entire journey specifically as it applies to South African-trained doctors — from confirming that an MBChB from a South African medical school satisfies GMC eligibility rules, through primary source verification, booking exams, and budgeting in rand for a pathway priced in pounds sterling. It also covers where South African candidates can sit the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), why the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) must be taken in Manchester, how South Africa's compulsory community service year interacts with GMC requirements, and the realistic timeline and common pitfalls that shape how long the whole process actually takes.

South African doctors are not newcomers to the UK medical workforce. Graduates of institutions such as the University of Cape Town, the University of the Witwatersrand, Stellenbosch University, and the University of Pretoria have practised within the NHS for decades, and this established diaspora means that much of the practical, on-the-ground knowledge about how the process really works is already available through community and professional networks. This guide sets out the formal requirements and pairs them with the practical detail that matters for planning a successful application.

The UKMLA Route From South Africa: A Quick Overview

Before working through each stage in detail, it helps to see the whole pathway from HPCSA registration to NHS practice at a glance. The route for a South African-trained doctor generally runs as follows:

  1. Confirm eligibility: Verify that your MBChB from an HPCSA-accredited South African medical school is accepted by the GMC, and understand how your compulsory community service year fits alongside GMC internship-equivalent requirements.
  2. Create a GMC Online account and begin primary source verification of your medical qualification through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials).
  3. Sit and pass an approved English language test — IELTS Academic or OET Medicine — unless you hold an accepted exemption.
  4. Book and pass the UKMLA AKT (the PLAB 1 equivalent for IMGs), sat at a Pearson VUE test centre, including centres within South Africa.
  5. Book and pass the UKMLA CPSA (the PLAB 2 equivalent), which can only be sat in person at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester.
  6. Apply for GMC registration with a licence to practise once both assessments are passed and all supporting documents are verified.
  7. Apply for NHS posts — typically clinical fellow roles, trust-grade positions, or Foundation Year 2-equivalent posts — and begin practising in the UK.

Each of these stages carries its own processing time, cost, and set of common delays, all covered in the sections below. Understanding the full sequence early prevents the single most common planning mistake: assuming that passing the exams is the hard part, when in practice credential verification and document logistics often take longer than exam preparation itself. Many South African candidates who have already completed rigorous undergraduate assessments, or who are used to the relatively self-contained structure of HPCSA processes, are surprised by how much of the UKMLA pathway depends on external parties responding promptly — medical school registrars, the HPCSA head office, and GMC caseworkers — rather than on their own study effort alone. Treating the administrative track and the exam preparation track as two separate projects, run in parallel rather than one after the other, is the single biggest determinant of how quickly a South African candidate reaches registration.

HPCSA Recognition and UKMLA Eligibility for South African Doctors

The starting point for any South African doctor considering the UKMLA route is confirming basic eligibility. The GMC requires that your primary medical qualification comes from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools and that your degree is recognised by the relevant national regulator — in South Africa's case, the Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA). Graduates of HPCSA-accredited institutions such as the University of Cape Town, the University of the Witwatersrand, Stellenbosch University, and the University of Pretoria, along with South Africa's other HPCSA-recognised medical schools, generally satisfy this first eligibility hurdle without difficulty, provided the school was accredited at the time of graduation.

Beyond the medical school itself, the GMC also expects evidence of the clinical training and supervised practice that followed graduation. South African medical training includes a two-year internship followed by a compulsory one-year community service placement, both regulated by the HPCSA, before a doctor is granted independent registration. In broad terms, this internship-plus-community-service structure serves a similar purpose to the internship year required in many other jurisdictions, giving newly qualified doctors a period of supervised clinical practice before full registration. However, exactly how the GMC treats the community service year for a specific applicant can depend on individual circumstances, so South African candidates should verify their own case directly with the GMC or through their EPIC application rather than assuming a blanket equivalence. A Certificate of Status confirming your HPCSA registration and disciplinary history is typically required as part of your GMC application, and this must be requested directly from the HPCSA — a step many candidates leave too late, given that South African regulatory bodies can take several weeks to process such requests.

Full details of who is permitted to sit the exam, including specific rules for degrees awarded outside standard timeframes or through non-standard routes, are set out in the GMC's guidance on confirming who can sit the exam. It is worth reading this in full before committing time and money to the process, since eligibility is assessed on a case-by-case basis for some qualification types. For a broader view of the requirements that apply across all international medical graduates, see the site's guide to UKMLA eligibility requirements. In short, the UKMLA for South African doctors hinges on three things working together: an accredited medical school, complete supervised-practice documentation, and a clean HPCSA record.

Primary Source Verification via EPIC

Once basic eligibility is confirmed, the GMC requires primary source verification of your South African medical degree — meaning your qualification is confirmed directly with your medical school rather than accepted on the strength of a certificate alone. This is handled through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials), the platform used globally for this purpose. South African medical schools vary in how quickly they respond to EPIC verification requests; well-established schools with dedicated international or alumni relations offices tend to respond within four to eight weeks, while others can take three months or longer, particularly during examination periods or academic holidays.

Full detail on how primary source verification works, what documents are required, and how the GMC uses the resulting evidence is available directly from the regulator: see the GMC's primary source verification requirements for international medical graduates. This process is identical for every internationally trained doctor regardless of country of origin, and the site's detailed walkthrough of how EPIC verification works in practice — although written with an Indian example — describes exactly the same steps a South African applicant will follow. Candidates are strongly advised to read the official GMC guidance before initiating their EPIC application, as incomplete submissions are one of the most common causes of delay.

What HPCSA Recognition Means in Practice

It is worth being precise about what "HPCSA-recognised" actually means for GMC purposes, because the two regulators are assessing slightly different things. HPCSA registration confirms that you are entitled to practise medicine within the South African health system; it does not, on its own, transfer any rights or recognition in the UK. The GMC instead looks at three separate elements: whether your medical school appears on the World Directory of Medical Schools for the relevant graduation year, whether the degree programme itself met minimum duration and curriculum standards, and whether you can produce primary evidence — degree certificate, full academic transcripts, and internship or community service completion documentation — that stands up to independent verification. A doctor can hold perfectly valid HPCSA registration and still face delays at the GMC stage if, for example, academic transcripts are incomplete or the community service documentation does not clearly map onto what the GMC is asking for. Checking your medical school's current WDOMS listing before you begin is a five-minute task that can save weeks of confusion later.

Documents South African Candidates Should Gather Early

Beyond the primary degree certificate itself, most South African applicants need a consistent set of supporting documents, and gathering them in parallel rather than sequentially saves considerable time:

  • MBChB degree certificate and full academic transcripts, year by year, from your medical school.
  • Internship completion certificate confirming you completed your two-year HPCSA-supervised internship.
  • Community service completion certificate, since the GMC may request evidence of this compulsory year as part of assessing your overall clinical training, even though its exact treatment is decided case by case.
  • HPCSA Certificate of Status, confirming your registration standing and disciplinary record, requested directly from the HPCSA head office in Pretoria.
  • South African passport, valid well beyond your expected registration date.
  • Evidence of any name changes — marriage certificates or statutory declarations — where your degree name and passport name differ.
  • Certified translations of any document not already issued in English, from a recognised professional translation service.

Requesting the HPCSA Certificate of Status early is particularly important, since this document is issued by a separate national body from your medical school and follows its own, often slower, administrative timeline. Consequently, candidates who request it in the same week they begin their EPIC application rarely find it becomes a bottleneck later.

How to Apply for the UKMLA From South Africa: Step by Step

With eligibility confirmed, the practical application process for the UKMLA for South African doctors follows six sequential steps. Each is described below in the order it should typically be actioned.

Step 1: Create Your GMC Online Account

Registration begins with creating a GMC Online account at the GMC's official website. This account becomes your central hub for the entire process — you will use it to track your EPIC verification status, book both the AKT and CPSA, submit your English language evidence, and eventually complete your registration application. Use a personal email address you will retain long-term, not a university or hospital email that may be deactivated after you leave a post, since GMC Online correspondence can span well over a year for most South African candidates.

Step 2: Begin EPIC Verification Early

As covered above, EPIC verification of your MBChB degree is usually the single longest step in the entire pathway. Start this as early as possible — ideally before finalising your English test date or exam bookings — because it runs in parallel with other preparation and does not require your active involvement once submitted, beyond following up with your medical school if there are delays. Keep a written record of every communication with your medical school's registrar, as this becomes useful evidence if you need to escalate a delayed response.

Step 3: Gather and Submit English Language Evidence

In parallel with EPIC verification, sit your English language test (IELTS Academic or OET Medicine — full detail in the dedicated section below) and upload your certificate to GMC Online once results are issued. Many South African candidates assume that because their medical education was conducted in English, this requirement can be waived; in practice, formal evidence is still required in almost all cases, so it is worth booking a test slot early rather than waiting to find out whether an exemption applies.

Step 4: Book and Prepare for the AKT

Once your GMC Online account is active, you can book the AKT through the Pearson VUE booking system linked from your account. Booking windows typically open several months ahead of each testing period, and popular South African test centre dates fill quickly, so book as soon as a suitable slot appears rather than waiting until the last moment. For the full list of centre locations, booking procedures, and what to expect on exam day, see the dedicated guide to AKT test centres and booking. Preparation should draw on the official MLA content map, which defines exactly what conditions, presentations, and professional knowledge areas are examinable, and a structured question bank — see the guidance on choosing a good AKT question bank and mock test resources for a comparison of the main options available to South African candidates.

Step 5: Book and Prepare for the CPSA

The CPSA can only be booked once you have passed the AKT. Because the CPSA is only available in Manchester, South African candidates need to plan a UK trip around this stage, factoring in visa arrangements, flights, and several days of accommodation for preparation immediately before the assessment. Many candidates combine the trip with a short period of UK clinical observation or shadowing beforehand, which helps with the specific communication and consultation style the CPSA examines. Full detail on the exam format itself is covered in the site's guide to the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment.

Step 6: Submit Your GMC Registration Application

Once both the AKT and CPSA are passed, and your EPIC verification, English language evidence, and any outstanding documents (HPCSA Certificate of Status, internship and community service completion certificates, identity documents) are all confirmed, you can submit your full application for GMC registration with a licence to practise. Full step-by-step detail on this final stage is available in the site's GMC registration process guide, which covers document checklists, processing times, and what to do if your application is queried.

UKMLA AKT Test Centres for South African Candidates

One of the most practical advantages of the UKMLA pathway for South African doctors is that the AKT can often be sat locally rather than requiring international travel. The AKT is delivered through the Pearson VUE global network of test centres, which includes locations within South Africa, most commonly in Johannesburg, Pretoria, Cape Town, and Durban, although exact availability fluctuates by testing window. Candidates should check the live list of centres and open dates directly through their GMC Online account or the Pearson VUE booking portal, and the site's dedicated AKT test centres guide sets out how to search for and book the nearest available slot.

This is a significant contrast with the CPSA. Unlike the AKT, the CPSA cannot be sat anywhere in South Africa, or indeed anywhere outside the UK — it is held exclusively at the GMC's purpose-built Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional additional London dates in periods of high demand. This means every South African candidate must, at minimum, make one trip to the UK to complete their UKMLA pathway, even if the AKT is passed entirely from Johannesburg or Cape Town without leaving the country. Budgeting for this single mandatory UK trip — flights, a UK visitor visa if required, accommodation, and living costs for the preparation and exam period — should be built into financial planning from the very start of the process, not left until after the AKT is passed.

UKMLA Exam Cost for South African Doctors: Fees in Rand

Cost is consistently one of the biggest practical concerns for anyone researching the UKMLA for South African doctors, both because the fees themselves are substantial in absolute terms and because the rand has experienced sustained volatility against the pound in recent years. The table below sets out the core GMC and Pearson VUE fees in pounds sterling alongside an approximate rand equivalent, calculated at a broadly indicative exchange rate of roughly £1 to R23, so South African candidates can budget realistically. For the full IMG fee breakdown across every route, covering re-sit charges and every fee across the pathway, see the dedicated fees guide.

Fee ItemAmount (GBP)Approximate Amount (ZAR)When Payable
AKT / PLAB 1 exam fee£283~R6,500At booking
CPSA / PLAB 2 exam fee£1,036~R23,800At booking
GMC initial registration fee£481~R11,050On application, after both exams passed
Annual retention fee£431~R9,900Annually in January, once registered
Total core fees (first attempt)£1,800~R41,400Across the full pathway to registration

These rand figures are indicative only and calculated at an approximate, clearly-labelled exchange rate; they will shift with market movements between the time this guide is written and the time you actually make a payment. Exchange rates between the rand and the pound fluctuate significantly, and a gap of even a few weeks between budgeting and paying can materially change the rand cost of a given fee. Therefore, always verify the live exchange rate at the point of payment rather than relying on a figure calculated months in advance, and always verify current rules and current fee amounts directly on the official GMC website before applying, since the GMC reviews its fee schedule periodically.

Beyond these core fees, South African candidates should budget separately for EPIC verification charges, English language test fees, flights and accommodation for the mandatory Manchester CPSA trip, UK visitor visa costs if applicable, and study materials. Together these additional costs commonly add £1,500–£3,000 on top of the core GMC fees, so a realistic total budget for a first-attempt pass across the whole pathway is closer to £3,300–£4,800 once every cost is included — roughly R76,000–R110,000 at the same indicative rate.

Because so much of this budget is denominated in pounds while income and savings are typically held in rand, many South African candidates choose to build their fund gradually over several months rather than attempting a single large currency purchase close to a payment deadline. Spreading currency purchases over time, using a specialist international transfer provider rather than a standard bank counter rate, and tracking the mid-market rate rather than relying on a single bank's quoted rate are all practical ways to reduce the effective cost of converting rand to pounds across the pathway. Some candidates also time larger purchases around periods of relative rand stability rather than during periods of sharp depreciation, although this requires ongoing attention to the foreign exchange market rather than a one-off decision.

English Language Requirements for South African Doctors

English is one of South Africa's official languages and the primary language of instruction throughout South African medical education, which leads many candidates researching the UKMLA for South African doctors to assume that formal English testing will not apply to them. In practice, the GMC requires documentary evidence of English language proficiency from the great majority of international medical graduates regardless of the language of their medical training, and South African degrees are not automatically exempt in every case. Candidates should plan for formal testing as a standard part of the pathway rather than assuming a waiver will apply, although some South African applicants may qualify for an exemption depending on their specific educational history — this should always be confirmed directly with the GMC rather than assumed.

Two tests are accepted: IELTS Academic, where the GMC requires an overall score of 7.0, with no individual component (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking) below 7.0, and OET (Occupational English Test), Medicine version, where the GMC requires a minimum of Grade B in all four sub-tests. OET is often more intuitive for practising doctors because its scenarios are built entirely around healthcare communication — referral letters, doctor-patient consultations, and clinical case notes — rather than general academic topics. IELTS Academic, by contrast, draws on a broader range of subject matter and has widely available preparation material and test centres across South Africa.

Both test certificates have a defined validity period from the date of the test, so timing matters: sitting the test too early relative to your overall pathway risks the certificate expiring before you submit your registration application, which would require a costly and time-consuming re-sit. Full detail on accepted scores, accepted test providers, and how evidence is submitted is available directly from the regulator at the GMC's English language evidence requirements. It is worth reading this page in full, since the specific evidentiary requirements are updated from time to time and always take precedence over general summaries.

Life and Career for South African Doctors in the NHS After Registration

Once GMC registration is secured, South African doctors join a long-established diaspora community within the NHS. South African-trained doctors have practised in the UK for decades, and South Africa remains consistently among the notable source countries for internationally trained doctors on the UK medical register. This means new arrivals typically find peer networks, informal mentorship, and professional associations already in place to help with the practical realities of settling into UK practice, particularly in regions with a strong existing South African medical community.

Career progression after registration generally follows one of several routes: a trust-grade or clinical fellow post (the most common entry point for newly registered IMGs, offering FY2-equivalent clinical responsibility without requiring a Foundation Programme place), a Specialty Training application once sufficient UK experience and portfolio evidence are in place, or locum work through NHS staff banks and agencies for those who want flexibility while building their UK clinical track record. Many South African doctors who enter through clinical fellow posts subsequently progress into core or specialty training within two to three years, particularly in specialties experiencing significant workforce shortages such as emergency medicine, anaesthetics, and general internal medicine, where competition ratios for IMG applicants tend to be more favourable. Once registered, understanding what happens after passing the UKMLA and registering with the GMC is the natural next step, since finding the right first NHS post shapes how quickly a doctor progresses afterwards.

Salary progression follows the standard NHS pay scale for the relevant grade and banding, and this structure applies identically regardless of which country a doctor trained in. The site's detailed guide to NHS pay scales, banding, and take-home pay — although written with an Indian audience in mind — sets out the same basic pay structure, on-call supplements, and progression points that apply to a South African doctor joining the NHS. It is worth noting that the broad shape of this journey — degree recognition, credential verification, English testing, a knowledge exam, a clinical skills exam, then registration — mirrors the equivalent route taken by Nigerian-trained doctors, another African diaspora with a substantial and well-established presence in the NHS, even though the specific verification bodies and typical processing times differ between the two countries. It also broadly mirrors the route followed by doctors trained in India, the single largest source country for IMGs on the UK register, which is a useful comparison point for understanding how universal the overall UKMLA structure really is.

Day-to-day life for newly registered South African doctors in the NHS typically involves an adjustment period covering both clinical and cultural dimensions. Clinically, the biggest early adjustments tend to be around NHS documentation standards (structured handover formats such as SBAR), a strong institutional emphasis on multidisciplinary team working, and a more codified approach to consent and capacity than some candidates are accustomed to from South African practice, although the underlying clinical training itself is generally very compatible with NHS expectations. Socially, South African doctors moving to the UK are joining an established migrant professional community, with active networks in London, Manchester, and several other regions with substantial South African populations. Most South African doctors who remain committed to the pathway report that the structured nature of UK training — clear competency frameworks, protected training time, and defined progression points — compares favourably with systems elsewhere, and this consistency is part of what continues to draw South African graduates towards the NHS.

Timeline: From Graduation in South Africa to NHS Registration

Candidates mapping out the UKMLA for South African doctors consistently underestimate how long the full pathway takes, largely because exam preparation time is easy to plan for while administrative processing time is not. The table below sets out a realistic stage-by-stage timeline based on typical current processing periods.

StageTypical DurationKey Variable
HPCSA Certificate of Status request2 to 8 weeksHPCSA processing backlog
EPIC primary source verification4 weeks to 4 monthsSpeed of your medical school's response
English language test preparation and sitting1 to 4 monthsBaseline English proficiency
AKT preparation3 to 6 monthsStudy intensity, prior NHS knowledge
AKT booking to sitting (South African centres)4 to 12 weeksTest centre date availability
CPSA preparation, including UK travel planning2 to 4 monthsVisa processing, budget readiness
CPSA booking to sitting (Manchester)2 to 6 monthsLimited sitting capacity
GMC registration application processing4 to 8 weeksDocument completeness
NHS job search and start date1 to 3 monthsNHS recruitment cycles, specialty demand

Adding these stages together, most South African candidates should plan for a realistic total timeline of 18 to 30 months from beginning EPIC verification to starting their first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. Candidates who fail either assessment on their first attempt, or who face delays at the HPCSA or EPIC stage, should expect this to extend further. Planning around the longer end of this range from the outset avoids the financial and psychological strain of a timeline that assumes everything goes smoothly.

Common Mistakes South African Doctors Make on This Route

Several avoidable errors recur consistently among candidates pursuing the UKMLA for South African doctors. Most of these mistakes are not clinical — they are planning and administrative errors that have nothing to do with medical knowledge, yet they are responsible for a disproportionate share of the delays and extra costs South African candidates experience. Being aware of them in advance, before committing money to a specific exam date or travel booking, can save months of delay and hundreds of pounds in wasted fees.

  • Starting EPIC verification too late. Because EPIC processing time depends heavily on your medical school's administrative speed rather than your own effort, this step should be initiated before — not after — you begin AKT preparation. Candidates who wait until they are ready to book the AKT before starting EPIC often lose two to three months they could have used productively.
  • Assuming the community service year automatically satisfies GMC requirements. South Africa's compulsory community service year is a rough parallel to an internship in other jurisdictions, but exactly how the GMC treats it can vary by individual case. Candidates should confirm their specific situation with the GMC or through their EPIC application rather than assuming automatic equivalence, since acting on an incorrect assumption here can cause delays later in the process.
  • Underestimating the true cost of the Manchester CPSA trip. The £1,036 exam fee itself is only part of the cost. Flights from South Africa, several nights of Manchester accommodation, local transport, food, and — for many candidates — a short UK preparation course before the exam, can together add £1,000–£2,000 on top of the fee. Candidates who budget only for the headline fee frequently find themselves scrambling for additional funds close to their exam date.
  • Not accounting for rand exchange rate volatility. Because all UKMLA and GMC fees are charged in pounds sterling, a South African candidate who calculates their rand budget many months before actually paying can find the real cost has risen substantially by the time payment is due. Build a buffer of at least 15–20% above your initial rand budget calculation, and monitor exchange rates in the weeks leading up to each payment rather than assuming an old estimate still holds.
  • Letting English test certificates expire. Both IELTS and OET certificates are valid for a defined period from the test date. Candidates who sit their English test early in the process — before EPIC verification or AKT preparation is complete — sometimes find their certificate has expired by the time they are ready to submit their full registration application, forcing a costly re-sit. Sequence your English test so its validity period comfortably covers your expected registration application date.
  • Treating document translation and certification casually. Any document not already in English requires a certified professional translation, not an informal translation from a bilingual colleague. Underestimating the time this takes, or submitting translations that do not meet the GMC's certification standard, is a common cause of application delay.
  • Assuming AKT and CPSA preparation can be compressed into the final weeks. Both assessments draw on a specific, defined content base, and candidates who begin serious preparation only a few weeks before their booked date typically underperform relative to their actual clinical knowledge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can South African doctors sit the UKMLA AKT without leaving South Africa?

Yes, in most testing windows. The AKT is delivered through Pearson VUE test centres, and South Africa has centres in cities including Johannesburg, Pretoria, Cape Town, and Durban where the AKT can typically be sat without international travel. However, candidates should always confirm current centre availability through their GMC Online account or the Pearson VUE booking portal, as available dates and locations can vary between testing windows.

Do South African doctors have to travel to the UK for the CPSA?

Yes, this step cannot be avoided. The CPSA is held exclusively at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional additional London dates during periods of high demand. There is no option to sit the CPSA remotely or anywhere within South Africa, so every South African candidate must budget for at least one trip to the UK to complete their UKMLA pathway.

Is a South African MBChB degree automatically accepted by the GMC?

Degrees from HPCSA-accredited South African medical schools generally satisfy the GMC's basic eligibility requirement, provided the school was properly accredited at the time you graduated and your qualification is listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools. However, acceptance also depends on completing primary source verification through EPIC and providing evidence of your internship and community service training. Always confirm your specific situation against the current guidance on confirming who can sit the exam rather than assuming eligibility automatically.

How much does the whole UKMLA process cost in rand?

The core GMC and exam fees — AKT, CPSA, and initial registration — total approximately £1,800, which converts to roughly R41,400 at a broadly indicative exchange rate, though this figure moves with the market and should always be rechecked before paying. Once English testing, EPIC verification, Manchester travel and accommodation, and study materials are included, a realistic total budget is closer to £3,300–£4,800, or roughly R76,000–R110,000 at the same indicative rate. Because the rand has fluctuated against the pound in recent years, candidates should recheck current rates and current GMC fee levels close to the time of each payment rather than relying on an estimate made months in advance.

Does South Africa's compulsory community service year count as the internship the GMC requires?

South Africa's community service year is a rough parallel to the internship year required in many other training systems, since both provide a period of supervised clinical practice after graduation. However, exactly how the GMC treats the community service year can depend on individual circumstances, so this should be verified directly with the GMC or clarified during the EPIC verification process rather than assumed to be an automatic equivalence.

Do South African doctors need to take an English language test if their degree was taught in English?

In most cases, yes, although some South African applicants may qualify for an exemption depending on their specific educational background — this should always be confirmed directly with the GMC. Most South African candidates need to submit a valid IELTS Academic score (7.0 overall, no component below 7.0) or OET Medicine result (Grade B minimum in all four components) as part of their application, so it is safest to plan for formal testing as a standard part of the pathway.

What is the realistic timeline from graduating in South Africa to working in the NHS?

Most candidates should plan for 18 to 30 months from starting EPIC verification to beginning their first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. This accounts for HPCSA document processing, EPIC verification delays, English test preparation, exam preparation for both assessments, and the GMC registration application itself. Candidates who need to re-sit either exam, or who face slower-than-average verification, should expect this timeline to extend.

How does the South African route compare to the route from other major source countries?

The overall structure — degree recognition, primary source verification, English testing, a knowledge exam, a clinical skills exam in the UK, then registration — is broadly the same across major IMG source countries, including the equivalent route taken by Nigerian-trained doctors and the route followed by doctors trained in India. What differs between countries is the specific verification body, typical processing times, local AKT test centre availability, and the size and structure of the existing diaspora network available to support new arrivals. For a full breakdown of how fees and requirements compare across every IMG route, see the guide to the full IMG fee breakdown across every route.

Conclusion

The UKMLA for South African doctors is a well-defined pathway supported by decades of South African medical migration to the UK, but it rewards early planning around credential verification, realistic budgeting in rand, and careful sequencing of English testing against exam dates. Candidates who start EPIC verification early, confirm exactly how their community service year is treated by the GMC, prepare systematically against the official content map, and plan properly for the mandatory Manchester trip consistently move through the pathway faster and with fewer costly setbacks. Check the full GMC registration process →
