post_title: UKMLA AKT Test Centres: Where You Can Sit the Exam in India, Pakistan, the UK and Worldwide
post_excerpt: A full list of where candidates can sit the UKMLA AKT through Pearson VUE test centres, covering India, Pakistan, the UK and other major IMG source countries.
seo_title: UKMLA AKT Test Centres: Full Location Guide
seo_description: UKMLA AKT test centres worldwide: Pearson VUE locations in India, Pakistan, the UK and beyond, plus why the CPSA must always be sat in Manchester.
html_content:
UKMLA AKT test centres exist across a genuinely global network, which is one of the most reassuring facts for international medical graduates (IMGs) evaluating this pathway for the first time. The Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) is delivered through Pearson VUE, the same commercial test-centre network used for dozens of professional examinations worldwide, which means most candidates can sit the AKT in or near their home country without ever booking a flight. The Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA), by contrast, cannot be decentralised in the same way — it is held only at the GMC's purpose-built Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional additional London sittings released during periods of especially high demand.

This single contrast — a worldwide network for one exam, a single UK city for the other — is the most important practical fact in this guide, and it shapes almost every planning decision an IMG needs to make. Get it wrong, and you risk assuming both exams can be sat locally, only to discover late in your planning that a mandatory UK trip is unavoidable. This guide sets out exactly where UKMLA AKT test centre locations exist across India, Pakistan, the UK, and other major IMG source regions, how the booking process actually works through GMC Online and Pearson VUE, what to expect on the day itself, and why the CPSA remains centralised while the AKT does not.

Throughout, we cite the GMC's own guidance on the MLA assessments as the authoritative source, and we deliberately avoid presenting any single city list as permanent — Pearson VUE centre availability changes between sitting windows, sometimes significantly, and the only reliable way to confirm a specific location is currently open is through your GMC Online account at the time you are ready to book.

The Quick Answer: Where Can You Sit the UKMLA AKT and CPSA?

The AKT is a computer-based knowledge test delivered through the global Pearson VUE test centre network, so most IMG candidates can sit it in their home country or a nearby regional hub rather than travelling internationally. The CPSA, however, is a face-to-face clinical and communication skills assessment that can only be sat at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional supplementary London dates added when demand is unusually high. In practical terms, this means a typical IMG candidate sits the AKT locally — in a city such as Delhi, Lahore, Lagos, or Dubai — and then makes exactly one dedicated trip to the UK later in the pathway purely to complete the CPSA.

This split exists because the two assessments test fundamentally different things. The AKT examines applied medical knowledge and clinical decision-making through several hundred multiple-choice-style questions, a format that Pearson VUE's standardised, secure computer terminals can deliver identically anywhere in the world. The CPSA, however, requires candidates to interact with simulated patients across a circuit of timed stations, assessed in person by trained examiners using a standardised marking framework — something that genuinely cannot be replicated reliably at a rented computer terminal on the other side of the world. Understanding this distinction early prevents the single most common planning error IMG candidates make: assuming that because the AKT is available locally, the CPSA might eventually follow the same model. It will not, at least not under the current assessment design, and budgeting from day one for a UK trip avoids a nasty surprise later in the process.

For a broader look at what the AKT itself actually tests, rather than where it is delivered, see the dedicated guide to AKT format and preparation, and for the full official specification of both assessments, consult the AKT exam pattern pillar page and CPSA exam pattern pillar page directly.

How Booking a UKMLA AKT Test Centre Actually Works

Booking an AKT sitting is not a standalone process — it runs entirely through your GMC Online account, which links directly to the Pearson VUE booking portal once you are confirmed as eligible to sit the exam. Candidates do not book with Pearson VUE independently; instead, GMC Online authorises your eligibility, generates the booking window, and hands you off to Pearson VUE's system to choose a specific date, time, and test centre location.

Step-by-Step: From Eligibility to a Confirmed Seat

  1. Confirm eligibility and create or update your GMC Online account. This account is the single hub for the entire UKMLA pathway, and it is where your booking window opens once your documentation and English language evidence are in order.
  2. Watch for the booking window announcement. The GMC publishes exam sitting periods well in advance, and booking windows for each period open ahead of the sitting itself — often several months prior — so candidates who register their interest early get first access to the widest choice of centres and dates.
  3. Log into GMC Online and follow the link through to the Pearson VUE booking portal. From here you select your preferred country, city, and specific test centre, along with a date and time slot within the current sitting window.
  4. Pay the AKT fee to confirm your booking. Your seat is not secured until payment clears, so avoid a slow bank transfer method close to a popular date if you are worried about losing your preferred slot.
  5. Receive and retain your confirmation, including the exact test centre address and reporting time. Keep this accessible on your phone and in printed form, since you may be asked to show it during check-in.

Because popular centres and dates fill quickly, particularly in high-demand cities such as Delhi/NCR, Lagos, and Dubai, candidates should book as soon as a suitable slot appears rather than waiting for a "better" date closer to their target. In addition, candidates preparing around other commitments — a current job, a visa timeline, or a planned CPSA trip later in the year — should treat the AKT booking window as a hard planning constraint rather than a flexible target, since a missed window typically means waiting for the next sitting period several months later.

Matching Test Centre Bookings to the Wider UKMLA Calendar

Test centre availability does not exist in isolation from the rest of the UKMLA calendar, so it helps to think of booking a specific AKT test centre as one part of a wider scheduling exercise rather than an isolated task. The GMC publishes the exam sitting periods, application deadlines, and results dates for both the AKT and the CPSA well in advance, and cross-referencing these against your own eligibility timeline — EPIC verification, English language test validity, and any visa planning — avoids the common mistake of finding an available test centre but discovering too late that you have missed the application deadline for that particular sitting period. For the full, up-to-date schedule of sitting periods, application windows, and results dates, always check the site's UKMLA key dates pillar page rather than relying on a remembered date from a previous year, since exact dates shift annually.

Furthermore, because booking windows for popular test centres close well before the sitting period itself begins, candidates should treat the published key dates as a countdown to action rather than simply a calendar reference. A candidate who checks the key dates page only once, months before their intended sitting, risks missing the exact week a booking window opens for their preferred city. Revisiting the key dates page periodically as your target sitting period approaches is a simple habit that meaningfully reduces the risk of losing a preferred test centre slot to another candidate.

Country-by-Country UKMLA AKT Test Centre Overview

The sections below summarise where UKMLA AKT test centre locations have typically been available for the largest IMG candidate populations. However, every single list in this guide should be treated as indicative rather than fixed — Pearson VUE adds, removes, and temporarily suspends centres between sitting windows based on demand, contractual arrangements, and local operating conditions, and the GMC itself directs candidates to confirm the live, current list at the time of booking rather than relying on any previously published guide, including this one.

UKMLA AKT Test Centres in India

India represents the single largest source of AKT candidates of any country, and Pearson VUE's network there reflects this. Major cities that have historically hosted Pearson VUE test centres suitable for the AKT include Delhi/NCR, Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai, Hyderabad, and Kolkata, alongside a number of smaller regional centres in cities with strong Pearson VUE coverage. Candidates based in India therefore usually have a genuine choice of city rather than being tied to a single national location, which in turn creates more flexibility around travel time and cost compared with candidates in countries served by only one or two centres.

That said, this list should not be treated as exhaustive or permanently fixed. Centre availability in India shifts between sitting windows, and a city that hosted a centre during one testing period may have reduced capacity or a different specific venue during the next. Consequently, Indian candidates should always cross-check the live list of open centres and dates directly through GMC Online or the Pearson VUE booking portal before finalising travel plans around a specific city, rather than assuming a friend's or forum post's experience from a previous sitting still applies. For the fuller route-level detail relevant to Indian candidates — eligibility, credential verification, and the realistic end-to-end timeline — see the dedicated guide on how to become a doctor in the UK from India, which remains the single largest audience for this pathway.

UKMLA AKT Test Centres in Pakistan

Pakistan is another major source country, and Pearson VUE centres suitable for the AKT have typically been available in Karachi, Lahore, and Islamabad, covering the country's three largest metropolitan areas. As with India, this list is not guaranteed to remain static, and candidates should confirm current availability before assuming a particular city will have an open slot in their target sitting window. Detailed route-specific guidance — including the credential verification process through the Pakistan Medical and Dental Council and a realistic timeline from graduation to NHS registration — is covered in the dedicated guide to the UKMLA pathway for Pakistani doctors, which also discusses the local test-centre landscape in more depth.

UKMLA AKT Test Centres in the UK

UK-based candidates, whether medical students sitting the AKT as part of their undergraduate assessment or IMGs already resident in the UK, benefit from the widest and most stable test-centre coverage of any single country, since Pearson VUE maintains multiple permanent centres across major UK cities including London, Manchester, Birmingham, Edinburgh, and several other regional hubs. This density means UK-based candidates typically have considerably more flexibility over date and location than candidates relying on a single national centre elsewhere, and it also means a missed or rescheduled booking is usually easier to accommodate, since an alternative nearby centre is often available within the same sitting window.

UKMLA AKT Test Centres in Other Major IMG Source Regions

Beyond India and Pakistan, Pearson VUE's global footprint extends into most of the other major IMG source regions that feed the UK medical workforce, including Nigeria, the Gulf and wider Middle East, Bangladesh, Egypt, Sri Lanka, and South Africa. In Nigeria, for example, Pearson VUE centres have typically served candidates in Lagos and Abuja; in the Gulf, hubs such as Dubai and other major cities commonly host centres serving candidates from across the Middle East. Rather than repeat every country's centre list here, each of these routes has its own dedicated guide with more detailed, country-specific coverage: see the guides for the UKMLA pathway for Nigerian doctors, the UKMLA pathway for Bangladeshi doctors, the UKMLA pathway for Egyptian doctors, the UKMLA pathway for Gulf-region doctors, the UKMLA pathway for Sri Lankan doctors, and the UKMLA pathway for South African doctors. Each of those guides pairs its local test-centre discussion with the specific credential verification body, English language expectations, and typical timeline relevant to that country.

UKMLA AKT Test Centre Locations at a Glance

The table below summarises the broad regional picture described above. As with every list in this guide, treat it as a starting point for planning, not a guarantee of current availability — always verify the live list before booking.

Country / RegionTypical Cities With Pearson VUE CentresWhere to Confirm the Current List
IndiaDelhi/NCR, Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai, Hyderabad, KolkataGMC Online / Pearson VUE booking portal
PakistanKarachi, Lahore, IslamabadGMC Online / Pearson VUE booking portal
NigeriaLagos, AbujaGMC Online / Pearson VUE booking portal
Gulf / Middle EastDubai and other major regional hubsGMC Online / Pearson VUE booking portal
United KingdomLondon, Manchester, Birmingham, Edinburgh, and other regional citiesGMC Online / Pearson VUE booking portal
CPSA (all candidates worldwide)Manchester only, with occasional additional London datesGMC Online

Notice that every row in this table, apart from the CPSA row, points back to the same instruction: confirm the current list yourself. This is not a hedge added for legal caution — it genuinely reflects how Pearson VUE manages capacity, opening and closing specific centres in response to real-time demand across dozens of professional exams that share the same physical infrastructure, not just the UKMLA.

What to Do if There Is No Test Centre in Your City or Country

Not every candidate lives within easy reach of an open Pearson VUE centre for a given sitting window, and this is a genuinely common situation rather than a rare edge case. If your city or country currently shows no available AKT slot, or if every centre in your usual location is fully booked for your target window, you have several realistic options.

  • Travel to the nearest available centre in a neighbouring city or country. Many candidates from smaller cities or countries with limited Pearson VUE coverage already plan to travel to a regional hub — for example, a candidate from a smaller Gulf state travelling to Dubai, or a candidate from a Pakistani city without a dedicated centre travelling to Lahore or Karachi.
  • Check adjacent sitting windows rather than fixating on one date. Test centre capacity is not evenly distributed across every window; a fully booked date in one period is often more available a few weeks later once the next window opens.
  • Book as early as your eligibility allows. Since seats fill on a first-come basis, candidates who wait until close to a sitting period consistently find the widest and most convenient range of options already gone.
  • Build travel time and cost into your overall budget from the outset, rather than treating it as an unplanned extra expense discovered only once you try to book. This mirrors the same principle used when budgeting for the mandatory Manchester CPSA trip, discussed below.

In addition, candidates in this position should resist the temptation to delay their AKT attempt indefinitely while waiting for a more convenient local centre to appear. Given that centre availability is not guaranteed to improve in any specific timeframe, planning around the nearest currently available centre is usually the more reliable strategy than waiting on the possibility of a new local option opening.

Choosing Between a Local and a Regional Test Centre

Even within a single country, candidates often have a genuine choice between sitting the AKT in their home city or travelling to a larger regional hub, and this decision deserves more thought than it usually gets. A candidate in a smaller Indian city, for example, might weigh a shorter, cheaper journey to a nearby state capital against a longer trip to a larger metropolitan test centre that historically has offered a wider range of dates. Similarly, a candidate based outside the three largest Pakistani cities may need to decide between Karachi, Lahore, and Islamabad based on flight availability, cost, and which city has family or friends who can host them overnight before the exam.

Several practical factors are worth weighing when more than one UKMLA AKT test centre option exists within reach. Firstly, consider which centre has historically offered the greater number of available dates within your target sitting window, since a wider spread of dates gives you more flexibility if your preparation timeline shifts. Secondly, factor in the reliability of travel to each option — a short flight with frequent alternatives if one is delayed is often a safer choice than a single long-distance train journey with no realistic backup plan on exam morning. Thirdly, weigh the value of arriving the night before against attempting to travel and sit the exam on the same day; the latter introduces unnecessary risk for very little cost saving, particularly for a high-stakes assessment where a late arrival could mean losing your booking entirely.

For candidates travelling any meaningful distance to a test centre, arriving the previous evening and staying locally overnight is usually the safer choice, even where a same-day journey is theoretically possible. This buffer absorbs minor disruptions — a delayed flight, unexpected traffic, or a late train — without threatening your ability to reach the centre by the required arrival time. Many candidates who have sat the AKT report that the modest additional cost of an overnight stay is a worthwhile trade against the far higher cost, in money and lost time, of missing a booked sitting entirely and having to wait for the next available window.

What the AKT Test Centre Experience Is Like on the Day

The AKT is delivered as a computer-based examination under secure, standardised conditions similar to other major international professional exams run through Pearson VUE. Consequently, the day-to-day experience is broadly consistent regardless of which country or city you sit in, which is itself a reassurance for candidates unfamiliar with the specific venue.

Before You Arrive

Candidates should plan to arrive at the test centre well ahead of their scheduled start time — Pearson VUE centres typically ask candidates to arrive at least 30 minutes before the appointment to allow time for check-in procedures, and arriving late can result in being refused entry and losing the booking entirely. It is worth confirming the exact recommended arrival time on your specific confirmation email or GMC Online booking record, since this can vary slightly by centre.

Identification and Security Procedures

On arrival, candidates must present valid, unexpired photographic identification that matches the name used on their GMC Online booking exactly — typically a passport, since this is the identification document most consistently accepted across Pearson VUE's international network. Centre staff will verify your identity, may take a digital photograph and signature, and will ask you to empty your pockets and store personal belongings, including phones, watches, and study materials, in a secure locker for the duration of the exam. Security procedures at Pearson VUE centres are deliberately strict and standardised worldwide, mirroring the security protocols used for other major professional and academic examinations, in order to protect the integrity of the assessment for every candidate.

During the Exam

Once checked in, candidates are seated at an individual computer terminal in a monitored testing room, with the exam interface displaying questions and answer options on screen and controlled by mouse and keyboard. The room is typically monitored both in person and via CCTV, and candidates are not permitted to communicate with other test-takers or bring in any unauthorised material. Scheduled optional breaks, where the exam format includes them, do not stop the exam clock in most Pearson VUE-delivered assessments, so candidates should plan their pacing with this in mind rather than assuming break time is added on top of the total testing time.

After the Exam

At the end of the sitting, candidates typically hand back any provided materials, retrieve their belongings from the secure locker, and leave the centre. Results are not usually available immediately at the centre itself; instead, they are communicated through GMC Online according to the GMC's published results timeline for that sitting period.

Common Exam-Day Logistics Questions

Beyond the formal check-in and security steps, several practical logistics questions come up repeatedly among first-time AKT candidates. Parking and public transport access vary considerably between individual Pearson VUE locations, so it is worth checking the specific centre's address and transport options in the days before your sitting rather than assuming every centre is easily reached by the same method. Similarly, food and drink are not usually permitted inside the testing room itself, so candidates should eat beforehand and plan around any scheduled breaks rather than expecting to bring refreshments to the desk. Finally, candidates with a disability or a condition requiring reasonable adjustments — extra time, a separate room, or assistive equipment — should raise this with the GMC well in advance of booking, since adjustments are arranged through the GMC rather than negotiated directly with the test centre on the day.

Rescheduling and Cancellation: What to Expect

Circumstances change, and candidates sometimes need to reschedule or cancel a booked AKT sitting. Rather than quoting a specific deadline or fee here — since these are exactly the kind of detail that shifts between sitting periods and that this guide cannot promise remains accurate by the time you read it — the safest approach is to treat GMC Online and the Pearson VUE booking portal as the only authoritative source for current rescheduling and cancellation terms. In general terms, professional testing providers such as Pearson VUE typically apply a tiered system where changes made well in advance of the exam date carry little or no penalty, while changes made close to the sitting date carry a higher fee or may not be possible at all. Candidates who anticipate any uncertainty around their availability — for example, a pending visa decision, a work rota that has not yet been confirmed, or ongoing travel restrictions — should check the exact current policy before booking, rather than assuming standard consumer-friendly terms will apply. This is a case where verifying the live policy directly protects both your money and your exam date.

Why the CPSA Cannot Be Decentralised Like the AKT

It is worth explaining explicitly why the CPSA remains fixed to Manchester rather than following the AKT's global model, since candidates unfamiliar with the format sometimes assume this is simply an administrative choice that could change at any time. In practice, it reflects a genuine structural difference between the two assessments.

The AKT is a knowledge test: it presents standardised questions on a secure computer terminal, and delivering it correctly requires nothing more than a controlled room, a monitored terminal, and a trained invigilator — infrastructure that Pearson VUE already operates identically in hundreds of cities worldwide for many different examinations. The CPSA, however, is a simulated clinical encounter: candidates rotate through a circuit of timed stations, each involving a trained role-player acting as a patient, and a qualified examiner assessing the candidate's clinical reasoning, communication, and professionalism in real time against a standardised marking framework. Reproducing this reliably requires purpose-built clinical simulation space, a large pool of trained role-players and examiners working to identical standards, and rigorous quality assurance across every station and every sitting — none of which can be outsourced to a generic computer-testing vendor the way a knowledge exam can.

Consequently, the GMC has chosen to concentrate this infrastructure at a single, purpose-built Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester rather than attempting to replicate it at dozens of international sites, which would risk inconsistent marking standards between locations. Occasional additional CPSA sittings in London are added during particularly high-demand periods, but this remains an extension of the same centralised model rather than a step toward a genuinely global network like the AKT's. For IMG candidates, the practical implication is straightforward: budget for exactly one mandatory UK trip — flights, a UK visa if required, several nights of accommodation, and living costs during preparation and the assessment itself — as a fixed, non-negotiable part of the pathway, rather than something that might be avoided with enough searching for a local alternative.

Budgeting for the Manchester Trip Once Your AKT Test Centre Sitting Is Passed

Because the AKT can be sat locally while the CPSA cannot, candidates sometimes budget carefully for the exam fees themselves but underestimate the surrounding travel costs of the Manchester stage. A realistic budget for the UK trip should include return flights from your home country, a UK visitor visa application if your nationality requires one, several nights of accommodation in or near Manchester for preparation and the assessment itself, local transport, and food and incidental costs for the duration of the stay. Many candidates also choose to arrive several days early to adjust to the time zone, attend a short preparation course, or simply settle their nerves before the assessment, all of which add further nights of accommodation to the total. Treating this trip as a single, clearly budgeted line item — planned as far in advance as your AKT test centre booking — rather than an afterthought once the CPSA becomes bookable, avoids the scramble for funds that many first-time candidates report experiencing.

Once both assessments are passed, the remaining steps to full GMC registration are administrative rather than exam-related, and the site's dedicated GMC registration guide walks through exactly what happens next — document submission, processing times, and what to do if your application is queried. Reading this guide alongside your CPSA travel planning helps you sequence the final stages of the pathway correctly, since some registration documentation can be prepared and submitted while you are still finalising your Manchester travel arrangements.

Planning Around Limited Sitting Windows

Because both the AKT and the CPSA are only offered during defined sitting periods rather than on demand, planning around these windows is as important as preparing for the exam content itself. Candidates should treat the overall UKMLA timeline as bounded by sitting-window availability, not simply by how quickly they finish revising. In addition, since the CPSA can only be booked once the AKT has been passed, a missed or delayed AKT sitting has a knock-on effect on the entire remaining timeline, potentially pushing a candidate's CPSA attempt back by several months rather than a few weeks. For this reason, experienced advisers consistently recommend booking the earliest realistic AKT sitting available to you, rather than waiting for a date that feels more comfortable relative to your revision schedule, since the downstream cost of delay is usually higher than the benefit of a few extra weeks of preparation.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I take the UKMLA AKT in India?

Yes. Pearson VUE has historically maintained AKT-suitable test centres in several major Indian cities, including Delhi/NCR, Mumbai, Bangalore, Chennai, Hyderabad, and Kolkata. However, exact centre availability changes between sitting windows, so Indian candidates should always confirm the live, current list through their GMC Online account or the Pearson VUE booking portal before finalising travel plans.

Are there UKMLA AKT test centres in Pakistan?

Yes. Pakistani candidates have typically had access to Pearson VUE centres in Karachi, Lahore, and Islamabad, covering the country's three largest cities. As with every country covered in this guide, candidates should verify current availability directly through GMC Online rather than relying on a previous sitting's centre list, since availability is not guaranteed to remain identical between testing periods.

Do I have to travel to the UK for the CPSA?

Yes, for every candidate worldwide. The CPSA is held exclusively at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional additional London dates released during high-demand periods, and there is no remote, computer-based, or overseas alternative. Every IMG candidate should budget for at least one dedicated UK trip to complete this stage of the pathway, regardless of where they sat the AKT.

How do I book a UKMLA AKT test centre?

Booking runs entirely through your GMC Online account, which links to the Pearson VUE booking portal once your eligibility is confirmed. You select your preferred country, city, specific test centre, and time slot within the current sitting window, then pay the exam fee to confirm your seat. Because popular centres and dates fill quickly, it is best to book as soon as a suitable slot becomes available rather than waiting closer to the sitting period.

What happens if there is no AKT test centre near me?

If your city or country has no currently available centre, or every local slot is fully booked, the realistic options are travelling to the nearest available centre in a neighbouring city or country, or checking a later sitting window where capacity may have opened up. Candidates in this position should book as early as their eligibility allows, since demand for the nearest alternative centre is often high among other candidates facing the same gap.

What should I bring to the AKT test centre on exam day?

You should bring valid, unexpired photographic identification, typically a passport, matching the name on your GMC Online booking exactly, along with your exam confirmation details. Personal items including phones, watches, and study materials are not permitted in the testing room and must be stored in a secure locker provided by the centre, and candidates should plan to arrive at least 30 minutes before their scheduled appointment time.

Can UKMLA AKT test centre availability change between sitting windows?

Yes, and this is one of the most important practical points in this entire guide. Pearson VUE opens, closes, and reallocates specific test centres in response to demand and operational factors, so a city that hosted a centre during one sitting period may have reduced capacity, a different specific venue, or no centre at all during a later period. Always confirm the live, current list through GMC Online or the Pearson VUE booking portal at the time you are ready to book, rather than relying on any previously published list, including the one in this guide.

Is the CPSA ever offered outside Manchester?

Occasionally. The GMC has, during periods of especially high demand, released additional CPSA sitting dates in London alongside the standard Manchester schedule. However, this remains an extension of the same centralised model rather than a step toward decentralising the assessment the way the AKT already is, so candidates should still expect Manchester to be the default and most consistently available location.

Conclusion

Finding the right UKMLA AKT test centre is usually far simpler than candidates initially expect, thanks to Pearson VUE's genuinely global network across India, Pakistan, the UK, and most other major IMG source regions. The CPSA, however, remains a fixed, unavoidable trip to Manchester for every candidate worldwide, and treating that trip as a planned, budgeted certainty from the very start of your pathway — rather than a late-stage surprise — is the single most useful piece of practical advice in this guide. Always confirm the current, live test centre list through GMC Online before booking either assessment, since availability shifts between sitting windows in ways no static guide can fully anticipate. Read the full AKT exam pattern guide →

post_title: GMC Registration and EPIC Verification for Doctors From India: A Step-by-Step Guide
post_excerpt: How EPIC primary source verification and GMC registration actually work for Indian medical graduates, with documents, timelines and common delays explained.
seo_title: GMC Registration From India: EPIC Verification
seo_description: GMC registration from India: how EPIC verification works, required documents, MCI/NMC certificates, processing times, and completing your GMC application.
html_content:
GMC registration from India is the final formal step in becoming a licensed doctor in the United Kingdom, and it can only happen once every other requirement in the pathway has already been satisfied. Before a doctor can register with the General Medical Council and hold a licence to practise, they must have passed both components of the UKMLA, submitted acceptable evidence of English language proficiency, and completed primary source verification of their Indian medical qualification through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials). Put simply, GMC registration is not a parallel task you can chip away at alongside exam preparation — it is the last domino, and it falls only after the others are already standing in place.

This guide goes deep into the two stages of the journey that Indian candidates consistently find most confusing and most time-consuming: EPIC verification of an Indian MBBS degree, and the registration application itself once EPIC, English evidence, and both exam passes are confirmed. It sits alongside the site's broader guide to how to become a doctor in the UK from India, which covers the full route end to end, but this article focuses specifically and procedurally on document verification and the mechanics of the GMC registration process for Indian doctors, including the India-specific complication of the 2020/2021 transition from the Medical Council of India to the National Medical Commission.

Indian doctors make up one of the largest single cohorts of international medical graduates on the UK medical register, and consequently the GMC's systems and guidance are well accustomed to Indian degree certificates, Indian transcripts, and Indian regulatory bodies. That familiarity does not, however, make the process instant. Indian medical schools vary enormously in how quickly their academic offices respond to verification requests, and the NMC transition has introduced a genuine, practical wrinkle that catches out candidates who registered under the old MCI system. This guide sets out exactly what to expect at each stage, with realistic timeframes and a clear document checklist, so that Indian applicants can plan the administrative side of their pathway with the same rigour they bring to exam preparation.

It is worth setting expectations honestly from the outset: nothing in this guide should be read as a guarantee of a specific processing time, since both EPIC verification and GMC registration processing depend on factors outside any single candidate's control. Instead, this guide focuses on giving Indian applicants a realistic planning framework — what typically happens, in what order, and what to do when something does not go to plan — so that decisions about when to book exams, when to travel, and when to request documents are made with genuine information rather than guesswork. Before beginning any of the steps below, it is also worth double-checking that you meet the underlying eligibility criteria in the first place; the site's dedicated UKMLA and GMC registration eligibility guide sets out the full requirements across qualification recognition, English language evidence, and health and character declarations that apply to every applicant regardless of country of training.

What GMC Registration With a Licence to Practise Actually Means

GMC registration with a licence to practise is the legal authorisation that allows a doctor to work as a doctor in the United Kingdom — without it, passing the UKMLA alone does not entitle anyone to practise medicine on the NHS or in private UK healthcare. Registration is granted by the General Medical Council only after a candidate has cleared four separate hurdles: eligibility confirmation, primary source verification of their medical qualification through EPIC, acceptable evidence of English language proficiency, and passes in both components of the UKMLA — the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA).

This ordering matters because many candidates mistakenly treat the GMC registration process for Indian doctors as something that can be started only after the exams are passed. In reality, EPIC verification and English language testing should both be initiated well before the exams are sat, since they run on their own independent timelines and do not depend on exam results. Sequencing GMC registration from India correctly — starting the document-heavy steps early and in parallel with exam preparation, rather than treating them as an afterthought — is the single biggest factor separating candidates who reach registration within two years of starting from those who take considerably longer.

It is also worth being precise about what registration confers. Registration with a licence to practise is what allows a doctor to take up medical employment in the UK; it is distinct from, and a prerequisite to, applying for specific NHS posts. Once registration is granted, the practical next step for most Indian doctors is applying for NHS jobs, typically starting with trust-grade or clinical fellow positions while building a UK employment history. Registering with the GMC from India therefore is not the end goal in itself — it is the gateway that makes the rest of a UK medical career possible.

There are, in practice, two broad categories of GMC registration relevant to doctors coming through the UKMLA route: provisional registration and full registration with a licence to practise. Most Indian IMGs entering through the UKMLA pathway move directly to full registration with a licence to practise, since provisional registration is more commonly associated with UK medical graduates entering the Foundation Programme straight after their primary qualification. Understanding which category applies to your specific situation matters because the supporting evidence required, and the point at which you become eligible to apply, differ slightly between the two. If in doubt about which registration type applies to your circumstances, GMC Online's own eligibility checker, together with the guidance referenced throughout this article, is the most reliable way to confirm before you submit anything.

What EPIC Is and How Primary Source Verification Works

EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials) is the online platform the GMC uses to carry out primary source verification of an international medical graduate's qualifications — meaning the degree, the transcripts, and often the internship record are confirmed directly with the institution that issued them, not simply accepted on the strength of a certified photocopy. For Indian doctors, this means your MBBS degree certificate and academic transcripts are verified directly with your Indian medical school, and this step is a mandatory, non-negotiable part of GMC registration from India — it cannot be skipped, expedited by paying a larger fee, or substituted with a notarised copy of your documents.

The distinction between EPIC verification and simply submitting a certified copy is the point most candidates misunderstand at the outset. A certified copy only confirms that a photocopy matches an original document you physically hold; it says nothing about whether the issuing institution itself confirms the degree was genuinely awarded. EPIC verification, by contrast, involves the GMC's verification partner contacting your medical school directly and requesting that the school confirm, from its own records, that you were awarded the qualification claimed, on the date claimed, following a programme of the length and content claimed. This is a materially higher bar, and it is precisely why it takes weeks rather than days.

Creating an EPIC Account and Submitting Your Documents

The EPIC verification process begins with creating an EPIC account, which is done through a link accessed via your GMC Online account once you have confirmed basic eligibility. Within EPIC, you register your Indian medical school, your MBBS degree details, and your graduation year, and you then submit the required supporting documents for that qualification. The platform routes a verification request to your medical school's designated EPIC liaison or academic/examination office, which is then asked to confirm the details directly with EPIC rather than with you.

Because the verification request goes to the institution rather than to you, your own responsiveness is not the limiting factor once the request has been submitted — your medical school's is. This is an important mental shift for candidates used to systems where personal effort directly speeds up an outcome. Once you have submitted your EPIC application correctly, the most productive thing you can do is monitor progress and follow up periodically with your medical school's registrar, rather than assume there is nothing more you can do until a decision arrives.

Full, authoritative detail on how the process works, exactly which documents are required, and how the GMC uses the resulting verification is published directly by the regulator: see the GMC's guidance on primary source verification for international medical graduates. Candidates should read this page in full before creating an EPIC account, since it is updated periodically and takes precedence over any general summary, including this one.

How EPIC Differs From Submitting a Certified Copy

It is worth restating this distinction because it is the most common point of confusion for first-time applicants. Submitting a certified copy of your degree certificate demonstrates that the document in front of the GMC matches an original you hold; it does not demonstrate that the original itself is genuine or that the underlying academic record is complete and accurate. EPIC verification, in contrast, requires your Indian medical school to independently confirm your qualification details from its own institutional records, communicated directly to EPIC's verification team rather than relayed through you. Consequently, a candidate cannot shortcut the process by presenting an especially well-notarised or apostilled certificate — the verification always has to originate from the institution itself, regardless of how the paperwork is presented.

This distinction exists precisely because credential fraud is a genuine, historically documented problem across international medical migration generally, and primary source verification is the tool regulators worldwide use to guard against it. For Indian candidates specifically, this means that even a completely genuine degree from a well-regarded medical school still has to go through the identical verification pathway as any other applicant — there is no fast-track, no reputational shortcut, and no way to have EPIC verification waived on the basis of institutional prestige alone. Every applicant, regardless of which of India's medical schools they attended, is verified against the same standard.

What EPIC Verification Costs and Who Pays

EPIC verification carries its own fee, separate from GMC registration fees, exam fees, and English testing costs, and this fee is generally payable by the candidate at the point of submitting the EPIC application. Because EPIC fees, like all charges across the pathway, are subject to periodic revision, candidates should confirm the current amount directly on the EPIC platform or through GMC Online rather than budgeting from a figure that may already be out of date by the time they apply. Building this cost into your overall pathway budget from the outset, alongside AKT and CPSA fees and the eventual registration fee, avoids an unwelcome surprise partway through the process.

The MCI-to-NMC Transition and What It Means for Indian Applicants

India's medical regulatory landscape changed substantially in 2020 and 2021, when the Medical Council of India (MCI) was dissolved and replaced by the National Medical Commission (NMC). This transition has direct, practical consequences for Indian doctors pursuing GMC registration, because the correct body to approach for a Certificate of Good Standing or equivalent registration confirmation now depends on when and how you were originally registered.

In broad terms, doctors whose registration and disciplinary history predate the transition may need documentation that reflects both their historical MCI registration and their current status under the NMC or the relevant State Medical Council, whereas doctors who registered more recently will generally deal exclusively with NMC-era processes and records. However, exactly how each individual case is handled by the GMC — and precisely which certificates or confirmations are accepted as sufficient evidence of good standing — can depend on details specific to your registration history, your State Medical Council, and the current state of record migration from MCI to NMC systems.

This is an area where the site cannot responsibly generalise with full confidence, because the transition involved considerable administrative reorganisation, and the practical experience of individual Indian doctors requesting registration confirmation has varied. Candidates should verify their own specific requirement directly with the GMC and with the NMC or their State Medical Council before assuming a particular document will be accepted, rather than relying on a general guide such as this one for a case-specific answer. The safest approach is to request confirmation of exactly which certificate the GMC expects for your particular registration history as early as possible in your EPIC application, since resolving any ambiguity here late in the process is one of the more common causes of delay for Indian candidates specifically.

How Indian Medical Schools Typically Respond to EPIC Requests

Response times from Indian medical schools to EPIC verification requests vary widely, and this variation is one of the most significant sources of unpredictability in the entire GMC registration process for Indian doctors. Well-resourced institutions with a dedicated international affairs or examinations office — particularly those that regularly handle similar verification requests from other countries' regulators — often respond within four to ten weeks. Other institutions, especially where verification requests are handled informally or by administrative staff without a dedicated process for this kind of request, can take three to six months, and in a minority of cases considerably longer.

These figures are indicative rather than guaranteed, and they should be treated as planning ranges rather than promises. Response speed depends on factors outside any individual candidate's control: whether the request arrives during an academic term or examination period, whether the school's records for your specific graduating cohort are digitised or still held on paper, and whether the person responsible for EPIC correspondence is a permanent staff member or someone administering the task alongside other duties. Given this variability, initiating EPIC verification as early as possible — ideally well before AKT preparation begins in earnest — is the most reliable way to prevent it from becoming the bottleneck that determines your overall timeline.

Anecdotally, candidates who graduated more recently from institutions with digitised records tend to experience faster turnaround than those relying on paper archives from cohorts several years or decades in the past, since a digitised record can be located and confirmed far more quickly than one requiring a physical file to be retrieved from storage. This is not a hard rule, and older graduates from well-organised institutions can still experience swift verification; nevertheless, it is a pattern worth being aware of when estimating your own likely timeline. If your medical school has not responded within what feels like a reasonable interval, a polite, specific follow-up email — referencing your EPIC application number and the date of your original request — is generally more effective than a vague inquiry, since it gives the academic office a concrete reference point to act on rather than requiring them to search for your file from scratch.

Document Checklist for Indian Candidates

Indian applicants pursuing GMC registration typically need to gather a consistent set of documents, and assembling them in parallel rather than one at a time saves considerable elapsed time over the course of the pathway.

DocumentPurposeTypical Source
MBBS degree certificateConfirms the primary medical qualification claimedIssuing Indian medical university
Full academic transcriptsConfirms subjects studied, duration, and academic performanceMedical school examinations/academic office
Internship completion certificateConfirms the mandatory rotatory internship year was completedMedical school or teaching hospital where internship was served
NMC or State Medical Council registration certificateConfirms current, unrestricted registration to practise in IndiaNational Medical Commission or relevant State Medical Council
Certificate of Good Standing (or equivalent confirmation)Confirms registration status and disciplinary historyNMC/State Medical Council — exact issuing body depends on registration history
Valid passportIdentity verificationRegional Passport Office
Evidence of name discrepancies, if applicableResolves mismatches between degree name and passport nameGazette notification, marriage certificate, or statutory declaration
Certified translationsRequired for any document not already issued in EnglishRecognised professional translation service

Two items in this list are worth flagging specifically because they recur so often as sources of delay. First, the internship completion certificate is sometimes overlooked by candidates who assume their degree certificate alone is sufficient evidence of qualification, when in fact the GMC treats the completed internship as a distinct and necessary piece of evidence. Second, name discrepancies are common among Indian applicants whose degree certificate, passport, and NMC registration were issued at different times using slightly different name formats — addressing this with a gazette notification or statutory declaration before submitting your application avoids a query later that would otherwise pause your file.

A practical approach many successful candidates use is to build a single, well-organised digital folder containing every document on this checklist, named consistently and scanned at a resolution that keeps text clearly legible, before starting the EPIC application itself. Doing this once, early, rather than hunting for individual documents each time a platform asks for a new upload, considerably reduces the friction of the whole administrative process. It is also worth keeping physical originals safe and accessible throughout the pathway, since some stages — particularly identity verification and, in some cases, direct GMC queries — may still ask to see an original document rather than accepting a scanned copy alone.

Step-by-Step: The GMC Registration Application Process

Once EPIC verification, English language evidence, and passes in both UKMLA components are all confirmed, the registration application itself follows a defined sequence. Below is that sequence in the order candidates should typically action it.

  1. Create or return to your GMC Online account. This account, established at the very start of the pathway, is where the registration application itself is ultimately submitted, alongside EPIC tracking, exam bookings, and English language evidence uploads.
  2. Confirm all supporting evidence is complete. Before submitting, check that EPIC verification shows as complete, your English test certificate is valid (not expired) and correctly uploaded, and both AKT and CPSA results are recorded against your account.
  3. Submit the registration application itself, including any outstanding declarations regarding health, criminal record, and professional conduct — these are standard requirements for every applicant, Indian or otherwise, and should be answered accurately and completely rather than left blank or minimised.
  4. Complete identity verification. The GMC requires robust proof of identity as part of registration, which for many Indian candidates is completed in conjunction with a UK visit (often the same trip used for the CPSA) or through an approved identity-verification route specified by the GMC for applicants still overseas.
  5. Pay the registration fee and submit the completed application for processing.
  6. Await the outcome. Processing times vary, but candidates whose supporting documents are complete and unambiguous typically experience considerably faster processing than those whose files require the GMC to raise a query — covered in detail below.

Processing times for a complete, query-free application are generally measured in a small number of weeks; however, this should be treated as an indicative range rather than a guarantee, since the GMC's own processing capacity and the complexity of an individual file both affect the actual outcome. Always check current expected processing times directly on the GMC's own website rather than relying on a fixed number quoted in any third-party guide, since these timeframes are reviewed and adjusted periodically by the regulator. The GMC's official resources and guidance pages are the authoritative reference point for current fees, processing benchmarks, and required forms, and should be checked directly before every major step in the pathway rather than relied upon only at the start.

Registration fees are payable online through GMC Online at the point of application submission, and the GMC accepts standard international payment methods for overseas applicants, including Indian candidates paying from an Indian bank account or card. As with every fee mentioned in this guide, the exact amount is reviewed periodically by the regulator, so candidates should confirm the current registration fee directly on the GMC's website immediately before paying, rather than budgeting from a fee quoted at an earlier point in their planning.

Identity Verification in Practice

Identity verification deserves particular attention because it is a step some Indian candidates plan for too late. Given that many Indian applicants are still resident in India at the point their EPIC verification and exam results come through, coordinating identity verification — which may require an in-person element — alongside a planned UK trip for the CPSA is usually more efficient than treating it as a separate, later journey. Confirming exactly which identity verification route applies to your circumstances, directly through your GMC Online account guidance, well before you finalise UK travel plans avoids an unnecessary second trip.

Some Indian candidates ask whether identity verification can be completed entirely remotely, without any UK travel at all. In some circumstances an approved remote route is available; however, given that most Indian candidates already need to travel to the UK for the CPSA, combining both requirements into a single trip is usually the more efficient choice even where a remote option exists, since it avoids managing two separate verification processes on two separate timelines.

Common Delays Specific to Indian Candidates and How to Avoid Them

Several recurring issues account for a disproportionate share of the delays experienced specifically by Indian applicants during GMC registration. Recognising these in advance allows candidates to build in the kind of buffer time that prevents a minor administrative hiccup from becoming a multi-month setback.

  • Confusion between State Medical Council and NMC documentation. Because registration and disciplinary records for Indian doctors can sit with a State Medical Council, the NMC, or a combination of both depending on registration history, candidates sometimes request the wrong certificate from the wrong body, only discovering the mismatch when the GMC queries the submission. Confirming the exact required document and issuing authority before requesting anything avoids this entirely.
  • Incomplete academic transcripts. Some Indian medical schools issue transcripts that summarise overall results rather than providing the year-by-year, subject-by-subject detail the GMC's verification process expects. Requesting the most detailed transcript format your institution can issue, rather than the standard summary transcript typically given to graduates, reduces the likelihood of a follow-up request.
  • Slow academic office response to EPIC requests. As covered above, this is highly institution-dependent. Keeping a written record of every communication with your medical school's registrar or examinations office is valuable both for your own tracking and as evidence should you need to escalate an unusually slow response.
  • Translation certification that does not meet the GMC's standard. A translation completed informally by a bilingual friend or relative, however accurate, typically does not satisfy the GMC's certification requirements. Use a recognised professional translation service and confirm in advance that its certification format is one the GMC accepts.
  • Name mismatches across documents. Indian degree certificates, passports, and NMC/State Medical Council records are sometimes issued at different points in time with slightly different name renderings — for example, a middle name present on one document and abbreviated or omitted on another. Resolving this with formal evidence before submission, rather than waiting for it to be flagged, avoids an otherwise easily preventable query.
  • Sitting the English test too early or too late relative to the rest of the pathway. The English language requirement that runs alongside EPIC verification has its own certificate validity window, and timing it so the certificate remains valid through to your registration application submission requires deliberate sequencing rather than sitting the test at the first convenient opportunity.

If Your Application Is Queried by the GMC

A query from the GMC is not automatically a sign that something has gone seriously wrong — it is a routine part of the process for a meaningful proportion of applicants, and it typically means one specific piece of evidence needs clarifying or replacing rather than the whole application being in doubt. Common reasons a query is raised include an ambiguous or incomplete transcript, a name discrepancy across documents that has not been formally explained, uncertainty over which Indian regulatory body's certificate applies to your registration history given the MCI-to-NMC transition, or a translation that does not meet the required certification standard.

When a query is raised, the GMC will specify exactly what additional information or document is required, and the most effective response is a prompt, complete, and precisely targeted reply — providing exactly what has been asked for, in the format requested, rather than resubmitting a broader set of documents in the hope that one of them resolves the issue. If the query concerns which Indian body should confirm your registration status, it is worth contacting the GMC directly for clarification on the specific evidence they will accept for your circumstances, rather than guessing and risking a second round of queries. Keeping copies of every document submitted, along with dates and reference numbers for each communication, makes responding to a query considerably faster than having to reconstruct your submission history from scratch.

Most queries, once answered completely, do not significantly extend the overall timeline; however, an incomplete or vague response can trigger a second query, which does meaningfully add to processing time. Treating the first query response as the one opportunity to resolve the issue cleanly is therefore worth the extra care it takes to get right.

It is also worth understanding, in general terms, why queries happen more often for some applicants than others. Applications built from a complete, internally consistent document set — where names match across every document, transcripts are detailed rather than summarised, and the correct current Indian regulatory body has been approached for registration confirmation — are considerably less likely to generate a query in the first place. Consequently, the time invested in assembling a clean, complete file before submission is rarely wasted; it is usually recovered many times over by avoiding a query-and-response cycle that can otherwise add weeks to the overall timeline.

Realistic Timeline Planning for GMC Registration From India

Bringing every stage together, Indian candidates should plan for a realistic overall timeline rather than an optimistic best case. EPIC verification alone can take anywhere from a few weeks to several months, English language testing and its associated preparation typically adds further time, and the AKT and CPSA each carry their own preparation and booking lead times on top of the underlying administrative track. Consequently, most Indian candidates who plan carefully and avoid major setbacks should expect somewhere in the region of eighteen months to three years from beginning EPIC verification to holding full GMC registration, though individual circumstances — particularly EPIC response speed and exam performance on the first attempt — can move this substantially in either direction.

This range is deliberately wide, and it should be read as a planning tool rather than a precise prediction, since no third-party guide can account for the specific administrative speed of your particular medical school or the current processing capacity at the GMC at the time you apply. What a realistic range does allow, however, is better decision-making around finances, employment, and family planning during the pathway — candidates who assume registration will take six months are far more likely to experience financial and psychological strain than those who plan from the outset around a multi-year horizon and treat any earlier completion as a welcome bonus rather than an expectation.

Running the administrative track and the exam preparation track genuinely in parallel, rather than sequentially, is the single most effective lever available to compress this overall timeline. In practice, this means starting EPIC verification and English language testing during the same months you begin AKT preparation, rather than waiting until you feel "ready" for the exams before addressing the paperwork. Because EPIC verification in particular depends on external response times rather than personal effort, there is no efficiency gained by delaying it — every week spent waiting to start is simply a week added to your total timeline that could otherwise have been running invisibly in the background while you studied.

How This Fits the Broader India Route

EPIC verification and GMC registration sit within a wider pathway that also includes confirming basic eligibility, sitting an approved English test, and passing both UKMLA components. The underlying principle behind all of this verification activity is a straightforward one: the GMC needs to be confident that an Indian MBBS qualification is genuinely valid and equivalent to the standard expected of a UK medical graduate before granting registration, and EPIC together with the wider document checklist is simply the mechanism by which that confidence is established. Understanding this underlying purpose makes the individual steps easier to follow, since each document requested serves a specific piece of that overall confirmation rather than being arbitrary bureaucracy.

It is also worth noting who is eligible to begin this pathway in the first place. Not every Indian medical graduate automatically qualifies to sit the UKMLA or pursue GMC registration — the eligibility rules covering who can sit the exam set out specific requirements around medical school accreditation and qualification recognition that should be confirmed before investing time in EPIC verification. For the complete overview of the whole India-to-UK pathway, including exam booking, fees, and the CPSA travel requirement, see the site's dedicated guide to the full route to becoming a doctor in the UK from India.

It is also useful to see this process in contrast with another major source country. The EPIC verification experience described for Nigerian doctors follows the same underlying structure — primary source verification through the same platform, a national regulator whose Certificate of Good Standing is required, and broadly comparable response-time variability by medical school — even though the specific Indian and Nigerian regulatory bodies and typical processing patterns differ in the details. Recognising that the EPIC mechanism itself is shared across countries, while the national-regulator specifics differ, helps Indian candidates understand which parts of their experience are universal to the platform and which are unique to the Indian system.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does EPIC verification take for an Indian medical degree?

Response times vary considerably by medical school, with well-resourced institutions often responding within four to ten weeks and others taking three to six months or longer. Because this variability is driven by your institution's administrative speed rather than your own effort, starting EPIC verification as early as possible in your overall pathway is the most effective way to prevent it from delaying your registration.

Can I use a certified copy of my MBBS certificate instead of EPIC verification?

No. A certified copy only confirms that a photocopy matches an original document, whereas EPIC verification requires your Indian medical school to independently confirm your qualification details from its own institutional records. EPIC verification is a mandatory step in GMC registration from India and cannot be substituted with a certified or notarised copy, however official that copy appears.

Do I need documentation from the Medical Council of India or the National Medical Commission?

This depends on your specific registration history, since the MCI was replaced by the NMC in 2020/2021 and the correct current body for a Certificate of Good Standing or equivalent confirmation can vary accordingly. Because individual cases differ, candidates should confirm directly with the GMC and the NMC or their State Medical Council exactly which certificate is required for their circumstances rather than assuming a single answer applies to every applicant.

What happens if the GMC queries my registration application?

A query typically asks for one specific piece of clarification, such as an incomplete transcript, a name discrepancy, or ambiguity over which Indian body should confirm your registration status — it does not usually mean the whole application is in jeopardy. Respond promptly and precisely with exactly what has been requested, since an incomplete first response risks triggering a second query and further delay.

Can I start the GMC registration application before passing both UKMLA components?

No. Registration with a licence to practise can only be granted once both the AKT and CPSA have been passed, alongside completed EPIC verification and valid English language evidence. However, EPIC verification and English testing should both be started well before the exams are sat, since they run on independent timelines and are frequently the true bottleneck in the overall pathway.

How do I prove my Indian medical school qualification is genuine if the school is slow to respond?

Persistent, documented follow-up with your medical school's registrar or examinations office is the main lever available, since EPIC verification must originate from the institution itself and cannot be substituted by any document you hold personally. Keeping a written record of every request and response also gives you a clear escalation trail if the delay becomes extreme, and in some cases contacting the GMC for guidance on next steps is worthwhile if a school has been unresponsive for an extended period.

Does GMC registration from India let me start working in the NHS immediately?

Registration with a licence to practise is the legal authorisation to work as a doctor in the UK, but it is a prerequisite to, not the same as, having a job. Once registered, the practical next step is following the pathway from UKMLA success to an NHS job, which typically starts with applying for trust-grade or clinical fellow posts.

Conclusion

The GMC registration process for Indian doctors rewards candidates who treat EPIC verification, English language evidence, and document preparation as parallel, early-starting tasks rather than a final checklist to complete once the exams are behind them. Understanding the India-specific detail of the MCI-to-NMC transition, gathering a complete document set in advance, and responding to any GMC query promptly and precisely are the practical differences between a registration that proceeds smoothly and one that stalls for months. For the full eligibility criteria that determine whether you can begin this pathway at all, see the site's dedicated guide. Read the full GMC registration guide →
