post_title: English Language Requirements for the UKMLA: IELTS and OET Scores Explained
post_excerpt: The exact IELTS and OET scores the GMC requires for UKMLA registration, how Indian candidates can meet them, and how long certificates stay valid.
seo_title: English Language Requirement for UKMLA India
seo_description: English language requirement for UKMLA India: required IELTS Academic and OET Medicine scores, exemptions, certificate validity, and how to prepare.
primary_keyword: English language requirement for UKMLA India
html_content:
The English language requirement for UKMLA India catches many Indian medical graduates off guard, precisely because it seems, on the surface, like it should not apply to them at all. Medical education across India — from the MBBS curriculum to clinical postings, ward rounds, and university examinations — is conducted almost entirely in English. Consequently, a large number of Indian doctors assume that this alone should satisfy the General Medical Council (GMC) that their English is good enough to practise safely in the NHS. It does not, and understanding why is the first step in planning a realistic UKMLA timeline.

This guide sets out, in detail, exactly what the GMC expects from Indian candidates on English language testing: the two accepted tests, the exact scores required, where to sit each test across India, how long a certificate remains valid, what limited exemptions exist, and how much the whole process typically costs in rupees. It also places this requirement in context alongside the rest of the UKMLA pathway — including GMC registration and EPIC verification for Indian doctors and the broader question of whether an Indian MBBS degree is valid in the UK — so that English testing is planned as part of a single coherent pathway rather than tackled in isolation.

Because English testing is one of the earliest formal steps most Indian candidates take, getting the sequencing and score requirements right from the outset avoids one of the most common and entirely avoidable causes of delay and extra cost on the route to becoming a doctor in the UK from India: sitting the test too early, too late, or with too narrow a preparation margin around the required score. Indeed, of all the administrative hurdles on the pathway, the English language requirement for UKMLA India is arguably the one most within a candidate's direct control — unlike EPIC verification timelines, which depend heavily on how quickly a medical school's registrar responds, the timing and outcome of an English test rests almost entirely on the candidate's own preparation and planning.

This matters because Indian doctors otherwise very well prepared clinically sometimes underestimate how much dedicated, test-specific preparation the English requirement demands, treating it as a box-ticking exercise rather than as an examination in its own right. As later sections set out, both accepted tests apply a strict all-components scoring rule, meaning genuinely excellent English can still fall short of the GMC's threshold in a single sub-skill if that sub-skill has not been specifically practised under exam conditions.

Quick Answer: Do Indian Doctors Need an English Test for the UKMLA?

Yes. The English language requirement for UKMLA India applies to almost every Indian medical graduate, regardless of the fact that their medical education was conducted entirely in English. The GMC requires documentary evidence of English language proficiency from the great majority of international medical graduates (IMGs), and this evidence must come from an approved, standardised test — not from a statement about the language of instruction at your medical school, however strong your spoken and written English genuinely is in practice.

Two tests are accepted for GMC registration purposes: IELTS Academic, where the required score is an overall 7.0, with no individual component (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking) below 7.0, and OET (Occupational English Test), Medicine version, where the required score is a minimum of Grade B in all four sub-tests (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking). Meeting either of these thresholds satisfies the GMC's English language evidence requirement as part of the wider registration application. This is a genuinely common misconception among Indian candidates specifically, since it mirrors a similar misunderstanding documented among Nigerian doctors applying for GMC registration, whose medical education is likewise conducted in English yet who are still required to sit a formal test.

The remainder of this guide works through each test in detail, compares them directly, explains certificate validity and sequencing, covers the narrow exemption criteria that do exist, and sets out realistic costs and preparation timelines specifically for candidates sitting these tests from India.

Why English Language Education Alone Does Not Exempt Indian Doctors

It is worth understanding the GMC's reasoning here, because it explains why this requirement is applied so consistently. The GMC's English language evidence requirement is not really a test of whether you can read a textbook or write an exam answer in English — Indian medical graduates clearly can do both. Instead, it is designed to confirm a specific, standardised, and independently verifiable level of communicative English across four separate skills: listening comprehension under real-world conditions, reading comprehension of dense professional material, written communication suitable for clinical documentation, and — critically — spoken fluency and clarity in the kind of unscripted, two-way conversation that defines a doctor-patient consultation.

Classroom and clinical training conducted in English does not, on its own, generate independently verifiable evidence of all four of these skills to a single, comparable international standard. Furthermore, the GMC applies this requirement uniformly across IMGs from dozens of source countries with widely varying language-of-instruction arrangements; carving out an exception for Indian-trained doctors specifically would undermine the consistency of the standard the GMC is trying to apply. As a result, Indian candidates should treat the English test not as a bureaucratic formality to be waived, but as a standard, expected stage of the pathway that deserves the same serious preparation as the UKMLA examinations themselves.

The GMC publishes the definitive, current version of this requirement directly, and it should always be checked before booking a test: see the GMC's evidence of your knowledge of English guidance. This page is updated periodically, and since scores, accepted tests, and evidentiary detail can shift, it should always take precedence over summaries such as this one.

IELTS Academic: Scores, Format, and Availability in India

IELTS Academic is the more widely recognised of the two accepted tests among Indian candidates, largely because it is also the standard test used for study-abroad and skilled migration applications, meaning most Indian test-takers already have some familiarity with its format before they ever consider it for GMC purposes.

Exact IELTS Score Requirements for GMC Registration

For UKMLA and GMC registration purposes, the required IELTS Academic score is an overall band score of 7.0, and — this is the detail that trips up otherwise strong candidates — no individual component may fall below 7.0. In other words, a candidate who scores 8.5 in Reading, 8.0 in Listening, and 7.5 in Writing, but only 6.5 in Speaking, does not meet the requirement, even though their overall average comfortably exceeds 7.0. Each of the four components — Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking — must independently clear the 7.0 threshold. This all-components rule is the single most common reason Indian candidates need to re-sit the test, and it is worth planning preparation time around whichever of the four skills is genuinely weakest, rather than around the test as a whole.

IELTS Academic Test Format

IELTS Academic is structured across four sections, sat in a single day for the Listening, Reading, and Writing components, with Speaking either on the same day or scheduled separately within a seven-day window depending on the test centre:

  • Listening (approximately 30 minutes): four recorded sections of increasing difficulty, covering everyday conversations, monologues, and academic discussions, each followed by a set of comprehension questions.
  • Reading (60 minutes): three long academic passages drawn from journals, books, and periodicals, testing detailed comprehension, inference, and the ability to locate specific information quickly.
  • Writing (60 minutes): two tasks — a shorter report describing a graph, chart, or diagram (Task 1), and a longer discursive essay responding to a point of view or argument (Task 2).
  • Speaking (11 to 14 minutes): a face-to-face or video-call interview with a certified examiner, covering general questions about familiar topics, a two-minute individual long-turn on a given cue card, and a follow-up discussion exploring more abstract aspects of the same topic.

Consequently, IELTS Academic tests general academic English rather than healthcare-specific English, which means the subject matter in the Reading and Listening sections can range across topics as varied as environmental science, urban planning, or the history of art — content Indian medical graduates have not necessarily encountered since school, even though their command of English itself is strong.

Where Indian Candidates Can Sit IELTS Academic

IELTS is very widely available across India, with test centres operated by the British Council and IDP Education present in all major metropolitan areas — including Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Chennai, Hyderabad, Kolkata, Pune, and Ahmedabad — as well as a large number of tier-two cities. Both paper-based and computer-delivered formats are typically offered, with computer-delivered sittings generally available multiple times per week and results issued considerably faster (often within 3 to 5 days) than the paper-based format (13 days). This breadth of availability is one of IELTS Academic's genuine practical advantages for Indian candidates, since it usually means no long-distance domestic travel is required to sit the test, unlike some other stages of the UKMLA pathway.

Typical IELTS Preparation Timeline for Indian Candidates

Most Indian medical graduates with strong general English already in place need somewhere between four and twelve weeks of focused preparation to reliably clear 7.0 in all four components, though this varies considerably based on prior exposure to standardised English testing and — most importantly — which specific component is weakest. Candidates who have never taken a Speaking interview under timed, examiner-assessed conditions before often need disproportionately more practice time on that component than the raw complexity of the task would suggest, simply because the format itself is unfamiliar. Building a preparation plan around targeted practice of the specific sub-skill that is lagging, rather than generic overall revision, is consistently the more time-efficient approach.

A useful practical benchmark is to sit a full official practice test under strict timed conditions in the first week of preparation, before doing any further study. This establishes a genuine baseline band score across all four components and tells a candidate immediately whether their gap to 7.0 is a single-component problem — most commonly Speaking or Writing — or a broader issue affecting the whole test. Candidates who skip this diagnostic step and instead simply "revise generally" for several weeks often discover, only when their real result arrives, that they comfortably cleared three components but fell narrowly short in the fourth, at which point they must repeat the entire test rather than simply sharpening one skill.

OET Medicine: Scores, Format, and Availability in India

OET (Occupational English Test) is the second GMC-accepted route, and it is increasingly popular among Indian doctors specifically because its content is built entirely around real healthcare communication rather than general academic topics.

Exact OET Score Requirements for GMC Registration

For UKMLA and GMC registration purposes, OET Medicine requires a minimum of Grade B in all four sub-tests: Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking. As with IELTS, this is an all-components requirement — a candidate scoring Grade A in three sub-tests but Grade C in one does not meet the GMC's standard, and would need to re-sit at least the sub-test that fell short (OET, unlike IELTS, does allow re-sitting individual sub-tests rather than the entire test in some circumstances, which can meaningfully reduce both cost and time for candidates who are close to the required grade in only one skill).

OET Medicine Test Format

OET's structure mirrors IELTS in broad shape — four sections covering the same four skills — but every task is built around genuine clinical scenarios relevant to practising healthcare professionals:

  • Listening (approximately 45 minutes): recordings of consultations and healthcare-related presentations, testing the ability to follow a doctor-patient conversation and extract clinically relevant detail.
  • Reading (60 minutes): healthcare-related texts, including a timed skimming/scanning task and detailed comprehension passages drawn from genuine medical and healthcare-policy material.
  • Writing (45 minutes, profession-specific): for the Medicine sub-test, candidates write a referral letter based on a set of case notes — a task that closely mirrors real clinical correspondence a junior doctor would produce in NHS practice.
  • Speaking (approximately 20 minutes, profession-specific): two role-played consultations with a trained interlocutor playing the part of a patient or relative, assessed against criteria including clinical communication, empathy, and structure — again, a close simulation of real consultation skills.

Because every task is grounded in clinical context, many practising doctors find OET considerably more intuitive than IELTS Academic. Writing a referral letter or conducting a role-played consultation draws directly on skills Indian doctors already use daily in clinical practice, whereas IELTS Academic's general-interest reading passages and discursive essay topics require a different, less familiar kind of preparation. Candidates who already think fluently in the register of clinical documentation and doctor-patient conversation — which describes most working Indian doctors — often report that OET preparation feels closer to revision than to learning an unfamiliar test format.

Where Indian Candidates Can Sit OET

OET is administered in India at test centres in major cities including Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Chennai, Hyderabad, Kochi, and several other locations, with both paper-based and computer-delivered on-screen sittings offered. Test dates are typically available multiple times per month, and computer-delivered sittings generally return results considerably faster than the paper-based format. Availability has expanded substantially in India over recent years as OET has grown in popularity among healthcare professionals applying to the UK, Australia, New Zealand, and Ireland, so candidates outside the largest metros should check current centre locations directly through OET's official booking system rather than assuming a particular city has a sitting available on a given date.

One further practical point worth noting: because OET offers profession-specific Writing and Speaking sub-tests, Indian candidates must ensure they book the Medicine version specifically, rather than the Nursing, Dentistry, or Pharmacy versions of the test, since the GMC's accepted score applies only to the Medicine sub-test content. This distinction is straightforward but occasionally overlooked by candidates booking through unfamiliar portals, and confirming the correct profession stream at the point of booking avoids a wasted sitting entirely.

IELTS Academic vs OET Medicine: Direct Comparison

Choosing between IELTS Academic and OET Medicine is one of the first practical decisions an Indian candidate needs to make, and the right choice depends heavily on individual strengths, prior exam experience, and comfort with clinical versus general-academic English. The table below sets out the key differences side by side.

FactorIELTS AcademicOET Medicine
Required score for GMC/UKMLAOverall 7.0, no component below 7.0Grade B minimum in all four sub-tests
Content focusGeneral academic English (varied topics)Healthcare-specific scenarios (referral letters, consultations, case notes)
FormatListening, Reading, Writing, Speaking (same content for all candidates)Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking (Medicine-specific Writing/Speaking tasks)
Typical cost in IndiaApproximately ₹17,000–₹18,000 (subject to change)Approximately ₹19,000–₹20,500 (subject to change)
Availability in IndiaVery wide — most major and many tier-two citiesWide and expanding — concentrated in major metros
Results turnaround (computer-delivered)Typically 3–5 daysTypically around 5–10 working days
Re-sit flexibilityMust re-sit the entire testMay re-sit individual sub-tests in some circumstances
Best suited toCandidates comfortable with general academic English and formal essay writingPractising doctors comfortable with clinical documentation and consultation-style spoken English

In practice, many Indian candidates who have recently been working clinically — writing discharge summaries, taking histories, and communicating with patients day to day — find OET's healthcare framing plays to existing strengths. Meanwhile, candidates who are more comfortable with formal academic writing, or who are already preparing for IELTS for an unrelated purpose such as a visa application, may find it more efficient to sit a single IELTS test that serves both goals. Neither test is inherently easier than the other; the better choice is the one that matches an individual candidate's existing communication style.

Certificate Validity and Why Timing Matters

Both IELTS and OET certificates carry a defined validity period from the date of the test, and this detail matters enormously for how an Indian candidate sequences their overall UKMLA pathway. Sitting the English test too early — for example, immediately after graduation, well before EPIC verification is complete and before the AKT and CPSA are even booked — creates a real risk that the certificate will expire before the full registration application is actually submitted. Because GMC registration application timing depends on multiple parallel processes (credential verification, exam scheduling, document collection) that are not entirely within a candidate's control, a certificate sat too early can force an unplanned and entirely avoidable re-sit, adding both cost and delay at exactly the stage of the pathway when a candidate is trying to finish, not restart a step.

The safest practical approach is to treat English testing as a mid-pathway milestone rather than a first-day task: begin it once EPIC verification is underway and the AKT is being actively prepared for, so that the certificate's validity window comfortably covers the expected date of the full registration application, including a reasonable buffer for unexpected delays elsewhere in the process. The GMC's own guidance sets out the current validity period and evidentiary detail in full, and it should always be checked directly before finalising a test date: see the GMC's English language evidence requirements, since validity rules are exactly the kind of administrative detail that can be updated between the time this guide is written and the time a candidate reads it.

This sequencing question is not unique to Indian candidates — the same expiry risk is flagged for Nigerian doctors navigating the same English testing requirement — but it deserves particular attention from Indian applicants given how long primary source verification through EPIC can sometimes take, which is precisely the kind of delay that can quietly erode a certificate's remaining validity window if the test is sat too soon.

How English Testing Fits Into the Wider UKMLA Pathway From India

The English language requirement for UKMLA India does not sit in isolation — it is one stage within a longer sequence that also includes confirming basic eligibility, verifying an Indian MBBS degree through EPIC, sitting the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA), and finally submitting a full application for GMC registration with a licence to practise. Understanding where English testing sits relative to these other stages is what allows a candidate to sequence it correctly, rather than treating it as a stand-alone task disconnected from the rest of the timeline.

In practice, most Indian candidates run English test preparation in parallel with the early stages of EPIC verification, since GMC registration and EPIC verification for Indian doctors can take anywhere from a few weeks to several months depending on how quickly a medical school's academic office responds to primary source verification requests. Sitting the English test during this window — rather than before EPIC has even begun, or after AKT preparation is already well underway — tends to produce the best balance between certificate validity and overall pathway efficiency, since it avoids both extremes: sitting so early that the certificate risks expiring, and sitting so late that it becomes a bottleneck immediately before the registration application.

Before committing time and money to English testing at all, however, it is worth confirming the more fundamental question of whether an Indian medical degree is accepted by the GMC in the first place. The guide to whether an Indian MBBS degree is valid in the UK sets out the specific eligibility criteria — accreditation status, World Directory of Medical Schools listing, and internship completion — that determine whether a given Indian qualification satisfies the GMC's basic threshold before English testing, EPIC verification, or exam booking become relevant at all.

Once eligibility is confirmed and English testing is under way, candidates should also begin structured preparation for the AKT itself, since the two workstreams draw on different skills and can run entirely in parallel without one delaying the other. The site's dedicated guide to UKMLA preparation for IMGs sets out how to build a study plan that covers the AKT's clinical content alongside — rather than after — English test preparation, which is the more time-efficient sequencing for most Indian candidates working towards a realistic overall pathway timeline.

Possible Exemptions From the English Language Test

The GMC does allow limited exemptions from the standard English language testing requirement in certain specific circumstances — for example, doctors who have recently and substantially practised medicine within another majority-English-speaking healthcare system may, in some cases, be able to rely on that experience instead of sitting a fresh test. However, exemption criteria of this kind are narrowly defined, assessed case by case, and can change over time as GMC policy is reviewed. It would be misleading to present a fixed, general list of exemption scenarios here, since doing so risks giving an Indian candidate false confidence about a route that may not actually apply to their specific circumstances, or that may have been updated since this guide was written.

Rather than assuming an exemption applies, Indian candidates should check the GMC's current exemption criteria directly and in detail before making any decision to skip test preparation or delay booking a test slot: see the GMC's official English language evidence page for the authoritative, current position. In the great majority of cases, an Indian medical graduate applying directly from India — without a period of substantial recent practice in a majority-English-speaking health system — should plan on sitting either IELTS Academic or OET Medicine as a standard part of the pathway, rather than pursuing an exemption that is unlikely to apply.

Preparation Tips for Indian Candidates

Indian test-takers, as a group, tend to show a fairly consistent pattern of relative strengths and weaknesses across both tests, and preparation time is generally best spent addressing the specific areas where this pattern tends to show up rather than revising uniformly across all four skills.

  • Speaking is the most common weak component. Indian medical education builds excellent reading and writing fluency through textbooks, case write-ups, and examinations, but offers comparatively little structured practice in extended, examiner-assessed spoken English on unfamiliar or abstract topics. Regular timed mock speaking sessions — ideally recorded and reviewed, or practised with a partner who can give honest feedback on fluency and coherence rather than just grammar — consistently produce the largest score improvements for Indian candidates.
  • Writing Task 2 (IELTS) or the referral letter (OET) rewards structure over vocabulary. Many Indian candidates already have strong vocabulary but lose marks on organisation, paragraphing, and directly addressing the exact question or clinical scenario asked. Practising with a clear, repeatable structural template for each task type improves scores faster than expanding vocabulary alone.
  • Listening under exam conditions differs from everyday listening comprehension. Indian candidates who consume a great deal of English-language media may still under-score on Listening because the exam format demands sustained concentration, rapid note-taking, and comprehension of varied native-speaker accents (including Australian, Scottish, and North American accents) within a single sitting, which is a different skill from passive listening.
  • Use official practice material first. Both the British Council/IDP (for IELTS) and Cambridge Boxhill Language Assessment (for OET) publish official practice tests and sample answers, and these should form the backbone of preparation before turning to third-party resources, since official material most accurately reflects actual scoring criteria.
  • Book a mock speaking session with a certified or experienced tutor close to the actual test date. Simulated speaking practice under realistic time pressure, ideally with feedback benchmarked against the real band descriptors or OET criteria, is consistently reported as the single most useful preparation activity by candidates who move from a borderline score to a comfortably passing one.
  • Practise topic breadth for IELTS specifically. Because IELTS Academic draws on general topics rather than clinical ones, Indian candidates should read broadly outside medical subject matter — current affairs, science journalism, and opinion writing — to build comfort with the kind of vocabulary and argument structures the Reading and Writing sections actually use.

These preparation strategies apply in addition to, not instead of, the wider exam preparation habits covered in the site's general guide to UKMLA preparation for IMGs, since English test preparation and AKT/CPSA preparation draw on overlapping but distinct skills and are best planned as parallel, rather than sequential, workstreams.

Cost Considerations: Test Fees and the Cost of a Re-Sit

Test fees in India for both IELTS Academic and OET are charged in rupees and are subject to periodic revision by the test providers, so the figures below should be treated as approximate and always cross-checked against the official current fee at the time of booking. As a general guide, IELTS Academic in India typically costs in the region of ₹17,000–₹18,000, while OET typically costs in the region of ₹19,000–₹20,500, with on-screen/computer-delivered sittings sometimes priced slightly differently from paper-based sittings at the same centre.

The more significant cost risk, however, is not the initial test fee itself but the cost of a re-sit for candidates who narrowly miss the required score in one component. Because both tests apply an all-components minimum — 7.0 across the board for IELTS, Grade B across the board for OET — a candidate who scores comfortably above the threshold in three skills but marginally below it in the fourth must, in most circumstances, pay and sit the test again in full (OET allows single-sub-test re-sits in certain circumstances, which can reduce this cost). Consequently, a realistic total budget for English testing should assume at least one possible re-sit, particularly for candidates whose weakest skill is Speaking, which data from both test providers consistently shows to be the most common component to fall short of the required band or grade among IMG candidates generally.

Beyond the test fee itself, Indian candidates should also budget for preparation costs — coaching classes, official practice material, and mock speaking sessions — which together commonly add a further ₹3,000–₹15,000 depending on how much structured coaching is used versus self-study. Treating English test preparation with the same seriousness (and budget) as AKT preparation, rather than as an afterthought, is one of the more reliable ways to avoid the added cost and delay of an unplanned re-sit.

It is also worth factoring in the indirect cost of a re-sit, which is often larger than the fee itself. A failed component typically adds four to eight weeks to the overall timeline once a new test slot is booked, preparation is refreshed, and results are issued — time that, for many Indian candidates, overlaps with other fixed deadlines such as an AKT booking window or a planned resignation date from a current post. Building a modest contingency of both time and money into the original plan, rather than assuming a first-attempt pass across all four components, is a more realistic approach than budgeting for the best-case outcome only. As with every fee mentioned in this guide, candidates should treat the rupee figures above as indicative rather than fixed, and should always confirm the live, current fee directly with IELTS or OET's official Indian booking portals before making a payment, since both providers revise fees periodically and without prior notice to third-party guides.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do Indian doctors need IELTS for UKMLA?

Yes, in almost all cases. The GMC requires documentary evidence of English language proficiency from the great majority of IMGs, including Indian medical graduates, regardless of the fact that Indian medical education is conducted in English. Indian candidates can satisfy this requirement with either IELTS Academic (overall 7.0, no component below 7.0) or OET Medicine (Grade B minimum in all four sub-tests) — IELTS is not the only accepted option, but it is one of the two standard routes.

Is OET accepted for GMC registration?

Yes. OET (Occupational English Test), Medicine version, is fully accepted by the GMC as an alternative to IELTS Academic, provided the candidate achieves a minimum of Grade B in all four sub-tests — Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking. Many practising doctors, including Indian candidates, find OET more intuitive than IELTS Academic because its tasks are built around real clinical scenarios such as referral letters and patient consultations rather than general academic topics.

How long is an IELTS certificate valid for GMC registration?

IELTS and OET certificates both carry a defined validity period from the date of the test, and the GMC will not accept a certificate that has expired by the time a registration application is submitted. Because the exact current validity period is exactly the kind of detail the GMC updates from time to time, candidates should confirm it directly on the GMC's English language evidence page before finalising their test date, and should sequence their test so its validity comfortably covers their expected application date rather than sitting it too early in the pathway.

What IELTS score is needed for GMC registration from India?

Indian candidates need an overall IELTS Academic score of 7.0, with no individual component — Listening, Reading, Writing, or Speaking — scoring below 7.0. This is an all-components requirement, so a strong overall average does not compensate for a single component falling short of 7.0; each of the four skills must independently clear the threshold.

Can Indian doctors take OET instead of IELTS for the UKMLA pathway?

Yes. The GMC treats IELTS Academic and OET Medicine as equally valid routes to satisfying the English language evidence requirement, and Indian candidates are free to choose whichever test suits their strengths, provided they meet the required score in each of the four sub-tests (7.0 for IELTS, Grade B for OET). Neither test is preferred by the GMC over the other; the choice is entirely a matter of which format and content style suits the individual candidate.

Are there any exemptions from the English test for Indian doctors?

The GMC does allow limited exemptions in specific circumstances, such as recent, substantial practice within another majority-English-speaking healthcare system, but these criteria are narrowly applied, assessed case by case, and can change over time. Most Indian medical graduates applying directly from India should not assume an exemption applies and should instead check the GMC's current exemption criteria directly before deciding whether to sit a test.

How much does the IELTS or OET test cost in India?

As an approximate guide, subject to change, IELTS Academic in India typically costs around ₹17,000–₹18,000, while OET typically costs around ₹19,000–₹20,500. Because both tests apply an all-components minimum score, candidates should also budget for the realistic possibility of a re-sit if one component falls narrowly short, and should always confirm current fees directly with the relevant test provider before booking.

Which English test is easier for Indian doctors, IELTS or OET?

Neither test is objectively easier; the better fit depends on individual strengths. Indian doctors who are comfortable with formal academic writing and general-interest reading often do well with IELTS Academic, while those who are more comfortable with clinical documentation and consultation-style spoken English — which describes most practising doctors — often find OET Medicine's healthcare-specific scenarios more intuitive to prepare for.

When should Indian doctors sit their English test relative to the AKT?

Most Indian candidates get the best outcome by sitting their English test once EPIC verification is underway and AKT preparation has begun, rather than immediately after graduation or only once the AKT is already booked. This timing keeps the certificate's validity window comfortably ahead of the eventual GMC registration application, while still allowing English test preparation to run in parallel with, rather than delay, wider UKMLA preparation.

Conclusion

The English language requirement for UKMLA India is not a formality that Indian medical graduates can expect to have waived on the strength of an English-medium medical education; it is a standard, structured stage of the pathway that deserves dedicated preparation time, careful test choice between IELTS Academic and OET Medicine, and deliberate sequencing so a valid certificate is in hand when the full GMC registration application is submitted. Candidates who treat this stage with the same seriousness as AKT question bank preparation, budget realistically in rupees for both the test and a possible re-sit, and check the GMC's current requirements directly before booking consistently move through this part of the pathway faster and with fewer costly surprises. For the full set of criteria that determine whether you can sit the exam at all, see the site's guide to UKMLA eligibility requirements, and for the step-by-step registration process that follows successful English testing, see the GMC registration process guide. Check the GMC's official English language and registration resources now →

post_title: Do Experienced Doctors Need to Take the UKMLA? Exemptions for Specialists and GPs
post_excerpt: Whether GPs, specialists, MRCP holders and doctors already working in the NHS still need to sit the UKMLA, and what exemptions or alternative registration routes actually exist.
seo_title: UKMLA Exemptions for Specialist Doctors
seo_description: UKMLA exemptions for specialist doctors: whether GPs, MRCP holders and doctors already working in the NHS still need to sit the AKT and CPSA to register.
html_content:
UKMLA exemptions for specialist doctors are far narrower than many experienced clinicians expect. The short answer is straightforward: the great majority of doctors seeking first-time GMC registration with a licence to practise must sit and pass the UKMLA — the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) — regardless of how senior, specialised, or experienced they already are. This includes consultants, general practitioners, and doctors who already hold MRCP(UK), MRCS, or an equivalent royal college postgraduate qualification from their home country. Postgraduate specialist experience does not exempt a doctor from these entry-level licensing assessments, because the UKMLA and a specialty membership exam are testing two fundamentally different things.

This distinction trips up a surprising number of highly qualified doctors every year. A consultant physician with fifteen years of specialist practice, a GP who has run their own list for a decade, or a doctor who passed a demanding postgraduate membership examination overseas can all reasonably assume that seniority should count for something in the UK system. It generally does — but not in the way many expect, and not by removing the requirement to sit the UKMLA. This guide sets out, clearly and without overstating the exceptions, exactly which doctors genuinely fall outside the standard requirement, why specialist and GP experience does not itself confer an exemption, and what experienced doctors should actually do differently in their preparation given that they must still sit the exam.

Because the narrow exemption routes described below are assessed case-by-case and their criteria can change, this guide is written to be honest about the limits of what can be said in general terms. Wherever a rule depends on individual circumstances, that is stated plainly, and readers are directed to confirm their specific position with the GMC directly rather than relying on a blog post to make the final call.

The confusion around UKMLA exemptions for specialist doctors is understandable, since the UK's system genuinely does recognise seniority and specialist status in other contexts — for entry to the Specialist Register once already licensed, for example, or for consultant-level job applications once a doctor is already registered. However, the UKMLA sits at an earlier and different stage of the process: it is the entry point into UK medical practice altogether, not a further hurdle layered on top of specialist recognition. Consequently, a doctor's specialist standing in their home country, however well earned, answers a different question from the one the UKMLA asks. This guide separates the two clearly, works through each of the genuine exemption routes in turn, and then sets out what actually changes — and what does not — for an experienced doctor's preparation once it is confirmed that the UKMLA does apply to them.

Quick Answer: Do Experienced Doctors Need to Take the UKMLA?

Yes. For the overwhelming majority of doctors applying for first-time GMC registration with a licence to practise, the UKMLA is required regardless of prior specialist or GP experience, and regardless of any postgraduate qualification already held. The UKMLA exists to test whether a doctor can practise safely as a generalist within the UK healthcare system at the point of registration — it is not a test of specialty expertise, and it is not something that seniority, years in practice, or a specialty membership certificate can substitute for.

There are only two genuinely distinct pathways that sit outside the standard requirement, and both are considerably narrower than most doctors assume:

  • The Registrar route, available only to doctors with GMC-recognised specialist or GP registration from a specific, approved list of countries or systems, which is assessed on an individual basis by the GMC.
  • Sponsorship via an Approved Practice Setting (APS), which grants a time-limited registration tied to a specific supervised post rather than a full, unrestricted licence, and which depends on individual eligibility criteria that can change.

Outside of these two routes, holding MRCP(UK), MRCS, FRCOG, or an equivalent royal college membership does not exempt a doctor from the UKMLA, and neither does having worked as a GP overseas for any length of time. Working in an NHS post already — as a Trust Grade doctor, a locum, or in a training-adjacent role — does not exempt a doctor either, if that doctor has not yet secured full GMC registration with a licence to practise through one of the recognised routes. Each of these points is expanded in detail in the sections that follow, alongside a direct comparison of the exemption route, the standard IMG route, and the UK medical school route, and a look at what genuinely experienced doctors should focus on differently in their preparation.

It is worth stating this quick answer even more directly, because so much confusion stems from doctors reasoning from analogy rather than from the actual rule. A cardiologist with twenty years of consultant experience, a psychiatrist who has led a department, and a GP who has trained junior doctors for a decade are all, from the GMC's perspective, first-time applicants for UK registration in exactly the same sense as a newly qualified graduate applying for the first time. Seniority earned within a different regulatory system does not transfer automatically into the UK system, however logical that might seem from the doctor's own vantage point. This is not a punitive rule; it reflects the straightforward reality that the GMC has no independent means of verifying a doctor's current generalist competence against UK standards except by requiring the same assessment of every first-time applicant, with the two narrow exceptions described immediately below.

The Narrow Cases Where UKMLA Exemptions Genuinely Apply

Understanding where UKMLA exemptions for specialist doctors actually exist starts with recognising how narrow the qualifying criteria are. Two routes exist outside the standard AKT-and-CPSA pathway, and both depend on specific, individually assessed circumstances rather than general seniority or years of experience.

The Registrar Route via GMC-Recognised Specialist or GP Registration

The first genuine exemption route applies to doctors who already hold specialist or GP registration recognised by the GMC through what is sometimes referred to as a relevant comparable system — broadly, registration gained through a recognised specialist or GP register in an approved country or system whose training and assessment standards the GMC accepts as broadly equivalent to the UK's own. Doctors who qualify under this route may, in some circumstances, be able to apply directly for entry to the GMC's Specialist Register or GP Register without sitting the UKMLA, because the underlying training and assessment they have already completed is treated as having satisfied an equivalent standard.

This is a materially different and much narrower pathway than the standard international medical graduate (IMG) route that most overseas-trained doctors use, and it should not be confused with it. Crucially, whether a given doctor's qualifications and registration history actually meet the criteria is assessed case-by-case by the GMC, not by a simple checklist a blog post can reproduce reliably. Countries and systems recognised for this purpose, the specific evidence required, and the exact assessment criteria can all change, and this guide deliberately does not attempt to list who qualifies, since doing so risks giving false reassurance to a reader whose specific situation does not fit. Any doctor who believes they may fall into this category should consult the GMC's own specialist register guidance directly and, where uncertainty remains, contact the GMC before making any assumptions about their eligibility or committing to a particular application route.

It is worth being explicit about why this route exists at all: it recognises that a small number of doctors have already undergone training, supervised practice, and formal specialist or GP assessment through a system the GMC judges to be broadly comparable to UK specialty or GP training, rather than assuming that any postgraduate qualification, from any country, automatically clears the bar. This is a meaningfully higher and more specific threshold than simply holding a specialist qualification or having practised as a specialist for a number of years, which is precisely why it does not extend to the much larger population of doctors who hold, for example, MRCP or MRCS from a training programme that the GMC does not recognise as equivalent to UK specialty training for this purpose.

Sponsorship via an Approved Practice Setting (APS)

The second route that sits outside the standard UKMLA requirement involves sponsorship into a time-limited registration through an Approved Practice Setting. In broad terms, this route allows a doctor to be sponsored by an approved organisation — historically, certain NHS bodies and other approved settings — into a supervised post without first completing the full standard registration route. However, this registration is time-limited and tied to the specific sponsoring post rather than granting the same unrestricted licence to practise that passing the UKMLA leads to, and the doctor's ability to work outside that sponsored setting is correspondingly restricted.

The exact eligibility criteria, which organisations currently hold approved status, and how this route interacts with the UKMLA and broader registration requirements are all points that can and do change over time, so this guide deliberately hedges rather than stating fixed rules that might be stale by the time a reader acts on them. Any doctor considering this route should treat it as something to verify directly and currently with the GMC and with the sponsoring organisation itself, rather than something to plan a career move around based on a general description. In practice, most doctors exploring this route are also simultaneously exploring the standard route, since sponsorship arrangements are inherently narrower in scope and duration than full GMC registration with a licence to practise gained through the AKT and CPSA.

Taken together, these two routes are genuinely narrow exceptions rather than a broad category experienced doctors can generally expect to fall into. Doctors who do not clearly and specifically meet the criteria for one of these two routes should assume, by default, that the standard UKMLA requirement applies to them, and should plan their preparation and timeline accordingly rather than delaying study in the hope that an exemption will eventually apply.

Why These Routes Are Deliberately Kept Narrow

It is worth pausing on why UKMLA exemptions for specialist doctors are drawn so tightly, rather than treating the narrowness as an arbitrary bureaucratic choice. Both genuine exemption routes rest on the GMC having independent, structured evidence that a doctor's prior training and assessment already covers broadly the same ground the UKMLA covers — not merely that the doctor is experienced, but that a comparable external body has already tested and confirmed the relevant competencies through a recognised, auditable process. A specialist qualification earned through an unrecognised or unassessed training pathway, however demanding in its own right, does not give the GMC that same structured assurance. This is precisely why the Registrar route depends on the specific country or system of registration rather than on the doctor's job title, years of practice, or the difficulty of the exams they have already passed. In addition, because medical training standards, regulatory relationships between countries, and the GMC's own assessment frameworks all evolve over time, the list of recognised systems for the Registrar route and the eligibility conditions for Approved Practice Setting sponsorship are both reviewed periodically rather than fixed permanently. A route that was closed to a particular country or qualification profile in the past may not necessarily remain closed indefinitely, and the reverse is equally true — a route open at one point could be tightened later. This is exactly the kind of live regulatory detail that a guide like this one cannot responsibly guarantee will still be accurate by the time a reader acts on it, which is why every mention of these routes throughout this guide is paired with a direct instruction to verify current status with the GMC.

MRCP, MRCS, and GP Experience: Why Specialty Qualifications Do Not Exempt You

This is one of the most common points of confusion, so it deserves a direct, unambiguous answer before any further explanation. Holding MRCP(UK), MRCS, or an equivalent royal college membership from your home country does not exempt you from the UKMLA. Similarly, having practised as a GP overseas — for any number of years — does not exempt you either. Both of these facts surprise many experienced doctors, so it is worth explaining precisely why, rather than simply stating the rule.

Why MRCP(UK) or an Equivalent Qualification Does Not Exempt You

MRCP(UK), MRCS, and comparable royal college memberships are specialty knowledge examinations. They test deep, focused competence within a defined specialty — internal medicine, surgery, obstetrics and gynaecology, and so on — at a postgraduate level, typically after a doctor has already completed foundational generalist training and moved into specialty-specific practice. The UKMLA, by contrast, is the GMC's generalist licensing assessment. It exists specifically to confirm that a doctor entering the UK medical register for the first time can practise safely as a generalist across the breadth of UK clinical practice, UK-specific prescribing conventions, UK legal and ethical frameworks, and UK healthcare system structures — not to test how deeply they understand a single specialty.

Consequently, these two types of assessment are not substitutes for one another, however impressive the postgraduate qualification is in its own right. A doctor could, in principle, hold an excellent specialty qualification and still lack current, demonstrable familiarity with UK-specific generalist practice — precisely the gap the UKMLA is designed to check. This is also why doctors preparing for the AKT after holding a specialty qualification should not assume their postgraduate exam preparation transfers directly; the content, question style, and generalist breadth differ meaningfully, a point explored further in the preparation section below. Doctors relying on this reasoning to understand their own position should also check the detailed rules on who is eligible to sit the exam, since eligibility and exemption are related but distinct questions.

Why Overseas GP Experience Does Not Exempt You

The same underlying logic applies to GPs. Having run a general practice list overseas for many years demonstrates real, valuable generalist clinical experience — and, as covered in the preparation section below, that experience is a genuine asset for parts of the UKMLA. However, it does not confirm that a doctor's practice matches current UK-specific generalist standards: UK prescribing conventions, the specific referral pathways and NHS system structures a UK generalist must navigate, UK-specific safeguarding and consent frameworks, and the particular consultation and communication standards the CPSA assesses. Overseas GP registration is not GMC-recognised GP registration, and the two should not be conflated. Unless a doctor's specific registration history qualifies them for the narrow Registrar route described above — which is assessed individually by the GMC, not assumed from job title alone — the standard UKMLA requirement applies in the same way it would to any other first-time applicant for a licence to practise.

It is worth being blunt about this because the confusion is genuinely common and genuinely costly if it leads to poor planning: neither an impressive specialty qualification nor extensive GP experience overseas changes the answer to whether the UKMLA is required. What they do change, meaningfully, is how a doctor should approach preparing for it — which is covered later in this guide.

Why This Rule Applies Even to Very Senior Specialists

Some of the most persistent questions about UKMLA exemptions for specialist doctors come from consultant-level clinicians who have led departments, trained junior doctors, and published research in their specialty, precisely because it can feel counterintuitive that a doctor at that level of seniority must sit an assessment aimed, in part, at confirming basic generalist safety. However, the logic holds even at this level of seniority: a consultant neurologist, for instance, may not have actively managed an undifferentiated acute abdomen, an unwell child, or a mental health crisis presentation in a general setting for many years, simply because their day-to-day practice has narrowed to their specialty. The UKMLA is not questioning their specialist competence; it is confirming something the specialist qualification was never designed to test in the first place — current, broad, safe generalist practice at the point of entry to the UK register. Framed this way, sitting the UKMLA is less a demotion and more a distinct, additional confirmation that sits alongside, rather than beneath, a doctor's specialist standing.

Doctors Already Working in the NHS Without Full Registration

A related and equally common source of confusion involves doctors who are already working within the NHS — often in a Trust Grade role, a locum position, or a training-adjacent post — but who have not yet completed the UKMLA or an equivalent recognised pathway to full GMC registration with a licence to practise. Some of these doctors reasonably wonder whether their current NHS employment itself amounts to a kind of practical exemption, on the basis that they are already demonstrably capable of working safely in a UK clinical environment.

It does not. Current NHS employment status has no bearing on whether a doctor still needs to complete the UKMLA. A doctor working in a Trust Grade post, for example, may hold provisional or limited registration, or may be working under a specific sponsorship or supervision arrangement that is itself time-limited — precisely the kind of arrangement described in the Approved Practice Setting section above. Unless that doctor's registration status has already been converted into full, unrestricted GMC registration with a licence to practise through the standard route, the Registrar route, or a properly confirmed APS-equivalent arrangement, the requirement to sit and pass the UKMLA remains in place regardless of how long they have already been working clinically in the UK.

This matters practically because doctors in this position sometimes delay UKMLA preparation on the assumption that ongoing NHS service will eventually be recognised as sufficient, or that a training programme will convert their status automatically. In reality, the pathway to full registration runs through the same assessments and processes described in the site's guide to the full GMC registration process for international doctors, and delaying engagement with that process does not shorten it. Doctors in this position should treat their current NHS role as valuable clinical experience relevant to the CPSA — much like the specialist and GP experience discussed above — rather than as a substitute for completing the formal registration pathway. Anyone unsure of their exact registration status, and therefore unsure whether they are required to sit the UKMLA, should check directly through their GMC Online account or contact the GMC, since registration categories and their implications are individual to each doctor's history and current arrangement.

This scenario is common enough to deserve a worked example. Consider a specialist doctor recruited into a Trust Grade post specifically because of their overseas specialist experience, working under supervision within a UK department for a year or more, gaining genuinely valuable exposure to NHS systems, documentation standards, and multidisciplinary working. That accumulated NHS experience is real and useful — indeed, it is likely to help considerably with both halves of the UKMLA once the doctor sits it, particularly the CPSA's UK-specific consultation and communication stations. However, none of that accumulated experience substitutes for the formal assessment itself unless the doctor's registration has separately and specifically been converted to full GMC registration with a licence to practise. Doctors in exactly this position should treat their Trust Grade post as an opportunity to prepare for the UKMLA under real clinical conditions, rather than as evidence that the exam has already, in effect, been satisfied by their day job.

Exemption Route vs Standard IMG Route vs UK Medical School Route

The table below sets out, in general terms, how the three main pathways to full GMC registration with a licence to practise compare. It is intended as an orientation tool rather than a definitive eligibility checklist — the GMC's own guidance should always be checked for a specific case, particularly given how individually the exemption routes are assessed.

RouteWho It Generally Applies ToUKMLA Required?What To Check With the GMC
Registrar route (specialist/GP register)Doctors with GMC-recognised specialist or GP registration gained through an approved country or comparable systemGenerally not required if the specific criteria are metWhether your registration history and country/system are currently recognised for this route, and what evidence is required
Sponsorship via Approved Practice SettingDoctors sponsored by an approved organisation into a specific, time-limited supervised postNot required for that specific time-limited registration, but the registration itself is restricted in scopeCurrent approved-setting status, eligibility criteria, and how this affects any later move to full registration
Standard IMG routeThe great majority of overseas-trained doctors, including specialists, GPs, and holders of MRCP/MRCS or equivalentYes — AKT and CPSA requiredDegree recognition, primary source verification, English language evidence, and current eligibility criteria
UK medical school routeStudents graduating from a UK medical school with GMC-approved undergraduate assessmentYes, integrated into final-year assessment rather than sat separately post-graduationHow your medical school has implemented UKMLA-equivalent assessment within its curriculum

Two things stand out from this comparison. First, the standard IMG route is overwhelmingly the applicable route for experienced specialists and GPs coming from outside the UK, precisely because the two exemption routes above depend on specific registration histories that most doctors — however experienced — do not hold. Second, the UK medical school route is not actually an exemption from UKMLA-equivalent assessment at all; UK students sit the same underlying assessment, just integrated into their final-year undergraduate training rather than as a standalone post-graduation exam. For a country-specific illustration of how the standard IMG route plays out in practice, including credential verification and exam booking, see the site's detailed guide on the established route followed by Nigerian-trained doctors, which mirrors the sequence most experienced IMGs — specialists and GPs included — will follow.

It is worth reading this table as a starting orientation rather than a final answer, particularly for the two exemption rows. A doctor might reasonably hold registration in a country broadly associated with recognised systems and still not personally qualify, if their specific specialty, registration category, or timing of qualification falls outside current criteria; equally, a doctor from a less obviously prominent system might qualify where the specific facts line up. This is exactly why the standard advice throughout this guide is to treat the exemption routes as something to check individually rather than infer from general impressions about a country's healthcare system or a doctor's specialty. By contrast, the standard IMG route and the UK medical school route are comparatively simple to self-assess, since eligibility for both depends mainly on objective, checkable facts — medical school accreditation, degree completion, English language evidence, and, for UK students, straightforward enrolment at a GMC-recognised medical school.

What Experienced Doctors Should Do Differently in Preparation

Even though experienced specialists and GPs must still sit the full UKMLA, this does not mean their years of clinical experience count for nothing in preparation. On the contrary, that experience is a genuine, measurable advantage in some areas of the assessment — it simply needs to be paired with focused preparation in the areas where it does not automatically transfer.

Where Experience Genuinely Helps: The CPSA

Substantial clinical experience is a real asset for the CPSA's clinical reasoning and consultation stations. Years of patient contact build the kind of clinical judgement, history-taking fluency, and diagnostic reasoning under time pressure that the CPSA is specifically designed to assess, and experienced doctors — including specialists working somewhat outside their usual scope during a generalist station — typically bring a level of clinical maturity that is difficult to manufacture through short-term revision alone. This is worth recognising explicitly: the CPSA rewards genuine clinical experience more directly than a knowledge-recall exam would, which is one reason many experienced doctors perform well on it once they have adjusted to its specific UK-based communication and consultation format.

Where Experience Does Not Automatically Transfer: The AKT

The AKT is a different matter, and this is where many experienced doctors underestimate the preparation required. The AKT's question style, format, and generalist breadth differ meaningfully from most postgraduate specialty exams, including MRCP and MRCS. A consultant who has not sat a broad, generalist, single-best-answer style knowledge exam in years may find the format itself — rather than the underlying clinical knowledge — is the harder adjustment. In addition, the AKT draws on a specific, defined content base, the official MLA content map, that spans far beyond any one specialty, and it tests UK-specific detail that even highly experienced overseas specialists and GPs are unlikely to already know: UK prescribing conventions and prescribing safety, NICE guideline-based management decisions, NHS referral pathways, and professionalism topics grounded in UK-specific legal and ethical frameworks such as the Mental Capacity Act 2005.

Consequently, experienced doctors preparing for the UKMLA should treat AKT preparation as a genuinely dedicated project rather than a formality to fit around existing clinical commitments. A realistic AKT preparation plan for an experienced specialist or GP should include structured practice with UK-specific single-best-answer questions to rebuild familiarity with the format, deliberate attention to UK prescribing and NICE-guideline conventions that may differ from home-country practice, and enough full-length timed practice to adjust to the AKT's particular time pressure, which can catch out doctors used to a different exam structure. Meanwhile, CPSA preparation can reasonably lean more heavily on existing clinical experience, with additional focus specifically on the UK-specific consultation and communication conventions the assessment expects, rather than on relearning clinical reasoning from scratch.

In short, experienced doctors should not assume their seniority reduces preparation time uniformly across both components. It genuinely reduces the burden for the CPSA's clinical reasoning demands, but it does very little to reduce the format-specific and UK-specific preparation the AKT requires — treating both halves identically is one of the more common planning mistakes this group makes.

Building a Realistic Preparation Timeline as an Experienced Doctor

Because experienced doctors are often also balancing existing clinical commitments, a realistic preparation timeline matters as much as the content of the preparation itself. Many specialists and GPs underestimate how much calendar time dedicated AKT preparation actually needs, particularly if their day-to-day clinical role does not require regular exposure to broad generalist material. A sensible approach is to treat AKT preparation as a distinct project with its own protected study time, rather than something absorbed passively through ongoing clinical work — unlike CPSA preparation, where ongoing clinical practice genuinely does double as preparation. In addition, experienced doctors should resist the temptation to under-schedule practice papers on the assumption that clinical knowledge alone will carry them through; the single-best-answer format, generalist breadth, and UK-specific detail mean that unfamiliarity with the exam itself, rather than a gap in medical knowledge, is the more common cause of an unexpectedly disappointing AKT result among senior candidates. Building in several full-length timed practice sittings well before the actual exam date, rather than relying solely on topic-by-topic revision, consistently produces better outcomes for this group.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do GPs need to take the UKMLA?

Yes, in almost all cases. Having practised as a GP overseas, however extensively, does not exempt a doctor from the UKMLA, because overseas GP registration is not the same as GMC-recognised GP registration. The only exception is the narrow Registrar route for doctors with GMC-recognised GP registration from an approved country or comparable system, which is assessed individually by the GMC.

Does MRCP exempt me from the UKMLA?

No. MRCP(UK) is a specialty knowledge examination, while the UKMLA is the GMC's generalist licensing assessment for first-time registration — the two test fundamentally different things, and holding one does not exempt a doctor from the other. This applies equally to MRCS and other royal college memberships gained outside the UK.

Can experienced doctors skip the AKT?

No, not on the basis of experience or seniority alone. Unless a doctor specifically qualifies for the narrow Registrar route or holds a valid Approved Practice Setting sponsorship arrangement, the AKT is required regardless of how many years of specialist or generalist experience that doctor already has.

What is the Registrar route and who qualifies for it?

The Registrar route allows doctors with GMC-recognised specialist or GP registration from an approved country or comparable system to apply for entry to the Specialist Register or GP Register without sitting the UKMLA, in some circumstances. Eligibility is assessed case-by-case by the GMC based on the doctor's specific registration history and country or system of qualification, so anyone considering this route should check the GMC's specialist register guidance directly rather than assuming eligibility from general seniority.

Does working as a locum or Trust Grade doctor in the NHS exempt me from the UKMLA?

No. Current NHS employment, including locum and Trust Grade roles, does not itself confer an exemption from the UKMLA. Unless a doctor has already secured full GMC registration with a licence to practise through the standard route, the Registrar route, or a confirmed Approved Practice Setting arrangement, the requirement to complete the UKMLA remains in place regardless of how long they have already worked clinically in the NHS.

Is the UKMLA harder or easier for experienced specialists, and what should they focus on?

It varies by component rather than being uniformly easier or harder. Experienced specialists and GPs generally find the CPSA's clinical reasoning stations play to their existing strengths, since years of patient contact build exactly the judgement and consultation fluency the assessment examines, so preparation there can reasonably lean on existing experience. However, the AKT's generalist breadth and UK-specific content, including prescribing conventions and NICE-guideline-based decisions, are often less familiar to an experienced specialist than to a recently trained generalist, so dedicated, timed practice with the official MLA content map still matters regardless of seniority.

Where can I check my personal eligibility for a UKMLA exemption?

Because both exemption routes are assessed individually and their criteria can change, the only reliable way to confirm personal eligibility is directly through the GMC — via a GMC Online account query or by checking current guidance on the GMC's own website, including its specialist register pages. The site's own guide to detailed UKMLA eligibility rules is a useful starting point for understanding the standard route, but it should not be treated as a substitute for a direct check of your specific case.

Conclusion

For the great majority of doctors, including experienced specialists, GPs, and holders of MRCP, MRCS, or equivalent royal college qualifications, UKMLA exemptions for specialist doctors simply do not apply — the AKT and CPSA remain the standard route to full GMC registration with a licence to practise. The only genuine exceptions are the narrow Registrar route for doctors with GMC-recognised specialist or GP registration from an approved country, and time-limited Approved Practice Setting sponsorship, both of which depend on individual circumstances that only the GMC can confirm. Doctors who do not clearly meet one of these narrow criteria should plan on sitting the full UKMLA, using their existing clinical experience to their advantage in CPSA preparation while giving the AKT's UK-specific content and format the dedicated attention it needs, in the same way covered in the site's complete guide to becoming a doctor in the UK and, once registration is confirmed as necessary, the detail on primary source verification through EPIC. Start your GMC registration journey the right way →
