post_title: GMC Registration Requirements for International Doctors: The Complete Process
post_excerpt: A nationality-agnostic breakdown of every GMC registration requirement for international medical graduates, from primary source verification to the final licence to practise.
seo_title: GMC Registration Requirements for IMGs
seo_description: GMC registration requirements for international doctors: EPIC verification, English testing, the UKMLA, and how to complete your application.
primary_keyword: GMC registration requirements for international doctors
html_content:
GMC registration requirements for international doctors come down to five conditions that apply regardless of which country a doctor trained in: confirmed eligibility, completed primary source verification of the primary medical qualification, accepted evidence of English language proficiency, passes in both components of the UKMLA, and a successfully processed application through GMC Online. Only once every one of these conditions is satisfied does the General Medical Council grant registration with a licence to practise — the legal authorisation that allows a doctor to work as a doctor anywhere in the United Kingdom. Passing the UKMLA alone does not confer this authorisation; it is one of five pieces that all have to be in place together.

This guide sets out the GMC registration process for doctors trained outside the UK in generic, country-agnostic terms, since the underlying framework — eligibility, EPIC verification, English testing, the exam, and the registration application itself — is identical whether a doctor qualified in India, Nigeria, Pakistan, Egypt, the Gulf, or anywhere else on the World Directory of Medical Schools. What differs by country is the fine detail: which documents a specific national regulator issues, how quickly a particular medical school typically responds to a verification request, and where the nearest test centre for exams or English tests happens to be. Consequently, this article deliberately avoids country-specific procedural detail and instead points to the site's dedicated country posts wherever that nuance matters, so international medical graduates (IMGs) from any background can use this as their master reference and then drill into the post written for their own country.

Because registering with the GMC as an IMG sits inside a much larger journey — one that also includes exam preparation, visa planning, and eventually finding NHS employment — this guide focuses specifically on the registration and verification mechanics rather than repeating ground already covered elsewhere on the site. For the complete end-to-end journey, including how the UKMLA fits alongside registration and what comes after, see the site's guide to how to become a doctor in the UK. It is also worth checking, before investing time in any of the steps below, whether the full UKMLA route even applies to your situation — some specialists and GP-trained doctors qualify for a different route entirely, covered in the site's guide to exemptions from the standard UKMLA requirement.

The Five Requirements for GMC Registration With a Licence to Practise

Full GMC registration with a licence to practise is granted only once eligibility is confirmed, primary source verification is complete, English language evidence is accepted, and both UKMLA components are passed — after which the registration application itself is submitted and processed. These five requirements apply in exactly the same order and to the same standard regardless of a doctor's nationality or country of qualification; the GMC does not operate a faster or slower track based on where a candidate trained, only different supporting evidence depending on the qualifying country's own documentation and regulatory structures.

Understanding this five-part structure matters because it reframes what "GMC registration" actually means in practice. It is not a single form submitted at the end of the process; it is the final administrative step that follows naturally once four separate, largely independent workstreams have already been completed. In addition, three of those four prerequisite workstreams — eligibility confirmation, primary source verification, and English language testing — do not depend on exam results at all, and therefore can and should be started well before a candidate feels "ready" to sit the AKT. Doctors who treat GMC registration requirements for international doctors as a checklist to begin only after the exams are behind them routinely add many months to their overall timeline for no good reason.

The five requirements, at a glance, are as follows:

  • Eligibility — a primary medical qualification from a school and programme that meets the GMC's recognition standards.
  • Primary source verification — independent confirmation of the qualification through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials).
  • English language evidence — an accepted IELTS or OET certificate meeting the minimum score thresholds.
  • UKMLA success — passes in both the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA).
  • The registration application — submission via GMC Online, identity verification, and payment of the registration fee.

Each of these is covered in turn below, with the country-agnostic mechanics explained in full and country-specific detail deliberately left to the site's dedicated posts for individual nationalities. The GMC's own overview of the full pathway — Before You Apply: A Guide for Doctors — is the authoritative source for all five stages and should be treated as the definitive reference whenever this guide and the GMC's own published guidance appear to differ, since GMC requirements are reviewed and updated periodically.

Eligibility: Does Your Medical Qualification Qualify?

Eligibility for GMC registration depends on holding a primary medical qualification from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS) for the year in which the qualification was awarded, and on that programme meeting the GMC's minimum standards for duration and curriculum content. This is the very first gate in the entire pathway, and it is worth confirming deliberately rather than assuming, since not every medical school and not every qualification automatically satisfies it.

The World Directory of Medical Schools is the internationally recognised register — maintained jointly by the World Federation for Medical Education and the Foundation for Advancement of International Medical Education and Research — that regulators including the GMC use to confirm a medical school was legitimately accredited to award medical degrees at the time a candidate graduated. Consequently, the relevant question is not simply "is my medical school on WDOMS today," but "was my medical school listed on WDOMS for the specific year I graduated," since accreditation status can change over time and the GMC checks against the historical listing relevant to your own qualification date. Before progressing further, it is worth reviewing the site's full eligibility criteria for GMC registration, which sets out these qualification-recognition rules alongside the health and character declarations that apply to every applicant.

Beyond the WDOMS listing itself, the GMC also expects the underlying programme to have met certain minimum standards — broadly, a full-time programme of an appropriate minimum duration, covering the breadth of clinical and pre-clinical training expected of a primary medical qualification, rather than an abbreviated or non-standard pathway. Most standard medical degree programmes worldwide satisfy this comfortably; however, candidates with an unusual academic history — for example, an accelerated programme, a qualification obtained through a non-standard route, or training interrupted and resumed at a different institution — should confirm their specific circumstances directly with the GMC rather than assume eligibility.

Because the detailed eligibility rules include several specific edge cases — recognised qualifications, dual-accreditation programmes, and how the GMC treats postgraduate qualifications obtained after the primary degree — the full detail is covered separately in the site's dedicated guide to UKMLA eligibility and who can sit the exam. Reading that guide before investing time in EPIC verification or exam booking is the safest way to avoid discovering an eligibility problem only after other steps are already under way.

Country-Specific Eligibility Nuances

Although the WDOMS-based eligibility test itself is universal, the practical experience of confirming eligibility varies by country, since national medical education systems differ in how consistently their schools appear on WDOMS, how programme duration is structured, and how postgraduate or specialist qualifications interact with the primary degree. For this reason, doctors from a specific country are generally better served checking the site's dedicated post for their own nationality first, where the common eligibility questions for that country's graduates are already addressed directly.

Primary Source Verification Through EPIC

EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials) is the online platform the GMC uses to carry out primary source verification of an international medical graduate's qualifications — meaning the degree, the transcripts, and typically the internship record are confirmed directly with the institution that issued them, rather than accepted on the strength of a certified copy alone. This step is mandatory for every IMG regardless of country of qualification, and it cannot be skipped, expedited through a higher fee, or substituted with a notarised or apostilled document.

The distinction between primary source verification and a certified copy is the single most common point of confusion for first-time applicants of any nationality. A certified copy confirms only that a photocopy matches an original document a candidate physically holds; it says nothing about whether the awarding institution itself confirms, from its own records, that the qualification was genuinely granted. EPIC verification, by contrast, involves the GMC's verification partner contacting the medical school directly and asking the school to confirm the degree, the graduation date, and the programme details from its own institutional records — a materially higher evidentiary bar, and the reason this step takes weeks rather than days for every applicant, everywhere.

Creating an EPIC Account and Submitting Documents

The process begins with creating an EPIC account, accessed via a link within GMC Online once basic eligibility has been confirmed. Within EPIC, a candidate registers their medical school, degree details, and graduation year, and then submits the required supporting documents for that qualification. EPIC then routes a verification request to the medical school's designated liaison or academic office, which is asked to confirm the details directly with EPIC rather than with the candidate.

Because the request goes to the institution rather than to the applicant, a candidate's own responsiveness stops being the limiting factor once the request has been submitted correctly — the medical school's administrative speed becomes the deciding variable instead. This is an important adjustment for candidates accustomed to processes where personal effort directly shortens the timeline; once an EPIC application has been submitted properly, the most productive action available is periodic, polite follow-up with the issuing institution, rather than assuming nothing further can be done.

The GMC publishes full, authoritative detail on exactly which documents EPIC requires and how the resulting verification feeds into the wider registration decision on its own website; see the GMC's guidance referenced within Before You Apply: A Guide for Doctors for the current, definitive requirements. Reading this page directly before creating an EPIC account is worthwhile, since it is updated periodically and takes precedence over any general third-party summary.

How Long EPIC Verification Typically Takes

Response times from medical schools to EPIC verification requests vary enormously, and this variability is one of the single biggest sources of unpredictability in the entire GMC registration process for international doctors. In broad, indicative terms, well-resourced institutions with a dedicated international affairs or examinations office often respond within a small number of weeks to a couple of months, whereas institutions handling verification requests informally, or relying on paper records rather than digitised ones, can take several months or longer. These ranges differ meaningfully by country and even by individual institution within the same country, so they should be read as broad planning bands rather than a fixed promise for any specific applicant.

Because EPIC response speed depends almost entirely on factors outside any individual candidate's control — whether the request lands during an academic term or an examination period, whether the relevant graduating cohort's records are digitised, and how the responsible administrative staff member manages competing duties — the single most effective planning decision available to any IMG, regardless of nationality, is to start EPIC verification as early as possible, ideally well before serious AKT preparation begins. Every week spent delaying the start of EPIC verification is simply a week added to the overall timeline that could otherwise have run quietly in the background while exam preparation continued in parallel.

Because typical response times, the exact documents requested, and the correct national regulatory body to approach for a registration-status confirmation all vary meaningfully by country, readers are strongly encouraged to consult the site's dedicated country post for a detailed, procedural walkthrough specific to their own qualifying country. The site's guide to GMC registration and EPIC verification for Indian doctors is a good example of the level of country-specific detail available — covering exact document lists, the national regulatory transition candidates from that country need to be aware of, and realistic response-time ranges for that country's medical schools specifically. Doctors from other countries will find an equivalent level of country-specific procedural depth in the post written for their own nationality.

What EPIC Verification Costs

EPIC verification carries its own fee, separate from GMC registration fees, exam fees, and English testing costs, and this fee is generally payable by the candidate at the point of submitting the EPIC application. Because fees across the entire pathway are reviewed periodically, candidates should confirm the current amount directly on the EPIC platform or through GMC Online rather than budgeting from a figure quoted in any third-party guide, including this one. Building the EPIC fee into an overall pathway budget from the outset, alongside AKT and CPSA costs and the eventual registration fee, avoids an unwelcome surprise partway through the process.

English Language Evidence for GMC Registration

Acceptable English language evidence for GMC registration means either an IELTS Academic score of 7.0 overall with no individual component below 7.0, or an OET Medicine result of Grade B or higher in all four sub-tests (Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking). These thresholds apply uniformly to IMGs of every nationality, including doctors from countries where English is an official or widely used language of medical education, since the GMC's requirement is evidenced through a specific approved test rather than through self-declaration or prior exposure to English-medium training.

Both IELTS and OET certificates carry a validity window from the date of the test, and this window is one of the most common, entirely avoidable causes of delay across every nationality. Candidates who sit their English test too early relative to the rest of their pathway sometimes find their certificate has expired by the time they are ready to submit a complete registration application, forcing them to sit the test again at additional cost and with additional lead time. Sequencing the English test deliberately — neither so early that it expires before the rest of the pathway catches up, nor so late that it becomes the final bottleneck — therefore matters just as much as achieving the required score in the first place.

Because English testing logistics, test centre availability, and the specific scoring nuances that trip up candidates preparing in a non-native-English-speaking environment are covered in far greater depth elsewhere on the site, readers should consult the dedicated guide to the English language requirement for the UKMLA for the full breakdown of scoring bands, test formats, and preparation strategy. That guide is written with India-specific examples in its title and framing, but the underlying English language requirement, test formats, and score thresholds it describes are identical for every nationality — the mechanics of IELTS and OET do not change based on a candidate's country of origin.

The UKMLA: AKT and CPSA as the Licensing Assessment

The UKMLA (United Kingdom Medical Licensing Assessment) is the licensing examination component of GMC registration, made up of two separate parts that must both be passed: the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), a computer-based knowledge examination, and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA), a practical, scenario-based clinical skills examination. Passing both components demonstrates the level of medical knowledge and clinical competence the GMC requires before granting registration, and — together with eligibility, EPIC verification, and English language evidence — it forms one of the five pillars covered in this guide.

Because detailed exam format, syllabus coverage, scoring, booking logistics, and preparation strategy for the AKT and CPSA are already covered extensively elsewhere on the site, this guide deliberately does not repeat that detail here. Instead, this section exists only to place the UKMLA correctly within the wider registration pathway: it is the assessment component, sitting alongside — not instead of — eligibility confirmation, EPIC verification, and English language evidence, all of which are typically progressing at the same time as exam preparation rather than only afterwards.

One planning point is worth stating plainly here, since it recurs across every nationality: passing the UKMLA does not, by itself, grant the right to work as a doctor in the UK. Registration is only granted once the UKMLA passes are combined with completed EPIC verification, accepted English language evidence, and a successfully processed registration application. Consequently, candidates should never treat exam success as the finish line — it is one of five conditions, and treating it as the only one is a common, avoidable planning mistake.

The Registration Application: From GMC Online to a Licence to Practise

Once eligibility, EPIC verification, English language evidence, and both UKMLA passes are all confirmed, the registration application itself follows a defined, largely administrative sequence through GMC Online. This final stage is where every strand of the preceding pathway comes together into a single submitted application, and it is generally the fastest-moving stage of the whole process provided the supporting evidence is complete and internally consistent.

The sequence typically runs as follows for candidates of any nationality:

  1. Create or return to a GMC Online account. This account, generally established at the very start of the pathway, is where EPIC tracking, exam results, English language evidence, and eventually the registration application itself all come together in one place.
  2. Confirm all supporting evidence is complete. Before submitting, candidates should check that EPIC verification shows as complete, that the English test certificate remains valid and correctly uploaded, and that both AKT and CPSA results are recorded against the account.
  3. Submit the registration application itself, including standard declarations regarding health, criminal record, and professional conduct — requirements that apply identically to every applicant regardless of nationality, and which should be answered fully and accurately.
  4. Complete identity verification. The GMC requires robust proof of identity as part of registration, which many overseas candidates complete in conjunction with a UK visit — often the same trip used for the CPSA — or through an approved remote identity-verification route where available.
  5. Pay the registration fee and submit the completed application for processing.
  6. Await the outcome. Processing times vary, but applications with complete, unambiguous supporting documents typically move considerably faster than those where the GMC needs to raise a query.

Processing times for a complete, query-free application are generally measured in a small number of weeks; however, this figure should be treated as an indicative range rather than a guarantee, since the GMC's own processing capacity and the complexity of an individual file both affect the actual outcome for any given applicant. Registration fees are payable online through GMC Online at the point of submission, and the GMC accepts standard international payment methods from overseas applicants. As with every fee referenced in this guide, the exact current amount should always be confirmed directly on the GMC's website immediately before paying, rather than budgeted from a figure quoted here or in any other third-party source.

What Happens If Your Application Is Queried

A query from the GMC is not automatically a sign that something has gone seriously wrong; it is a routine part of the process for a meaningful proportion of applicants across every nationality, and it typically means one specific piece of evidence needs clarifying or replacing rather than the whole application being in doubt. Common reasons a query is raised include an ambiguous or incomplete transcript, a name discrepancy across documents that has not been formally explained, uncertainty over which national regulatory body's confirmation applies to a candidate's specific registration history, or a translation that does not meet the GMC's required certification standard.

When a query is raised, the GMC specifies exactly what additional information or document is required, and the most effective response is a prompt, complete, and precisely targeted reply — providing exactly what has been asked for, in the format requested, rather than resubmitting a broader set of documents in the hope one of them resolves the issue. Most queries, once answered completely, do not significantly extend the overall timeline; however, an incomplete or vague response can trigger a second query, which does meaningfully add to processing time. Keeping organised copies of every document submitted, together with dates and reference numbers for each communication, makes responding to a query considerably faster than reconstructing a submission history from scratch.

Identity Verification in Practice

Identity verification deserves particular attention because it is a step some overseas candidates plan for too late, regardless of nationality. Given that many IMGs remain resident outside the UK at the point their EPIC verification and exam results come through, coordinating identity verification — which may require an in-person element — alongside a planned UK trip for the CPSA is usually more efficient than treating it as a separate, later journey. Confirming exactly which identity verification route applies to a candidate's own circumstances, directly through GMC Online guidance, well before finalising UK travel plans, avoids an unnecessary second trip. This travel planning naturally overlaps with visa arrangements, which is why it is worth reading alongside the site's UK visa guide for international doctors, since visa timing and identity verification travel frequently need to be planned together rather than as two unrelated tasks.

Realistic Timeline: GMC Registration Requirements for International Doctors

Bringing every stage together, IMGs of any nationality should plan around a realistic overall timeline rather than an optimistic best case. EPIC verification alone can take anywhere from a few weeks to several months depending on the responding institution, English language testing and its associated preparation typically adds further time, and the AKT and CPSA each carry their own preparation and booking lead times on top of the underlying administrative track. The table below synthesises indicative ranges for each stage; because every figure depends on institutional and individual variables outside this guide's control, these ranges should be read as planning bands, not guarantees, and always cross-checked against current GMC guidance before major decisions are made.

StageTypical Indicative RangeRuns in Parallel With
Confirming eligibility (WDOMS check)Days to a few weeksCan be done first, before anything else starts
EPIC account creation and document submissionA few weeks of active preparationAKT preparation, English test booking
EPIC primary source verification (institution response)Several weeks to several monthsAKT/CPSA preparation and booking
English language test (IELTS/OET) preparation and sittingA few weeks to a few monthsEPIC verification, AKT preparation
AKT preparation and bookingSeveral months, candidate-dependentEPIC verification, English testing
CPSA preparation, travel, and sittingWeeks to a few months after AKT passIdentity verification travel
Registration application processingA small number of weeks once completeFinal stage — depends on all prior stages
Overall pathway, start to full registrationRoughly eighteen months to three yearsVaries substantially by individual circumstances

This overall range is deliberately wide, and it should be read as a planning tool rather than a precise prediction, since no general guide can account for the administrative speed of any specific medical school or the GMC's own processing capacity at the time an individual candidate applies. What a realistic range does allow, however, is better decision-making around finances, employment, and family planning throughout the pathway — candidates who assume registration will take six months are far more likely to experience financial and psychological strain than those who plan from the outset around a multi-year horizon and treat any earlier completion as a welcome bonus rather than an expectation.

Running the administrative track and the exam preparation track genuinely in parallel, rather than sequentially, remains the single most effective lever available to compress this overall timeline, regardless of nationality. In practice, this means starting EPIC verification and English language testing during the same months AKT preparation begins, rather than waiting until a candidate feels "ready" for the exams before addressing the paperwork.

Where Country-Specific Detail Matters

Although the five-part structure covered in this guide is genuinely universal, several important details do vary meaningfully by country, and this guide has deliberately avoided papering over that variation with false generality. Specifically, the exact documents a national medical regulator issues, the typical response speed of medical schools to EPIC requests, the nearest available test centres for the UKMLA and for English language tests, and any national regulatory transitions or quirks that affect how registration-status confirmation works, all differ meaningfully from one country to another.

For this reason, once the generic framework in this guide is understood, readers are strongly encouraged to move to the dedicated post written for their own country for the procedural depth that a single generic article cannot responsibly provide. The site currently maintains detailed, country-specific posts covering several major source countries, including:

  • The site's in-depth guide to GMC registration and EPIC verification for Indian doctors, covering exact document requirements and the national regulatory transition relevant to that country.
  • The complete route overview in how to become a doctor in the UK from India, covering the full pathway end to end for that nationality.
  • The dedicated guide to UKMLA registration for Nigerian doctors, covering the equivalent EPIC and registration-confirmation process specific to that country's regulatory structure.

Readers from other countries not yet covered by a dedicated post should still apply the same universal five-part framework described in this guide, while confirming country-specific document requirements, typical medical-school response times, and local test centre availability directly with the GMC, EPIC, and their own national medical regulator, since these details are the ones most likely to differ from the examples given above.

Common Mistakes Across All Nationalities

Several mistakes recur across IMGs from every country, and recognising them in advance is one of the simplest ways to prevent a minor administrative delay from becoming a multi-month setback.

  • Starting EPIC too late. Because EPIC verification depends on institutional response speed rather than personal effort, delaying its start until after exam preparation is under way — or worse, until after exams are passed — routinely adds months to the overall timeline for no benefit whatsoever.
  • Letting English test certificates expire. IELTS and OET certificates carry a validity window, and candidates who sit the test too early relative to the rest of their pathway sometimes discover their certificate has lapsed by the time they are ready to submit a complete registration application, forcing an unnecessary and costly resit.
  • Underestimating document translation and certification time. Any document not already issued in English requires certified translation to a standard the GMC accepts, and sourcing a recognised professional translation service, then allowing time for the translation itself, is frequently underestimated by candidates planning their timeline.
  • Assuming exam success alone is sufficient. Passing both the AKT and CPSA is necessary but not sufficient; registration is only granted once EPIC verification and English language evidence are also complete, and treating the exams as the final hurdle rather than one of five is a persistent, avoidable misunderstanding.
  • Name mismatches across documents. Degree certificates, passports, and national regulator registration records are sometimes issued at different points in time using slightly different name formats, and resolving this with formal evidence before submission avoids an otherwise easily preventable query.
  • Treating registration travel and visa planning as unrelated tasks. Identity verification, CPSA attendance, and visa arrangements frequently need to happen within the same UK trip, and planning them separately rather than together often results in avoidable extra travel and cost.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the minimum GMC registration requirements for international doctors?

The minimum requirements are a primary medical qualification from a school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools for the relevant graduation year, completed primary source verification through EPIC, accepted English language evidence, and passes in both the AKT and CPSA components of the UKMLA. Only once all four are satisfied can the registration application itself be submitted and processed through GMC Online.

Do GMC registration requirements differ by country of qualification?

The underlying five-part framework — eligibility, EPIC verification, English language evidence, UKMLA passes, and the registration application — is identical for every nationality. However, the specific documents required, typical medical-school response times, and local test centre availability vary by country, which is why the site maintains dedicated posts covering the procedural detail for individual countries.

How long does the GMC registration process take for an international doctor?

Most candidates who plan carefully and avoid major setbacks should expect somewhere in the region of eighteen months to three years from beginning EPIC verification to holding full GMC registration, though individual circumstances — particularly EPIC response speed and exam performance — can move this substantially in either direction. This range should be treated as a planning tool rather than a precise prediction, since no general guide can account for a specific medical school's administrative speed or the GMC's processing capacity at any given time.

Can I start GMC registration before passing the UKMLA?

Full registration cannot be granted until both UKMLA components are passed, but the earlier stages — confirming eligibility, creating an EPIC account, and sitting an English language test — should all begin well before the exams, since they run on independent timelines. Starting these workstreams early, in parallel with exam preparation, is the single most effective way to shorten the overall pathway.

What documents do I need for EPIC verification?

Generic requirements include a degree certificate, full academic transcripts, evidence of internship or equivalent clinical training completion, and confirmation of current registration status from the relevant national medical regulator, alongside identity documents such as a passport. Because exact document names and issuing bodies vary by country, readers should consult the dedicated country post for their own nationality or confirm directly with EPIC for a precise list.

Is a certified copy of my medical degree enough for GMC registration?

No. A certified copy only confirms that a photocopy matches an original document a candidate holds; it does not confirm, from the issuing institution's own records, that the qualification was genuinely awarded. Primary source verification through EPIC is mandatory for every international medical graduate and cannot be substituted with a certified or notarised copy, however official that copy appears.

What English test score do I need for GMC registration?

The GMC accepts either an IELTS Academic score of 7.0 overall with no component below 7.0, or an OET Medicine result of Grade B or higher in all four sub-tests. Both certificates carry a validity window, so timing the test so the certificate remains valid through to registration application submission requires deliberate sequencing rather than sitting it at the first convenient opportunity.

Conclusion

GMC registration requirements for international doctors follow the same five-part structure everywhere in the world — eligibility, EPIC verification, English language evidence, UKMLA success, and the registration application itself — even though the fine detail of documents, response times, and test centres varies meaningfully by country. Treating EPIC verification and English testing as early, parallel tasks rather than a final checklist, keeping documents complete and internally consistent, and consulting the dedicated country-specific posts for procedural nuance are the practical habits that separate a registration that proceeds smoothly from one that stalls for months. Read the full GMC registration guide →

post_title: How to Become a Doctor in the UK: The Complete GMC Registration Guide for IMGs
post_excerpt: The full, country-agnostic route to becoming a GMC-registered doctor in the UK as an international medical graduate, from eligibility through to your first NHS post.
seo_title: How to Become a Doctor in UK: Full GMC Guide
seo_description: How to become a doctor in UK as an international medical graduate: eligibility, EPIC verification, English testing, the UKMLA, and full GMC registration.
primary_keyword: how to become a doctor in UK
html_content:
Working out how to become a doctor in UK practice can feel overwhelming at first, because the process looks different depending on where you trained, whether you are already a qualified doctor or still a student, and which specialty you eventually want to pursue. In simple terms, the route to becoming a doctor in the UK for the vast majority of international medical graduates (IMGs) runs through six stages: confirming eligibility, completing primary source verification of your medical degree through EPIC, providing evidence of English language proficiency, passing the UK Medical Licensing Assessment (UKMLA), applying for General Medical Council (GMC) registration with a licence to practise, and then securing the right visa before starting an NHS post.

This guide is the site's complete, country-agnostic overview of that pathway. It exists because most people searching for how to become a doctor in the UK have not yet worked out which of the site's more specific guides applies to them — whether that is a country-specific walkthrough, a deep dive into one exam component, or the visa process itself. Rather than duplicating that detail, this page synthesises the whole journey at a sensible depth and links out to dedicated posts wherever a topic deserves fuller treatment.

Three genuinely different routes lead to UK medical registration, and conflating them is the single most common source of confusion for readers researching this topic. The first, and by far the most common route for readers of this site, is the international medical graduate (IMG) pathway described above, built around the UKMLA. The second is qualifying via a UK medical school as an undergraduate, which is a longer route entirely separate from the IMG process, since it leads to provisional GMC registration automatically on graduation rather than requiring separate exams. The third is a narrow set of specialist and GP registrar routes, reserved for doctors who already hold recognised postgraduate qualifications and meet stringent criteria, which are covered in detail in the dedicated post on exemptions available to specialist and GP doctors. This guide focuses primarily on the first route, while explaining clearly how the other two differ.

It is worth being explicit, too, about who this particular guide is written for. If you already know you trained in India, Nigeria, Pakistan, or one of the other countries the site covers in detail, you may prefer to jump straight to that country's dedicated walkthrough rather than reading this overview in full — the "find your country" section further down links directly to each of them. However, if you are still forming a general picture of the route to practising medicine in the UK, or you are comparing the UK against other destinations before committing to a specific pathway, this page is designed to be read start to finish. It deliberately avoids duplicating the fine detail already covered elsewhere on the site, focusing instead on how every stage of becoming a doctor in the UK connects to the next.

The Quick Answer: How to Become a Doctor in the UK as an International Medical Graduate

For an international medical graduate, the complete route to becoming a doctor in the UK follows a defined, sequential pathway. Understanding the shape of this journey before diving into the detail of any single stage prevents the most common planning mistake: treating the UKMLA exams as the only hard part, when in practice the surrounding administrative and immigration steps often take just as long.

  1. Confirm your eligibility — verify that your primary medical qualification comes from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS) and that your programme met minimum GMC standards.
  2. Begin primary source verification of your medical degree through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials), the platform the GMC uses to confirm your qualification directly with your medical school.
  3. Provide evidence of English language proficiency, usually through IELTS Academic or OET Medicine, unless an accepted exemption applies.
  4. Pass the UKMLA, which consists of two components: the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) and the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA).
  5. Apply for GMC registration with a licence to practise once both assessments are passed and all supporting evidence is verified.
  6. Secure the correct visa to work in the UK, most commonly the Skilled Worker visa sponsored by an NHS employer.
  7. Find an NHS post and begin practising medicine in the United Kingdom.

This sequence represents the standard route to practising medicine in the UK for the overwhelming majority of internationally trained doctors. However, it is worth restating that two other, quite different routes exist. A UK medical student completing an undergraduate MBBS or MBChB at a GMC-approved UK medical school does not need to sit the UKMLA as an IMG-style exam at all in the same sense — they sit it as an integrated part of their degree and move automatically to provisional registration on graduation, a genuinely different and considerably longer route that begins years earlier, at the point of university admission. Meanwhile, a small number of doctors with specific specialist or GP postgraduate qualifications recognised as equivalent to UK training can apply through narrower registration routes that bypass parts of the standard IMG process, detailed further in the post on UKMLA exemptions for specialist and GP doctors. Because these three routes are so often confused in general search queries, the comparison table further down this page sets out their differences at a glance.

Eligibility: Who Can Pursue the IMG Route to UK Medical Registration

Eligibility for the IMG pathway is defined primarily by where and how you qualified as a doctor, rather than by your nationality. In short, the GMC requires that your primary medical qualification was awarded by a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS) for the year you graduated, and that the programme met certain minimum standards around duration, curriculum, and supervised clinical training.

In practice, this means graduates of the great majority of established medical schools across South Asia, Africa, the Middle East, and elsewhere satisfy this first hurdle without difficulty, provided their institution held valid accreditation at the time of graduation. However, eligibility is not simply a matter of holding a medical degree — the GMC also expects evidence of full, unrestricted registration with your home country's medical regulator (following completion of any mandatory internship or housemanship year), since provisional or temporary registration status is not sufficient on its own.

Because eligibility rules include specific provisions for unusual qualification types, part-time programmes, and degrees awarded through non-standard routes, every prospective candidate should check their own circumstances against the site's dedicated UKMLA eligibility requirements guide before committing time and money to the process. Confirming your medical school's current WDOMS listing takes only a few minutes and is one of the most valuable early steps in becoming a doctor in the UK, since a lapsed listing can cause confusion much later in the process if left unchecked.

Eligibility also depends on the minimum programme standards the GMC expects any recognised medical degree to meet. In practice, this means a full undergraduate medical programme of appropriate length, incorporating both a theoretical curriculum and a substantial period of supervised clinical placement, examined through a formal assessment process recognised by the relevant national regulator. Degrees earned through accelerated, part-time, or otherwise non-standard programme structures are not automatically excluded, but they are more likely to trigger additional scrutiny during primary source verification, so candidates in this position should read the eligibility guidance particularly carefully before assuming their situation is straightforward. Consequently, candidates whose programme structure differs meaningfully from the conventional model should budget extra time for this stage rather than assuming eligibility will be confirmed as quickly as it is for graduates of more conventional programmes.

It is also worth noting that eligibility is assessed at the level of the individual applicant, not simply the medical school as an institution. Two graduates of the same medical school, one who graduated while the school held valid WDOMS accreditation and one who graduated during a lapse in that accreditation, can face entirely different outcomes at this stage. For this reason, checking your own graduation year specifically against the WDOMS record, rather than assuming your medical school's current listing status applies retroactively, is a small but genuinely important piece of due diligence before beginning the wider process of becoming a doctor in the UK.

Primary Source Verification: How EPIC Fits Into the Process

Primary source verification is the GMC's mechanism for confirming that your medical qualification is genuine, verified directly with the issuing institution rather than accepted on the strength of a certificate alone. This is handled through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials), a global verification platform used by medical regulators, including the GMC, around the world.

In broad terms, the EPIC process involves creating an account, submitting your degree and internship documents, and then waiting while EPIC contacts your medical school directly to confirm the qualification's authenticity. Processing time varies enormously by institution — well-resourced medical schools with dedicated international relations offices often respond within weeks, whereas others can take several months, particularly around examination periods. Because this step depends on your medical school's administrative speed rather than your own effort, most successful candidates begin the EPIC process as early as possible, running it in parallel with exam preparation rather than treating it as a final formality.

This overview deliberately keeps things at a summary level, since EPIC's document requirements, submission mechanics, and common delay points are covered in full in the dedicated post on GMC registration requirements for international doctors. That guide is the right place to look for detail on exactly which documents EPIC requires and how to avoid the most common submission errors.

What matters most at this overview level is understanding why EPIC verification so often becomes the pacing item for the entire route to becoming a doctor in the UK. Unlike exam preparation, which is largely within a candidate's own control, EPIC's timeline depends on a third party — your medical school's registrar or international office — responding to a verification request in a timely manner. Some institutions have streamlined, dedicated processes for handling these requests and respond within a few weeks; others, particularly smaller or under-resourced institutions, may take several months, especially if the request arrives during an examination period or an academic holiday. Therefore, treating EPIC verification and exam preparation as two separate, parallel projects, rather than a strict sequence where one must finish before the other begins, is consistently the single most effective piece of planning advice for candidates starting this journey.

In addition, candidates should keep a written record of every communication exchanged with their medical school during this process. If a verification request stalls for an unusually long period, having a clear paper trail makes it considerably easier to escalate the matter, whether directly with the institution or, in some cases, through EPIC's own support channels. This single habit — treated as routine administrative housekeeping rather than an afterthought — resolves a meaningful share of the delays candidates report during this stage.

English Language Requirements for Becoming a Doctor in the UK

Almost every IMG pursuing UK medical registration must submit formal evidence of English language proficiency, regardless of whether their medical education was conducted in English. The GMC accepts two tests for this purpose: IELTS Academic, where the required overall score is 7.0, with no individual component below 7.0, and OET (Occupational English Test), Medicine version, where the requirement is a minimum of Grade B across all four sub-tests.

Both certificates carry a defined validity period from the test date, so timing matters considerably. Candidates who sit their English test too early in their overall journey risk their certificate expiring before they are ready to submit a full registration application, which then forces an unwelcome and costly re-sit. For this reason, sequencing the English test against your expected registration date deserves real thought rather than being treated as an early box-ticking exercise.

Because accepted score thresholds, exemption criteria, and submission mechanics are subject to periodic review, this overview intentionally avoids restating every detail. Readers preparing to book a test should consult the GMC's own current guidance directly, cited in full below, and can also find broader country-specific context in some of the site's dedicated guides on this exact requirement.

Choosing between IELTS and OET is a genuinely practical decision worth thinking through rather than defaulting to whichever test is more widely available locally. OET's scenarios are built entirely around healthcare communication — referral letters, clinical case notes, and doctor-patient consultations — which many practising clinicians find more intuitive than the broader academic subject matter covered by IELTS Academic. Meanwhile, IELTS preparation material tends to be more widely available in some regions, and its test centre network is often larger, which can matter for candidates in areas with fewer local testing options. Neither test is inherently easier, so the right choice generally comes down to which format suits an individual candidate's strengths and which is more logistically convenient to sit.

What the UKMLA Actually Tests: AKT and CPSA Explained

The UK Medical Licensing Assessment is the single national examination that both UK medical students and IMGs must pass to demonstrate the knowledge and skills required for safe practice in the UK. It consists of two distinct components, each testing something different, and both must be passed to gain GMC registration with a licence to practise.

The Applied Knowledge Test (AKT)

The Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) is a computer-based examination testing clinical knowledge, decision-making, and the professional judgement expected of a newly qualified doctor. It is delivered through Pearson VUE test centres, which means IMG candidates can often sit it in their home country or region rather than travelling to the UK — a genuinely useful practical advantage over the CPSA. The AKT draws on the official MLA content map, a defined syllabus of clinical presentations, conditions, and professional knowledge areas, so preparation is most effective when built directly around that published content rather than around general revision material.

The Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA)

The Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) is the practical, scenario-based component, examining clinical examination technique, patient communication, data interpretation, and professionalism through a structured circuit of stations. Unlike the AKT, the CPSA can only be sat in person at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre, which for the great majority of candidates means a mandatory trip to the UK regardless of how much of the earlier process was completed remotely. Candidates should therefore build travel, accommodation, and visa considerations into their planning for this stage well in advance, rather than leaving it until after the AKT result arrives.

Passing both components in sequence, AKT before CPSA, is a strict requirement of the pathway, and candidates cannot book the CPSA until they hold a passing AKT result. Because becoming a doctor in the UK ultimately depends on clearing both assessments, most successful candidates treat AKT and CPSA preparation as two related but distinct projects, each requiring its own study plan, timeline, and — for the CPSA in particular — its own logistical planning.

It is worth being clear about what each assessment is actually trying to measure, since candidates sometimes prepare for the wrong thing. The AKT is fundamentally a test of applied clinical reasoning under exam conditions — it rewards candidates who can quickly identify the most appropriate next step in a clinical scenario, drawing on UK-specific guidelines such as those published by NICE, rather than simply recalling textbook facts. The CPSA, in contrast, rewards candidates who can demonstrate safe, structured clinical behaviour in real time — taking a focused history, performing an examination correctly, and communicating clearly with a patient or colleague under some time pressure. Consequently, revising AKT content in isolation does not automatically prepare a candidate for the CPSA, and vice versa; each requires a distinct form of practice, ideally including realistic mock scenarios and, where possible, structured feedback from someone familiar with the assessment format.

GMC Registration With a Licence to Practise

Once both UKMLA components are passed and all supporting evidence is verified, the final step before working as a doctor in the UK is applying for GMC registration with a licence to practise. This dual status is what actually grants the legal right to practise medicine in the UK — registration alone confirms your name is on the medical register, while the licence to practise is the specific authorisation that allows you to prescribe, sign death certificates, and hold a substantive NHS post. In other words, both halves of this status are necessary; one without the other does not permit clinical practice.

The application itself draws together every strand of the process covered so far: your verified EPIC evidence, your English language test certificate, your passing UKMLA results, and a set of identity and good-standing documents from your home country's medical regulator. The GMC reviews the full application before granting registration, and processing typically takes a matter of weeks once every document is in order, though incomplete submissions are a common and avoidable source of delay.

In practice, the strongest applications are the ones where every prior stage was completed with the final registration application already in mind, rather than treated as an isolated hurdle to clear and forget. For example, a candidate who requests a Certificate of Good Standing from their home regulator only after passing the CPSA often faces an unwelcome extra wait at exactly the point they expected to be closest to registration, whereas a candidate who requested it in parallel with CPSA preparation avoids this bottleneck entirely. Similarly, keeping certified copies of every document — degree certificate, transcripts, internship completion evidence, identity documents — organised and readily accessible throughout the process, rather than searching for them after both exams are passed, meaningfully shortens the final stretch of becoming a doctor in the UK.

Because this stage involves detailed document checklists, common query points, and specific guidance on what to do if an application is queried or delayed, readers should consult the site's dedicated GMC registration process guide and the deeper post on GMC registration requirements for international doctors for full step-by-step detail. For the GMC's own authoritative description of what happens before you apply, see the GMC's official guide for doctors before you apply, which should always take precedence over any third-party summary, including this one, whenever a detail appears to have changed.

Visa and Immigration Considerations

Passing the UKMLA and gaining GMC registration does not, on its own, grant the right to live and work in the UK — a separate immigration process runs alongside, and in practice often overlaps with, the registration pathway. Most IMGs enter the UK workforce on a Skilled Worker visa, sponsored by an NHS trust or other approved healthcare employer, though a small number of candidates use other routes depending on their personal circumstances, such as a Health and Care Worker visa variant or family-based immigration status.

Because visa rules, sponsorship requirements, and application costs change relatively often and depend heavily on individual circumstances, this overview deliberately stays at a high level. Readers should consult the dedicated UK visa guide for international doctors for a full breakdown of visa categories, sponsorship mechanics, dependants' rights, and the immigration health surcharge, since getting the immigration side wrong can delay a start date every bit as much as a failed exam attempt. In addition, candidates already inside the UK on another visa category — for example, a spouse or graduate visa — should check whether their existing status already permits NHS employment before assuming a fresh sponsored visa application is required.

Timing is the aspect of immigration planning most commonly underestimated. Sponsorship cannot begin until a specific NHS employer has actually made a job offer, which in turn usually cannot happen until GMC registration is secured, meaning the visa stage sits firmly at the end of the pathway rather than something that can be meaningfully accelerated in parallel with the earlier exam stages. However, candidates can still do useful preparatory work well in advance — gathering the supporting documents a visa application will eventually require, understanding the costs involved (including the visa fee itself and the immigration health surcharge), and, where dependants are involved, researching how family members are affected by a change in immigration status. Doing this groundwork early means the final visa stage, once it becomes actionable, moves as quickly as possible rather than introducing yet another unplanned delay right at the point of starting an NHS post.

Find Your Country: Navigating the Country-Specific Nuances

Although the core pathway described above applies broadly to every international medical graduate, the practical detail of becoming a doctor in the UK varies meaningfully by country of qualification — different national medical regulators, different typical EPIC processing times, different local AKT test centre availability, and different currency and cost considerations. For this reason, the site maintains a growing library of country-specific guides, each written for the practical realities of a particular training background.

  • Doctors trained in India can consult the detailed walkthrough on how to become a doctor in the UK from India, covering Medical Council of India/NMC recognition, EPIC timelines, and rupee-denominated cost planning.
  • Nigerian-trained doctors are covered in the guide to the UKMLA route for Nigerian doctors, addressing MDCN recognition and the realities of the mandatory Manchester CPSA trip from West Africa.
  • Pakistani-trained doctors have their own dedicated post addressing PMDC/PMC recognition and local test centre logistics.
  • Doctors trained in the Philippines can refer to the guide on the UKMLA pathway for Filipino doctors, which sets out Philippine Medical Association recognition and typical processing timelines.
  • Doctors from a range of smaller or less frequently covered training countries should see the round-up guide to country-specific UKMLA routes for international medical graduates, which consolidates practical guidance for candidates whose country does not yet have its own dedicated post.
  • Candidates pursuing this pathway as refugees or asylum seekers face a distinct set of practical and documentary challenges, from replacing lost qualification evidence to navigating immigration status alongside GMC requirements, addressed specifically in the guide on the UKMLA route for refugee doctors.

Readers who are still deciding between the UK and another major English-speaking destination for postgraduate medical practice — Australia or Canada, for instance — may also find it useful to consult the comparison guide on how the UKMLA compares with the AMC and MCCQE pathways before committing to a particular country's licensing exam, since preparation resources, exam structure, and typical timelines differ meaningfully between the three systems.

Realistic Timeline and Typical Total Cost

One of the most persistent misconceptions among candidates researching how to become a doctor in the UK is underestimating how long the full journey actually takes. Exam preparation time is relatively easy to plan for, whereas administrative processing time — EPIC verification, document requests from a home regulator, GMC application processing — depends on external parties and is far less predictable.

Combining every stage from beginning EPIC verification through to starting a first NHS post, most IMG candidates should realistically plan for a total timeline of 18 to 30 months, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. Candidates who need to re-sit either assessment, or who experience delays at the credential verification stage, should expect this range to extend further. This estimate is necessarily a synthesis across many different starting countries and personal circumstances, so treating it as a planning guideline rather than a guarantee is the sensible approach.

Cost follows a similarly wide but estimable range. Core GMC and Pearson VUE fees — covering the AKT, the CPSA, and initial GMC registration — typically total in the region of £1,800, before accounting for English test fees, EPIC verification charges, mandatory UK travel for the CPSA, visa costs, and study materials. Once every additional cost is included, most candidates should budget a realistic total in the region of £3,000 to £5,000 across the full pathway, though this varies by home country and by how many additional costs, such as flights, are involved. For exact, currently applicable figures broken down stage by stage, and for guidance on budgeting in a range of local currencies, see the dedicated full IMG fee breakdown, since fee schedules are reviewed periodically and this overview should not be treated as a substitute for checking current figures before making a payment.

Both the timeline and the cost figures above are, by necessity, averages drawn across many different starting countries, personal circumstances, and exam outcomes, so they should be read as planning benchmarks rather than fixed guarantees. A candidate whose medical school responds to EPIC within a few weeks, who passes both the AKT and the CPSA on the first attempt, and who secures visa sponsorship promptly after registration will likely land toward the shorter and cheaper end of these ranges. Meanwhile, a candidate who faces a slow EPIC response, needs to re-sit one assessment, or experiences delays gathering documents from their home regulator should expect both the timeline and the total cost to extend meaningfully beyond these figures. Building a financial and psychological buffer into your own planning, rather than assuming the shortest possible version of the pathway, is consistently the more realistic approach.

Life After Registration: Finding an NHS Post and Career Progression

Gaining GMC registration with a licence to practise is a genuine milestone, but it is the beginning of a UK medical career rather than its conclusion. Most newly registered IMGs enter the NHS workforce through a trust-grade or clinical fellow post, offering FY2-equivalent clinical responsibility without requiring a Foundation Programme place, since the Foundation Programme after the UKMLA is primarily the route taken by UK medical school graduates moving directly from provisional to full registration.

From this entry point, career progression typically follows one of several paths: applying for Specialty Training once sufficient UK clinical experience and portfolio evidence are in place, taking on locum work through NHS staff banks for those who want flexibility while building a UK track record, or pursuing General Practice training through the standard GP registrar pathway. The practicalities of this transition — how to search for suitable posts, what NHS recruiters look for from IMG applicants, and how trust-grade experience translates into a stronger training application — are covered in detail in the dedicated post on the pathway from UKMLA registration to an NHS job.

Newly registered doctors should also expect a genuine adjustment period covering both clinical and cultural dimensions of NHS practice, including structured handover formats, a strong institutional emphasis on multidisciplinary working, and a more codified approach to consent and capacity than many candidates are accustomed to from their home health systems. This adjustment is a normal, well-documented part of settling into UK practice rather than a sign that something has gone wrong, and most doctors who remain committed to the pathway describe the structured nature of UK training — clear competency frameworks and defined progression points — as a genuine long-term benefit once the initial adjustment period has passed.

It is also worth acknowledging, honestly, that this entire journey is a long one, and the administrative uncertainty involved can weigh on candidates just as much as the exams themselves. Readers who find the process stressful, particularly during long verification waits or after a difficult exam attempt, may find it useful to read the site's guide on managing UKMLA exam anxiety, which addresses the psychological side of this pathway directly rather than treating it as a purely administrative exercise.

Career progression beyond the first NHS post also tends to reward doctors who plan deliberately rather than simply staying in a trust-grade role indefinitely. Building a structured portfolio from the outset — audits, teaching experience, courses relevant to a target specialty — strengthens a future Specialty Training application considerably, and doctors who start this portfolio work from their first day in an NHS post generally progress faster than those who wait until they feel fully settled before beginning. Similarly, actively seeking feedback from consultants and educational supervisors during the trust-grade period, rather than treating it purely as a stepping stone, helps build the reference base that a competitive Specialty Training application requires. In this sense, life after registration is very much a continuation of the same deliberate planning that got a candidate through the earlier stages of becoming a doctor in the UK, rather than a point at which planning can stop.

Comparing the Three Routes to UK Medical Registration

Because search queries about becoming a doctor in the UK often conflate genuinely different routes, the table below sets out the three main pathways side by side, summarising eligibility, typical duration, and outcome at a glance.

RouteWho It's ForTypical DurationOutcome
IMG route via the UKMLADoctors who qualified outside the UK at a WDOMS-listed medical school18 to 30 months from starting EPIC verification to an NHS postGMC registration with a licence to practise, entry usually via a trust-grade or clinical fellow post
UK medical school (undergraduate)Students admitted to a GMC-approved UK medical school5 to 6 years of undergraduate study, plus 2 years of Foundation ProgrammeAutomatic provisional registration on graduation, then full registration after Foundation Year 1
Specialist/GP registrar exemption routesDoctors holding recognised postgraduate specialist or GP qualifications equivalent to UK trainingVaries significantly by qualification and specialty; can bypass some standard UKMLA requirementsRegistration via a narrower assessment route, often directly into specialist or GP practice

As the table illustrates, the IMG route and the UK undergraduate route lead to the same destination — full GMC registration with a licence to practise — by entirely different means and on very different timescales. Meanwhile, the specialist and GP registrar exemption routes serve a much smaller, more experienced population and depend heavily on individual qualification recognition, which is why they are addressed separately in the post on UKMLA exemptions for specialist and GP doctors rather than folded into this general overview.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does it take to become a doctor in the UK from abroad?

For an international medical graduate, most candidates should plan for a realistic total timeline of 18 to 30 months from beginning EPIC verification to starting a first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. Candidates who need to re-sit either assessment, or who experience delays at the credential verification stage, should expect this timeline to extend. This estimate synthesises typical processing times across many source countries, so individual circumstances can shift it in either direction.

Do I need to retake exams to become a doctor in the UK?

Yes, in almost every case. Regardless of how many postgraduate exams you may have already passed in your home country, or how many years of clinical experience you hold, international medical graduates must pass both components of the UKMLA — the AKT and the CPSA — to gain GMC registration with a licence to practise. The only exceptions are the narrow specialist and GP registrar routes described in the post on UKMLA exemptions for specialist and GP doctors, which apply to a small number of doctors with specific recognised postgraduate qualifications.

Can I become a doctor in the UK without sitting the UKMLA?

Only in two circumstances. UK medical students who qualify through a GMC-approved undergraduate medical school move to provisional registration automatically rather than sitting the UKMLA in the same way as an IMG, since the assessment is integrated into their degree. Separately, a small number of doctors holding recognised specialist or GP postgraduate qualifications may qualify for one of the narrower exemption routes, though these apply to a limited population and require individual assessment.

What qualifications do I need to become a doctor in the UK as an international graduate?

You need a primary medical qualification from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools for your graduation year, full and unrestricted registration with your home country's medical regulator, evidence of English language proficiency, and passing results in both components of the UKMLA. Full detail on specific edge cases, including non-standard qualification routes, is available in the site's dedicated UKMLA eligibility requirements guide.

How much does it cost to become a doctor in the UK from another country?

Core GMC and exam fees for the AKT, CPSA, and initial registration typically total around £1,800, though a realistic total budget once English testing, EPIC verification, mandatory UK travel, visa costs, and study materials are included usually falls between £3,000 and £5,000. Because fee schedules are reviewed periodically and vary somewhat by home country, candidates should consult the dedicated full IMG fee breakdown for current, stage-by-stage figures rather than relying on a single estimate.

Is the UK a good place to practise medicine compared with other countries?

This is ultimately a personal decision that depends on career goals, family circumstances, and which licensing pathway feels most achievable, rather than one this guide can answer definitively. However, candidates weighing the UK against other major destinations such as Australia or Canada may find it useful to compare exam structures and typical timelines directly in the guide on how the UKMLA compares with the AMC and MCCQE pathways before committing to a particular country's licensing process.

Do I need a visa to become a doctor in the UK?

Yes, unless you already hold the right to live and work in the UK through another route, such as an existing family or settled status. Most international medical graduates enter the NHS workforce on a Skilled Worker visa sponsored by an NHS employer, and immigration considerations should be planned alongside, not after, the UKMLA and GMC registration process. Full detail is available in the dedicated UK visa guide for international doctors.

What is the first practical step to becoming a doctor in the UK?

The first practical step is confirming that your medical school appears on the World Directory of Medical Schools for your graduation year and that you hold full, unrestricted registration with your home country's medical regulator. Once this is confirmed, the next action is creating a GMC Online account and beginning primary source verification of your degree through EPIC, since this stage usually takes the longest and should start well before exam preparation is finished. Reviewing the site's complete guide to what the UKMLA is is a useful next step for candidates who want to understand the exam itself before committing to a preparation timeline.

Conclusion

Becoming a doctor in the UK as an international medical graduate is a genuinely achievable, well-defined pathway, but it rewards candidates who plan the whole journey — credential verification, English testing, both UKMLA components, GMC registration, and immigration — as a single connected project rather than a series of unrelated hurdles tackled one at a time. Understanding which of the three routes actually applies to your situation, and reading the country-specific and topic-specific guides linked throughout this page for the depth that a single overview cannot provide, gives candidates the clearest possible view of what lies ahead. Check your UKMLA eligibility now →
