post_title: UKMLA for Filipino Doctors: Eligibility, Fees and the Route to GMC Registration
post_excerpt: A complete route guide for Filipino medical graduates covering UKMLA eligibility, PRC-to-GMC recognition, exam costs in pesos, and GMC registration.
seo_title: UKMLA for Filipino Doctors: Route & Fees
seo_description: UKMLA for Filipino doctors: PRC-to-GMC recognition, AKT and CPSA costs in Philippine pesos, English testing, and the full 2026 GMC registration route.
html_content:
UKMLA for Filipino doctors is becoming one of the most searched pathways among Philippine-trained physicians looking to practise in the United Kingdom, reflecting a steady rise in interest from graduates of Philippine medical schools who already work across the Gulf, the United States, and increasingly the NHS itself. For doctors holding a medical degree recognised by the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC), the route to sitting the UKMLA and eventually securing full GMC registration follows a defined sequence: credential verification, English language testing, two structured examinations, and a formal registration application. None of these stages is individually difficult, but together they demand a level of administrative patience, budgeting discipline, and forward planning that catches many first-time Filipino applicants off guard.

This guide walks through the entire journey specifically as it applies to doctors trained in the Philippines — from confirming that an MD degree from a PRC-recognised Philippine medical school satisfies GMC eligibility rules, through primary source verification, booking exams, and budgeting in Philippine pesos for a pathway priced in pounds sterling. It also covers where Filipino candidates can sit the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), why the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) must be taken in Manchester, and the realistic timeline and common pitfalls that shape how long the whole process actually takes.

Filipino doctors are not newcomers to international medical migration. Physicians trained at institutions such as the University of the Philippines College of Medicine, the University of Santo Tomas Faculty of Medicine and Surgery, and the Ateneo School of Medicine and Public Health have long pursued postgraduate careers in the Gulf, North America, and Australasia, and a growing number are now turning towards the NHS as the UKMLA replaces PLAB as the single entry exam for international medical graduates (IMGs). This guide sets out the formal requirements and pairs them with the practical detail that matters for planning a successful application from Manila, Cebu, Davao, or anywhere else in the Philippines.

The UKMLA Route From the Philippines: A Quick Overview

Before examining each stage in detail, it helps to see the whole pathway from PRC registration to NHS practice at a glance. The route for a Philippine-trained doctor generally runs as follows:

  1. Confirm eligibility: Verify that your Doctor of Medicine (MD) degree from a PRC-recognised Philippine medical school is accepted by the GMC, and that you hold a valid, unrestricted Physician licence issued by the Professional Regulation Commission following your postgraduate internship.
  2. Create a GMC Online account and begin primary source verification of your medical qualification through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials).
  3. Sit and pass an approved English language test — IELTS Academic or OET Medicine — unless you hold an accepted exemption.
  4. Book and pass the UKMLA AKT, the knowledge-based examination sat at a Pearson VUE test centre, with detail on where Filipino candidates can sit it covered in the dedicated test-centre guide.
  5. Book and pass the UKMLA CPSA, the practical clinical skills assessment, which can only be sat in person at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester.
  6. Apply for GMC registration with a licence to practise once both assessments are passed and all supporting documents are verified.
  7. Apply for NHS posts — typically clinical fellow roles, trust-grade positions, or Foundation Year 2-equivalent posts — and begin practising in the UK.

Each of these stages carries its own processing time, cost, and set of common delays, covered in the sections below. Understanding the full sequence early prevents the single most common planning mistake among Filipino candidates: assuming that passing the exams is the hard part, when in practice credential verification and document logistics often take longer than exam preparation itself. Many Filipino doctors who have already sat licensure examinations under the PRC, or who are accustomed to the relatively streamlined structure of Philippine regulatory processes, are surprised by how much of the UKMLA pathway depends on external parties responding promptly — medical school registrars, the PRC head office, and GMC caseworkers — rather than on their own study effort alone. Treating the administrative track and the exam preparation track as two separate projects, run in parallel rather than one after the other, is the single biggest determinant of how quickly a Filipino candidate reaches registration. In addition, candidates who have already worked in the Gulf or elsewhere often need to factor employer notice periods and contract-release timelines into their overall planning, since these can add several months to the practical timeline even after every GMC-side requirement has been met.

PRC Recognition and UKMLA Eligibility for Filipino Doctors

The starting point for any Filipino doctor considering the UKMLA pathway is confirming basic eligibility. The GMC requires that your primary medical qualification comes from a medical school listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools and that your degree is recognised by the relevant national regulator — in the Philippines' case, the Professional Regulation Commission (PRC), acting through its Board of Medicine. Graduates of PRC-recognised institutions such as the University of the Philippines College of Medicine, the University of Santo Tomas Faculty of Medicine and Surgery, and the Ateneo School of Medicine and Public Health generally satisfy this first eligibility hurdle without difficulty, provided the school was properly accredited at the time of graduation and appears on the World Directory of Medical Schools for that graduation year.

Beyond the medical school itself, the GMC also expects evidence that you passed the Philippine Physician Licensure Examination administered by the PRC and that you hold a valid, unrestricted Physician licence rather than a provisional or lapsed one. A Certificate of Good Standing confirming your registration status and disciplinary history is typically required as part of your application, and this must be requested directly from the PRC — a step many candidates leave too late, given that processing times for official certificates from Philippine government agencies can run into several weeks, particularly during peak licensure-exam release periods.

Full detail of who is permitted to sit the exam, including specific rules for degrees awarded outside standard timeframes or through non-standard routes, is set out in the GMC's guidance on eligibility, covered in depth in the site's general GMC registration requirements for international doctors. It is worth reading this in full before committing time and money to the process, since eligibility is assessed on a case-by-case basis for some qualification types. For a broader view of the requirements applying across every international medical graduate route, see the site's guide to UKMLA eligibility requirements.

Primary Source Verification via EPIC

Once basic eligibility is confirmed, the GMC requires primary source verification of your Philippine medical degree — meaning your qualification is confirmed directly with your medical school rather than accepted on the strength of a certificate alone. The GMC handles this through EPIC (Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials), the platform used globally for this purpose. Philippine medical schools vary considerably in how quickly they respond to EPIC verification requests; for example, well-established schools with dedicated registrar or alumni-relations offices tend to respond within four to eight weeks, whereas others can take three months or longer, particularly around graduation season or during academic holidays.

The GMC publishes full detail on how primary source verification works, what documents candidates need, and how caseworkers use the resulting evidence: see the GMC's primary source verification requirements for international medical graduates. The EPIC process itself is identical for every IMG regardless of home country, and the guide to GMC registration and EPIC verification — although written with an Indian example — walks through the same document upload sequence, verification stages, and typical caseworker queries that a Filipino applicant will encounter. Candidates are strongly advised to read both resources before initiating their EPIC application, as incomplete submissions are one of the most common causes of delay.

What Counts as PRC Recognition in Practice

It is worth being precise about what "PRC-recognised" actually means for GMC purposes, because the two regulators assess slightly different things. A PRC Physician licence confirms that you may practise medicine within the Philippine health system; however, it does not, on its own, transfer any rights or recognition in the UK. The GMC instead looks at three separate elements: whether your medical school appears on the World Directory of Medical Schools for the relevant graduation year, whether the degree programme itself met minimum duration and curriculum standards, and whether you can produce primary evidence — degree certificate, full transcripts, and internship completion documentation — that stands up to independent verification. A doctor can hold perfectly valid PRC registration and still face delays at the GMC stage if, for example, their medical school's World Directory of Medical Schools listing lapsed for a period, or if academic transcripts are incomplete. Checking your medical school's current listing status before you begin is a five-minute task that can save weeks of confusion later.

Documents Filipino Candidates Should Gather Early

Beyond the primary degree certificate itself, most Filipino applicants need a consistent set of supporting documents, and gathering them in parallel rather than sequentially saves considerable time:

  • Doctor of Medicine (MD) diploma and full academic transcript of records, from your medical school's registrar.
  • PRC Board Certificate confirming you passed the Physician Licensure Examination and hold a valid professional licence.
  • PRC Certificate of Good Standing, confirming your registration status and disciplinary record, requested directly from the PRC central office in Manila.
  • Certificate of internship completion from your training hospital, confirming you completed the mandatory post-graduate internship year.
  • Philippine passport, valid well beyond your expected registration date.
  • National Bureau of Investigation (NBI) clearance or equivalent police clearance, sometimes requested to support the good-standing evidence.
  • Evidence of any name changes — marriage certificates or court orders — where your degree name and passport name differ.

Requesting the PRC Certificate of Good Standing early is particularly important, since this document is issued by a separate national body from your medical school and follows its own, often slower, administrative timeline. Many Filipino candidates who have previously registered with regulators in the Gulf or elsewhere will already have a sense of how long PRC document requests take; even so, it is worth budgeting extra time given the volume of applications the PRC processes across all licensed professions, not just medicine.

How to Apply for the UKMLA From the Philippines: Step by Step

With eligibility confirmed, the practical application process for a Filipino doctor follows six sequential steps. Each is described below in the order it should typically be actioned.

Step 1: Create Your GMC Online Account

Registration begins with creating a GMC Online account at the GMC's official website. This account becomes your central hub for the entire process; consequently, you will use it to track your EPIC verification status, book both the AKT and CPSA, submit your English language evidence, and eventually complete your registration application. Use a personal email address you will retain long-term, not a hospital or agency email that may be deactivated after you change employer, since GMC Online correspondence can span well over a year for most Filipino candidates.

Step 2: Begin EPIC Verification Early

As covered above, EPIC verification of your MD degree is usually the single longest step in the entire pathway. Start this as early as possible — ideally before you have finalised your English test date or exam bookings — because it runs in parallel with other preparation and does not require your active involvement once submitted, beyond following up with your medical school if there are delays. Keep a written record of every communication with your medical school's registrar, as this becomes useful evidence if you need to escalate a delayed response.

Step 3: Gather and Submit English Language Evidence

In parallel with EPIC verification, sit your English language test (IELTS Academic or OET Medicine — full detail in the dedicated section below) and upload your certificate to GMC Online once results are issued. Many Filipino candidates assume that because English is a co-official language of instruction throughout Philippine medical education, this requirement can be waived; in practice, formal evidence is still required in almost all cases, so it is worth booking a test slot early rather than waiting to find out whether an exemption applies.

Step 4: Book and Prepare for the AKT

Once your GMC Online account is active, you can book the AKT through the Pearson VUE booking system linked from your account. Booking windows typically open several months ahead of each testing period; furthermore, popular test centre dates fill quickly, so book as soon as a suitable slot appears rather than waiting until the last moment. Preparation should draw on the official MLA content map, which defines exactly what conditions, presentations, and professional knowledge areas are examinable, and a structured question bank — see the guidance on choosing a good AKT question bank for a comparison of the main preparation resources available to Filipino candidates.

Step 5: Book and Prepare for the CPSA

The CPSA can only be booked once you have passed the AKT. Because the CPSA is only available in Manchester, Filipino candidates need to plan a UK trip around this stage, factoring in visa arrangements, long-haul flights, and several days of accommodation for preparation immediately before the assessment. Many candidates combine the trip with a short period of UK clinical observation or shadowing beforehand, which helps with the specific communication and consultation style the CPSA examines, particularly given the differences between UK and Philippine consultation norms around consent, shared decision-making, and structured history-taking.

Step 6: Submit Your GMC Registration Application

Once both the AKT and CPSA are passed, and your EPIC verification, English language evidence, and any outstanding documents (Certificate of Good Standing, internship completion certificate, identity documents) are all confirmed, you can submit your full application for GMC registration with a licence to practise. Full step-by-step detail on this final stage is available in the site's GMC registration process guide, which covers document checklists, processing times, and what to do if your application is queried. For a country-agnostic walkthrough of the same process, the generic GMC registration requirements for international doctors guide is a useful companion reference.

UKMLA AKT Test Centres for Filipino Candidates

One of the most practical questions Filipino candidates ask is where they can actually sit the AKT without an expensive international flight. Pearson VUE delivers the AKT through its global network of test centres; however, availability in Southeast Asia fluctuates by testing window rather than staying fixed. The most reliable way to check current options is the dedicated UKMLA AKT test centres guide, which sets out how to search live Pearson VUE availability and what to do if no centre is currently listed in the Philippines for a given sitting period; in that situation, candidates typically look to the nearest available regional centre, commonly in other parts of Southeast Asia, rather than travelling all the way to the UK for the AKT alone.

This is a significant contrast with the CPSA. Unlike the AKT, the CPSA cannot be sat anywhere in the Philippines, or indeed anywhere outside the UK — the GMC holds it exclusively at its purpose-built Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional additional London dates during periods of high demand. This means every Filipino candidate must, at minimum, make one long-haul trip to the UK to complete their UKMLA pathway, even in scenarios where the AKT is sat at a regional Pearson VUE centre without ever leaving Southeast Asia. Budgeting for this single mandatory UK trip — flights, a UK visitor visa if required, accommodation, and living costs for the preparation and exam period — should be built into financial planning from the very start of the process, not left until after the AKT is passed.

UKMLA Exam Cost for Filipino Doctors: Fees in Philippine Pesos

Cost is consistently one of the biggest practical concerns for Filipino doctors evaluating the UKMLA pathway, both because the fees themselves are substantial in absolute terms and because the peso, like most emerging-market currencies, fluctuates against the pound over time. The table below sets out the core GMC and Pearson VUE fees in pounds sterling alongside an approximate peso equivalent, calculated at a broadly indicative exchange rate of roughly £1 to ₱73, so Filipino candidates can budget realistically. For the full IMG fee breakdown across every route, covering re-sit charges and country-by-country comparisons, see the dedicated fees guide.

Fee ItemAmount (GBP)Approximate Amount (PHP)When Payable
AKT exam fee£283~₱20,700At booking
CPSA exam fee£1,036~₱75,600At booking
GMC initial registration fee£481~₱35,100On application, after both exams passed
Annual retention fee£431~₱31,500Annually in January, once registered
Total core fees (first attempt)£1,800~₱131,400Across the full pathway to registration

These peso figures are indicative and calculated at a broadly representative exchange rate; they will shift with market movements between the time this guide is written and the time you make a payment. Exchange rates between the peso and the pound move continuously, and a gap of even a few months between budgeting and paying can noticeably change the peso cost of a given fee. The GMC reviews its fee schedule periodically; therefore, always verify current rules and current fee amounts on the official GMC website before applying, and check live exchange rates at the time you actually transfer funds rather than relying on figures calculated months in advance.

Beyond these core fees, Filipino candidates should budget separately for EPIC verification charges, English language test fees, flights and accommodation for the mandatory Manchester CPSA trip, a UK visitor visa if applicable, and study materials. Together these additional costs commonly add £1,500–£3,000 on top of the core GMC fees, so a realistic total budget for a first-attempt pass across the whole pathway is closer to £3,300–£4,800 once every cost is included. Consequently, candidates who plan only around the headline GMC and Pearson VUE fees frequently underestimate their real cash requirement by a significant margin.

Because so much of this budget is denominated in pounds while income and savings are typically held in pesos, many Filipino candidates choose to build their fund gradually over several months rather than attempting a single large currency purchase close to a payment deadline. Spreading currency purchases over time, using a specialist international money transfer provider rather than a standard bank counter rate, and tracking the mid-market rate rather than relying on a single remittance-centre quotation are all practical ways to reduce the effective cost of converting pesos to pounds across the pathway. Some candidates also time larger purchases around periods of relative peso stability rather than during periods of sharp depreciation, although this requires ongoing attention to the foreign exchange market rather than a one-off decision.

English Language Requirements for Filipino Doctors

English is a primary medium of instruction throughout Philippine medical education, which leads many Filipino doctors to assume that formal English testing will not apply to them. In practice, the GMC requires documentary evidence of English language proficiency from the great majority of international medical graduates regardless of the language of their medical training, and Philippine degrees are not automatically exempt. Candidates should plan for formal testing as a standard part of the pathway rather than hoping for a waiver.

The GMC accepts two tests: IELTS Academic, which requires an overall score of 7.0, with no individual component (Listening, Reading, Writing, Speaking) below 7.0, and OET (Occupational English Test), Medicine version, which requires a minimum of Grade B in all four sub-tests. Many candidates find OET more intuitive for practising doctors because its scenarios are built entirely around healthcare communication — referral letters, doctor-patient consultations, and clinical case notes — rather than general academic topics. IELTS Academic, by contrast, draws on a broader range of subject matter, although both tests have well-established preparation centres across Metro Manila, Cebu, and other major Philippine cities.

Both test certificates have a defined validity period from the date of the test, so timing matters: sitting the test too early relative to your overall pathway risks the certificate expiring before you submit your registration application, which would require a costly and time-consuming re-sit. Full detail on accepted scores, accepted test providers, and how evidence is submitted is available directly from the regulator at the GMC's English language evidence requirements. It is worth reading this page in full, since the specific evidentiary requirements are updated from time to time and always take precedence over general summaries.

Life and Career for Filipino Doctors in the NHS After Registration

Once a doctor secures GMC registration, they join a rapidly growing Filipino community within the NHS. The Philippines has long been one of the largest source countries for internationally trained nurses working in the UK, and this existing nursing diaspora — spread across nearly every NHS trust — means that new Filipino doctors often arrive to find compatriot colleagues, community networks, and church-based support groups already well established, even where the specifically medical diaspora is still relatively young by comparison with countries such as Nigeria or India. This existing network provides genuinely useful practical support during the settling-in period, from housing advice to informal orientation on NHS culture.

Career progression after registration generally follows one of several routes: a trust-grade or clinical fellow post (the most common entry point for newly registered IMGs, offering FY2-equivalent clinical responsibility without requiring a Foundation Programme place), a Specialty Training application once sufficient UK experience and portfolio evidence are in place, or locum work through NHS staff banks and agencies for those who want flexibility while building their UK clinical track record. Many Filipino doctors who enter through clinical fellow posts subsequently progress into core or specialty training within two to three years, particularly in specialties experiencing significant workforce shortages such as general internal medicine, geriatric medicine, and emergency medicine, where competition ratios for IMG applicants tend to be more favourable.

It is worth noting that the broad shape of this journey — degree recognition, credential verification, English testing, a knowledge exam, a clinical skills exam, then registration — mirrors the equivalent route followed by doctors from other major IMG source countries. The route for doctors coming from India, the largest single IMG source country for the UK, follows the same overall structure despite differing verification bodies and processing times, as does the well-established pathway taken by doctors from Nigeria. Furthermore, candidates who have already researched routes from other, smaller-volume countries may find it useful to compare notes against the site's round-up guide to UKMLA routes from other countries, which sets out how the pathway varies for IMGs from a range of source nations beyond the largest few.

Day-to-day life for newly registered Filipino doctors in the NHS typically involves an adjustment period covering both clinical and cultural dimensions. Clinically, the biggest early adjustments tend to be around NHS documentation standards (structured handover formats such as SBAR), a strong institutional emphasis on multidisciplinary team working, and a more codified approach to consent and capacity than many candidates are accustomed to from Philippine hospital practice. Socially, Filipino doctors moving to the UK benefit from one of the largest existing Filipino professional communities in the country, thanks to decades of Filipino nurses, carers, and allied health professionals already working across the NHS and social care sector. Salary progression follows the standard NHS pay scale for the relevant grade, and while it can take time to reach specialty training, most Filipino doctors who remain committed to the pathway report that the structured nature of UK training — clear competency frameworks, protected training time, and defined progression points — compares favourably with less standardised systems elsewhere, including some of the private hospital settings common in the Philippines and the Gulf.

Timeline: From Graduation in the Philippines to NHS Registration

Filipino candidates consistently underestimate how long the full pathway takes, largely because exam preparation time is easy to plan for while administrative processing time is not. The table below sets out a realistic stage-by-stage timeline based on typical current processing periods.

StageTypical DurationKey Variable
PRC Certificate of Good Standing request2 to 8 weeksPRC processing backlog
EPIC primary source verification4 weeks to 4 monthsSpeed of your medical school's response
English language test preparation and sitting1 to 4 monthsBaseline English proficiency
AKT preparation3 to 6 monthsStudy intensity, prior NHS knowledge
AKT booking to sitting4 to 12 weeksRegional test centre date availability
CPSA preparation, including UK travel planning2 to 4 monthsVisa processing, budget readiness
CPSA booking to sitting (Manchester)2 to 6 monthsLimited sitting capacity
GMC registration application processing4 to 8 weeksDocument completeness
NHS job search and start date1 to 3 monthsNHS recruitment cycles, specialty demand

Adding these stages together, most Filipino candidates should plan for a realistic total timeline of 18 to 30 months from beginning EPIC verification to starting their first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. Candidates who fail either assessment on their first attempt, or who face delays at the PRC or EPIC stage, should expect this to extend further. Planning around the longer end of this range from the outset avoids the financial and psychological strain of a timeline that assumes everything goes smoothly.

Common Mistakes Filipino Doctors Make on This Route

Several avoidable errors recur consistently among Filipino candidates pursuing the UKMLA pathway. Most of these mistakes are not clinical — they are planning and administrative errors that have nothing to do with medical knowledge, yet they are responsible for a disproportionate share of the delays and extra costs Filipino candidates experience. Being aware of them in advance, before committing money to a specific exam date or travel booking, can save months of delay and hundreds of pounds in wasted fees.

  • Starting EPIC verification too late. Because EPIC processing time depends heavily on your medical school's administrative speed rather than your own effort, this step should be initiated before — not after — you begin AKT preparation. Candidates who wait until they are ready to book the AKT before starting EPIC often lose two to three months they could have used productively.
  • Underestimating the true cost of the Manchester CPSA trip. The £1,036 exam fee itself is only part of the cost. Long-haul flights from Manila or Cebu, several nights of Manchester accommodation, local transport, food, and — for many candidates — a short UK preparation course before the exam, can together add £1,200–£2,200 on top of the fee. Candidates who budget only for the headline fee frequently find themselves scrambling for additional funds close to their exam date.
  • Not accounting for peso exchange rate movement. Because all UKMLA and GMC fees are charged in pounds sterling, a Filipino candidate who calculates their peso budget many months before actually paying can find the real cost has risen by the time payment is due. Build a buffer of at least 15–20% above your initial peso budget calculation, and monitor exchange rates in the weeks leading up to each payment rather than assuming an old estimate still holds.
  • Letting English test certificates expire. Both IELTS and OET certificates are valid for a defined period from the test date. Candidates who sit their English test early in the process — before EPIC verification or AKT preparation is complete — sometimes find their certificate has expired by the time they are ready to submit their full registration application, forcing a costly re-sit. Sequence your English test so its validity period comfortably covers your expected registration application date.
  • Assuming employment abroad automatically satisfies GMC requirements. Many Filipino doctors have already worked in the Gulf, Ireland, or elsewhere before considering the UK route. However, prior overseas employment does not substitute for GMC-specific requirements — EPIC verification, the AKT, and the CPSA must still be completed regardless of how much international clinical experience a candidate already holds.
  • Compressing AKT and CPSA preparation into the final weeks. Both assessments draw on a specific, defined content base, and candidates who begin serious preparation only a few weeks before their booked date typically underperform relative to their actual clinical knowledge, particularly on CPSA communication stations that reward familiarity with UK consultation structure rather than raw clinical knowledge alone.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can Filipino doctors sit the UKMLA AKT without travelling to the UK?

In many testing windows, yes. The AKT is delivered through the global Pearson VUE network, and candidates should check current centre listings for the Philippines or the wider Southeast Asia region through the UKMLA AKT test centres guide, since availability changes between sitting periods. When no local centre is open, the nearest available regional Pearson VUE centre is generally a shorter, cheaper trip than travelling to the UK solely for the AKT.

Do I have to travel to the UK for the CPSA?

Yes, this step cannot be avoided. The CPSA is held exclusively at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, with occasional additional London dates during periods of high demand. There is no option to sit the CPSA remotely or anywhere within the Philippines, so every Filipino candidate must budget for at least one trip to the UK to complete their UKMLA pathway.

Is a Philippine MD degree automatically accepted by the GMC?

Degrees from PRC-recognised Philippine medical schools generally satisfy the GMC's basic eligibility requirement, provided the school was properly accredited at the time you graduated and your qualification is listed on the World Directory of Medical Schools. However, acceptance also depends on completing primary source verification through EPIC and holding a valid, unrestricted PRC Physician licence. Always confirm your specific situation against the current GMC registration requirements for international doctors rather than assuming eligibility automatically.

How much does the whole UKMLA process cost in Philippine pesos?

The core GMC and exam fees — AKT, CPSA, and initial registration — total approximately £1,800, which converts to roughly ₱131,000 at broadly representative exchange rates, though this figure moves with the market. Once English testing, EPIC verification, Manchester travel and accommodation, and study materials are included, a realistic total budget is closer to £3,300–£4,800. Because the peso fluctuates against the pound over time, candidates should recheck current rates and current GMC fee levels close to the time of each payment rather than relying on an estimate made months in advance.

Do Filipino doctors need to take an English language test if their degree was taught in English?

In almost all cases, yes. Philippine medical education being conducted largely in English does not automatically exempt a candidate from the GMC's formal English language evidence requirement. Most Filipino candidates need to submit a valid IELTS Academic score (7.0 overall, no component below 7.0) or OET Medicine result (Grade B minimum in all four components) as part of their application.

What is the realistic timeline from graduating in the Philippines to working in the NHS?

Most candidates should plan for 18 to 30 months from starting EPIC verification to beginning their first NHS post, assuming first-attempt passes at both the AKT and CPSA. This accounts for PRC document processing, EPIC verification delays, English test preparation, exam preparation for both assessments, and the GMC registration application itself. Candidates who need to re-sit either exam, or who face slower-than-average verification, should expect this timeline to extend.

How does the Filipino route compare to routes from other major IMG source countries?

The overall structure — degree recognition, primary source verification, English testing, a knowledge exam, a clinical skills exam in the UK, then registration — is broadly the same across major IMG source countries, including the equivalent route for doctors coming from India and the established route for doctors coming from Nigeria. What differs between countries is the specific verification body, typical processing times, local AKT test centre availability, and the size and structure of the existing diaspora network available to support new arrivals. For a full breakdown of how fees and requirements compare across every IMG route, see the guide to the full IMG fee breakdown across every route, and for routes from smaller-volume source countries, see the round-up guide covering other UKMLA country routes.

Where can Filipino candidates find good AKT preparation resources?

The official MLA content map, published by the GMC, defines the exact clinical presentations and professional knowledge areas the AKT examines and should form the backbone of any study plan. In addition, a structured question bank helps candidates practise under timed, exam-style conditions; the site's guide to choosing a good AKT question bank compares the main evaluation criteria candidates should use when selecting preparation resources, without endorsing any single paid product.

Conclusion

The UKMLA for Filipino doctors is a clearly defined, increasingly well-travelled pathway, and it rewards candidates who plan early around credential verification, budget realistically in pesos, and sequence their English test carefully against their overall timeline. Candidates who start EPIC verification promptly, prepare systematically using the official content map, and plan properly for the mandatory Manchester trip consistently move through the pathway faster and with fewer costly setbacks. Doctors trained at the University of the Philippines College of Medicine, the University of Santo Tomas, the Ateneo School of Medicine and Public Health, or any other PRC-recognised institution can approach the UKMLA with confidence provided they treat the administrative and exam-preparation tracks as two parallel projects rather than one sequential one. Check the full GMC registration process →

post_title: UKMLA for International Medical Graduates: Country-by-Country Quick Reference
post_excerpt: A quick-reference guide to the UKMLA route for IMGs from Ghana, Nepal, Malaysia, Ethiopia, Sudan, Zimbabwe, Oman, the Caribbean and Southeast Asia more broadly.
seo_title: UKMLA for International Medical Graduates
seo_description: UKMLA for international medical graduates from Ghana, Nepal, Malaysia, Ethiopia, Sudan, Zimbabwe, Oman and the Caribbean: eligibility and fees.
html_content:
The UKMLA for international medical graduates is exactly the same exam and the same registration pathway no matter which country a candidate trained in, yet most of the site's deep-dive guides understandably focus on the largest single source countries — India, Nigeria, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Egypt, the Gulf states, Sri Lanka, South Africa, and the Philippines. This guide exists for everyone else. It is a quick-reference companion for doctors trained in Ghana, Nepal, Malaysia, Ethiopia, Sudan, Zimbabwe, Oman, the wider Caribbean, and Southeast Asia more generally — countries and regions that each send smaller but genuinely real numbers of doctors into the NHS every year, and whose graduates deserve the same clear, honest signposting as candidates from higher-volume countries.

Because the underlying process is identical regardless of home country, this post does not repeat the full step-by-step registration explanation for each nation covered. Instead, it recaps the shared GMC/UKMLA process once, briefly, and then gives each country or region its own short, focused section covering the national medical regulator, any well-known accredited medical schools worth mentioning as illustrative examples, World Directory of Medical Schools (WDOMS) considerations, and the currency you will likely see referenced in fee planning. Readers who want the complete, exhaustive walkthrough of eligibility, verification, and registration should follow the links to the site's dedicated generic guides throughout, since this post deliberately trades depth-per-country for breadth-of-coverage.

Throughout this guide, the emphasis is on honesty about what can be stated confidently and what cannot. Regulator names, for example, can generally be stated with confidence, since they are matters of public record. Exact test-centre cities, precise fee figures in local currency, and the current accreditation status of any specific medical school, however, all change over time, and this guide directs readers to the GMC, EPIC, and Pearson VUE for that live detail rather than guessing at specifics that could quickly become outdated.

Quick Answer: The UKMLA Process Is the Same Wherever You Trained

Every international medical graduate, regardless of home country, follows fundamentally the same route to a GMC licence: confirm your primary medical qualification is listed correctly with the World Directory of Medical Schools, complete Electronic Portfolio of International Credentials (EPIC) primary source verification, meet the GMC's English language requirement through IELTS or OET, pass the Applied Knowledge Test (AKT), pass the Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA), and then apply for full registration with a licence to practise. Consequently, a doctor trained in Accra, Kathmandu, or Muscat follows exactly the same sequence as a doctor trained in Mumbai or Lagos — the destination process does not change, only the starting paperwork and local logistics differ slightly by country.

This post therefore functions as a country-by-country quick reference rather than a full step-by-step manual. For candidates who want the complete, detailed walkthrough of every stage of the pathway, the site's dedicated guide on how to become a doctor in the UK through GMC registration covers the entire journey in depth, and the companion post on GMC registration requirements for international doctors goes further still into the specific documentary and verification requirements every IMG must satisfy. If your home country is India, Nigeria, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Egypt, a Gulf state, Sri Lanka, South Africa, or the Philippines, this site already has a dedicated, considerably deeper guide written specifically for your route, and you should read that instead of this reference post.

In addition, it is worth stating plainly why this particular group of countries has been bundled together rather than split into individual posts covering the UKMLA for international medical graduates from each single nation. Ghana, Nepal, Malaysia, Ethiopia, Sudan, Zimbabwe, Oman, the Caribbean, and Southeast Asia each generate real, ongoing search interest from prospective candidates, but none generates the sustained volume that would justify a full 5,500-word dedicated treatment on its own. Grouping them together, rather than ignoring them, means every one of these countries still gets a genuinely useful answer rather than silence.

The Shared GMC/UKMLA Process, Briefly Recapped

Before looking at individual countries, it helps to recap the shared skeleton every IMG candidate moves through, since the country-specific sections below assume this background rather than repeating it. The process begins with confirming that your medical school and qualification appear correctly in the World Directory of Medical Schools, since the GMC uses WDOMS as its reference point for recognising a primary medical qualification from outside the UK. Candidates then register for EPIC, the verification service that confirms directly with your medical school and licensing authority that your degree and good standing are genuine, rather than relying on documents you submit yourself.

Once identity and qualification checks are underway, candidates must also satisfy the GMC's English language requirement, typically through an acceptable IELTS or OET score, unless they qualify for an exemption. From there, the two UKMLA assessments follow: the AKT, a computer-based knowledge test delivered globally through Pearson VUE, and the CPSA, a face-to-face clinical and communication skills assessment held only at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester. Passing both, together with satisfying the "good standing" and identity requirements, allows a candidate to apply for full registration with a licence to practise. For the exact, current requirements around primary source verification, the GMC's own guidance page on primary source verification for international medical graduates remains the single most authoritative reference, and it is worth reading directly rather than relying solely on any third-party summary, including this one.

Because AKT test centre availability varies meaningfully by country and changes between sitting windows, candidates from every country covered in this guide should also consult the dedicated UKMLA AKT test centre guide before assuming a particular city has an open slot. In addition, since the full cost of the pathway adds up across EPIC verification, English testing, the AKT, the CPSA, travel, and registration fees, candidates budgeting in Ghanaian Cedi, Nepalese Rupee, Malaysian Ringgit, or any other currency should read the comprehensive UKMLA fees for IMGs guide, which breaks down every fee across the entire route regardless of home country.

Once both assessments are passed, the remaining steps toward full registration are largely administrative rather than exam-related. The dedicated GMC registration guide pillar page walks through exactly what happens after the CPSA, including document submission and typical processing times, and is worth reading well before you reach that stage so nothing about the final administrative steps comes as a surprise.

Why the Starting Paperwork Differs More Than the Exam Itself

Although the AKT and CPSA are identical for every candidate, the paperwork stage that precedes them is where country-specific differences actually show up, and this is precisely why a country-by-country reference is useful even though the exam itself is uniform. WDOMS listings, the exact documents a national regulator issues for "good standing," and how quickly EPIC can verify a given country's records all vary, sometimes considerably, from one country to the next. A candidate from a country with a long-established, digitised regulatory system may find EPIC verification proceeds relatively smoothly, whereas a candidate from a country whose regulatory infrastructure has faced recent disruption may need to budget significantly more time for the same stage.

Therefore, the single most valuable thing a candidate from any of the countries covered in this guide can do early on is contact their own national regulator directly to confirm exactly what a "certificate of good standing" or equivalent document looks like in their system, how long it typically takes to obtain, and whether it needs to be sent directly to EPIC or can be issued to the candidate first. This single question, asked early, prevents the most common source of delay reported by IMG candidates from smaller-volume countries: discovering, months into the process, that the certificate they assumed would take two weeks actually takes two months.

UKMLA for International Medical Graduates: Country-by-Country Quick Reference

The sections below cover each country or region in turn. Each one names the relevant national medical regulator, mentions well-known accredited medical schools purely as illustrative examples rather than an exhaustive list, and notes the typical local currency referenced in fee planning. However, candidates should treat every specific detail below as a starting point for their own research rather than a final answer, and should always confirm current requirements directly with the GMC, EPIC, and their own national regulator before making any firm plans.

Ghana

Medical education in Ghana is regulated by the Medical and Dental Council of Ghana, which maintains the register of qualified doctors and oversees standards at the country's medical schools. Well-known Ghanaian medical schools that IMG candidates sometimes reference as examples include the University of Ghana Medical School and the Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology School of Medicine and Dentistry, though candidates should always confirm their own specific institution's current WDOMS listing rather than assuming any particular school is included, since accreditation status can change over time.

Ghanaian doctors pursuing the UKMLA pathway should verify their qualification against the World Directory of Medical Schools early, since EPIC verification depends on this listing being accurate and current. Furthermore, when budgeting for the pathway, Ghanaian candidates will typically see UK fees quoted in pounds sterling and will need to convert these into the Ghanaian Cedi for personal financial planning; given that exchange rates fluctuate, it is sensible to check a current conversion shortly before each payment rather than relying on a figure calculated months earlier. Ghana also has an established, if smaller, community of NHS doctors already, and candidates researching this route can reasonably expect to find compatriots further along the same pathway willing to share practical, if informal, experience.

Nepal

The Nepal Medical Council is the regulatory body responsible for registering doctors and overseeing medical education standards in Nepal. Institutions frequently cited as examples of accredited Nepali medical schools include the Institute of Medicine at Tribhuvan University and the B.P. Koirala Institute of Health Sciences, although, as with every country in this guide, candidates must verify their own specific school's current WDOMS status directly rather than assuming inclusion based on general reputation.

Nepali candidates should also confirm their good standing certificate and verification documents meet the Nepal Medical Council's own export requirements before beginning EPIC verification, since processing times for these certificates can vary and may affect the overall UKMLA timeline. In terms of fee planning, Nepali doctors will most commonly reference the Nepalese Rupee when converting UK-denominated costs, and given the meaningful exchange-rate gap between the Nepalese Rupee and pounds sterling, building a realistic sterling-equivalent budget early in the process is particularly important for this route. Because Nepal's total volume of UKMLA candidates is smaller than that of the larger South Asian source countries, Nepali candidates may also find it useful to read the wider South Asian route context covered in the guide to how to become a doctor in the UK from India, purely as a comparison of how a considerably larger neighbouring route operates, even though the specific regulator and documentation differ.

Malaysia

Malaysia's medical profession is regulated by the Malaysian Medical Council, which maintains professional registers and oversees the country's medical schools. Examples of well-known Malaysian institutions include Universiti Malaya's Faculty of Medicine and the International Medical University, though, in line with the same caveat that applies throughout this guide, candidates should verify their exact medical school's current WDOMS listing rather than relying on institutional reputation alone.

Malaysia also functions as a useful reference point for the wider Southeast Asian region covered later in this guide, since it is the Southeast Asian country most commonly represented among UKMLA candidates from the region and has a comparatively well-established path through EPIC verification. Malaysian candidates typically reference the Malaysian Ringgit when budgeting for UK-denominated fees, and given Malaysia's broadly stable exchange rate relative to some other countries covered in this guide, currency planning tends to be somewhat more predictable here than for candidates converting from currencies with greater volatility. Malaysian candidates should nonetheless still confirm current AKT test centre availability directly rather than assuming a centre remains open indefinitely, since Pearson VUE capacity anywhere in the world is subject to change between sitting windows.

Ethiopia

Ethiopian medical regulation sits with the Ethiopian Food, Medicine and Healthcare Administration and Control Authority, alongside the relevant professional medical council responsible for registering practitioners domestically; candidates should confirm precisely which body issues their good standing certificate, since regulatory structures in this area have evolved over recent years. Addis Ababa University's School of Medicine is frequently cited as a well-known example of an Ethiopian medical school, although, as always, the specific WDOMS status of any given institution must be checked directly rather than assumed.

Because Ethiopia's regulatory landscape has seen structural changes, Ethiopian candidates should build extra time into their planning for confirming exactly which authority handles verification requests and good standing certificates before submitting EPIC applications. When it comes to fee planning, Ethiopian doctors will generally convert UK costs into the Ethiopian Birr, and given periodic exchange-rate movements, checking a current conversion close to each payment date is a sensible habit rather than an optional extra. Ethiopian candidates should also treat early, direct contact with EPIC as a priority, since confirming exactly which office to approach for verification documents can otherwise consume weeks of otherwise avoidable delay.

Sudan

The Sudan Medical Council is the national body responsible for registering doctors and overseeing medical education standards in Sudan. Well-known Sudanese medical schools sometimes referenced as examples include the University of Khartoum Faculty of Medicine, though, in keeping with the honesty this guide aims for throughout, candidates should verify their own institution's current WDOMS listing directly, particularly given how significantly ongoing instability in Sudan has affected administrative and educational infrastructure in recent years.

Given the disruption Sudan has experienced, Sudanese candidates pursuing the UKMLA pathway should expect that obtaining good standing certificates, transcripts, or verification documents may take considerably longer or require alternative arrangements compared with candidates from countries with stable regulatory infrastructure, and should contact the GMC and EPIC directly to discuss their specific circumstances rather than assuming a standard timeline applies. For fee planning, the Sudanese Pound is the relevant currency for local budgeting, though candidates should treat any specific exchange-rate figure as provisional given the currency's documented volatility, and should always check a current rate immediately before converting or transferring funds. Sudanese doctors already form part of the NHS workforce, and this existing community is itself evidence that the pathway, while it may take longer to navigate in current circumstances, remains genuinely achievable.

Zimbabwe

Zimbabwean doctors are regulated by the Medical and Dental Practitioners Council of Zimbabwe, which registers practitioners and sets standards for medical education within the country. The University of Zimbabwe College of Health Sciences is commonly cited as a well-known example of an accredited Zimbabwean medical school, although candidates should, as always, verify their own school's current WDOMS status directly rather than relying on general reputation.

Zimbabwe deserves a particularly strong currency caveat in this guide. The country has experienced well-documented and severe currency instability over the past two decades, including periods of hyperinflation and multiple changes to the official currency itself. Consequently, this guide deliberately avoids naming any specific Zimbabwean currency figure or exchange rate as reliable, and Zimbabwean candidates should treat currency conversion as something to check immediately before every single payment, using an official, current source, rather than assuming any rate remains valid even for a few weeks. In addition, given the practical challenges this instability can create around international bank transfers, Zimbabwean candidates should budget extra time and contingency funds for the payment stages of the UKMLA pathway, and should confirm with their bank in advance exactly how an international transfer to a UK-based fee collector will be processed, since this single step has reportedly caused avoidable delay for some candidates from currency-unstable economies.

Oman

Medical regulation in Oman involves both the Oman Medical Specialty Board and the Omani Ministry of Health, depending on the specific stage and type of registration or verification required; candidates should confirm with the relevant body exactly which certificates and verifications they need for GMC purposes, since responsibilities can be split between institutions. Sultan Qaboos University's College of Medicine and Health Sciences is a well-known example of an Omani medical school, though its WDOMS status, like every institution mentioned in this guide, should be verified directly rather than assumed.

Because Oman sits within the wider Gulf region, candidates researching this route may also find it useful to read the site's dedicated guide on the UKMLA pathway for Gulf-region doctors, which covers shared regional considerations relevant to Omani candidates alongside those from neighbouring Gulf states, even though Oman's own regulatory specifics differ in detail. For fee planning, Omani doctors will generally reference the Omani Rial, a currency that trades at a notably different scale to pounds sterling, so candidates should double-check decimal places carefully when converting figures to avoid costly arithmetic errors. Given Oman's geographic and professional links with the wider Gulf healthcare sector, Omani candidates already working alongside doctors from neighbouring Gulf states may also find informal peer guidance more readily available than candidates from some of the other countries in this guide.

The Caribbean

Medical regulation across the Caribbean varies meaningfully from island nation to island nation, and this guide deliberately avoids naming a single regulator, since no single body covers the entire region. Many Caribbean nations operate within the broader context of the Caribbean Community (CARICOM), which supports regional cooperation on professional standards in various fields, but candidates should confirm the specific national medical council or regulatory body relevant to the exact country where they trained, rather than assuming a single pan-Caribbean regulator applies.

Well-known medical schools across the Caribbean include institutions in Jamaica, Trinidad and Tobago, and several other islands, though given the genuine diversity of the region, this guide intentionally avoids naming specific schools as a representative list, since doing so risks implying an endorsement or completeness this section cannot honestly provide. Currency likewise varies considerably by island nation — from the Jamaican Dollar to the Trinidad and Tobago Dollar and several others — so Caribbean candidates should identify and confirm the correct currency for their own specific country before budgeting, rather than assuming any single Caribbean currency applies regionally. Historically, Caribbean-trained doctors have contributed to the NHS for decades, in some cases across multiple generations of the same family, and this long-standing connection is itself a reassuring signal for newer candidates that the pathway, though it requires careful country-specific verification, is well-trodden.

Southeast Asia More Broadly

Beyond Malaysia, which is covered in its own section above as the region's most commonly represented country among UKMLA candidates, several other Southeast Asian countries — including Indonesia, Vietnam, and Thailand — also send doctors through the UKMLA pathway, albeit in smaller numbers. Regulatory bodies, medical school accreditation status, and currencies differ meaningfully between these countries, so this guide intentionally avoids generalising across them and instead recommends that candidates from Indonesia, Vietnam, Thailand, or any other Southeast Asian country not covered by its own dedicated post on this site treat the Malaysia section above as a useful reference point for the shared regional process, while confirming their own country's specific regulator, medical school WDOMS status, and currency directly.

Given how much detail varies country to country within Southeast Asia, candidates from this wider region should place particular emphasis on early, direct verification with the GMC and EPIC, rather than assuming a neighbouring country's experience will map cleanly onto their own. This is, admittedly, a lighter-touch treatment than the dedicated country sections elsewhere in this guide, and that is a deliberate choice: the honest answer for many Southeast Asian countries beyond Malaysia is that specifics vary enough that a confident, detailed claim here would risk being wrong for at least some readers. Consequently, candidates from Indonesia, Vietnam, Thailand, and neighbouring countries should treat their national regulator's own published guidance, together with direct correspondence with EPIC, as the primary source of truth for their specific situation, using this guide only as an orientation point.

Planning Your Timeline Across Any of These Routes

Regardless of which country listed above applies to you, the practical planning sequence for the UKMLA for international medical graduates is broadly consistent, and it helps to think of it as four overlapping phases rather than a single long queue. The first phase covers confirming WDOMS status and initiating EPIC verification, which should begin as early as possible given that this stage is where country-specific delays are most likely to appear. The second phase covers English language testing, which candidates can often complete in parallel with EPIC verification rather than waiting for it to finish first. The third phase covers booking and sitting the AKT, ideally at a Pearson VUE centre reasonably local to the candidate, and the fourth phase covers the mandatory trip to Manchester for the CPSA, which every candidate worldwide must complete regardless of nationality.

Because the CPSA cannot be booked until the AKT has been passed, a delay at any earlier stage has a compounding effect on the whole timeline, pushing the final registration date back by more than the length of the original delay. Therefore, candidates from any of the countries covered in this guide benefit from starting the EPIC and English-language stages considerably earlier than they might initially expect, rather than treating those steps as quick formalities to be completed shortly before the exams themselves. In addition, candidates should periodically revisit the site's UKMLA AKT test centre guide as their own target sitting period approaches, since test-centre booking windows open and close on a schedule that rewards early, active checking rather than a single check made many months in advance.

Consolidated Summary Table

The table below draws together the regulator, typical test-centre note, and general planning note for each country or region covered in this guide. As with every specific detail in this post, treat the test-centre column as a starting point rather than a guarantee — Pearson VUE availability changes between sitting windows, and the only reliable way to confirm a current slot is through GMC Online or the Pearson VUE booking portal directly.

Country / RegionNational Medical RegulatorTypical AKT Test Centre AvailabilityNotes
GhanaMedical and Dental Council of GhanaCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsConfirm WDOMS status of your specific medical school; budget in Ghanaian Cedi
NepalNepal Medical CouncilCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsAllow extra time for good standing certificate export; budget in Nepalese Rupee
MalaysiaMalaysian Medical CouncilCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsMost established Southeast Asian route; budget in Malaysian Ringgit
EthiopiaEthiopian Food, Medicine and Healthcare Administration and Control Authority / relevant professional councilCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsConfirm exact issuing authority for verification documents; budget in Ethiopian Birr
SudanSudan Medical CouncilCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsExpect possible delays given recent instability; budget in Sudanese Pound with heavy currency-rate caution
ZimbabweMedical and Dental Practitioners Council of ZimbabweCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsCurrency has experienced severe historical instability; verify exchange rate immediately before every payment
OmanOman Medical Specialty Board / Ministry of Health OmanCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsGulf-adjacent route; budget in Omani Rial, checking decimal places carefully
Caribbean (varies by island)Varies by national regulator; CARICOM provides regional context onlyCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsConfirm the specific regulator and currency for your own island nation
Southeast Asia (beyond Malaysia)Varies by countryCheck live GMC Online / Pearson VUE listingsTreat Malaysia section as a general reference; confirm your own country's specifics directly

Notice that the test-centre column repeats the same instruction for every single row. This is deliberate rather than lazy, since it reflects a genuine fact about how Pearson VUE manages its global network: capacity is allocated dynamically in response to demand across many professional examinations sharing the same infrastructure, not fixed permanently by country, so no static guide can promise a specific city remains open indefinitely.

Why This Batch of Countries Deserves Its Own Reference Guide

None of the countries covered in this guide individually generates the search volume of India, Nigeria, or Pakistan, and that is precisely why the UKMLA for international medical graduates from each of these nations lacks its own dedicated 5,000-word deep-dive post on this site. However, individually smaller does not mean collectively insignificant, and the NHS workforce includes real, established diaspora communities of doctors trained in every country covered above. Ghanaian, Nepali, Malaysian, Ethiopian, Sudanese, Zimbabwean, and Omani doctors all appear within the UK medical workforce in meaningful numbers, and Caribbean-trained and Southeast Asian-trained doctors have contributed to the NHS for decades, in some cases going back generations.

Therefore, a candidate from Kathmandu or Accra deserves the same clarity and quality of guidance as a candidate from Mumbai or Lagos, even though the total search audience for "UKMLA for Nepali doctors" alone would not justify a full standalone post at the same depth as the site's flagship country guides. Consolidating these countries into one genuinely useful reference, rather than ignoring them altogether or producing several separate thin posts, better serves both the readers researching these routes and the site's overall coverage of genuine IMG search demand. In addition, grouping these countries together allows for direct, honest acknowledgement of where certainty is high, such as the shared GMC process and the identity of most national regulators, and where it is necessarily lower, such as specific currency stability, exact test-centre cities, or precise regulatory structures in countries undergoing institutional change.

Readers whose home country sits closer to the site's larger dedicated guides may also find it useful to compare routes. For example, the established UKMLA pathway for Nigerian doctors illustrates how a larger African diaspora route has developed over time, while the guide to becoming a doctor in the UK from India shows how the single largest source-country route operates at scale. Similarly, candidates researching Southeast Asian or Philippine options specifically should read the dedicated UKMLA pathway for Filipino doctors, since the Philippines has its own full guide separate from the lighter regional treatment given here. Comparing a smaller-volume route against a larger, more thoroughly documented one is often reassuring in itself, since it demonstrates that the underlying UKMLA process scales identically regardless of how many candidates from a given country attempt it in any given year.

Finally, this guide is also a statement of intent: as search demand for any individual country covered here grows, or as enough country-specific detail becomes reliably verifiable to support a full dedicated treatment, that country may eventually earn its own standalone guide in the same depth as the site's existing India, Nigeria, and Gulf posts. Until then, this consolidated reference aims to give every reader from these countries a genuinely useful, honestly hedged starting point rather than no guidance at all.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is a Ghanaian medical degree recognised by the GMC?

A Ghanaian medical degree can be recognised for GMC registration purposes provided the specific medical school is correctly listed in the World Directory of Medical Schools and the qualification successfully passes EPIC primary source verification. Candidates should confirm their own institution's current WDOMS status directly rather than assuming recognition based on general reputation, since listings can change over time. Once verification is complete, Ghanaian graduates follow the same AKT and CPSA pathway as any other international medical graduate.

Can Nepali doctors take the UKMLA?

Yes, Nepali doctors are fully eligible to pursue the UKMLA pathway, provided their medical qualification is recognised through the World Directory of Medical Schools and successfully verified through EPIC. Nepali candidates should confirm their good standing certificate meets the Nepal Medical Council's export requirements early, since certificate processing times can vary and may affect the overall timeline. From there, the AKT and CPSA process is identical to that followed by candidates from any other country.

Does the GMC accept medical degrees from Malaysia?

The GMC can accept Malaysian medical degrees for registration purposes provided the relevant medical school appears correctly in the World Directory of Medical Schools and the qualification is verified through EPIC. Malaysia is among the more established Southeast Asian routes into the UKMLA pathway, and candidates should still confirm their own specific institution's current WDOMS listing directly before assuming recognition. The remaining steps, including the English language requirement, AKT, and CPSA, apply identically to Malaysian candidates as to any other IMG.

What currency should Zimbabwean candidates use when budgeting for UKMLA fees?

Given Zimbabwe's well-documented history of currency instability, this guide deliberately avoids naming a specific reliable exchange rate for Zimbabwean candidates. Instead, candidates should check the current official exchange rate immediately before each payment rather than budgeting from a figure calculated weeks or months in advance. Building in a financial contingency and allowing extra time for international transfers is a sensible precaution given the currency's documented volatility.

Do doctors from Sudan face any additional challenges with UKMLA verification?

Sudanese candidates may experience longer processing times for good standing certificates, transcripts, and other verification documents than candidates from countries with more stable administrative infrastructure, given the disruption Sudan has experienced in recent years. Consequently, Sudanese doctors should contact the GMC and EPIC directly and early to discuss their specific circumstances rather than assuming a standard processing timeline will apply. Beyond this practical consideration, the underlying AKT and CPSA pathway remains identical to that followed by any other international medical graduate.

Is there a UKMLA test centre in Oman?

Test centre availability in Oman, as in every country covered in this guide, changes between sitting windows, so candidates should check the live, current list through their GMC Online account or the Pearson VUE booking portal rather than relying on any static list, including this guide's. The dedicated UKMLA AKT test centre guide explains how the booking process works in more general detail. Omani candidates researching wider regional options may also find the site's guide on the UKMLA pathway for Gulf-region doctors a useful cross-reference.

Which countries does this guide not cover in full depth?

This guide intentionally covers Ghana, Nepal, Malaysia, Ethiopia, Sudan, Zimbabwe, Oman, the Caribbean, and Southeast Asia more broadly at a lighter, reference level, since none individually generates the search volume that would justify its own full-length dedicated post. Candidates from India, Nigeria, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Egypt, the Gulf states, Sri Lanka, South Africa, or the Philippines should instead read this site's dedicated, considerably deeper country-specific guides, which cover the full eligibility, verification, and timeline detail relevant to those larger source countries.

Do Caribbean medical graduates need to take the UKMLA?

Yes, Caribbean-trained doctors seeking full GMC registration follow the same UKMLA pathway as any other international medical graduate, although the specific national regulator and documentation requirements vary by individual island nation rather than being governed by a single Caribbean-wide body. Candidates should confirm their own country's regulator and their medical school's WDOMS status directly before beginning EPIC verification. Once eligibility is confirmed, the AKT and CPSA stages proceed identically to every other route covered on this site.

Conclusion

The UKMLA for international medical graduates follows one consistent process regardless of home country, and the differences between Ghana, Nepal, Malaysia, Ethiopia, Sudan, Zimbabwe, Oman, the Caribbean, and Southeast Asia lie almost entirely in local regulatory names, verification logistics, and currency, rather than in the exam or registration pathway itself. Consequently, candidates from any of these countries can approach the UKMLA with the same confidence as candidates from the site's larger dedicated country guides, provided they verify their own institution's WDOMS status, confirm their national regulator's exact certificate requirements, and budget carefully using a current currency conversion rather than an outdated figure. Wherever your journey begins, the safest starting point is always to confirm your own eligibility directly. Check your UKMLA eligibility now →
