post_title: UKMLA vs AMC and MCCQE: Comparing the UK Route to Australia and Canada
post_excerpt: How the UKMLA compares with Australia's AMC exam and Canada's MCCQE on cost, difficulty, timeline and career prospects, for doctors choosing between destination countries.
seo_title: UKMLA vs AMC Exam Comparison: UK or Australia?
seo_description: UKMLA vs AMC exam comparison: cost, difficulty and career prospects for doctors deciding between the UK, Australia (AMC) and Canada (MCCQE) routes.
html_content:
For a doctor deciding between the UK, Australia and Canada as a destination country, the honest short answer is this: each country requires passing a distinct national licensing assessment before you can register and practise, and none of the three can be substituted for another. The UKMLA vs AMC exam comparison ultimately comes down to the UK's Medical Licensing Assessment (AKT plus CPSA), Australia's AMC pathway (a computer-based knowledge test plus a separate clinical exam), and Canada's MCCQE (Part I and Part II) sitting alongside very different registration bodies, cost structures, and post-exam job markets. All three lead to a legitimate, internationally respected medical career, but the practical experience of getting there — timeline, cost, test-centre access from your home country, and how easily you land a first clinical post — differs enough that the decision deserves a proper side-by-side comparison rather than a guess based on forum chatter.

This guide sets out that comparison directly. It briefly recaps the UKMLA structure (with links to this site's dedicated exam-format posts for the full detail), then walks through the AMC pathway for Australia and the MCCQE pathway for Canada at a high, appropriately hedged level, before bringing all three together in a single comparison table covering exam structure, approximate cost, approximate timeline, test-centre accessibility, English language requirements, and how easy it typically is to find a first clinical post. It also looks at how candidates actually decide between the three destinations in practice, and at the small but real group of doctors who pursue more than one pathway at once. Throughout, figures for the AMC and MCCQE routes are presented as approximate and subject to change — candidates should always verify exact current fees and formats directly with the Australian Medical Council and the Medical Council of Canada before making financial or timeline commitments, since this site's core authority is the UK process, not Australian or Canadian regulatory specifics.

It is worth being clear from the outset why this three-way comparison matters more than it once did. A growing number of IMGs no longer default to a single destination country; instead, they research the UK, Australia and Canada in parallel, often alongside the US route covered in the UKMLA vs USMLE comparison, before committing serious time and money to any one licensing exam. Making that choice well requires comparing exam structure, cost, and timeline honestly across all three destinations rather than relying on outdated assumptions about which country is "easiest" — a claim none of the relevant regulators actually make about their own exams.

Many candidates researching this comparison arrive from a different starting point again: rather than choosing between the UK, Australia and Canada from scratch, they are Indian, Pakistani, Nigerian, or other Commonwealth-trained doctors who have already weighed a domestic postgraduate route against an international one, in the way the UKMLA vs NEET PG comparison sets out for Indian MBBS graduates choosing between NEET PG and the UK pathway. For that group, the UK-vs-Australia-vs-Canada question typically arises only after they have already decided against staying in their home country's postgraduate system, which is a genuinely separate decision from the one this guide focuses on. Therefore, readers who have not yet settled whether an international route makes sense for them at all may want to read that comparison first, before working through the UK-Australia-Canada breakdown that follows.

Furthermore, it is worth noting that "easiest" is doing a lot of unhelpful work in most informal comparisons of these three exams. None of the GMC, the AMC, or the Medical Council of Canada publishes a cross-country difficulty ranking, and for good reason: each body sets its own content map, its own pass standard, and its own clinical context, so a candidate's subjective sense of which exam felt "easier" says more about their own training background and language comfort than about any objective difference in rigour between the three assessments. Consequently, this guide deliberately avoids ranking the three exams by difficulty and instead focuses on the comparison points that actually are measurable and verifiable: structure, approximate cost, approximate timeline, test-centre access, and typical post-exam job-market experience.

UKMLA vs AMC vs MCCQE at a Glance

Before going into detail on each pathway, the table below summarises the headline differences between the three routes. It compares exam structure, rough total cost, and typical total timeline from a standing start to being eligible for a first clinical post, so readers can orient themselves before reading the fuller breakdown of each pathway.

FeatureUK (UKMLA)Australia (AMC)Canada (MCCQE)
Governing bodyGeneral Medical Council (GMC)Australian Medical Council (AMC) / Medical Board of AustraliaMedical Council of Canada (MCC) + provincial/territorial colleges
Exam structureTwo components: AKT (knowledge) and CPSA (clinical/OSCE)Two stages: AMC Computer Adaptive Test (knowledge) and AMC clinical examTwo parts: MCCQE Part I (knowledge) and MCCQE Part II (clinical skills, where required)
Approximate total costLower of the three routes, broadly in the low-thousands of pounds once registration is includedModerate to high; generally more than the UK route once both AMC stages and registration are factored in — verify current AMC fees directlyModerate to high; broadly comparable to or higher than the AMC route once both MCCQE parts and provincial licensing are included — verify current MCC fees directly
Approximate total timelineOften the fastest route from first booking to first clinical post, commonly well under two years for a well-prepared candidateTypically longer than the UK route once both AMC stages, registration processing, and job-seeking are combinedOften the longest of the three once both MCCQE parts, provincial licensing, and — for many IMGs — a mandatory practice-ready assessment or residency period are combined
Test-centre accessibility internationallyAKT widely available at Pearson VUE centres worldwide; CPSA in-person only in ManchesterKnowledge test available at computer-based test centres in a number of countries, though less universally than UK/US options; clinical exam traditionally concentrated in AustraliaPart I generally available at computer-based test centres in several countries; Part II has historically required travel to Canada, though format and delivery have evolved and should be checked directly

This table is deliberately high-level, because exact current AMC and MCCQE fee, format, and processing detail changes over time and is best confirmed directly with each regulator rather than asserted here as a fixed fact. However, the broad shape holds consistently across recent years: the UK route tends to be the cheapest and fastest of the three from exam to first job offer, while Australia and Canada both involve additional registration and, in many cases, additional practice-readiness steps beyond simply passing the licensing exam itself, which lengthens the realistic timeline for most IMGs.

The UKMLA Structure: A Brief Recap

The UKMLA is the Medical Licensing Assessment administered by the GMC, and it consists of exactly two components. The Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) is a single sitting of 200 single-best-answer questions delivered at Pearson VUE test centres worldwide, and it assesses applied clinical knowledge against the official MLA content map rather than pure theoretical recall. The Clinical and Professional Skills Assessment (CPSA) is an 18-station OSCE-style clinical exam sat in person at the GMC's Clinical Assessment Centre in Manchester, testing history-taking, examination, communication, and practical clinical skills.

This site covers the UKMLA's structure, fees, and scoring in far greater depth elsewhere, so rather than repeating that detail here, readers who want the full mechanics of the UK route should consult the complete guide to becoming a doctor in the UK and GMC registration, which sets out the entire pathway from eligibility through to a first NHS post. For the specific question of how the AKT is marked and what pass rates actually look like by candidate group, the AKT pass mark and pass rate explained is the relevant reference. Candidates who arrived at the UKMLA by way of the older PLAB numbering, or who are actively comparing the UK route against other established pathways, may also find the UKMLA vs PLAB pillar page and the dedicated UKMLA vs PLAB comparison post useful, since they cover in detail how PLAB 1 and PLAB 2 map onto today's AKT and CPSA. The GMC itself publishes the authoritative description of how the MLA assessments work in its official guide to the MLA assessments, and this remains the single source candidates should treat as definitive over any third-party summary, including this one.

What matters for this comparison is less the fine mechanics of the AKT and CPSA — covered thoroughly elsewhere on this site — and more how those two components stack up structurally against the AMC and MCCQE pathways discussed below. In broad terms, all three countries use a similar two-stage logic: a computer-based knowledge test followed by a practical clinical skills assessment. However, the specifics of format, cost, and where each stage can be sat differ meaningfully between the UK, Australia and Canada, and those differences are often more decisive for a candidate's actual choice of destination than the underlying exam content itself.

The AMC Pathway: Australia's Route to Registration

Australia's pathway for internationally trained doctors is coordinated by the Australian Medical Council (AMC), which assesses whether an IMG's qualifications and competence meet the standard required for registration with the Medical Board of Australia. Broadly, the AMC pathway for most IMGs not already eligible for a competent-authority or specialist pathway involves two main assessment stages, followed by registration.

The AMC Computer Adaptive Test (CAT)

The first stage is generally known as the AMC Computer Adaptive Test (CAT), a computer-based multiple-choice examination that assesses medical knowledge applied to clinical scenarios, broadly analogous in purpose to the UKMLA's AKT. It is delivered at computer-based test centres, and historically has been available in a number of countries internationally, though the exact list of test locations, sitting windows, and fee levels changes periodically and should always be confirmed directly on the AMC's own website rather than assumed from older sources. Candidates should treat any second-hand description of the CAT's exact question count, timing, or current fee as indicative rather than definitive, since this is precisely the kind of operational detail regulators update from time to time.

The AMC Clinical Exam

The second stage is the AMC clinical exam, a practical, multi-station assessment of clinical skills, communication, and professional judgement, broadly analogous in purpose to the UKMLA's CPSA. It has traditionally been delivered primarily at assessment centres within Australia, which is an important practical consideration for IMGs based overseas, since it generally implies at least one, and sometimes more than one, trip to Australia specifically for this stage of assessment. As with the CAT, exact current format, station count, and fee should be verified directly with the AMC, since clinical exam delivery arrangements are the kind of detail that can and does change between cohorts.

From AMC Assessment to Registration

Passing both AMC stages is generally a step towards eligibility for registration with the Medical Board of Australia, most commonly initially as general or limited registration, depending on the candidate's specific circumstances, supervision arrangements, and any additional requirements set by the Board. This registration process sits alongside, and is administratively separate from, the AMC's own assessment process, and candidates should expect a distinct application, documentation, and processing step with the Medical Board once AMC requirements are satisfied. Consequently, the realistic timeline from a first AMC booking to being registered and job-ready in Australia typically extends well beyond simply the time needed to pass the CAT and clinical exam themselves, since registration processing, visa arrangements, and — for many IMGs — securing a supervised position add further months to the overall journey.

Given how much of this pathway depends on current AMC policy, candidates seriously considering Australia should treat this section as an orientation rather than a substitute for the AMC's own published guidance, and should verify exact current fee levels, test-centre locations, and registration requirements directly with the Australian Medical Council and the Medical Board of Australia before committing time or money to this route.

It is also worth noting that not every IMG follows the standard AMC-assessment pathway described above. Doctors who qualified in certain recognised jurisdictions, or who hold specialist qualifications already recognised through a competent-authority or specialist-pathway route, may bypass some or all of the CAT and clinical exam requirements, subject to the Medical Board's own assessment of their individual circumstances. Consequently, candidates should not assume the two-stage AMC route described here automatically applies to them without first checking their own eligibility category, since Australia's registration framework is genuinely more layered than a single uniform pathway for every IMG. Additionally, supervised practice conditions attached to initial registration are common for IMGs entering through the standard pathway, meaning a newly registered doctor may spend a defined period working under a level of supervision before qualifying for less restricted registration — a further practical difference from the UK route, where GMC registration itself does not typically carry an equivalent blanket supervision requirement tied to the registration category.

The MCCQE Pathway: Canada's Route to Licensure

Canada's principal national licensing examination for physicians is the Medical Council of Canada Qualifying Examination (MCCQE), administered by the Medical Council of Canada (MCC). Similarly to the UK and Australian pathways, it is structured in two parts, though the practical route from passing the MCCQE to actually practising in Canada involves an additional layer of provincial and territorial regulation that does not have a direct equivalent in the UK system.

MCCQE Part I: The Knowledge-Based Examination

MCCQE Part I is a computer-based examination assessing the medical knowledge, clinical decision-making, and professional competencies expected of a physician beginning independent practice in Canada, and it is broadly analogous in purpose to the UKMLA's AKT and the AMC's CAT. It is generally available at computer-based test centres in a range of countries, which has historically made it one of the more internationally accessible stages across the three pathways compared here, though the precise list of eligible countries, test-centre network, and current fee should be confirmed directly with the MCC rather than assumed from prior years' information.

MCCQE Part II: The Clinical Skills Examination

MCCQE Part II assesses clinical skills, most commonly through an objective structured clinical examination format similar in spirit to the UK's CPSA and Australia's clinical exam, testing history-taking, physical examination, and clinical communication against simulated patients. The format, eligibility requirements, and delivery arrangements for Part II have evolved over recent years, and at various points MCC has changed how and where this component is delivered; consequently, candidates should check current MCC guidance directly for exact format and whether in-person travel to a Canadian test centre is required for their specific eligibility route, rather than relying on this or any other third-party summary.

From MCCQE to a Provincial Licence

Passing the MCCQE is a necessary step towards licensure, but it is not sufficient on its own — licensure in Canada is ultimately granted by the relevant provincial or territorial College of Physicians and Surgeons, not by the Medical Council of Canada directly. This means an IMG's practical pathway to practising in Canada depends significantly on which province or territory they intend to work in, since requirements, additional assessments, and the availability of practice-ready assessment programmes or residency positions for IMGs vary by jurisdiction. For many IMGs, securing a residency position or a recognised practice-ready assessment placement is a substantial additional hurdle beyond the MCCQE itself, and this step is often the single biggest source of delay in the overall Canadian pathway. Candidates should research their target province's specific college requirements directly, since generalising across all of Canada risks missing jurisdiction-specific detail that materially affects the realistic timeline.

As with the AMC pathway, this section is intended as a high-level, honestly hedged orientation rather than a definitive account of current MCCQE fees or formats. Anyone seriously pursuing the Canadian route should verify exact current detail directly with the Medical Council of Canada and their target province's medical regulatory college before making firm plans.

A further point worth flagging honestly is that many IMGs targeting Canada find the residency-matching stage, rather than the MCCQE itself, to be the most competitive part of the entire pathway. Canada runs its own national residency matching process for postgraduate training positions, and IMG applicants typically compete for a comparatively small pool of positions specifically designated for internationally trained candidates, separate from the larger pool available to Canadian medical graduates. This dynamic means that passing MCCQE Part I and Part II, while a genuine achievement, does not by itself guarantee that a candidate will secure a residency position in their preferred specialty or province, and some IMGs spend one or more additional years building Canadian clinical experience, research involvement, or reapplying to the match before securing a position. Candidates should research current match statistics and provincial IMG quotas directly through the relevant Canadian match organisation, since these figures shift from year to year and vary considerably by specialty.

UKMLA vs AMC vs MCCQE: The Full Comparison Table

Bringing the UK, Australian, and Canadian pathways together in a single table makes the practical trade-offs easier to weigh. The figures and descriptions below are deliberately comparative and hedged rather than stated as precise, fixed facts, since exact current AMC and MCCQE fees, formats, and processing times should always be verified directly with the relevant body before a candidate commits to a route.

Comparison PointUK (UKMLA)Australia (AMC)Canada (MCCQE)
Exam structureAKT (knowledge) + CPSA (clinical OSCE)AMC CAT (knowledge) + AMC clinical examMCCQE Part I (knowledge) + MCCQE Part II (clinical skills)
Approximate total costGenerally the lowest of the three routes; see the full UKMLA fee breakdown for IMGs for exact current figuresModerate to high relative to the UK route once both AMC stages and Medical Board registration are included; confirm exact current fees with the AMCModerate to high relative to the UK route once both MCCQE parts and provincial licensing fees are included; confirm exact current fees with the MCC and target province
Approximate total timelineOften the shortest, commonly under two years from first booking to a first clinical post for a well-prepared candidateTypically longer than the UK route; combined AMC processing, registration, and job-seeking frequently extends the timeline furtherOften the longest of the three, particularly where a residency position or practice-ready assessment placement is required before independent practice
Test-centre accessibility internationallyAKT widely available via Pearson VUE globally; CPSA is in-person in Manchester onlyCAT available at a number of international computer-based centres; clinical exam has traditionally required travel to AustraliaPart I available at computer-based centres in several countries; Part II delivery arrangements have varied and may require travel to Canada — verify current format
English language requirementIELTS or OET at GMC-specified score bandsEnglish language testing required at AMC/Medical Board-specified score bands, broadly similar in spirit to UK requirements but verify exact current thresholds with the AMCEnglish language testing required at MCC/provincial college-specified score bands; verify exact current thresholds directly, since requirements can differ slightly by province
Ease of finding a first clinical postComparatively direct — IMGs apply for NHS posts through NHS Jobs and trust recruitment once GMC-registered, with no centralised matching algorithmGenerally requires securing a supervised position tied to registration conditions; can be more competitive in popular states/cities and for candidates without existing Australian networksOften the most involved of the three, since many IMGs must also secure a residency position or practice-ready assessment placement, which is itself competitive and province-dependent

Two things stand out from this comparison. First, the UK route's combination of a single in-country clinical exam venue (Manchester) with no centralised job-matching algorithm makes its post-exam job-hunting phase comparatively predictable, whereas both Australia and Canada typically add a further layer of registration or placement-seeking that can extend the realistic timeline considerably. Second, the English language requirement is broadly similar in spirit across all three countries — each requires a recognised test at a defined score band — but the exact accepted tests and score thresholds differ by regulator and, in Canada's case, sometimes by province, so candidates should never assume a score that clears one country's bar automatically clears another's.

How Candidates Typically Decide Between the UK, Australia and Canada

In practice, the decision between these three destinations is rarely made on exam structure alone. Several recurring factors shape how real candidates choose, and naming them explicitly is more useful than pretending the decision is purely technical.

  • Family ties and existing connections. Candidates with relatives, a spouse's job opportunities, or an existing support network already established in one of the three countries frequently weight that factor above exam cost or timeline differences, since relocating close to family materially affects quality of life during what is already a demanding transition.
  • Cost of living in the destination country. Beyond the exam and registration fees themselves, the ongoing cost of living — housing, healthcare, taxation, and general affordability — varies significantly between the UK, Australia and Canada, and between cities within each country, and candidates budgeting realistically should research current cost-of-living data for their likely city of practice, not just the headline exam costs compared above.
  • Long-term immigration pathway. Each country's route from a work visa to permanent residency, and eventually citizenship, differs in structure, points criteria, and processing time. Candidates who prioritise a clear, well-understood long-term immigration pathway sometimes let this factor outweigh a marginally faster or cheaper exam route, since permanent settlement plans span decades, not months.
  • Existing diaspora community. A strong existing diaspora and professional network from a candidate's home country — whether Indian, Pakistani, Nigerian, Filipino, or otherwise — in a particular city or region often eases the practical and cultural transition considerably, and candidates frequently factor in where their compatriots have already established themselves professionally.
  • English-speaking test availability in the home country. Practical test-centre access matters more than candidates often expect before researching it properly. A route whose knowledge test is available at a computer-based centre close to home, without requiring international travel just to sit the first stage, is meaningfully more attractive to many candidates than a route requiring an expensive trip abroad even for the earliest assessment stage.

Consequently, two candidates with near-identical academic backgrounds can reasonably make different choices, and neither is wrong — the "right" destination genuinely depends on a mix of professional and personal priorities that goes well beyond which exam has the lower headline fee.

In addition to the factors above, many candidates also weigh how each country's health system is structured day to day, since this affects working life far more than the licensing exam itself does. The NHS is a single, nationally coordinated public system with standardised training grades and pay scales, which some candidates find reassuring for its predictability, while Australia's mixed public-private system and Canada's provincially administered healthcare system each offer a different balance of autonomy, private-practice opportunity, and regional variation in working conditions. Similarly, candidates who have already worked or trained under a particular country's clinical guidelines — for example, doctors from Commonwealth countries whose undergraduate curricula were historically modelled on UK practice — sometimes find one system's clinical conventions more immediately familiar than another's, which can meaningfully shorten the adjustment period after registration regardless of how the licensing exams themselves compare on paper.

Pursuing More Than One Pathway: Parallel and Sequential Approaches

Some doctors choose not to commit to a single destination early, instead pursuing more than one of the UKMLA, AMC, or MCCQE pathways in parallel or in sequence, as a deliberate hedge against uncertainty in any one country's job market, registration processing, or visa policy. This is logistically possible, since nothing in principle prevents a candidate from holding a UKMLA pass alongside AMC or MCCQE credentials, but it carries real, often underestimated costs that deserve honest consideration before committing to a dual-track strategy.

The most obvious cost is financial: pursuing two national licensing pathways roughly doubles direct exam and registration spending, and when travel for in-person clinical exam components — Manchester for the UK, Australia for the AMC clinical exam, and potentially Canada for MCCQE Part II — is added, the combined cost of a genuine dual-track approach can be substantial. Time is the scarcer resource in practice, however. Preparing properly for the UK's guideline-specific AKT and CPSA content draws on a different knowledge base than preparing for the AMC or MCCQE equivalents, even though all three test broadly similar core clinical competence, and most candidates find they cannot productively prepare for two full assessment cycles at full intensity simultaneously. The more sustainable pattern, in practice, is to sequence the two pathways — completing one substantially before beginning serious preparation for the other — rather than interleaving study for both from day one.

One practical question candidates often ask is whether credential-verification work can be reused across pathways. Primary source verification of medical degrees, transcripts, and internship completion is typically required by each country's regulator independently, and while the underlying documents are the same, each regulator generally runs its own verification process through its own preferred service, meaning the administrative work of gathering, certifying, and submitting documentation usually cannot simply be copied from one application to another. Consequently, candidates planning a dual-track approach should budget for genuinely separate verification and application administration for each pathway, rather than assuming that having already been verified once for one country meaningfully speeds up the equivalent process for another. English language test scores, by contrast, are sometimes more portable in principle — a strong IELTS or OET result can potentially satisfy more than one country's requirement — but candidates should confirm this directly against each regulator's current accepted-test list, since score bands and accepted test versions differ by country and are not automatically interchangeable.

Ultimately, whether a dual-track strategy makes sense depends on a candidate's tolerance for spreading preparation time thin, their financial capacity to fund two parallel administrative processes, and how much they value keeping multiple destination doors open against the efficiency of committing fully to the single route that best fits their circumstances. Neither approach is inherently wiser — it is a genuine trade-off between optionality and focus that each candidate needs to weigh against their own priorities.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is the UKMLA harder than the AMC exam?

There is no independently verified, official ranking of UKMLA versus AMC difficulty, since the two assessments are set by different regulators using different content maps, formats, and standard-setting methods. What can be said honestly is that the two pathways test broadly similar core clinical competence through a comparable two-stage structure — a knowledge test followed by a clinical skills exam — but comparing exact "difficulty" across them is not something either regulator publishes data to support.

Which is cheaper, UKMLA or MCCQE?

On available evidence, the UKMLA route tends to be the less expensive of the two once core exam and registration fees are combined, though exact current MCCQE and provincial licensing fees should always be confirmed directly with the Medical Council of Canada and the relevant provincial college, since fee schedules change periodically. Candidates should also factor in travel costs for any required in-person components — Manchester for the UK's CPSA, and potentially a Canadian test centre for MCCQE Part II — when comparing genuine total cost rather than headline exam fees alone.

Can I sit the AMC exam outside Australia?

The AMC's computer-based knowledge test (the CAT) has historically been available at test centres in a number of countries outside Australia, though the exact current list of locations should be confirmed directly with the AMC. The AMC clinical exam, however, has traditionally been delivered primarily within Australia, meaning most IMGs should expect to travel to Australia specifically for that stage regardless of where they sit the knowledge test.

Do I need to travel to Canada for the MCCQE?

MCCQE Part I is generally available at computer-based test centres in several countries, reducing the need for international travel for that stage alone. MCCQE Part II's delivery arrangements have varied over recent years, and depending on current MCC policy and a candidate's specific eligibility route, travel to a Canadian test centre may or may not be required — this should be confirmed directly with the Medical Council of Canada rather than assumed from prior years' arrangements.

Which route is fastest: UK, Australia, or Canada?

On the evidence available, the UK route is generally the fastest of the three from a first exam booking to starting a clinical post, often achievable in under two years for a well-prepared candidate, partly because there is no centralised job-matching process standing between GMC registration and applying for NHS posts. Both the Australian and Canadian routes typically involve additional registration or placement-seeking steps — such as securing a supervised position in Australia or a residency/practice-ready assessment placement in Canada — that tend to extend the realistic overall timeline.

Can I use the same documents for UKMLA, AMC, and MCCQE applications?

Generally, no in a fully interchangeable sense. Each regulator — the GMC, the AMC, and the Medical Council of Canada together with the relevant provincial college — typically runs its own independent primary source verification process, even though the underlying degree certificates, transcripts, and internship records are the same documents. Candidates pursuing more than one pathway should budget time and administrative effort for separate verification and application processes with each body rather than assuming one verification satisfies all three.

Is it realistic to prepare for UKMLA and AMC or MCCQE at the same time?

It is logistically possible but demanding, since each pathway's knowledge test is mapped to a different national clinical guideline set and each clinical skills exam has its own station format and expectations. Most candidates who genuinely want to keep more than one destination open find it more sustainable to sequence their preparation — completing one pathway substantially before committing to serious preparation for another — rather than attempting to peak for two full assessment cycles simultaneously.

Does passing the UKMLA, AMC, or MCCQE guarantee a job?

No, for any of the three. Passing the UKMLA makes an IMG eligible for GMC registration and, from there, eligible to apply directly for NHS posts, which is a comparatively direct process without a centralised matching algorithm. Passing the AMC pathway or the MCCQE similarly makes a candidate eligible for registration or licensure, but securing an actual clinical post in Australia or Canada typically still depends on separate job-seeking, supervised-position arrangements, or — in Canada's case — a competitive residency or practice-ready assessment process.

Conclusion

Comparing UKMLA and AMC, or comparing UKMLA and MCCQE, is ultimately a comparison of three legitimate but structurally different national licensing systems, each leading to a respected medical career, but each demanding a different mix of cost, time, and administrative persistence. The UK route tends to offer the fastest and least expensive path from exam to first clinical post, while the AMC and MCCQE pathways in Australia and Canada often involve additional registration or placement-seeking steps that lengthen the realistic timeline, though both remain well-established, credible routes for IMGs who prioritise those destinations for personal, professional, or immigration reasons. Whichever country a candidate ultimately chooses, the decision deserves the same standard of accurate, current, regulator-verified information, rather than assumptions carried over from forums or outdated blog posts. Readers who have concluded that the UK route fits their circumstances best should confirm their standing against the GMC's requirements before booking anything.

post_title: UKMLA AKT Pass Mark and Pass Rate Explained
post_excerpt: How the UKMLA AKT pass mark is set using the Angoff method, where to find official pass-rate data, and what factors actually influence a candidate's chance of passing.
seo_title: UKMLA AKT Pass Mark and Pass Rate Explained
seo_description: UKMLA AKT pass mark explained: how the Angoff-based cut score works, where to find official GMC pass-rate data, and what affects your chance of passing.
html_content:
The UKMLA AKT pass mark is one of the most searched, and most misunderstood, figures in the whole examination. Candidates often want a single number — "what percentage do I need?" — but the honest answer is that the UKMLA AKT does not use a fixed percentage that stays the same every sitting. Instead, the exact number of marks needed to pass is set individually for each sitting through a formal process called Angoff standard setting, which means the cut score can move slightly from one diet to the next depending on that sitting's specific mix of questions. This post takes the practical angle: what the pass mark actually means for you, how to read your own result against it, what is honestly known about pass rates, and which preparation factors genuinely move the needle on your chances of meeting the standard.

Before going further, it is worth being clear about what this post does not do. The detailed mechanics of how the pass mark is calculated — the expert panels, the Minimally Competent Candidate concept, the item-by-item ratings — are covered in full in our dedicated piece on Angoff standard setting. Here, the focus stays on interpretation: what your sitting's pass mark tells you, how it compares with other sittings, and how to use published pass-rate patterns sensibly rather than anxiously.

Quick Answer: What Is the UKMLA AKT Pass Mark?

The UKMLA AKT pass mark is not a fixed percentage such as 60% that applies to every sitting. It is a criterion-referenced cut score, expressed as a specific number of marks out of the total available, that is recalculated for each individual sitting using the Angoff method. Consequently, the exact mark you need to pass in your sitting may differ slightly from the mark a candidate needed in the previous or following sitting, because each sitting uses a different set of questions with a different overall difficulty profile.

This is a genuinely important distinction for candidates to internalise early in their preparation. A candidate who fixates on a remembered "60% target" from a forum post or a well-meaning senior colleague is anchoring to the wrong number — that figure may have applied to a completely different sitting with a different difficulty profile. In addition, because the AKT cut score is set before most candidates even sit the paper (the standard-setting panel does its work independently of live candidate performance, subject to later psychometric adjustment), there is no meaningful sense in which "most people pass around X%" translates into a target for you to aim for.

The GMC publishes the specific pass mark for each sitting alongside results, rather than a single number that applies indefinitely. Therefore, the only pass mark that matters to you is the one published for your own sitting, communicated through your results report. Aiming to comprehensively demonstrate competency across the full MLA content map, rather than chasing a remembered percentage, is the only strategy that is robust to this variation.

It also helps to be clear about what kind of number the AKT cut score actually is. It is not a percentile rank, it is not a curve fitted to how the cohort happened to perform, and it is not adjusted afterwards to produce a target pass rate. It is a raw mark, out of the total number of scored items in that sitting, derived from expert judgement about what a borderline-competent candidate should be able to achieve. Once you accept that the number itself is sitting-specific, several common candidate anxieties resolve themselves fairly quickly. Comparing your predicted score against a friend's remembered pass mark from a different diet, for example, is simply comparing two different measurements that were never meant to be equivalent in the first place.

Why the Pass Mark Varies Between Sittings

The UKMLA AKT pass mark varies sitting-to-sitting because each sitting is built from an entirely different set of questions, and each of those question sets is independently judged for difficulty by a panel of clinical and educational experts before the exam is sat. This is not an oversight or an inconsistency in the system — it is a deliberate design feature that exists specifically to keep the exam fair when question difficulty inevitably fluctuates between diets.

In plain terms, here is what happens behind the scenes:

  • A new bank of questions is assembled for each sitting, drawn from a much larger item pool.
  • A panel of experienced clinicians, educators, and psychometricians reviews that specific question set and estimates, item by item, how a borderline-competent candidate would be expected to perform.
  • Those item-level judgements are summed to produce the pass mark for that sitting alone.
  • If that sitting's questions happen to be, on the whole, more demanding than the previous sitting's, the resulting pass mark will typically be lower — and conversely, an easier question set produces a higher pass mark.

Consequently, the pass mark is best understood as a reflection of that sitting's specific difficulty, not as a moving average or a random fluctuation. The full mechanics of this process — including the Minimally Competent Candidate concept, the rating rounds, and the psychometric adjustments applied afterwards — are explained step by step in our companion post on Angoff standard setting explained, and it is worth reading if you want the "why" behind the number rather than just the practical implications covered here.

Why This Is Fairer Than a Fixed Percentage

It might initially feel more reassuring to have one unchanging number to aim for. However, a fixed percentage pass mark would actually be less fair to candidates, not more, because it assumes every sitting is equally difficult — an assumption that is empirically untrue for any exam drawing from a large, rotating item bank. If a fixed 60% pass mark were applied regardless of difficulty, candidates unlucky enough to sit a harder paper would be penalised twice: once by facing harder questions, and again by being held to the same raw score as candidates who sat an easier paper.

By contrast, a variable, expert-calibrated pass mark corrects for this automatically. A harder sitting produces a lower cut score; an easier sitting produces a higher one. As a result, a candidate's chance of passing depends on their competency relative to the clinical standard, rather than on the luck of which sitting they happened to be allocated. This is precisely the same logic used by other major English-language licensing examinations, including PLAB 1 and the Australian Medical Council's MCQ examination, both of which also use Angoff-based, criterion-referenced standard setting rather than a static percentage.

There is also a subtler fairness argument worth making explicit. A fixed percentage pass mark effectively outsources the definition of competency to whichever question writers happened to draft that year's paper, since an unusually easy paper would let more borderline candidates through at 60%, while an unusually hard paper would fail candidates who were genuinely competent but simply encountered tougher items. An expert-calibrated, sitting-specific pass mark instead keeps the definition of competency constant and lets the raw number move instead — which is precisely what should happen if the goal is a stable standard rather than a stable-looking number.

How This Compares With Norm-Referenced Grading

It is worth briefly contrasting this criterion-referenced approach with norm-referenced grading, since many candidates are more familiar with the latter from undergraduate coursework, where marks are sometimes adjusted to fit a target grade distribution across a cohort. Under a norm-referenced system, a fixed proportion of candidates would pass regardless of how competent the whole cohort actually was — meaning a uniformly weak cohort could see most candidates pass, while a uniformly strong cohort could see many candidates fail simply because they were compared against unusually capable peers. The UKMLA AKT deliberately avoids this model. Your result depends on your own performance against the clinical standard, not on how the rest of your specific cohort happened to perform.

Where to Find Your Sitting's Pass Mark and Your Own Result

Candidates find out their own sitting's pass mark, and how their own result compares to it, through the official results reporting channel for their route into the profession — GMC Online for most candidates, with UK medical schools typically also communicating results through their own examination boards for finalists sitting the AKT as part of their degree. The results report does not simply tell you "pass" or "fail" in isolation; it situates your score against that sitting's specific cut score, so you can see exactly how far above or below the threshold you fell.

When your results become available, a few things are worth checking carefully:

  • The specific pass mark that applied to your sitting, rather than any percentage you may have seen quoted for a previous diet.
  • Your own raw score, expressed against that same scale, so the comparison is meaningful.
  • Any domain-level or content-area breakdown provided, which can indicate where your performance was strongest and weakest relative to the standard.

Interpreting this report properly is a skill in itself, particularly for candidates who narrowly failed and are trying to work out what to change before a resit. Our dedicated guide on the UKMLA results feedback report walks through how to read every section of that report in detail, including how to translate a domain breakdown into a targeted revision plan. It is worth reading alongside this post, since the pass mark on its own only tells you whether you cleared the bar — the feedback report tells you why.

For a broader view of how results, scoring, and reporting fit together across both AKT and CPSA, our results and scoring guide provides the pillar-level overview, while this post focuses specifically on how to interpret the AKT cut score itself.

Reading a Borderline Result Correctly

Candidates who score very close to the pass mark — whether just above or just below — often find this the hardest result to interpret emotionally, even though it is one of the most statistically explicable outcomes in the whole system. No examination measures competency with perfect precision; every test score carries a margin of measurement error, meaning a candidate's true underlying competency could plausibly have produced a slightly higher or slightly lower observed score on a different day, even with no change in their actual knowledge. A result within a few marks of the cut score therefore sits in a genuinely uncertain zone, and it is worth resisting the urge to over-interpret a narrow pass as proof of mastery or a narrow fail as proof of inadequacy. Both outcomes call for the same sensible response: identify the specific content or question-style gaps visible in the results feedback report, and address them directly.

What Is Known About UKMLA AKT Pass Rates

Published pass-rate statistics for the UKMLA are released periodically by the GMC, often in collaboration with the Medical Schools Council where UK final-year students are concerned, since final-year students sit the AKT as an integrated part of their medical degree rather than as a standalone licensing step. It is important to be precise here: this post deliberately avoids quoting a specific pass-rate percentage, because that figure changes between cohorts and sittings, and any number written into a static blog post risks becoming stale or misleading almost immediately.

What can be said honestly, in qualitative terms, is that the pattern of results tends to differ meaningfully between the two main candidate groups:

  • UK final-year medical students sitting the AKT as a compulsory part of their degree generally benefit from structured, longitudinal preparation embedded in their curriculum, cohort-wide mock exams, and institutional exam-technique support built up over several years of training within the same assessment culture the AKT was designed to sit alongside.
  • IMG candidates sitting the AKT as their route to GMC registration typically come from a much wider variety of undergraduate training backgrounds, educational systems, and prior assessment styles, and often prepare independently, without an institution running structured mock sittings or dedicated exam-technique teaching aligned specifically to the UKMLA's format.

As a result of these differing preparation contexts, published aggregate figures have generally shown a different pass-rate pattern between the two groups, with IMG cohorts tending to show a lower aggregate pass rate than UK final-year cohorts. This is not a reflection of clinical ability; rather, it reflects the fact that IMG candidates are, on average, sitting an examination that is calibrated to a specific national curriculum and NHS-based clinical-guideline style that they may not have been immersed in during undergraduate training, and are frequently doing so without the same institutional scaffolding that UK final-year students receive by default.

The table below summarises this pattern in qualitative terms, deliberately without invented percentages, since exact figures should always be checked against the GMC's own published data rather than a static summary like this one.

FactorUK Final-Year StudentsIMG Candidates
Preparation structure Embedded in the medical degree, with cohort-wide mocks and faculty-led revision Usually self-directed, often alongside full-time clinical work
Exposure to NHS/NICE guideline style Continuous, built up over years of UK-based clinical placements Variable, often requiring deliberate additional study
Institutional exam-technique support Typically provided by the medical school's assessment team Typically not provided; candidates source this independently
Aggregate pass-rate pattern Generally higher, per published GMC/MSC data Generally lower, per published GMC data, reflecting the factors above

None of these differences are fixed or inevitable, and they describe an average pattern across large cohorts rather than a prediction about any individual candidate. An IMG candidate who deliberately builds NHS-guideline familiarity and structured mock practice into their preparation is directly addressing the factors behind the aggregate gap, rather than being bound by it.

For the exact, current pass-rate figures, which are the only figures worth relying on for planning purposes since they are updated as new data becomes available, readers should consult the GMC's own published statistics directly via its official MLA assessments page, which remains the authoritative source on both methodology and outcomes. Any third-party site quoting a specific fixed pass-rate percentage without linking back to that primary source should be treated with appropriate caution.

It is also worth putting these figures into a broader international context. Readers weighing up the UKMLA against other licensing routes — for instance, candidates deciding between the UK, Australia, and Canada, or simply wanting a sense of relative exam difficulty — may find our UKMLA, AMC, and MCCQE comparison useful context, since pass-rate patterns and preparation demands differ meaningfully across these systems too.

Factors That Genuinely Influence Your Chance of Passing

While the pass mark itself is outside any individual candidate's control, several preparation factors are firmly within it, and these are the practical levers most worth focusing your energy on. Understanding the mechanics of standard setting is useful context, but it does not, by itself, get you across the line — deliberate preparation does.

Systematic Coverage of the Official Content Map

The single most reliable predictor of AKT success is comprehensive, systematic coverage of the official MLA content map, rather than an ad hoc revision approach built around whichever topics feel most comfortable or most recently taught. Because the exam is blueprinted against this content map — meaning questions are drawn from it in proportion to clinical importance and frequency — candidates who work through it presentation by presentation, rather than skipping around, are directly aligning their preparation with how the exam is actually constructed. Furthermore, treating high-frequency, high-stakes presentations (chest pain, breathlessness, sepsis, and similarly common acute scenarios) as non-negotiable revision priorities reflects the fact that these areas are judged, by the standard-setting panel, to be ones a minimally competent candidate should reliably get right.

In practice, systematic coverage means working from a checklist rather than a feeling of familiarity. Many candidates unconsciously gravitate towards topics they already find interesting or that were taught particularly well at their medical school, while quietly avoiding presentations they find dull or that were covered only briefly during undergraduate training. Over a long revision period, this creates exactly the kind of uneven coverage the content map is designed to catch, since the AKT samples proportionally across the whole breadth of UK clinical practice rather than concentrating on any single specialty. Working through the content map methodically, marking off each presentation as genuinely revised rather than merely recognised, is a simple but effective safeguard against this bias.

It is equally important not to interpret "systematic" as "exhaustive to the point of diminishing returns." Some rare, highly specialised presentations receive comparatively little weight in the standard-setting panel's judgements precisely because they are rare, and spending disproportionate revision time on them at the expense of common acute presentations is a poor use of a candidate's limited preparation time. A sensible approach allocates revision time roughly in proportion to clinical frequency and stakes, mirroring how the exam itself is blueprinted.

Exposure to UK-Specific NHS and NICE-Guideline Question Style

For many IMG candidates specifically, the gap between undergraduate clinical knowledge and AKT performance is not really a knowledge gap at all — it is a question-style gap. Candidates trained under different national guidelines may know the underlying clinical science perfectly well, yet still select the "wrong" answer on an AKT-style single-best-answer question because the correct option reflects a specific NHS pathway or a NICE guideline recommendation rather than a more generic international standard of care. This distinction matters enormously, because it means the fix is not "study harder" in a generic sense but rather deliberate, targeted exposure to NHS-based and NICE-guideline-based scenarios until that specific style of clinical reasoning becomes familiar. Practising with a large volume of UK-guideline-aligned single-best-answer questions, and consciously noting where your instinctive answer diverges from the NICE-recommended one, is one of the most effective ways to close this gap.

High-yield areas where this style gap shows up particularly often include prescribing decisions and drug-safety scenarios. Our post on pharmacology and prescribing safety is a good example of the kind of high-yield, NHS-specific content area that rewards this systematic, guideline-literate style of revision rather than generic pharmacology knowledge alone.

This question-style gap also tends to show up in areas such as referral pathways, safety-netting advice, and first-line management choices, where several answer options may all be clinically defensible in a general sense, but only one matches the specific pathway a NICE guideline or NHS protocol recommends. Candidates who have trained exclusively in health systems with different referral thresholds, different first-line drug choices, or different escalation criteria can find these questions disproportionately difficult relative to their actual clinical knowledge, simply because the "best" answer is defined by a specific national guideline rather than by general clinical reasoning. Recognising this pattern early in preparation, rather than mid-exam, allows candidates to deliberately seek out NICE-guideline-referenced question banks and revision resources rather than relying solely on general international clinical textbooks.

Realistic Mock Exam Practice Under Timed Conditions

Timed, full-length mock papers that replicate real sitting conditions are an essential preparation tool, both for content revision and for exam-technique reasons. Candidates who only practise questions in short, untimed bursts often discover on the actual exam day that time pressure changes their decision-making — rushed second-guessing, panic at unfamiliar question stems, or simply running out of time on the final block of questions. Building stamina and pacing through realistic mock conditions well before the real sitting therefore addresses a genuinely controllable variable in your performance.

Exam-day anxiety itself is also a real and controllable factor, and it is worth taking seriously rather than dismissing as unavoidable nerves. Because performance anxiety can meaningfully suppress a candidate's ability to demonstrate knowledge they genuinely have, our guide on managing UKMLA exam anxiety is directly relevant here — worrying excessively about where the pass mark will land, rather than focusing on your own preparation and technique, is exactly the kind of unproductive anxiety that guide addresses.

It is also worth understanding the practical exam-day format itself before sitting a mock, since unfamiliarity with logistics (timing per block, navigation between questions, flagging for review) can cost marks unrelated to clinical knowledge. Our AKT format and preparation guide covers this background in detail and is a useful companion to mock-exam practice.

Mock practice is most valuable when it is treated as a diagnostic tool rather than purely a confidence exercise. Simply completing a mock paper and noting an overall score tells you very little on its own; reviewing every incorrect answer, understanding precisely why the correct option was preferred over your choice, and tracking recurring error patterns across multiple mocks is what actually converts practice into improvement. Candidates who sit several mocks without ever returning to review their mistakes in depth often plateau well before the real exam, because they are repeating the same errors under slightly different clinical scenarios each time.

For Repeat Candidates: Targeted Analysis of a Previous Results Report

Candidates resitting the AKT face a specific, and very common, trap: treating a resit as an opportunity to simply "revise everything again, but harder." This is rarely the most efficient approach, and it often fails to address the actual reason the previous attempt fell short. Instead, a resitting candidate's first step should be a careful analysis of their previous results feedback report, identifying specific domains or content areas where performance was genuinely weak relative to the standard, rather than assuming the shortfall was evenly spread across the whole syllabus.

In practice, this means asking targeted questions of your own previous result: which content areas or presentation types showed weaker relative performance? Was there a pattern suggesting a question-style issue (for example, consistently choosing clinically plausible but non-NICE-aligned answers) rather than a pure knowledge gap? Answering these questions honestly, using your own data rather than generic advice, is what separates an efficient resit preparation plan from a repeat of the same mistakes.

What Happens If You Don't Meet the Pass Mark

If your score falls below your sitting's published pass mark, this is a meaningful signal that additional preparation is needed before entering independent practice — not a verdict on your long-term potential as a clinician, and importantly, not something that happens in isolation, since resitting is a well-established, structured pathway used by many successful doctors before them. The practical process — how many resit attempts are permitted, how soon you can sit again, and how to plan a resit strategically — is covered in full in our dedicated post on UKMLA resits and appeals, so it is not repeated in depth here.

The one point worth emphasising in the context of the pass mark specifically is this: a narrow miss, particularly one within a few marks of the published cut score for your sitting, reflects the genuine statistical uncertainty inherent in any examination rather than a dramatic shortfall in competency. Consequently, candidates in this position are often well served by a targeted review of their weaker domains rather than a wholesale re-revision of everything, exactly as described in the previous section.

It is also worth resisting two common but unhelpful reactions to a below-pass-mark result. The first is to assume the sitting's pass mark itself was set unfairly high — a belief that, however understandable emotionally, is not usually supported by the evidence, since the standard-setting process is applied consistently and independently of any individual candidate's outcome. The second is to over-correct by abandoning a structured revision plan altogether in favour of frantic, unfocused re-reading of everything at once. Neither response addresses the specific gap that produced the result. A calmer, evidence-based approach — reviewing the feedback report, identifying two or three genuinely weak domains, and building a focused plan around them — is consistently the more productive path towards a successful resit.

Candidates should also remember that a below-pass-mark result on one sitting does not retroactively change how that sitting's pass mark was calculated, nor does it affect the pass mark of any future sitting. Each sitting is assessed entirely on its own terms, which means a disappointing result is genuinely a fresh starting point rather than a permanent mark against a candidate's record within the examination system itself.

Common Misconceptions About the UKMLA AKT Pass Mark

A number of persistent myths circulate among candidates about how the pass mark works, often passed along informally between cohorts without ever being checked against the official methodology. Addressing these directly is useful, since believing them can lead to genuinely counterproductive preparation decisions.

Myth: "The pass mark is always around 60%." This figure may have applied to a specific past sitting, but treating it as a permanent benchmark misunderstands how the cut score is generated. Because the mark is recalculated for each sitting based on that sitting's specific question difficulty, a remembered historical figure tells you very little about what your own sitting will require.

Myth: "If more people fail, the exam board must lower the pass mark afterwards to fix it." The pass mark for a given sitting is fundamentally set by the standard-setting panel's judgement about question difficulty, subject to defined psychometric adjustments such as equating and the removal of flawed items — not by adjusting the outcome to hit a target pass rate after the fact. A high fail rate in a particular sitting is not, by itself, evidence that the standard was set incorrectly; it may simply reflect that a genuinely harder set of questions still produced a defensible standard.

Myth: "IMGs are held to a different, harder pass mark than UK students." This is inaccurate. Both groups sitting the same version of the AKT in the same sitting are held to the same pass mark for that sitting, since the standard is set against the examination content itself, not against the candidate's background or route into the profession. The pattern of differing aggregate pass rates described earlier in this post reflects differences in preparation context and guideline familiarity, not a differently calibrated standard applied to different groups.

Myth: "Scoring well above the pass mark in a question bank guarantees a pass on the real AKT." As covered above, question banks and the real examination often have different difficulty distributions and question-writing conventions, so a comfortable margin in a question bank does not translate directly into an equivalent margin on the actual AKT. Question bank performance remains a useful trend indicator, but it should not be treated as a precise forecast of your result against the sitting-specific pass mark.

Myth: "A single very difficult question means the whole paper's pass mark will be unreasonably high." The pass mark is derived from the sum of expert judgements across every question in the paper, not from any single item in isolation. A handful of difficult questions are typically balanced by others judged more accessible to a borderline-competent candidate, and the overall cut score reflects that full balance rather than the hardest or easiest individual question.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the pass mark for the UKMLA AKT?

The UKMLA AKT does not have one fixed pass mark that applies to every sitting. Instead, the pass mark is calculated individually for each sitting using the Angoff standard-setting method, meaning it can vary slightly depending on that sitting's specific question difficulty. The GMC publishes the exact pass mark for each sitting alongside candidates' results, so the only figure that applies to you is the one attached to your own sitting.

Does the pass mark change every sitting?

Yes, the pass mark can change from one sitting to the next, because each sitting uses a different set of exam questions that is independently assessed for difficulty by an expert panel before results are calculated. A sitting with a harder overall question set will typically produce a slightly lower pass mark, while an easier sitting will typically produce a slightly higher one, so that the standard being applied stays consistent even though the raw number moves.

Is the UKMLA AKT pass rate different for IMGs and UK students?

Published aggregate data has generally shown a different pattern between UK final-year medical students, who benefit from institutional exam-technique support and longitudinal preparation embedded in their degree, and IMG candidates, who often prepare independently from more varied educational backgrounds. This does not reflect a difference in underlying clinical ability, but rather a difference in exposure to the specific NHS and NICE-guideline-based question style the exam uses. Exact current figures are published by the GMC and should be checked directly rather than relied upon from secondhand sources.

How do I find out my own sitting's pass mark?

Your own sitting's pass mark is communicated as part of your official results report, typically through GMC Online or your medical school's examination board if you are a UK final-year student. This report situates your score directly against that sitting's specific cut score, rather than against a generic percentage, so you can see precisely how your result compares to the standard that applied to you.

Why doesn't the UKMLA just use a fixed percentage like 60%?

A fixed percentage pass mark would assume every sitting's questions are equally difficult, which is not a realistic assumption for a large, rotating exam question bank. Using expert judgement to calibrate the pass mark to each specific sitting's difficulty, instead of a static number, protects candidates from being unfairly disadvantaged simply because they were allocated a harder sitting than someone else.

What should I do if I don't meet the pass mark?

A result below the published pass mark for your sitting means further preparation is needed before you can demonstrate the required standard, and resitting is a well-established, structured route that many candidates use successfully. The most effective next step is usually a careful review of your previous results feedback report to identify genuine weak areas, rather than a generic repeat of your original revision plan, alongside understanding the formal resit process itself.

Does question bank performance predict my AKT pass mark accurately?

Question bank percentages are a useful directional guide to your progress, but they are not a precise prediction of your AKT outcome, because question banks and the real AKT often differ in their difficulty distribution and question style. It is more useful to track whether your performance is trending upward and where your specific content or domain weaknesses lie, rather than treating any single question bank percentage as equivalent to your likely AKT score.

Conclusion

The UKMLA AKT pass mark is not a number you can simply memorise in advance from a forum post or a previous cohort's result — it is calculated freshly for each sitting, reflecting that sitting's specific question difficulty as judged by an expert panel, and published to you individually alongside your own result. Rather than chasing a remembered percentage, the most reliable strategy is thorough, systematic preparation across the full content map, deliberate exposure to UK-specific guideline-based question style, realistic timed mock practice, and, for resitting candidates, a targeted review of genuine weak areas from a previous results report. Understood this way, the pass mark stops being a source of anxiety and becomes simply the standard your preparation needs to meet.

See the full UKMLA results and scoring guide →
